topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Vapour Phase Epitaxy and Molecular Beam Epitaxy, Lithography

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1 — VAPOUR PHASE EPITAXY, MOLECULAR BEAM EPITAXY & LITHOGRAPHY

## 1.1 Vapour Phase Epitaxy (VPE) — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Vapour Phase Epitaxy (VPE)* is a Chemical Vapour Deposition (CVD)-based epitaxial growth process in which a thin, single-crystalline semiconductor film is deposited on a heated single-crystal substrate through the chemical reaction of volatile precursor gases transported in a carrier gas stream.

The word *epitaxy* is Greek: **epi** (upon) + **taxis** (arrangement) — meaning the orderly arrangement of atoms **upon** a substrate in a perfect crystalline continuation.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: The "Snow on a Mirror" Concept
Imagine a perfectly polished mirror (the silicon substrate) placed in a warm room. When moist, controlled steam (the vapor of SiCl₄ + H₂) is gently blown across the mirror's surface, the water molecules do **not** form random ice droplets. Instead, they align themselves precisely to the atomic grid of the mirror's surface, building a **new, perfect crystal layer** on top. That is exactly what VPE does — it grows an atomically ordered single-crystal film from a vapor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Metric:** The substrate temperature in commercial VPE systems is typically held between **900 °C and 1250 °C**, with growth rates of **0.1 – 1 μm/min**. The process is a **chemical reaction–driven** growth, not a physical deposition.

---

## 1.2 Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE) — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)* is an Ultra-High Vacuum (UHV) physical vapor deposition technique in which thermally generated beams of atoms or molecules from independent effusion cells (Knudsen cells) are directed at a heated single-crystal substrate, producing layer-by-layer epitaxial growth with monolayer (≈ 0.3 nm for Si) precision.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: The "Atomic Spray Painting" Concept
Picture a master painter in a dust-free sealed room (UHV ≈ **10⁻¹⁰ Torr**), using several fine atomizers, each filled with a different colour paint (Ga, As, Al, dopants). Each atomizer is precisely temperature-controlled so the paint evaporates at a known rate. The painter sprays, layer by monolayer, onto a canvas (the substrate), and a camera (RHEED — Reflection High-Energy Electron Diffraction) tells him in real time exactly when to stop. That is MBE.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Metrics:** Background pressure is **< 10⁻¹⁰ Torr**; growth temperature is **500 °C – 700 °C**; growth rate is **1 – 2 μm/hr** (i.e., ≈ 1 monolayer/second). MBE is a **physical beam** technique, not a chemical reaction process.

---

## 1.3 Lithography — Core Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024):** *Lithography* in VLSI is the process of transferring a geometric pattern from a photomask (or direct-write source) onto a photosensitive polymer film (photoresist) coated on a semiconductor wafer, using selective exposure to photons, electrons, or X-rays, followed by chemical development to create a patterned mask for subsequent etching or doping.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy: The "Stencil Tattoo" Concept
Think of lithography as airbrushing through a stencil onto a T-shirt. The stencil (photomask) has the pattern cut out; the spray (UV light) goes only through the cut-outs onto the light-sensitive coating (photoresist). After "developing" (rinsing), the unexposed resist washes away, leaving the pattern that protects the shirt during the next colouring (etching) step.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Operating Metric:** The minimum printable feature size is governed by the **Rayleigh Resolution Criterion**: $R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$, where the proportionality constant **k₁ ≥ 0.25** is the theoretical lower bound for conventional optics.

---

## 1.4 Why These Three Processes Form the Backbone of VLSI

| Process | Role in VLSI | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|
| **VPE** | Thick epitaxial layers (e.g., buried layers, power-device drift regions) | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| **MBE** | Ultra-thin, atomically abrupt heterojunctions (HEMTs, quantum wells) | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| **Lithography** | Pattern transfer — the **enabling step** of every IC | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Vapour Phase Epitaxy — Operating Theory

### 2.1.1 The Governing Chemical Reaction (Silicon VPE)

The most industrially important VPE reaction is the **hydrogen reduction of silicon tetrachloride**:

$$\text{SiCl}_4 \, (g) + 2\,\text{H}_2 \, (g) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Si} \, (s) + 4\,\text{HCl} \, (g)$$

A secondary reaction is the **silane pyrolysis route** (lower temperature, often used for selective epitaxy):

$$\text{SiH}_4 \, (g) \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Si} \, (s) + 2\,\text{H}_2 \, (g)$$

### 2.1.2 Step-by-Step Growth Mechanism (Why and How)

- **Step 1 — Mass Transport (Convection/Diffusion):** The reactant species (SiCl₄) is carried by H₂ from the cold inlet zone to the hot reaction zone above the susceptor. The boundary layer thickness δ over the wafer controls this step.
- **Step 2 — Gas-Phase Reaction:** SiCl₄ decomposes (in part) in the gas phase to form SiCl₂ and SiHCl₃ intermediates. This is normally the rate-limiting step at high temperature.
- **Step 3 — Surface Adsorption:** Reactive species (mainly SiCl₂) adsorb on the substrate surface.
- **Step 4 — Surface Diffusion & Reaction:** Adsorbed atoms migrate on the hot surface, find proper lattice sites, and incorporate — releasing HCl as a byproduct.
- **Step 5 — Desorption:** HCl and H₂ desorb and exit through the exhaust.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off:** At low T (kinetic regime), the surface reaction is the rate-limiting step → **Arrhenius-type temperature dependence**. At high T (mass-transport regime), the growth rate saturates because gas-phase diffusion is the bottleneck.

### 2.1.3 VPE Reactor Geometries

- **Horizontal Hot-Wall Reactor** — Most common for production of 150–300 mm wafers.
- **Vertical (Pancake) Reactor** — Used for single-wafer processing.
- **Barrel Reactor** — Used historically for batch processing.
- **Radiantly Heated Single-Wafer Reactor** — Current industry standard (e.g., Applied Materials Centura).

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility of VPE
- Formation of **n⁻ epitaxial layers** on n⁺ substrates (power devices, IGBTs).
- **Selective epitaxial growth (SEG)** for raised source/drain in advanced CMOS.
- **Buried layers** (e.g., for smart-power ICs).

---

## 2.2 Molecular Beam Epitaxy — Operating Theory

### 2.2.1 The Knudsen Cell — Heart of MBE

A *Knudsen effusion cell* is a cylindrical crucible with a small orifice of area $A_o$ heated to temperature $T_K$. The equilibrium vapor pressure of the source material inside the cell is $P_{eq}(T_K)$. The flux $\Phi$ (atoms/cm²·s) leaving the orifice is:

$$\Phi = \dfrac{P_{eq}(T_K)}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T_K}}$$

where:
- $m$ is the molecular mass,
- $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant (**1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**),
- $P_{eq}(T_K)$ is the temperature-dependent equilibrium vapor pressure, fitted empirically as:

$$\log_{10} P_{eq}(\text{Torr}) = A - \dfrac{B}{T_K}$$

For **Gallium** (Ga): $A = 7.99$, $B = 13\,730$ K. This exponential sensitivity means a **1 °C change in $T_K$ changes the flux by ≈ 1 %**, which is why MBE cells are PID-controlled to ± 0.1 °C.

### 2.2.2 The MBE Growth Process

- **Step 1 — Beam Generation:** Each effusion cell produces a collimated molecular beam.
- **Step 2 — Beam Transport:** Beams travel in UHV (no collisions) to the substrate.
- **Step 3 — Surface Migration:** Atoms impinge on the substrate, migrate to find step edges or kink sites.
- **Step 4 — RHEED Monitoring:** A 10–50 keV electron beam strikes the surface at a grazing angle (~1°); the diffraction pattern intensity oscillates with each completed monolayer — enabling **in-situ growth-rate measurement**.
- **Step 5 — Shutters:** Mechanical shutters (response < 0.1 s) at each cell open/close to switch materials → producing **abrupt heterojunctions within 1–2 atomic layers**.

### 2.2.3 Why MBE is Special (Engineering Utility)

- **Layer thickness precision:** ± 1 monolayer (≈ 0.283 nm for Si).
- **Doping profiles:** δ-doped layers, modulation doping for HEMTs.
- **Strained heterostructures:** SiGe, GaAs/AlGaAs, InGaAs/InP.
- **Quantum structures:** Quantum wells, wires, dots — foundation of modern optoelectronics and quantum devices.

---

## 2.3 Lithography — Operating Theory

### 2.3.1 The 8-Step Lithographic Sequence

1. **Wafer Cleaning** — RCA clean to remove organic/inorganic contaminants.
2. **Dehydration Bake** — 150 °C, removes adsorbed moisture.
3. **Priming (HMDS)** — Hexamethyldisilazane promotes photoresist adhesion.
4. **Spin Coating** — Photoresist dispensed; spun at **2000–5000 rpm**; final thickness $t$ follows $t \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$ (where ω is angular velocity).
5. **Soft Bake** — 90–100 °C, removes residual solvent.
6. **Exposure** — UV/electron/X-ray through mask or by direct write.
7. **Development** — TMAH (tetramethylammonium hydroxide) for positive resist; the exposed/ unexposed regions dissolve depending on resist polarity.
8. **Hard Bake** — 120–150 °C, improves etch resistance.

### 2.3.2 Positive vs. Negative Resist

| Type | Behaviour on Exposure | Trench Profile | Common Chemistries |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive** | Exposed region becomes soluble | Vertical/overcut | DNQ-novolac, PMMA |
| **Negative** | Exposed region cross-links & hardens | Undercut (nail-head) | SU-8, poly(glycidyl methacrylate) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *Positive = "P" for "Photo-destroyed"* — the photons destroy the resist. The unexposed pattern remains, and the exposed pattern is washed away.

### 2.3.3 Optical Lithography Resolution Physics

The **Rayleigh criterion** governs the minimum printable half-pitch:

$$R = k_1 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

The **Depth of Focus (DOF)** is:

$$\text{DOF} = k_2 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{(NA)^2}$$

Typical values: $k_1 \ge 0.25$, $k_2 \approx 1$ (industry), and **NA** = numerical aperture of the projection lens (currently up to 1.35 in immersion ArF systems).

### 2.3.4 Resolution Enhancement Techniques (RET)

- **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC)** — pre-distorts the mask to compensate for diffraction.
- **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM)** — introduces 180° phase shift between adjacent apertures to cancel diffraction.
- **Off-Axis Illumination (OAI)** — annular, quadrupole, dipole illumination improves resolution.
- **Immersion Lithography** — water (n = 1.44) replaces air, effectively reducing λ.
- **Multiple Patterning (LELE, SADP, SAQP)** — splits a single pattern into multiple exposures/etches to push resolution beyond the Rayleigh limit.

### 2.3.5 Beyond-Optical Lithography

| Technique | Wavelength / Probe | Resolution | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **E-beam (EBL)** | Electrons (10–100 keV) | < 10 nm | Mask making, R&D |
| **X-ray (Synchrotron)** | λ ≈ 0.5–1 nm | ~ 20 nm | Niche research |
| **EUV (Extreme UV)** | λ = 13.5 nm | ~ 8 nm (production) | HVM (TSMC, Samsung, Intel) |
| **Nanoimprint (NIL)** | Mechanical | < 10 nm | Memory, biotech |

---

## 2.4 🎯 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **must-memorize** set for KTU 2024 ESE — every Part B derivation or numerical in Module 1 traces back to one of these equations.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| F1 | $R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$ | Rayleigh resolution | nm |
| F2 | $\text{DOF} = k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{(NA)^2}$ | Depth of focus | nm |
| F3 | $\Phi = \dfrac{P_{eq}}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$ | MBE molecular flux | atoms/cm²·s |
| F4 | $P_{eq} = 10^{(A - B/T_K)}$ | Vapour pressure inside Knudsen cell | Torr |
| F5 | $v_g = \dfrac{h_M}{\rho N_A} \Phi$ | MBE growth rate | nm/s |
| F6 | $G_{VPE} = \dfrac{C \cdot P_{SiCl_4}}{\delta}$ | VPE growth rate (mass-transport limited) | μm/min |
| F7 | $G_{VPE} = k_s \cdot e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ | VPE growth rate (kinetically limited) | μm/min |
| F8 | $t_{resist} = \dfrac{K \cdot C^{1/2}}{\omega^{1/2}}$ | Spin-coated resist thickness | μm |
| F9 | $I_{RHEED}(t) = I_0 + A \cos(2\pi t/\tau + \phi)$ | RHEED intensity oscillation (MBE rate) | a.u. |
| F10 | $\text{Contrast} \, \gamma = \dfrac{1}{\log_{10}(E_0/E_{th})}$ | Photoresist contrast | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where used:** F1–F2 → Lithography; F3–F5, F9 → MBE; F6–F7 → VPE; F8, F10 → Resist processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory rule:** Every algebraic step is shown explicitly. No "…" or "after simplification" shortcuts. Multi-line equations are wrapped in `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` blocks.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Knudsen Cell Flux (Equilibrium Evaporation)

**Goal:** Derive the flux of atoms leaving a Knudsen cell orifice.

### Step 1 — Set up the kinetic picture
In a Knudsen cell, the pressure inside is so low that the mean free path of molecules is much larger than the orifice diameter. The gas inside is therefore in **local thermodynamic equilibrium**, characterized by pressure $P_{eq}(T)$ and temperature $T$.

### Step 2 — Use the Maxwell-Boltzmann speed distribution
The flux of molecules striking a wall from one side of a gas is, by kinetic theory:

$$\Phi_{\text{one-sided}} = \dfrac{1}{4} \, n \, \bar{c}$$

where $n$ is the number density (molecules/m³) and $\bar{c}$ is the mean molecular speed.

### Step 3 — Express number density via ideal gas law
The ideal gas law: $P = n k_B T$, hence:

$$n = \dfrac{P_{eq}}{k_B T}$$

### Step 4 — Express mean speed via Maxwell-Boltzmann statistics

$$\bar{c} = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}}$$

### Step 5 — Combine the expressions

$$\begin{aligned}
\Phi &= \dfrac{1}{4} \cdot \dfrac{P_{eq}}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P_{eq}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k_B T} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P_{eq}}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8 k_B T}{\pi m}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{k_B T} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P_{eq}}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{8}{\pi m k_B T}} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{P_{eq}}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Multiply by orifice area to get throughput (if required)
Total mass flow: $\dot{N} = \Phi \cdot A_o = \dfrac{P_{eq} A_o}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}}$

✅ **Final Knudsen Flux Equation:**

$$\boxed{\Phi = \dfrac{P_{eq}(T_K)}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T_K}} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{atoms}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}} \right]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** The flux is **linear in $P_{eq}$** and inversely proportional to $\sqrt{T}$. Since $P_{eq}$ itself is exponentially sensitive to $T$ (F4), the **dominant** control knob is the cell temperature $T_K$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: MBE Growth Rate from Beam Flux

**Goal:** Convert a measured flux $\Phi$ into a vertical growth rate $v_g$ [nm/s].

### Step 1 — Mass conservation at the substrate
If a flux $\Phi$ [atoms/m²·s] of atomic species of mass $m$ is fully incorporated into a film of density $\rho$ [kg/m³] and molar mass $M$ [kg/mol], then in time $\Delta t$ the volume of material added per unit area is:

$$\Delta V / A = \Phi \cdot \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{m}{\rho} = \Phi \cdot \Delta t \cdot \dfrac{M}{N_A \rho}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for the growth rate (height per unit time)
The growth rate is the thickness per unit time, i.e.:

$$v_g = \dfrac{\Delta V}{A \, \Delta t} = \Phi \cdot \dfrac{M}{N_A \rho}$$

### Step 3 — Express in monolayer units (optional)
If $h_{ML}$ is the monolayer thickness (e.g., $a_0/4$ for zincblende structures), the deposition rate in monolayers per second is:

$$\text{ML/s} = \dfrac{v_g}{h_{ML}}$$

✅ **Final Growth-Rate Equation:**

$$\boxed{v_g = \dfrac{M \cdot \Phi}{N_A \cdot \rho} \quad [\text{m/s}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical check (GaAs):** $\Phi = 10^{18}$ atoms/m²·s, $M(\text{Ga}) = 0.0697$ kg/mol, $\rho(\text{GaAs}) = 5320$ kg/m³, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ → $v_g \approx 0.21$ nm/s ≈ 1 ML/s. ✓ (matches the textbook MBE growth rate of 1 μm/hr for GaAs).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: VPE Growth Rate (Mass-Transport Limited Regime)

**Goal:** Derive the steady-state growth rate when diffusion through the boundary layer controls the process.

### Step 1 — Define the boundary layer
Above the heated susceptor, a stagnant boundary layer of thickness $\delta$ exists. SiCl₄ must diffuse through this layer.

### Step 2 — Use Fick's First Law for steady-state diffusion

$$J = -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dz} \approx D \, \dfrac{C_g - C_s}{\delta}$$

where:
- $C_g$ = gas-phase concentration of SiCl₄ above the boundary layer,
- $C_s$ = concentration at the substrate surface (taken to be 0 if surface reaction is fast),
- $D$ = gas-phase diffusivity of SiCl₄ in H₂.

### Step 3 — Convert to a growth rate
Using the molar volume of solid silicon $V_{m,Si}$:

$$G_{VPE} = \dfrac{J \cdot M_{Si}}{\rho_{Si}} = \dfrac{D \cdot C_g}{\delta} \cdot \dfrac{M_{Si}}{\rho_{Si}}$$

If we let $C = C_g \cdot M_{Si}/\rho_{Si}$ be an "effective concentration" parameter:

$$G_{VPE} = \dfrac{D}{\delta} \cdot C$$

### Step 4 — Recognize that $D/\delta$ is a mass-transfer coefficient $h_M$
For a horizontal reactor, $h_M \approx D/\delta$ is a weak function of temperature (via $D \propto T^{1.75}$), but **independent of the reaction kinetics**.

$$\boxed{G_{VPE} = h_M \cdot C \quad [\mu\text{m/min}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** In the mass-transport regime, the growth rate is **insensitive to substrate temperature** because $h_M$ depends only weakly on $T$. In contrast, in the kinetic regime, $G \propto e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ → strong temperature dependence. The transition occurs around **1100 °C** for SiCl₄/H₂.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Rayleigh Resolution Limit of Optical Lithography

**Goal:** Show how the minimum half-pitch depends on wavelength and numerical aperture.

### Step 1 — Two-beam interference image
The aerial image of a grating of period $p$ exposed by light of wavelength $\lambda$ through a lens of numerical aperture $NA$ is the interference of two plane waves. The grating is resolvable when the **first-order diffraction** of one beam is captured by the lens:

$$\sin\theta_1 = \lambda / p$$

### Step 2 — The capture condition
The lens captures diffraction up to $\sin\theta = NA$. Hence the **minimum period** is:

$$p_{\min} = \lambda / NA$$

### Step 3 — Convert to resolution (half-pitch) with process factor
Empirically, the actual printable feature is larger by a process-dependent factor $k_1 \ge 0.25$ (theoretical Rayleigh limit):

$$R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

### Step 4 — Derive Depth of Focus (DOF) by similar reasoning
The image of a point is an Airy disk; out-of-focus, defocus blurs it. The allowed defocus is set by quarter-wavelength criterion:

$$\text{DOF} = \pm \, \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \, (NA)^2}$$

Including the process factor $k_2$:

$$\boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \, \dfrac{\lambda}{(NA)^2}}$$

✅ **Final Resolution & DOF Equations:**

$$\boxed{R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}} \qquad \boxed{\text{DOF} = k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{(NA)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering trade-off:** Increasing NA reduces $R$ **but quadratically** reduces DOF. This is why high-NA (NA = 1.35) immersion systems require ultra-flat wafers and active focusing systems.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of the Three Techniques

| Parameter | VPE | MBE | Lithography |
|---|---|---|---|
| Process Type | Chemical (CVD) | Physical (UHV) | Pattern transfer |
| Operating Pressure | 10 – 760 Torr | < 10⁻¹⁰ Torr | Atmospheric (stepper) |
| Substrate Temperature | 900 – 1250 °C | 500 – 700 °C | Room T (resist process) |
| Growth/Throughput Rate | 0.1 – 1 μm/min | 1 – 2 μm/hr | Wafers/hr (25–200) |
| Layer Precision | ± 5 % | ± 1 monolayer | nm-scale |
| Dominant Limitation | Boundary layer diffusion | Flux uniformity | Wavelength & NA |
| Primary Diagnostic | Weight gain, FTIR | RHEED oscillations | CD-SEM, OI |

# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid diagrams below use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (per the safeguard rules) and **plain uppercase text inside double-quoted labels** (no bold/italic/HTML inside labels).

---

## 4.1 Block Diagram — VPE Reactor System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N2IN["N2 INLET FOR PURGE"] --> MIX[MIXING MANIFOLD]
    H2IN["H2 CARRIER GAS INLET"] --> MIX
    SICL4["SICL4 BUBBLER SOURCE"] --> MIX
    MIX --> REACTOR[HORIZONTAL HOT WALL REACTOR]
    REACTOR --> SUSC[SUSCEPTOR WITH WAFERS HEATED BY RF COIL]
    RFCOIL["RF INDUCTION COIL 1250C"] --> SUSC
    REACTOR --> EXH[EXHAUST HCl AND H2]
    EXH --> SCRUB[SCRUBBER FOR HCl REMOVAL]
    SCRUB --> VENT[VENT TO ATMOSPHERE]
    REACTOR -. MONITOR .-> TC1[THERMOCOUPLE]
    REACTOR -. MONITOR .-> PR1[PRESSURE GAUGE]
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — MBE System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    UHVCHAM["ULTRA HIGH VACUUM CHAMBER 10E-10 TORR"] --> SUBS[HEATED SUBSTRATE HOLDER 600C]
    CRYO["CRYOPANEL LN2 SHIELD"] --> UHVCHAM
    IONP["ION PUMP AND TSP"] --> UHVCHAM

    CELL1["K1 GA EFFUSION CELL"] -- BEAM --> SUBS
    CELL2["K2 AS EFFUSION CELL"] -- BEAM --> SUBS
    CELL3["K3 AL DOPANT CELL"] -- BEAM --> SUBS
    CELL4["K4 BE P TYPE DOPANT CELL"] -- BEAM --> SUBS

    SUBS -. RHEED PATTERN .-> RHEED[10 KEV RHEED GUN]
    RHEED --> CCD[CCD CAMERA AND OSCILLOSCOPE]
    SHUTTER["PNEUMATIC SHUTTERS PER CELL"] --> CELL1
    SHUTTER --> CELL2
    SHUTTER --> CELL3
    SHUTTER --> CELL4
```

---

## 4.3 Process Flow — Optical Lithography (8-Step Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STEP 1 WAFER CLEANING RCA PROCESS] --> B[STEP 2 DEHYDRATION BAKE 150C]
    B --> C[STEP 3 PRIMING WITH HMDS VAPOR]
    C --> D[STEP 4 SPIN COATING OF PHOTORESIST 3000 RPM]
    D --> E[STEP 5 SOFT BAKE 95C ON HOT PLATE]
    E --> F[STEP 6 MASK ALIGNMENT AND UV EXPOSURE]
    F --> G[STEP 7 DEVELOPMENT IN TMAH SOLUTION]
    G --> H[STEP 8 HARD BAKE 130C]
    H --> I[PATTERN TRANSFER READY FOR ETCH OR IMPLANT]
```

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Lithography Exposure Tool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LAMP["EXCIMER LASER SOURCE 193 NM ARF"] --> ILL[ILLUMINATOR OPTICS]
    ILL --> MASK[PHOTOMASK WITH CHROME PATTERN]
    MASK --> LENS[HIGH NA PROJECTION LENS NA 1.35 IMMERSION]
    LENS --> IMM[IMMERSION WATER FILM N 1.44]
    IMM --> WAF[PHOTORESIST COATED SILICON WAFER]
    WAF --> STAGE[PRECISION XY STAGE WITH INTERFEROMETRY]
    STAGE -. FEEDBACK .-> ALIGN[ALIGNMENT SYSTEM]
    ALIGN -. CORRECTION .-> STAGE
    LAMP --> MET[ENERGY MONITOR AND PUPIL FILL ADJUST]
    MET -. CONTROL .-> ILL
```

---

## 4.5 Decision Flow — When to Use VPE vs MBE vs CVD vs MOCVD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{NEED ATOMIC LAYER PRECISION}
    Q -- YES --> MBE_CHOICE[USE MBE FOR HETEROSTRUCTURES AND QUANTUM WELLS]
    Q -- NO --> Q2{NEED HIGH THROUGHPUT EPITAXY}
    Q2 -- YES --> VPE_CHOICE[USE VPE FOR THICK POWER DEVICE LAYERS]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{NEED COMPOUND SEMICONDUCTOR}
    Q3 -- YES --> MOCVD[USE MOCVD FOR LEDS AND HBT]
    Q3 -- NO --> CVD[USE LPCVD FOR POLY AND DIELECTRICS]
```
