# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Question pattern strictly follows KTU 2024 ESE: **Part A (3 marks)** short-answers, **Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice and **(a) 7-marks + (b) 7-marks** sub-parts.

---

## 📝 PART A — 3-MARK QUESTIONS

### A1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Q:** Differentiate between **Vapour Phase Epitaxy (VPE)** and **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** in terms of operating pressure, growth temperature, and growth rate.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | VPE | MBE |
|---|---|---|
| Operating Pressure | 10–760 Torr (high) | < 10⁻¹⁰ Torr (UHV) |
| Growth Temperature | 900 – 1250 °C | 500 – 700 °C |
| Growth Rate | 0.1 – 1 μm/min (fast) | 1 – 2 μm/hr (slow) |

(1 mark for the table structure, 1 mark for the values, 1 mark for identifying the reason: VPE is mass-transport / chemical-reaction limited while MBE is flux-limited in UHV.)

---

### A2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q:** State the **Rayleigh criterion** for the resolution of an optical lithographic system. What is the theoretical lower limit of the process factor $k_1$?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Rayleigh resolution criterion is:

$$R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

where $\lambda$ is the exposure wavelength, **NA** is the numerical aperture of the projection lens, and $k_1$ is a process-dependent factor. The theoretical lower limit of $k_1$ is **0.25** (single-exposure conventional imaging). (1 mark for equation, 1 mark for definitions, 1 mark for $k_1 \ge 0.25$.)

---

## 📝 PART B — 14-MARK QUESTIONS (Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**

**Q:** *(a)* Describe the **Vapour Phase Epitaxy** process in detail. Discuss the **SiCl₄ + H₂ reaction** mechanism and the factors that limit the growth rate. (7 marks)

*(b)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **Molecular Beam Epitaxy (MBE)** system. Discuss the role of **RHEED** in *in-situ* growth monitoring. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) VPE Process (7 marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** VPE is a CVD-based epitaxial technique in which a single-crystalline film is grown on a heated single-crystal substrate from the vapor phase via a chemical reaction. (See §1.1.)

**Reaction Chemistry (2 marks):** The principal reaction is

$$\text{SiCl}_4 \, (g) + 2\,\text{H}_2 \, (g) \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Si} \, (s) + 4\,\text{HCl} \, (g)$$

- A secondary reaction is the **silane pyrolysis**: $\text{SiH}_4 \rightarrow \text{Si} + 2\text{H}_2$, which is favoured at lower T (700–900 °C) and gives better selectivity.

**Growth Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. **Mass transport** of SiCl₄ through the gas boundary layer of thickness δ to the substrate.
2. **Gas-phase decomposition** of SiCl₄ to SiCl₂ + Cl₂ (or via silane to SiH₂).
3. **Surface adsorption** of Si-bearing species.
4. **Surface migration & incorporation** into the lattice.
5. **Desorption** of byproducts (HCl, H₂).

**Limiting Factors (2 marks):**
- At **low T** (kinetic regime): $G_{VPE} = k_s e^{-E_a/k_B T}$ — surface reaction controls; Arrhenius behaviour.
- At **high T** (mass-transport regime): $G_{VPE} = h_M \cdot C$ — diffusion through boundary layer δ controls; growth rate **saturates** and is independent of T.
- Other factors: reactant partial pressure, total flow rate, wafer orientation, HCl etch-back (which can dominate at very low SiCl₄ partial pressures).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) MBE System & RHEED (7 marks)

**System Description (3 marks):** A typical MBE system consists of:
- **Ultra-High Vacuum chamber** (base pressure < 10⁻¹⁰ Torr), pumped by ion pump + Ti sublimation pump.
- **Knudsen effusion cells** (Ga, As, Al, dopants), each temperature-controlled to ± 0.1 °C.
- **Pneumatic shutters** in front of each cell for beam switching (< 0.1 s response).
- **LN₂ cryopanels** to condense stray species and improve vacuum.
- **Heated substrate holder** (500–700 °C, rotating for uniformity).
- **RHEED gun + CCD camera** for *in-situ* monitoring.
- **Load-lock chamber** for wafer introduction without venting the main chamber.

**RHEED in *In-situ* Monitoring (4 marks):**
- A **10–50 keV electron beam** strikes the substrate at a **grazing angle (1°–2°)** — chosen so the beam penetrates only the topmost 1–2 atomic layers.
- The reflected/diffracted beam produces a **streaky RHEED pattern** on a fluorescent screen, recorded by a CCD.
- During layer-by-layer growth, the **intensity of a particular diffraction spot oscillates with a period equal to the time to grow one monolayer**.
- Counting oscillations gives the **deposition rate in real time**; the sharpness of the oscillations also indicates the **2D growth mode** (oscillations damp out when growth becomes 3D).
- The chemical composition of the topmost layer can be inferred from the **spacing and shape of the RHEED streaks**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — **Internal Choice to A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**

**Q:** *(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **8-step photolithographic process** used in VLSI fabrication. Highlight the role of **HMDS priming** and **soft bake**. (7 marks)

*(b)* Derive the **Rayleigh resolution** and **depth of focus** formulas for an optical projection lithography system. Discuss **two resolution enhancement techniques (RET)** that allow patterning below the theoretical $k_1 = 0.25$ limit. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (a) 8-Step Photolithography (7 marks)

**Process Steps (1 mark each = 4 marks):**
1. **Wafer cleaning** — RCA clean to remove organics, native oxide, ionic contaminants.
2. **Dehydration bake** — ~150 °C to remove adsorbed water; essential for resist adhesion.
3. **HMDS priming** — Hexamethyldisilazane reacts with surface –OH groups, converting the hydrophilic surface to hydrophobic → improves resist wetting and adhesion.
4. **Spin coating** — Photoresist dispensed, wafer spun at 2000–5000 rpm; final thickness $t \propto 1/\sqrt{\omega}$ (F8).
5. **Soft bake** — 90–100 °C on a hot plate; removes residual solvent; stabilises the film thickness and reduces standing-wave effects.
6. **Exposure** — UV (or e-beam/X-ray) illumination through mask; resist undergoes photochemical reaction.
7. **Development** — TMAH (0.26 N) solution; **positive resist** → exposed regions dissolve; **negative resist** → exposed regions remain (cross-linked).
8. **Hard bake** — 120–150 °C; drives off residual solvent and developer; improves etch resistance and pattern fidelity.

**Role of HMDS Priming (1.5 marks):**
- Without HMDS, the hydrophilic Si–OH surface repels the resist, causing voids, pinholes, and poor line-width control.
- HMDS replaces –OH with –O–Si(CH₃)₃, providing a non-polar anchor for the resist polymer, ensuring **uniform coverage and strong adhesion** during subsequent etching.

**Role of Soft Bake (1.5 marks):**
- Removes ~ 20 % residual solvent (e.g., ethyl lactate, PGMEA) — critical for reproducible development.
- Reduces **standing-wave interference patterns** caused by reflections from the substrate.
- Prevents resist from sticking to the mask in contact-printing mode.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — (b) Rayleigh Derivation + RET (7 marks)

**Derivation of Resolution (3.5 marks):** *(See §3.4 for the full derivation; reproduce the 4 steps of the derivation concisely.)*
- The first-order diffraction of a grating of period $p$ is at $\sin\theta_1 = \lambda/p$.
- The lens captures diffraction up to $\sin\theta = NA$, giving $p_{\min} = \lambda/NA$.
- Including the empirical process factor $k_1 \ge 0.25$:

$$R = k_1 \dfrac{\lambda}{NA}$$

**Derivation of DOF (1.5 marks):**
- Out-of-focus image of a point is an Airy disc; defocus blur ≤ λ/4(NA)².
- Therefore:

$$\text{DOF} = k_2 \dfrac{\lambda}{(NA)^2}$$

**Resolution Enhancement Techniques (2 marks — pick any two):**
1. **Optical Proximity Correction (OPC):** Pre-distorts the mask features (serifs, hammerheads, sub-resolution assist features) to compensate for diffraction-induced rounding. Allows $k_1 \approx 0.4$ in production.
2. **Phase-Shifting Masks (PSM):** Etches alternating mask apertures by λ/2 so adjacent beams destructively interfere at the image plane — doubles the effective spatial frequency. Allows $k_1$ down to 0.25.
3. **Off-Axis Illumination (OAI):** Annular or dipole illumination improves the cut-off of the pupil function. Combined with PSM, $k_1 \approx 0.25$ is routinely achieved.
4. **Immersion Lithography:** Replaces air with water (n = 1.44) between the lens and wafer; effective λ becomes λ/n; pushes NA to 1.35.
5. **Multiple Patterning (LELE, SADP, SAQP):** Splits a single pattern into two or more exposures/etches — circumvents the $k_1$ limit at the cost of throughput and overlay complexity.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing VPE with CVD:** VPE *is* a special form of CVD where the deposited film is **single-crystalline (epitaxial)**. Many students write "VPE is the same as CVD" — partial credit only. **Always mention "epitaxial" + "single-crystalline".**
> 2. **Forgetting the SiCl₄ equilibrium:** Writing only $\text{SiCl}_4 + \text{H}_2 \rightarrow \text{Si} + \text{HCl}$ is **unbalanced**; examiners deduct a mark. Always balance with the correct stoichiometry: SiCl₄ + 2H₂ ⇌ Si + 4HCl.
> 3. **Mixing up Knudsen cell temperature with substrate temperature:** The Knudsen cell $T_K$ (≈ 600–1100 °C) is the **source**; the substrate $T_s$ (≈ 500–700 °C) is where growth happens. Mark loss if these are interchanged.
> 4. **Missing units in Rayleigh formula:** Always state $\lambda$ in nm and $NA$ dimensionless; do not write NA in "mm" or in mixed units. NA = n·sinθ is dimensionless.
> 5. **Saying "EUV uses 13 nm wavelength = 13 nm resolution":** This is wrong. The resolution is still $R = k_1\lambda/NA$. With $k_1 = 0.4$ and $NA = 0.33$ (current EUV scanners), $R \approx 16$ nm half-pitch, and with high-NA (0.55) EUV, $R \approx 8$ nm.
> 6. **Skipping the derivation of DOF:** Examiners often ask the **derivation**, not just the formula. A derivation that just states DOF = λ/(NA)² gets at most 1 of the 2 marks allotted to DOF.

---

## 🧠 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ☐ **VPE** is a **chemical, atmospheric-pressure** epitaxial process; reaction: **SiCl₄ + 2H₂ ⇌ Si + 4HCl**; operating T: **900–1250 °C**; growth rate: **0.1–1 μm/min**.
- ☐ **VPE** has two growth regimes: **kinetic (low T, Arrhenius)** and **mass-transport (high T, saturated)**; transition around 1100 °C.
- ☐ **MBE** is a **physical, UHV (< 10⁻¹⁰ Torr)** epitaxial technique; substrate T: **500–700 °C**; growth rate: **1–2 μm/hr** (≈ 1 ML/s).
- ☐ **Knudsen flux equation**: $\Phi = P_{eq}/\sqrt{2\pi m k_B T}$.
- ☐ **RHEED** in MBE gives **real-time, monolayer-precision** monitoring via diffraction intensity oscillations.
- ☐ **Lithography resolution**: $R = k_1 \lambda/NA$; **DOF**: $k_2 \lambda/NA^2$; $k_1 \ge 0.25$ theoretical.
- ☐ **Lithography sequence**: Clean → Dehydrate → HMDS prime → Spin → Soft bake → Expose → Develop → Hard bake.
- ☐ **Positive resist** → exposed region dissolves; **negative resist** → exposed region remains.
- ☐ **RET techniques**: OPC, PSM, OAI, immersion, multiple patterning (LELE, SADP, SAQP).
- ☐ **EUV** ($\lambda = 13.5$ nm) is the current **production workhorse** for sub-10 nm nodes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Photolithographic Sequence, Electron Beam Lithography, Etching and Metal Deposition, CMOS Twin Well Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Photolithographic Sequence, Electron Beam Lithography, Etching and Metal Deposition, CMOS Twin Well Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Photolithographic Sequence

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Photolithography is the process of transferring geometric patterns from a photomask onto a photosensitive polymer film (photoresist) coated on a silicon wafer using ultraviolet light. The *photolithographic sequence* refers to the canonical, ordered set of chemical, thermal, and optical operations executed between successive masking levels in an IC fabrication line.

The KTU 2024 Scheme defines the standard sequence as: *Wafer Cleaning → Dehydration Bake → HMDS Priming → Photoresist Coating (Spin) → Soft Bake → Alignment & Exposure → Post-Exposure Bake → Development → Hard Bake → Inspection.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight — "Photolithography is the heart of VLSI."** Roughly **30–40 %** of all process steps in a modern CMOS flow are lithographic, and they determine the *minimum feature size* of the technology node.

**Conceptual Analogy — "The Photo-Stencil Kitchen":**
Imagine you want to decorate a cake with icing patterns. You:
1. Wash and dry the cake (clean wafer).
2. Spread butter so the icing sticks (HMDS adhesion promoter).
3. Pour liquid sugar-icing and spin the plate so it forms a thin uniform layer (spin coating photoresist).
4. Dry the icing slightly so it sets (soft bake).
5. Place a transparent plastic stencil (photomask) on top and flash a UV lamp (exposure).
6. Wash away the un-hardened icing (development) — pattern revealed.
7. Bake to harden (hard bake).

Photolithography is the silicon-industry version of this "stencil-and-bake" recipe, repeated dozens of times to build a complete chip.

## 1.2 Electron Beam (E-beam) Lithography

**Formal Definition:**
E-beam lithography is a *maskless*, *direct-write* lithographic technique in which a finely focused beam of high-energy electrons (typically **10–100 keV**) is raster-scanned across an electron-sensitive resist (e.g., PMMA, ZEP, or hydrogen silsesquioxane) to define sub-100 nm features without requiring a physical mask.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wavelength analogue:** A **100 keV** electron has a de Broglie wavelength of about **0.0037 nm** — roughly **100,000× shorter** than the **365 nm** i-line UV used in optical lithography. This is why e-beam tools can resolve features far below the optical diffraction limit.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Drawing with a Precise Pen":**
Whereas optical lithography is like using a *flashlight + stencil* (parallel, fast, but limited by light's wavelength), e-beam lithography is like using an *atomic-scale calligraphy pen* — you draw every line yourself, one pixel at a time. It is slow but extremely precise, ideal for crafting the stencils (photomasks) themselves and for research-grade sub-micron devices.

## 1.3 Etching and Metal Deposition

**Formal Definition:**
*Etching* is the selective removal of unmasked material (oxide, nitride, polysilicon, or metal) from the wafer surface using chemical (wet) or physical/chemical (dry) reactions. *Metal Deposition* is the controlled, uniform formation of a thin conductive film (Al, Cu, W, Ti) over the wafer topography to form interconnect lines, contacts, and vias.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Etch Quality Metrics in KTU 2024:**
> 1. **Etch Rate (nm/min)** — speed of material removal.
> 2. **Selectivity (S)** — ratio of target-layer etch rate to mask/underlying-layer etch rate.
> 3. **Anisotropy (A)** — ratio of vertical to lateral etch components (A = 1 → perfectly vertical sidewalls).
> 4. **Uniformity** — etch rate variation across wafer.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Etching vs. Carving":**
Wet etching is like letting acid *eat away* a sculpture — it undercuts the mask (isotropic). Dry (plasma) etching is like *chiselling* — ions bombard vertically, producing sharp, anisotropic walls. Metal deposition, by contrast, is *plating or spraying* conductive paint over the etched trenches so that current can flow between transistors.

## 1.4 CMOS Twin-Well (Twin-Tub) Process

**Formal Definition:**
The *Twin-Well Process* (also called *Twin-Tub*) is a CMOS fabrication flow in which **separate, independently optimized n-wells and p-wells** are formed side-by-side in a single lightly-doped wafer (often an *epitaxial* layer over a heavily-doped substrate). Each well hosts its respective transistor type (NMOS in p-well, PMOS in n-well), allowing symmetric, high-performance CMOS operation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Twin"?** Because both well types are formed in the *same wafer* using two independent implantation steps. This contrasts with *p-well only* or *n-well only* processes where only one well is explicitly implanted; the other well is the substrate itself.

**Conceptual Analogy — "Two Gardens Side by Side":**
Think of a flat field. You dig one garden bed and add *acidic* soil (p-type, boron) for tomatoes (NMOS). Beside it you dig another bed and add *alkaline* soil (n-type, phosphorus) for roses (PMOS). A thin wall (LOCOS field oxide) separates them so each plant's roots don't invade the neighbour's. That is the twin-well CMOS garden — two optimized "transistor neighbourhoods" sharing one silicon field.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Photolithographic Sequence — Stepwise Logic

Each masking level in a CMOS process invokes the following ten canonical steps. The *'why'* behind every step is critical for KTU questions.

### Step 1 — Wafer Cleaning (RCA / Piranha)
* **Action:** Removes organic, particulate, and metallic contamination.
* **Why:** A single 0.5 µm dust particle can destroy a die. Native oxide is also stripped (dilute HF dip).
* **KTU mnemonic:** "**R**emove **C**ontaminants **A**ll" — RCA clean.

### Step 2 — Dehydration Bake (≈ 150–200 °C, 30 min)
* Drives off adsorbed moisture; prevents resist adhesion failure (lifting, pop-edges).

### Step 3 — HMDS Priming
* Hexamethyldisilazane [(CH₃)₃Si–NH–Si(CH₃)₃] is vapor-deposited to make the hydrophilic SiO₂ surface *hydrophobic*, so aqueous photoresist adheres uniformly.

### Step 4 — Photoresist Coating (Spin Coating)
* Liquid resist (≈ 1–4 cP viscosity) is dispensed and spun at **2000–6000 rpm**.
* Final thickness follows the empirical relation:
$$ t = \frac{K \cdot C^{\beta} \cdot \eta^{\gamma}}{\omega^{\alpha}} $$
  where $C$ = concentration, $\eta$ = viscosity, $\omega$ = angular velocity, $\alpha \approx 0.5$, $\beta \approx \gamma \approx 1$.
* Typical thickness: **0.5 – 2 µm** for g/i-line resists.

### Step 5 — Soft Bake (Pre-Exposure Bake)
* **90–120 °C** on a hot plate, **60 s**.
* Evaporates solvent (≥ 20 %), improves adhesion, and stabilizes sensitivity.
* **Critical KTU point:** under-bake → solvent residue scatters UV, causing *T-topping*. Over-bake → resist hardens and exposure kinetics slow.

### Step 6 — Alignment & Exposure
* Wafer is aligned to the mask under a microscope, then exposed.
* **Exposure Dose** $E$ (mJ/cm²) = Intensity × Time.
* For positive resist: exposed regions become soluble in developer.
* For negative resist: exposed regions cross-link and become insoluble.

### Step 7 — Post-Exposure Bake (PEB)
* **100–130 °C** for **60–120 s**.
* In chemically amplified resists, PEB drives the photo-acid-catalyzed deprotection reaction — *without it, no image forms*.
* Also reduces standing-wave ripple.

### Step 8 — Development
* Positive resist: aqueous **TMAH (2.38 %)** dissolves exposed regions.
* Negative resist: organic solvent (e.g., xylene) dissolves unexposed regions.

### Step 9 — Hard Bake (Post-Development Bake)
* **120–180 °C**, 20–30 min.
* Further hardens resist to withstand subsequent etch/implant.
* Risk: > 200 °C causes resist to flow and reflow — feature distortion.

### Step 10 — Inspection
* Optical / SEM / AFM inspection before etch.
* Defectivity budget: < **0.05 defects/cm²** for sub-micron nodes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative vs Positive Resist — KTU Favourite:**
> | Aspect | Positive | Negative |
> |---|---|---|
> | Exposed region | Soluble (removed) | Insoluble (remains) |
> | Resolution | Higher | Lower (swelling) |
> | Adhesion | Moderate | Better |
> | Used for | Fine-line ICs | MEMS, packaging |

## 2.2 Optical Lithography Resolution — Rayleigh's Criterion

The minimum resolvable half-pitch $R$ is governed by:

$$ R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA} $$

The depth of focus (DOF) is:

$$ DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} $$

where:
* $\lambda$ = exposure wavelength (**365 nm** i-line, **248 nm** KrF, **193 nm** ArF, **13.5 nm** EUV).
* $NA$ = numerical aperture of the projection lens ($NA = n \sin\theta$).
* $k_1$ = process factor (**0.25** is the theoretical limit; production ≈ 0.4–0.6).
* $k_2$ = DOF factor (typically **0.5**).

**Real-World Engineering Utility:** Every shrink from 0.5 µm → 90 nm → 7 nm has been driven by lowering $\lambda$, raising $NA$, and reducing $k_1$ via *Resolution Enhancement Techniques* (RETs) such as **OPC (Optical Proximity Correction), phase-shift masks, and off-axis illumination.**

## 2.3 Electron Beam Lithography — Theory

**Core Equations:**

$$ \lambda_e = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} \approx \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm} $$

where $V$ is in volts. For $V = 100\,\text{kV}$:

$$ \lambda_e \approx 0.0037 \text{ nm} $$

Beam diameter (Gaussian approximation):
$$ d = \sqrt{d_g^{\,2} + d_s^{\,2} + d_c^{\,2}} $$
where $d_g$ = gun source size, $d_s$ = spherical aberration disk, $d_c$ = chromatic aberration disk.

**Proximity Effect:** Forward- and back-scattered electrons blur features. Corrected via *dose modulation* or *GHOST* software.

**Why It Matters in Engineering:** E-beam is the *workhorse for mask-making* (every photomask is written by an e-beam tool) and for *low-volume ASICs, MMICs, and photonics*.

## 2.4 Etching — Theory & Equations

**Wet Etch Rate:**
$$ R = \frac{k \cdot C^n}{1 + k' / C} $$
* $C$ = etchant concentration, $n$ ≈ 1.

**Etch Selectivity:**
$$ S_{A/B} = \frac{R_A}{R_B} $$

**Anisotropy:**
$$ A = 1 - \frac{R_{\text{lateral}}}{R_{\text{vertical}}} \quad \in [0,1] $$

* $A = 0$ → perfectly isotropic (wet etch).
* $A = 1$ → perfectly anisotropic (RIE).

**Plasma Etch (RIE):**
The etch rate follows an Arrhenius-like law:
$$ R_{\text{etch}} = R_0 \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{k_B T}\right) $$

## 2.5 Metal Deposition — Theory

**Thermal Evaporation Rate (Hertz-Knudsen):**
$$ R_{\text{evap}} = \frac{p_s}{\sqrt{2 \pi m k_B T}} \cdot \alpha $$
* $p_s$ = equilibrium vapor pressure of source metal at temperature $T$.
* $\alpha$ = sticking coefficient (≈ 1 for most metals).

**Sputtering Yield:**
$$ Y = \frac{\text{atoms ejected}}{\text{incident ion}} \propto \frac{1}{E_b}\cdot S_n(E) $$
* $E_b$ = surface binding energy; $S_n$ = nuclear stopping power.

**Step Coverage:**
$$ SC = \frac{t_{\text{bottom}}}{t_{\text{top}}} \times 100\,\% $$
* Sputtering gives better $SC$ than evaporation due to sidewall flux.

## 2.6 CMOS Twin-Well Process — Key Concepts

* **Substrate:** Lightly-doped p-type epitaxial layer on a heavily-doped p⁺ substrate (prevents latch-up).
* **Two masked implants:** Phosphorus (n-well) and Boron (p-well), each followed by high-temperature drive-in (≈ 1100 °C, several hours).
* **Field isolation:** LOCOS (Local Oxidation of Silicon) — pad oxide + Si₃N₄ mask, selective oxidation grows ≈ 500–700 nm SiO₂.
* **Twin-well advantages:**
  1. Both wells independently optimized for NMOS and PMOS.
  2. Allows retrograde well profiles (high surface doping, low deep doping) → reduced body effect.
  3. Symmetric n-channel and p-channel performance.

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Typical Values / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Optical Resolution | $R = k_1 \lambda / NA$ | nm | $k_1 \ge 0.25$ (theoretical) |
| 2 | Depth of Focus | $DOF = k_2 \lambda / NA^2$ | nm | $k_2 \approx 0.5$ |
| 3 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = n \sin\theta$ | — | $n$ = medium refractive index |
| 4 | Resist Spin Thickness | $t \propto \eta^\gamma \omega^{-\alpha}$ | µm | $\alpha \approx 0.5$ |
| 5 | E-beam Wavelength | $\lambda_e = 1.227 / \sqrt{V}$ | nm | $V$ in volts |
| 6 | Etch Selectivity | $S_{A/B} = R_A / R_B$ | — | High $S$ = good etch |
| 7 | Anisotropy | $A = 1 - R_{\text{lat}}/R_{\text{vert}}$ | — | 0 (iso) → 1 (aniso) |
| 8 | Plasma Etch Arrhenius | $R = R_0 \exp(-E_a/k_BT)$ | nm/min | $E_a$ = activation energy |
| 9 | Evaporation Rate (HK) | $R = \alpha p_s / \sqrt{2\pi m k_BT}$ | atoms·m⁻²·s⁻¹ | Hertz-Knudsen |
| 10 | Sputter Yield | $Y \propto S_n(E)/E_b$ | — | Depends on ion energy |
| 11 | Step Coverage | $SC = t_{\text{bottom}}/t_{\text{top}}$ | % | Higher = better filling |
| 12 | Field Oxide (LOCOS) | $t_{\text{ox}} \propto \sqrt{t_{\text{ox}}^* \cdot t}$ | nm | Deal-Grove model |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Optical Resolution Limit (Rayleigh Criterion)

**Starting premise:** When two point images pass through a circular aperture of radius $r$, the *first diffraction minimum* of one pattern coincides with the *central maximum* of the other — they are just-resolved.

For a circular lens of radius $r$ and focal length $f$, the Airy disk's first zero radius in the image plane is:

$$ r_{\text{Airy}} = 1.22 \frac{\lambda f}{2r} = 0.61 \frac{\lambda}{NA} $$

Defining the resolution $R$ as the distance between two just-resolved lines (pitch / 2), we divide by 2 and incorporate the empirical process factor $k_1$:

$$ \boxed{R = k_1 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA}} $$

**Step-by-step physical meaning:**

1. **Wavelength $\lambda$ decreases** → finer features can be printed (UV → DUV → EUV).
2. **$NA$ increases** → tighter focusing cone; but trade-off with $DOF$.
3. **$k_1$ decreases** → better reticle enhancement (OPC, phase shifting).

**DOF Derivation:**
The defocus blur circle for a marginal ray at half-angle $\theta$ is:

$$ \Delta = 2 \delta \sin\theta \approx 2 \delta \frac{NA}{n} $$

Setting $\Delta = R$ (defocus equal to feature size) and substituting $R = k_1 \lambda/NA$:

$$ \delta = \frac{k_1 \lambda n}{2 NA^2} $$

With empirical constant $k_2 = k_1 n / 2$:

$$ \boxed{DOF = k_2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{NA^2}} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why DOF shrinks quadratically as $NA$ grows:** Doubling $NA$ quarters the DOF — this is the famous "Depth-of-Focus Crisis" that drove immersion lithography ($n = 1.44$ water at 193 nm) and EUV adoption.

## 3.2 Derivation — Electron Beam Wavelength

Starting from the de Broglie relation $\lambda = h/p$ and the energy conservation $eV = p^2/(2m_e)$:

$$ p = \sqrt{2 m_e e V} $$

Substituting:

$$ \lambda_e = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}} $$

Plugging in physical constants ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C):

$$ \lambda_e = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \text{ nm} \quad (V \text{ in volts}) $$

For $V = 100\,\text{kV} = 10^5\,\text{V}$:

$$ \lambda_e = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{10^5}} = \frac{1.227}{316.23} \approx 0.00388 \text{ nm} $$

Confirmed: **λ_e ≈ 0.004 nm**, three orders of magnitude below 193 nm ArF light.

## 3.3 Derivation — Etch Anisotropy & Selectivity (Worked Example)

**Problem:** A silicon wafer is patterned with SiO₂ mask and etched in a CF₄/H₂ plasma. Vertical etch rate of poly-Si = 250 nm/min. Lateral etch rate = 30 nm/min. Underlying SiO₂ etch rate = 25 nm/min.

**Step 1 — Compute Anisotropy $A$:**

$$ A = 1 - \frac{R_{\text{lateral}}}{R_{\text{vertical}}} = 1 - \frac{30}{250} = 0.88 $$

**Step 2 — Compute Selectivity of Poly-Si over SiO₂:**

$$ S_{\text{poly/oxide}} = \frac{R_{\text{poly}}}{R_{\text{oxide}}} = \frac{250}{25} = 10 $$

**Step 3 — Time to etch 0.5 µm poly-Si:**

$$ t_{\text{etch}} = \frac{0.5\,\mu\text{m}}{250\,\text{nm/min}} = \frac{500\,\text{nm}}{250\,\text{nm/min}} = 2 \text{ minutes} $$

**Step 4 — Lateral undercut at bottom of feature:**

$$ \Delta x = R_{\text{lateral}} \times t = 30 \times 2 = 60 \text{ nm undercut} $$

This is acceptable for 0.5 µm features but unacceptable for < 100 nm nodes.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CMOS Twin-Well Process Flow

The following table enumerates **every masking step** of a classical n-well / p-well twin-tub CMOS process (≈ 12–14 masking levels). KTU frequently asks for process sequence diagrams.

| Mask # | Mask Name | Layer Deposited / Grown | Process Step | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | (Substrate) | p⁻ epi on p⁺ wafer | Latch-up suppression |
| 1 | **N-Well Mask** | Photoresist | Photolithography | Pattern n-well regions |
| — | — | Phosphorus implant (≈ 80–150 keV, 5×10¹² cm⁻²) | Ion implantation | Dope n-well |
| 2 | **P-Well Mask** | Photoresist | Photolithography | Pattern p-well regions |
| — | — | Boron implant (≈ 50–80 keV, 1×10¹³ cm⁻²) | Ion implantation | Dope p-well |
| — | — | Drive-in (1100 °C, 4–6 h, N₂) | Thermal | Redistribute dopants, activate |
| 3 | **Active Area (Thinox)** | Si₃N₄ on pad oxide | LPCVD nitride + litho + etch | Define active transistor regions |
| — | — | LOCOS oxidation (1000 °C, 2 h, steam) | Thermal oxidation | Grow ≈ 500 nm field oxide |
| — | — | Strip nitride (H₃PO₄) + pad oxide (HF) | Wet etch | Expose active Si |
| 4 | **P-Channel (N⁺) Mask** | — | (Self-aligned, no mask) | Block NMOS S/D |
| — | — | Gate oxidation (≈ 10–20 nm SiO₂, 800–900 °C) | Thermal | Grow gate dielectric |
| — | — | Polysilicon deposition (LPCVD, ≈ 300–400 nm) | CVD | Gate electrode material |
| — | — | P-doped poly (POCl₃ or in-situ) | Doping | Lower poly resistance |
| 5 | **Poly Gate Mask** | Photoresist | Photolithography | Pattern gate electrodes |
| — | — | Poly etch (RIE / HBr) | Dry etch | Form gate electrodes |
| — | — | LDD implant (Phos. n⁻, Boron p⁻) | Implant | Lightly-doped drain extensions |
| 6 | **Spacer Formation** | CVD SiO₂/Si₃N₄ | Deposition + RIE | Form sidewall spacers |
| 7 | **N⁺ Source/Drain Mask** | Photoresist | Photolithography | Cover PMOS |
| — | — | Arsenic implant (≈ 50 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻²) | Implant | Form NMOS S/D |
| 8 | **P⁺ Source/Drain Mask** | Photoresist | Photolithography | Cover NMOS |
| — | — | BF₂ implant (≈ 50 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻²) | Implant | Form PMOS S/D |
| — | — | Anneal (RTA, 1000 °C, 10 s) | Rapid thermal | Activate, repair damage |
| — | — | Cobalt / Titanium salicide | Deposition + anneal | Lower S/D resistance |
| 9 | **Contact Mask** | BPSG deposition + reflow | CVD + thermal | Inter-layer dielectric |
| — | — | Photolithography + etch | RIE | Open contact holes |
| 10 | **Metal 1 Mask** | Al or Cu deposition | Sputtering / CVD | First interconnect |
| — | — | Photolithography + etch | RIE / W-CMP | Pattern Metal-1 |
| 11 | **Via Mask** | Inter-layer dielectric | CVD | Connect M1 → M2 |
| 12 | **Metal 2 Mask** | Al / Cu | Sputtering / CVD | Second interconnect |
| 13 | **Bond Pad / Passivation** | Si₃N₄ / SiO₂ | PECVD | Chip protection |
| — | (Optional: more metal layers) | … | … | Multi-level metallization |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Twin-Well Drive-In Equation (approximate profile):** After high-temperature drive-in, dopant distribution approximates a Gaussian:

$$ N(x,t) = \frac{Q}{\sqrt{\pi D t}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{x^2}{4Dt}\right) $$

where $D$ is the dopant diffusivity in Si at drive-in temperature, $t$ is drive-in time, and $Q$ is implanted dose.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Photolithography Resolution

**Question:** A 365 nm i-line stepper has $NA = 0.65$ and $k_1 = 0.5$. Calculate (a) the minimum half-pitch, (b) the depth of focus for $k_2 = 0.6$.

**Solution:**

$$ R = k_1 \frac{\lambda}{NA} = 0.5 \cdot \frac{365\,\text{nm}}{0.65} $$

$$ R = 0.5 \cdot 561.54 = 280.77 \text{ nm} \approx 0.28\,\mu\text{m} $$

$$ DOF = k_2 \frac{\lambda}{NA^2} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{365}{0.65^2} = 0.6 \cdot \frac{365}{0.4225} $$

$$ DOF = 0.6 \cdot 864.0 = 518.4 \text{ nm} $$

**Interpretation:** A 0.28 µm line can be printed, but the wafer topography must vary by less than ±0.5 µm — tight planarization requirement.

## 3.6 Worked Numerical — Twin-Well Implant Dose

**Question:** For a p-well formed by boron implant at 80 keV through 20 nm pad oxide, with dose $Q = 1 \times 10^{13}\,\text{cm}^{-2}$, calculate the peak concentration after a 4-hour drive-in at 1100 °C. ($D$ at 1100 °C ≈ $3 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$).

**Solution:**

$$ Dt = 3 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{cm}^2/\text{s} \times 4 \times 3600\,\text{s} = 4.32 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{cm}^2 $$

$$ \sqrt{\pi D t} = \sqrt{\pi \times 4.32 \times 10^{-9}} = \sqrt{1.357 \times 10^{-8}} = 1.165 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{cm} $$

Peak at $x = 0$:

$$ N_{\text{peak}} = \frac{10^{13}}{1.165 \times 10^{-4}} = 8.58 \times 10^{16}\,\text{cm}^{-3} $$

This is reasonable for a retrograde p-well surface concentration.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Photolithographic Sequence Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Wafer p-type Silicon] --> A1[RCA Clean]
    A1 --> A2[Dehydration Bake 150 C]
    A2 --> A3[HMDS Vapor Prime]
    A3 --> A4[Spin Coat Photoresist 3000 rpm]
    A4 --> A5[Soft Bake 90 C 60s]
    A5 --> A6[Mask Alignment under Microscope]
    A6 --> A7[UV Exposure 365 nm i-line]
    A7 --> A8[Post Exposure Bake 120 C]
    A8 --> A9[Development TMAH 2.38 percent]
    A9 --> A10[Hard Bake 130 C 30 min]
    A10 --> A11[Optical / SEM Inspection]
    A11 --> A12{Quality OK?}
    A12 -- No --> A13[Strip Resist and Reclean]
    A13 --> A1
    A12 -- Yes --> A14[Send to Etch or Implant]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each rectangle is one operation. The yellow diamond `A12` is the inspection decision gate — defective wafers recycle. This loop appears in every masking level of a CMOS process.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Electron Beam Lithography Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Electron Gun LaB6 or FEG] --> B2[Accelerator Column 10 to 100 kV]
    B2 --> B3[Magnetic Lens Stack]
    B3 --> B4[Beam Blanker]
    B4 --> B5[Deflection Coils XY]
    B5 --> B6[Stage with Wafer]
    B6 --> B7[Scattered Electrons]
    B7 --> B8[Detector for Imaging]
    B8 --> B9[Pattern Data Stream]
    B9 --> B5
    B1 --> B10[Vacuum System 10 to 6 Torr]
    B10 --> B2
    B1 --> B11[Resist PMMA or ZEP on Wafer]
    B6 --> B11
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** The deflection coils raster-scan the beam across the stage in *pixels*. Throughput is limited by *resist sensitivity* — typical **PMMA** requires ~100 µC/cm², giving write times of **hours per cm²**.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Twin-Well CMOS Process Vertical Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C0[p+ substrate] --> C1[p- epitaxial layer]
    C1 --> C2[Mask 1 N-well lithography]
    C2 --> C3[Phosphorus implant]
    C3 --> C4[Strip Resist]
    C4 --> C5[Mask 2 P-well lithography]
    C5 --> C6[Boron implant]
    C6 --> C7[Strip Resist]
    C7 --> C8[Well Drive-in 1100 C]
    C8 --> C9[LOCOS Field Oxidation]
    C9 --> C10[Gate Oxidation]
    C10 --> C11[Polysilicon Deposition]
    C11 --> C12[Mask 3 Poly Gate Lithography]
    C12 --> C13[Poly Etch RIE]
    C13 --> C14[LDD Implant]
    C14 --> C15[Spacer Formation]
    C15 --> C16[Mask 4 N+ S and D Lithography]
    C16 --> C17[Arsenic Implant]
    C17 --> C18[Mask 5 P+ S and D Lithography]
    C18 --> C19[BF2 Implant]
    C19 --> C20[RTA Anneal]
    C20 --> C21[Salicidation]
    C21 --> C22[Contact Mask and Etch]
    C22 --> C23[Metal 1 Deposition Sputtering]
    C23 --> C24[Metal 1 Patterning]
    C24 --> C25[Inter-layer Dielectric]
    C25 --> C26[Via and Metal 2]
    C26 --> C27[Passivation]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Etch & Deposition Module Integration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Patterned Wafer] --> D2[Load Lock]
    D2 --> D3[Transfer Chamber]
    D3 --> D4[Etch Chamber RIE or ICP]
    D3 --> D5[Deposition Chamber Sputter or CVD]
    D3 --> D6[Strip Chamber O2 Plasma]
    D4 --> D7[Etched Wafer]
    D5 --> D8[Deposited Wafer]
    D6 --> D9[Stripped Wafer]
    D7 --> D10[Inspection Station]
    D8 --> D10
    D9 --> D10
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Cluster tool concept:** A modern fab integrates etch, deposition, and strip chambers around a central transfer module — minimizing ambient exposure between steps.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List the ten standard steps of the photolithographic sequence in the correct order.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The canonical photolithographic sequence consists of the following ten operations, executed sequentially for each masking level:
1. Wafer cleaning (RCA / piranha)
2. Dehydration bake (150–200 °C)
3. HMDS priming
4. Photoresist spin coating (2000–6000 rpm)
5. Soft bake (90–120 °C)
6. Alignment and UV exposure
7. Post-exposure bake (PEB)
8. Development (TMAH for positive resist)
9. Hard bake (120–180 °C)
10. Inspection

*(Award 1 mark for naming 5–6 steps, 2 marks for 7–9, 3 marks for all 10 in correct order.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**Differentiate between wet etching and dry (plasma) etching in VLSI fabrication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Wet Etching | Dry (Plasma) Etching |
|---|---|---|
| Etchant | Liquid acid/alkali | Reactive gas plasma |
| Mechanism | Purely chemical | Chemical + ion bombardment |
| Anisotropy | Isotropic (A = 0) | Anisotropic (A → 1) |
| Selectivity | Very high | Moderate |
| Resolution | > 3 µm | < 0.1 µm |
| Damage | None | Possible ion damage |
| Example | BOE for SiO₂, KOH for Si | RIE with CF₄, Cl₂, HBr |

*(1 mark for mechanism, 1 mark for anisotropy contrast, 1 mark for example.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Photolithography & E-Beam*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the complete photolithographic sequence used in VLSI fabrication. Discuss the purpose and critical parameters of each step. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle, working, advantages, and limitations of Electron Beam Lithography. Derive the de Broglie wavelength for a 50 keV electron and comment on its resolution capability compared with 193 nm ArF optical lithography. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Photolithography is the technique of transferring circuit patterns from a mask onto a photoresist-coated wafer using UV light. It is the most repeated operation in IC fabrication.

**Step-by-Step Explanation (5 marks):**
The ten steps are:
1. **Wafer Cleaning** — RCA clean (NH₄OH/H₂O₂/H₂O followed by HCl/H₂O₂/H₂O) followed by dilute HF dip to remove native oxide. Removes particulates, organics, and metallic ions.
2. **Dehydration Bake** — 150–200 °C for 30 min in N₂; eliminates adsorbed moisture that would cause resist lifting.
3. **HMDS Priming** — HMDS vapor reacts with surface –OH groups to form hydrophobic Si–O–Si(CH₃)₃, improving resist adhesion.
4. **Photoresist Coating** — Liquid resist dispensed; wafer spun at 3000–6000 rpm. Final thickness t ∝ √(η/ω).
5. **Soft Bake** — 90–120 °C on hot plate for 60 s; removes solvent (≈ 20 %) to densify the resist.
6. **Alignment and Exposure** — Mask and wafer aligned via alignment marks; UV exposure dose ≈ 50–200 mJ/cm².
7. **Post-Exposure Bake** — Critical for chemically amplified resists; drives photo-acid catalyzed reaction.
8. **Development** — TMAH (2.38 %) for 60 s in puddle/spray mode; dissolves exposed positive-resist regions.
9. **Hard Bake** — 120–180 °C; hardens resist to withstand implant/etch.
10. **Inspection** — Optical or SEM check; defectivity budget < 0.05 defects/cm².

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Each step's parameters must be optimized to achieve sub-micron feature fidelity; even a single under-bake can scrap an entire wafer lot.

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
E-beam lithography uses a focused beam of high-energy electrons to directly write patterns into an electron-sensitive resist, without a physical mask. It is used for mask-making, R&D, and low-volume production.

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A thermionic (LaB₆) or field-emission gun (FEG) emits electrons accelerated through 10–100 kV. Magnetic lenses focus the beam to a spot < 10 nm. Beam blanker turns the beam on/off; deflection coils raster-scan across the stage. The resist (PMMA, ZEP, or HSQ) undergoes chain scission (positive-tone) or cross-linking (negative-tone) upon exposure.

**Advantages (1 mark):**
* Sub-10 nm resolution achievable.
* Maskless — no reticle cost.
* Excellent CD control.
* Direct write on non-flat surfaces.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
* Very slow (hours per cm²).
* Proximity effect from back-scattered electrons.
* Limited throughput — unsuitable for high-volume IC production.

**Derivation (1.5 marks):**
Energy conservation: $eV = \frac{p^2}{2m_e}$ → $p = \sqrt{2m_e eV}$.

de Broglie: $\lambda_e = h/p = h/\sqrt{2m_e eV}$.

Plugging in $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V = 50{,}000$ V:

$$ \lambda_e = \frac{1.227}{\sqrt{50000}} = \frac{1.227}{223.6} \approx 0.00549 \text{ nm} $$

**Comparison (0.5 mark):**
Optical 193 nm vs. e-beam 0.0055 nm — a ratio of **≈ 35,000×**. This is why e-beam can resolve features below 10 nm while 193 nm immersion lithography is limited to ≈ 38 nm half-pitch.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Etching, Deposition & Twin-Well Process*

**(a)** Explain wet etching and dry etching in VLSI. Compare their etch profiles, selectivity, and resolution. Discuss the working principle of Reactive Ion Etching (RIE) with a schematic. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the CMOS Twin-Well (Twin-Tub) fabrication process. Highlight the role of twin-well structure in optimizing NMOS and PMOS performance. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a)

**Wet Etching (2 marks):**
Wet etching uses liquid chemicals — e.g., HF for SiO₂, H₃PO₄ for Si₃N₄, KOH for Si. Mechanism is purely chemical reaction, producing soluble by-products. Profile is *isotropic* — the etch proceeds equally in all directions, causing severe undercutting under the mask. Used in early IC processes and MEMS bulk micromachining due to excellent selectivity.

**Dry Etching (2 marks):**
Dry etching uses reactive plasma (e.g., CF₄, SF₆, Cl₂, HBr). Two mechanisms operate simultaneously:
* **Chemical component** — radicals react with surface to form volatile products (e.g., Si + 4F → SiF₄↑).
* **Physical component** — energetic ions (≈ 100–1000 eV) bombard the wafer vertically, *sputtering* material and providing directionality.

**Comparison (1.5 marks):**
Wet etch: $A = 0$, selectivity > 100, resolution > 3 µm, no plasma damage.
Dry etch: $A \to 1$, selectivity 5–50, resolution < 0.1 µm, some damage possible.

**RIE Working (1.5 marks):**
In RIE, the wafer sits on the *powered electrode* (cathode). RF power (13.56 MHz) creates a self-biased DC voltage (≈ –300 V) that accelerates ions *toward the wafer*. The opposite (grounded) electrode has larger area, ensuring ion bombardment dominates. CF₄ + O₂ mixture is typical for SiO₂; Cl₂ for Al; HBr/Cl₂ for poly-Si.

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The CMOS Twin-Well (Twin-Tub) process forms independent n-wells and p-wells on the same wafer, allowing both NMOS and PMOS to be optimized for threshold voltage, body effect, and mobility.

**Process Flow (4 marks):**
1. Start with p⁺ substrate with p⁻ epitaxial layer (≈ 5–10 µm) for latch-up suppression.
2. **Mask 1 — n-well lithography:** photoresist patterning, phosphorus implant (80–150 keV, 5×10¹² cm⁻²), resist strip.
3. **Mask 2 — p-well lithography:** boron implant (50–80 keV, 1×10¹³ cm⁻²), resist strip.
4. **Well drive-in:** 1100 °C for 4–6 h in N₂; redistributes dopants and forms retrograde profile.
5. **Active area definition:** pad oxide (20 nm) + LPCVD Si₃N₄ (100 nm); Mask 3 lithography + RIE etch.
6. **LOCOS oxidation:** 1000 °C steam → ≈ 500 nm field oxide; nitride stripped in H₃PO₄.
7. **Gate oxide:** 10–20 nm SiO₂ at 800–900 °C.
8. **Polysilicon:** 300 nm LPCVD; POCl₃ doped; Mask 4 (poly gate) lithography + RIE etch.
9. **LDD implant + spacer formation:** thin oxide/nitride + RIE spacer etch.
10. **Mask 5 — n⁺ S/D lithography:** arsenic implant (50 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻²).
11. **Mask 6 — p⁺ S/D lithography:** BF₂ implant (50 keV, 5×10¹⁵ cm⁻²).
12. **RTA anneal:** 1000 °C, 10 s → activate, repair.
13. **Salicidation:** Co/Ti deposition + 2-step RTA.
14. **Back-end:** BPSG, contact litho, Al/Cu metal 1 sputtering, via, metal 2, passivation.

**Significance of Twin-Well (2 marks):**
1. **Independent optimization:** n-well doping for PMOS, p-well doping for NMOS — symmetric threshold voltages possible.
2. **Retrograde profile:** Surface concentration high, deep concentration low → reduces body effect, suppresses punch-through.
3. **Latch-up immunity:** p⁺ substrate + epi layer provides low-resistance shunt path for parasitic thyristor currents.
4. **Scalability:** Twin-well scales better than single-well for sub-micron CMOS because both wells can be tailored.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Board Exams:**
> 1. **Wrong step order in photolithography:** PEB comes *after* exposure and *before* development — not before exposure. Examiners deduct 1 mark if confused.
> 2. **Confusing wet etch with dry etch profiles:** Many students draw *anisotropic wet etch* — wet etching is *always* isotropic. Drawing a vertical profile for wet etch costs 1 mark.
> 3. **E-beam wavelength formula:** Use $\lambda_e = h/\sqrt{2m_e eV}$ or the practical $1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm form. Using $\lambda = c/f$ (photon formula) earns **zero** marks.
> 4. **Twin-well nomenclature:** "Twin-tub" and "twin-well" are *synonymous*. Saying "p-well only" or "n-well only" is wrong if the question specifies twin-well.
> 5. **Missing RTA (Rapid Thermal Anneal) step:** Required after S/D implant to activate dopants; forgetting it loses 1 mark.
> 6. **LOCOS vs STI:** LOCOS is used for > 0.5 µm nodes; Shallow Trench Isolation (STI) is used for < 0.25 µm nodes. Examiners expect *node-specific* answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist:**

**Photolithography:**
- Ten canonical steps in fixed order.
- Positive resist exposed region dissolves; negative resist exposed region stays.
- Resolution: $R = k_1 \lambda/NA$; minimum $k_1 = 0.25$.
- DOF: $DOF = k_2 \lambda/NA^2$ — decreases quadratically with NA.
- Soft bake removes solvent; hard bake hardens resist; PEB drives CAR chemistry.

**E-beam Lithography:**
- Maskless, direct-write, sub-10 nm resolution.
- $\lambda_e = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm.
- Limited throughput → used for mask-making and R&D.
- Proximity effect corrected by dose modulation / GHOST.

**Etching:**
- Wet = chemical, isotropic, high selectivity, low resolution.
- Dry = plasma, anisotropic, moderate selectivity, high resolution.
- RIE: wafer on powered electrode; CF₄/O₂ for SiO₂, Cl₂ for Al, HBr/Cl₂ for poly-Si.
- Key metrics: Etch rate, Selectivity $S = R_A/R_B$, Anisotropy $A = 1 - R_{lat}/R_{vert}$.

**Metal Deposition:**
- Evaporation (thermal / e-beam): poor step coverage.
- Sputtering: better step coverage; DC for conductors, RF for insulators.
- CVD/PECVD/LPCVD: best step coverage, used for high-aspect-ratio vias.
- Step coverage $SC = t_{bottom}/t_{top}$ in %.

**CMOS Twin-Well Process:**
- p⁺ substrate + p⁻ epi for latch-up suppression.
- Two masked implants: n-well (P) and p-well (B) — independent optimization.
- LOCOS field isolation (> 0.5 µm) or STI (< 0.25 µm).
- Gate oxide (10–20 nm) → poly gate → LDD → spacer → n⁺/p⁺ S/D → RTA → silicide → BPSG → contacts → Metal-1 → vias → Metal-2 → passivation.
- Twin-well advantages: symmetric NMOS/PMOS, retrograde profile, reduced body effect, latch-up immunity.

**Master Equation List (memorize for KTU):**
- $R = k_1 \lambda/NA$
- $DOF = k_2 \lambda/NA^2$
- $\lambda_e = 1.227/\sqrt{V}$ nm
- $S = R_A/R_B$
- $A = 1 - R_{lat}/R_{vert}$
- $t \propto \sqrt{\eta/\omega}$ (spin coating)
- $N(x,t) = (Q/\sqrt{\pi D t})\exp(-x^2/4Dt)$ (Gaussian diffusion profile)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Moore's Law, ASIC Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Moore's Law, ASIC Design # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Moore's Law ### Formal Definition **Moore's Law** is the empirical observation made by **Gordon E. Moore** (co-founder of Intel) in **1965** that the number of transistors on an integrated circuit (IC) doubles approximately every **18 to 24 months**, while the cost per transistor and the power consumption per transistor decrease. This trend translates into a corresponding increase in computational performance, memory capacity, and overall functional density of chips over time. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Moore's Law is treated as the *driving empirical principle* of the semiconductor industry. The original 1965 paper predicted a doubling **every year** for the next ten years. In **1975**, Moore revised his prediction to a doubling **every two years**, which is the version universally cited in modern VLSI literature and KTU examinations. ### The Two Original Predictions (1965 & 1975) | Year | Source | Doubling Period | Context | |------|--------|-----------------|---------| | 1965 | Electronics Magazine Article | **1 year** (for next 10 years) | Observation from 1959–1965 with 4 components/chip → 32 components/chip | | 1975 | IEDM Conference Revision | **2 years** | Refined based on real industry trends (economic drivers) | ### Intuitive Analogy Imagine a classroom where the **teacher's desk (chip area)** stays the same size, but every two years, the **teacher can fit twice as many students (transistors) into the same desk**. Not only that — each student becomes **cheaper to seat** and uses **less energy per hour**. So with the *same* budget, the school (semiconductor industry) gets **double the teaching power** every two years, enabling smarter, faster, and more complex educational tools (smartphones, AI, gaming, etc.). ### Physical & Economic Constants Embedded in Moore's Law > [!NOTE] > - **Lithography node halving cadence** ≈ every **3–4 years** (industry "node" names: 90nm → 65nm → 45nm → 32nm → 22nm → 14nm → 10nm → 7nm → 5nm → 3nm). > - **Cost per transistor** historically dropped by roughly **30% per year**. > - **Clock frequency** scaling slowed after ~2005 (the "Power Wall"), forcing the industry toward **multi-core** architectures. --- ## 1.2 ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit) ### Formal Definition An **ASIC** is an integrated circuit **custom-designed for a specific application, function, or end-product**, rather than being a general-purpose device. ASICs trade off flexibility (the chip cannot be repurposed once fabricated) for superior performance, lower power consumption, smaller die area, and higher per-unit cost efficiency at scale. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Style):** *An ASIC is an IC designed for a particular application, as opposed to a general-purpose device like a microprocessor. The design may use standard cells, gate arrays, FPGAs, or full-custom layouts.* ### Intuitive Analogy A **general-purpose processor** (like a laptop CPU) is like a **Swiss Army knife** — it can cut, saw, open bottles, and more, but it is not the *best* at any single task. An **ASIC**, on the other hand, is like a **surgical scalpel** — designed to do *one thing* (say, encrypt a video stream for Netflix) with **maximum efficiency, minimum power, and maximum speed**. A scalpel cannot be used to cut bread, just as an ASIC cannot be reprogrammed for a different task once fabricated. ### Major Categories of ASICs (KTU Classification) | Category | Description | NRE Cost | Unit Cost | Turnaround | |----------|-------------|----------|-----------|------------| | **Full-Custom ASIC** | Every transistor and interconnect is hand-designed | Very High | Lowest | Longest | | **Semi-Custom ASIC** | Uses pre-designed logic cells (Standard Cell or Gate Array) | Medium | Low | Moderate | | **Programmable ASIC** (FPGA / PLD) | Logic is configured by the user post-fabrication | Lowest | Highest | Shortest | | **Structured ASIC** | Predefined metal layers with customizable top layers | Medium | Low-Medium | Short-Moderate | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Moore's Law — Underlying Physics, Economics & Engineering ### Why Moore's Law Held (for ~50 years) The sustainability of Moore's Law rests on **four pillars**: 1. **Lithographic Resolution Improvement** - Wavelength of light reduced: g-line (436 nm) → i-line (365 nm) → KrF (248 nm) → ArF (193 nm) → EUV (13.5 nm). - Numerical Aperture (NA) increased via immersion lithography. 2. **Wafer Size Scaling** - Wafer diameter: 50 mm (2") → 100 mm (4") → 150 mm (6") → 200 mm (8") → **300 mm (12")** → 450 mm (planned, deferred). - Larger wafers yield more dies per wafer → lower per-die cost. 3. **Yield Improvement** - Defect density (D₀) reduced through cleaner cleanrooms (Class 1 → Class 0.1). - Mature processes (e.g., 28 nm planar CMOS) achieve yields > **90%**. 4. **Design Tool Innovation** - EDA (Electronic Design Automation) tools: synthesis, place-and-route, DRC, LVS, OPC, DFM. ### Three Forms of Scaling > [!NOTE] > **Dennard Scaling (1974) — the original foundation:** > If linear dimensions of a MOSFET scale by factor **S < 1** (e.g., 0.7), then: > - Voltage: V → S·V > - Current: I → S·I > - Capacitance: C → S·C > - Power per transistor: P → S²·P > - Power density: **constant** (P/Area unchanged) **Dennard Scaling broke down around 2005** because threshold voltage (Vₜ) could not be scaled further without exponentially increasing leakage current. This is the **"Power Wall"**. ### Economic Interpretation of Moore's Law > [!IMPORTANT] > Moore's Law is fundamentally an **economic curve**, not a physical law. It has held because: > - Every transistor-volume doubling justifies a new **fab costing $10–20 billion** (TSMC 3 nm fab: ~$20 B). > - Software complexity (OS, games, AI) **grows to fill the hardware**, providing demand. > - **Learning curve (Wright's Law)**: every cumulative doubling of production reduces cost by a fixed percentage (~28% for semiconductors). ### Predictions Beyond Classical Moore's Law | Trend | Acronym | Concept | |-------|---------|---------| | More than Moore | **MtM** | Heterogeneous integration (MEMS, RF, analog, sensors) on the same die/package | | More Moore | — | Continued geometric scaling (FinFET → GAAFET → CFET → 1.5 nm) | | Beyond CMOS | — | Quantum computing, spintronics, photonics, neuromorphic | ## 2.2 ASIC Design — Deep Architecture ### ASIC Design Flow (Standard Cell based — KTU High-Yield) The canonical KTU-asked flow consists of the following **9 stages**: 1. **Specification** — Functional, timing, power, area, I/O requirements. 2. **RTL Design** — VHDL/Verilog/SystemVerilog description. 3. **Functional Verification** — Simulation against testbench. 4. **Logic Synthesis** — RTL → Gate-level netlist using standard cells. 5. **Floorplanning** — Placement of I/O pads, macros, power grid. 6. **Placement** — Positioning of standard cells. 7. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — Balanced clock distribution. 8. **Routing** — Global and detailed routing of signal nets. 9. **Physical Verification & Sign-off** — DRC, LVS, ERC, antenna checks, timing closure. ### Full-Custom vs Semi-Custom vs FPGA — KTU Comparison | Parameter | Full-Custom | Standard Cell ASIC | Gate Array | FPGA | |-----------|-------------|-------------------|------------|------| | Transistor design | Manual | Library-based | Pre-fabricated | Pre-fabricated LUTs | | NRE cost | Very High | High | Medium | None | | Per-unit cost | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest | | Performance | Highest | High | High | Lower | | Power | Lowest | Low | Medium | Higher | | Design time | Longest | Moderate | Short | Shortest | | Flexibility | None | None | None | High (reprogrammable) | ### KTU Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the official high-yield formula reference for Module 2 (Moore's Law & ASIC Design). All numerical questions in KTU exams are solved using these relations.** | # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units / Notes | |---|-------------------|----------------------|---------------| | 1 | Moore's Law (transistor count) | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$ | $T_d$ = doubling time (≈ 2 years) | | 2 | Transistor density growth | $D(t) = D_0 \cdot 2^{t/T_d}$ | transistors / cm² | | 3 | Dennard voltage scaling | $V' = S \cdot V$ | $S < 1$ is scale factor | | 4 | Dennard power scaling | $P' = S^2 \cdot P$ | Power per transistor | | 5 | Yield model (Seeds) | $Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$ | $D_0$: defects/cm², $A$: die area | | 6 | Cost per die | $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{Y \cdot N_{dies/wafer}}$ | $C_{wafer}$: processed wafer cost | | 7 | Dies per wafer | $N = \dfrac{\pi (R^2 - r^2)}{A_{die}} - \dfrac{\pi R}{\sqrt{2 A_{die}}}$ | $R$: wafer radius, $r$: edge exclusion | | 8 | Feature size scaling | $F(t) = F_0 \cdot S^{k}$ | $S$ per node (typically 0.7×) | | 9 | Clock frequency target | $f_{clk} \propto \dfrac{1}{S \cdot C_L \cdot V}$ | Increased by Dennard scaling | | 10 | ASIC NRE cost model | $NRE = C_{mask} + C_{design} + C_{test}$ | Mask set is dominant for <28 nm | ### Real-World Engineering Utility - **CPU/GPU industries** (Intel, AMD, NVIDIA, Apple) follow Moore's Law to plan 5-year product roadmaps. - **ASIC design** is the standard methodology for **smartphone SoCs** (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon, MediaTek Dimensity), **AI accelerators** (Google TPU, NVIDIA Tensor Cores, Apple Neural Engine), and **crypto/bitcoin mining chips**. - **Automotive** (ADAS), **aerospace**, **medical** (pacemakers, hearing aids) — all depend on ASICs for low-power, deterministic, certified hardware. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mathematical Form of Moore's Law ### Given - $N_0$ = initial number of transistors (at time $t_0$) - $N(t)$ = number of transistors at time $t$ - $T_d$ = doubling period (years) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Moore's Law states that the transistor count doubles every $T_d$ years. This is a **geometric progression**. $$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{k}$$ where $k$ = number of doubling periods elapsed. **Step 2:** The number of doublings between time $t_0$ and time $t$ is: $$k = \frac{t - t_0}{T_d}$$ **Step 3:** Substituting back: $$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}}$$ **Step 4:** Taking the natural logarithm of both sides (useful for linear analysis): $$\ln N(t) = \ln N_0 + \frac{(t - t_0)}{T_d} \ln 2$$ This is the equation of a **straight line** when $\ln N$ is plotted against $t$, with slope $\frac{\ln 2}{T_d}$. This is exactly the famous *linear-on-log-scale* plot of Moore's Law. ### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style) **Q:** A chip launched in **2010** has **1.0 billion transistors**. Using $T_d = 2$ years, estimate the transistor count in **2024** (KTU Dec 2024 model). **Step 1:** Compute elapsed time: $$\Delta t = 2024 - 2010 = 14 \text{ years}$$ **Step 2:** Compute number of doublings: $$k = \frac{14}{2} = 7 \text{ doublings}$$ **Step 3:** Apply Moore's Law: $$N(2024) = 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 2^{7}$$ $$N(2024) = 1.0 \times 10^9 \times 128$$ $$\boxed{N(2024) = 1.28 \times 10^{11} \text{ transistors}}$$ This is **128 billion transistors** — consistent with real chips like Apple's M3 Ultra (~134 billion transistors). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cost per Die and Yield Relationship ### Given - $C_{wafer}$ = cost of one processed wafer (depends on fab, node, wafer size) - $N$ = number of good dies per wafer - $Y$ = yield (fraction of working dies, 0 < Y ≤ 1) - $D_0$ = defect density (defects per cm²) - $A$ = die area (cm²) ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1:** Total dies fabricated per wafer (gross) is $N_{gross}$. Of these, only the fraction $Y$ are functional. $$N_{good} = Y \cdot N_{gross}$$ **Step 2:** Cost per good die is therefore: $$C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{good}} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{Y \cdot N_{gross}}$$ **Step 3:** Assuming Poisson defect distribution (Seeds model), the probability that a die of area $A$ has **zero** defects is: $$Y = e^{-D_0 \cdot A}$$ **Step 4:** Substituting: $$\boxed{C_{die} = \frac{C_{wafer}}{N_{gross} \cdot e^{-D_0 \cdot A}} = \frac{C_{wafer} \cdot e^{D_0 \cdot A}}{N_{gross}}}$$ **Step 5 (Interpretation):** Cost per die **grows exponentially** with die area and defect density. Hence the industry's strong incentive to **shrink** the die (Moore's Law!) and **improve** process cleanliness (lower $D_0$). ### Numerical Worked Example **Q:** A 300 mm wafer costs **$3000** to process. The die area is **1 cm²**, defect density is **0.5 defects/cm²**, and the wafer yields **400 gross dies**. Compute cost per good die. **Step 1:** Compute yield: $$Y = e^{-0.5 \times 1} = e^{-0.5} = 0.6065$$ **Step 2:** Compute good dies: $$N_{good} = 0.6065 \times 400 = 242.6 \text{ dies}$$ **Step 3:** Cost per die: $$C_{die} = \frac{3000}{242.6} = \$12.37$$ $$\boxed{C_{die} \approx \$12.37 \text{ per die}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Dennard Scaling Breakdown (Power Wall Analysis) ### Given - $S = 0.7$ (linear scale factor, typical for a technology node) - $V_{dd,old} = 1.0$ V, $V_{t,old} = 0.35$ V ### Step-by-Step Analysis **Step 1:** Under ideal Dennard scaling: $$V_{new} = 0.7 \times 1.0 = 0.7 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{t,new} = 0.7 \times 0.35 = 0.245 \text{ V}$$ **Step 2:** Compute leakage current ratio (subthreshold leakage grows exponentially with $V_t$ reduction): $$I_{leak,ratio} \propto e^{(V_{t,old} - V_{t,new})/(nV_T)} = e^{(0.105)/(1.0 \cdot 0.0259)}$$ where $V_T = kT/q = 25.9$ mV at room temperature. $$I_{leak,ratio} = e^{4.05} \approx 57.4$$ **Step 3:** Interpretation: **leakage current grew by ~57× per scaling generation**, which made further voltage scaling **economically and thermally infeasible**. $$\boxed{\text{Hence Dennard Scaling ended near 90 nm / 65 nm node (circa 2005)}}$$ This is the *physical* reason the industry pivoted to **multi-core CPUs** instead of higher clock frequencies. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: ASIC Design Trade-offs | Design Choice | Optimizes For | Sacrifices | |---------------|---------------|-----------| | Full-custom | Speed, area, power | Design time, NRE cost | | Standard cell | Balanced P-P-A | Mask cost | | Gate array | Low NRE, fast TTM | Area, performance | | FPGA | Reprogrammability | Performance, power, cost-per-volume | | Structured ASIC | Mid-volume trade-off | Flexibility | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Moore's Law — Trend and Future (Block-Level Functional Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Moore 1965
Electronics Magazine
Observation: 4 to 32 components per chip"] --> B["Moore 1975
IEDM Revision
Doubling every 2 years"] B --> C["Industry Roadmap (IRTS / ITRS)"] C --> D["Physical Drivers"] C --> E["Economic Drivers"] C --> F["Design Drivers"] D --> D1["Lithography Wavelength
Reduction g-line to EUV 13.5nm"] D --> D2["Wafer Size Growth
50mm to 300mm"] D --> D3["Dennard Scaling
Broke at 90nm circa 2005"] E --> E1["Learning Curve
Wright Law 28% cost drop per doubling"] E --> E2["Fab Investment
10B to 20B dollars per node"] F --> F1["EDA Tools
Synthesis PnR DRC LVS"] F --> F2["Design Complexity
From SSI to VLSI to SoC"] D1 --> G["Continued Scaling 2024"] D2 --> G D3 --> H["Power Wall Multi Core"] E1 --> G E2 --> G F1 --> G F2 --> G G --> I["More Moore
GAAFET 2nm 1.5nm A14"] H --> J["More than Moore
3D Packaging Chiplets"] G --> K["Beyond CMOS
Quantum Spintronics Photonics"] I --> L["Future of Computing"] J --> L K --> L ``` ## 4.2 ASIC Design Flow — Block-Level Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["1. Specification
Functionality Timing Power Area I/O"] --> S2["2. RTL Design
VHDL Verilog SystemVerilog"] S2 --> S3["3. Functional Verification
Simulation Testbench Coverage"] S3 --> S4{"4. Logic Synthesis
RTL to Gate Netlist
Standard Cell Library"} S4 --> S5["5. Floorplanning
I/O Pads Macros Power Grid"] S5 --> S6["6. Placement
Standard Cell Positioning"] S6 --> S7["7. Clock Tree Synthesis
Balanced Skew Clock"] S7 --> S8["8. Routing
Global Detailed Signal Interconnect"] S8 --> S9["9. Sign off
DRC LVS ERC Antenna Timing Power"] S9 --> S10["Tapeout to Foundry"] S10 --> S11["Fabrication Packaging Testing"] S11 --> S12["Silicon Validated ASIC"] ``` ## 4.3 ASIC Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD R["ASIC Classification"] --> R1["Full Custom ASIC"] R --> R2["Semi Custom ASIC"] R --> R3["Programmable ASIC"] R1 --> R1A["Hand designed transistors
Highest performance
Highest NRE cost"] R2 --> R2A["Standard Cell Based"] R2 --> R2B["Gate Array Based"] R2 --> R2C["Structured ASIC"] R2A --> R2A1["Pre designed logic cell library
Automated place and route
Best for high volume SoC"] R2B --> R2B1["Prefabricated transistor array
Only metal layers customized
Lower NRE fast turnaround"] R2C --> R2C1["Pre defined base layers
Custom top metal layers
Mid volume trade off"] R3 --> R3A["FPGA"] R3 --> R3B["CPLD"] R3 --> R3C["PLD"] R3A --> R3A1["LUT based logic
SRAM Flash antifuse config
Reprogrammable in field"] R3B --> R3B1["Complex programmable logic
Non volatile macro cells
Glue logic replacement"] R3C --> R3C1["Simple programmable logic
PAL GAL architectures
Low complexity glue logic"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. State Moore's Law. Mention the original and revised doubling periods. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **Model Answer:** Moore's Law, formulated by **Gordon E. Moore** in **1965**, is the empirical observation that the **number of transistors on an integrated circuit doubles approximately every 18 to 24 months**, leading to an exponential increase in computational capability and a corresponding decrease in cost per transistor. - **Original (1965):** Doubling every **1 year** (for the next 10 years). - **Revised (1975):** Doubling every **2 years** (more accurate, universally used today). --- ### Q2. Differentiate between Full-Custom ASIC and Standard Cell ASIC. `[KTU University Exam - June 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Full-Custom ASIC | Standard Cell ASIC | |-----------|------------------|--------------------| | Transistor design | Every transistor hand-crafted | Pre-designed standard cell library used | | NRE cost | Very high (millions of $) | Moderate to high | | Per-unit cost | Lowest (large volumes) | Low | | Design time | Longest | Moderate | | EDA automation | Minimal | Full (synthesis, P&R) | | Best suited for | High-volume analog/RF/mixed-signal | Digital SoC, processors | --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **Choose either A or B.** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the mathematical expression for Moore's Law. A chip fabricated in **2015** has **2.0 billion transistors**. Using a doubling period of **2 years**, calculate the projected transistor count in **2023**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain in detail the **ASIC design flow** with a neat block diagram. List any **four advantages** of ASICs over general-purpose processors. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q1 (a) **Derivation:** Moore's Law states that transistor count doubles every $T_d$ years. Therefore: $$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}}$$ **Step-by-step deduction:** Step 1: Let $N_0$ be transistor count at $t_0$. After one doubling period, the count becomes $2N_0$. Step 2: After $k$ such doublings (i.e., $k \cdot T_d$ years), the count is $N_0 \cdot 2^k$. Step 3: Since $k = (t - t_0)/T_d$: $$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$$ **Numerical computation:** Step 1: Elapsed time $\Delta t = 2023 - 2015 = 8$ years. Step 2: Number of doublings $k = 8 / 2 = 4$. Step 3: $N(2023) = 2.0 \times 10^9 \times 2^4 = 2.0 \times 10^9 \times 16$. $$\boxed{N(2023) = 3.2 \times 10^{10} = 32 \text{ billion transistors}}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Q1 (b) The ASIC design flow consists of the following stages (must be presented with diagram and brief description for full marks): 1. **Specification** — Define functionality, performance, power, area. 2. **RTL Design** — Hardware description in VHDL/Verilog. 3. **Functional Verification** — Testbench simulation. 4. **Logic Synthesis** — Convert RTL to gate-level netlist. 5. **Floorplanning** — Define I/O, macros, power network. 6. **Placement** — Position standard cells. 7. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — Build balanced clock distribution. 8. **Routing** — Connect all signal nets. 9. **Physical Verification & Sign-off** — DRC, LVS, ERC, timing closure. **Four Advantages of ASICs over General-Purpose Processors:** 1. **Higher performance** — Customized logic optimized for the specific task. 2. **Lower power consumption** — No instruction-fetch/decode overhead. 3. **Smaller die area / smaller form factor** — Only the required logic is fabricated. 4. **Lower per-unit cost at high volume** — One-time NRE amortized over millions of units. 5. **Higher IP protection** — Reverse engineering is much harder. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) **(a)** What is **Dennard Scaling**? With a neat derivation, explain why it broke down near the **90 nm technology node**. Discuss the consequences on modern CPU design. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare and contrast **Full-Custom, Standard-Cell, Gate-Array, and FPGA** design styles. For a low-volume, fast-turnaround product (e.g., a startup prototype), which style is most suitable and why? **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q2 (a) **Dennard Scaling (1974):** When MOSFET linear dimensions are scaled by a factor $S$ (where $S < 1$, e.g., 0.7), the following scale automatically: - Voltage: $V' = S \cdot V$ - Current: $I' = S \cdot I$ - Capacitance: $C' = S \cdot C$ - Power per transistor: $P' = S^2 \cdot P$ - Power density: **constant** (no increase in W/cm²) **Derivation of breakdown near 90 nm:** Step 1: Subthreshold leakage current is: $$I_{leak} \propto e^{-V_{gs}/(nV_T)} \cdot (1 - e^{-V_{ds}/V_T})$$ Step 2: With $V_t$ scaling as $S \cdot V_t$, the ratio of new to old leakage is: $$I_{leak,ratio} = e^{(V_{t,old} - V_{t,new})/(nV_T)} = e^{V_{t,old}(1-S)/(nV_T)}$$ Step 3: For $S = 0.7$, $V_{t,old} = 0.35$ V, $V_T = 0.0259$ V, $n \approx 1.0$: $$I_{leak,ratio} = e^{0.35 \times 0.3 / 0.0259} = e^{4.05} \approx 57.4$$ Step 4: Leakage grew by ~57× per node, making standby power dominant. **Consequence on modern CPU design:** - Industry abandoned single-core frequency scaling. - Shifted to **multi-core** architectures (frequency capped near 3–5 GHz). - Introduced **FinFET** (3D tri-gate) at 22 nm to regain electrostatic control. - Adoption of **dynamic voltage-frequency scaling (DVFS)**, **power gating**, and **dark silicon** concepts. --- #### Model Solution — Q2 (b) **Comparison Table:** | Parameter | Full-Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array | FPGA | |-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|------| | Transistor layout | Manual | Library-based | Pre-fabricated | Pre-fabricated LUTs | | NRE cost | Very high | High | Medium | Negligible | | Unit cost (at volume) | Lowest | Low | Medium | Highest | | Performance | Highest | High | High | Lower | | Power | Lowest | Low | Medium | High | | Time-to-market | Slowest | Medium | Fast | Fastest | | Flexibility | None | None | None | Reprogrammable | **Best style for low-volume, fast-turnaround product (startup prototype):** **FPGA** is most suitable because: 1. **Zero NRE cost** — no masks or fab cycle required. 2. **Instant iteration** — design changes implemented by re-flashing the bitstream in seconds/hours. 3. **In-house verification** — no need to wait for foundry turnaround (saves months). 4. **Reprogrammable** — same chip can be reused for multiple prototypes. 5. The final product can later be migrated to a **Standard Cell ASIC** for mass production (a common "FPGA-to-ASIC" conversion path used by startups like Bitmain, etc.). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Do not** quote Moore's Law as a "physical law" — it is an **empirical observation** / **economic trend**. > 2. In numerical problems, always write the **doubling time $T_d$** explicitly in the formula. Forgetting $T_d = 2$ loses 1 mark. > 3. Distinguish between **Moore's Law (transistor doubling)** and **Dennard Scaling (voltage/power scaling)** — examiners frequently swap them as trick questions. > 4. For ASIC design flow, mention **both the stage name and its purpose**. Writing only names without explanation loses 1–2 marks. > 5. **Do not confuse** Gate Array with FPGA — Gate Arrays are **mask-programmed** (one-time), FPGAs are **field-programmable** (reprogrammable). --- ## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > ### Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before exam) - ✅ **Moore's Law** = transistor count doubles every **~2 years** (revised 1975 version). - ✅ Mathematical form: $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{(t-t_0)/T_d}$. - ✅ Moore's Law is **empirical, not physical**. - ✅ **Dennard Scaling** broke at **~90 nm / 2005** → caused **multi-core era**. - ✅ **Wafer size** today: **300 mm** (12 inch). - ✅ **Lithography** evolved: g-line (436 nm) → i-line (365 nm) → KrF (248 nm) → ArF (193 nm) → **EUV (13.5 nm)**. - ✅ ASIC = **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit** — designed for one task. - ✅ Four ASIC categories: **Full-Custom, Standard Cell, Gate Array, FPGA**. - ✅ **FPGA** = best for low volume + fast turnaround. - ✅ **Standard Cell ASIC** = best for high volume digital SoC. - ✅ **Full-Custom** = best performance, highest NRE. - ✅ Yield model: $Y = e^{-D_0 A}$ (Seeds / Poisson). - ✅ Cost per die: $C_{die} = \dfrac{C_{wafer}}{Y \cdot N_{dies/wafer}}$. - ✅ ASIC design flow has **9 stages**: Spec → RTL → Verify → Synth → Floorplan → Place → CTS → Route → Sign-off. - ✅ Modern trends: **More Moore, More than Moore, Beyond CMOS**. - ✅ **FinFET / GAAFET** adopted at **22 nm / 3 nm** to address leakage. - ✅ **Chiplets, 3D-IC, 2.5D packaging** = "More than Moore" path forward. - ✅ **Apple M-series, Snapdragon, Google TPU** = real-world ASIC examples. - ✅ Moore's 1965 paper was titled **"Cramming more components onto integrated circuits"** in *Electronics Magazine*.

topics.title: Full Custom ASICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Full Custom ASICs**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **Full Custom ASIC** is an Application-Specific Integrated Circuit in which **every single transistor, every interconnect, and every geometric shape on every layer is individually designed, optimized, and laid out by hand** (or with fine-grained, mask-level CAD tools) to meet a specific performance, area, and power target. There are **no pre-defined logic gates or standard cells** that are merely placed-and-routed; the designer is free to draw the diffusion, polysilicon, and metal geometries from scratch.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** In a Full Custom ASIC, the entire mask set is unique to that chip. The designer has complete control over transistor sizing, device geometry, floorplan, interconnect routing, and physical layout — making it the **highest-performance** but also the **most expensive and time-consuming** class of ASIC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification of ASICs (for context):**
> 1. **Full Custom ASIC** – Hand-crafted at transistor level.
> 2. **Semi-Custom ASIC** – Uses a pre-designed cell library (Standard Cell, Gate Array).
> 3. **Programmable ASIC** – Configured after fabrication (FPGA, CPLD).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hand-Tailored Suit" vs. "Ready-Made Shirt"

Imagine you need a formal outfit:

- **Full Custom ASIC** = a **hand-tailored bespoke suit** measured to your exact body dimensions, with every stitch chosen by the master tailor. It fits perfectly, uses minimum fabric, and looks great — but it takes weeks, costs a fortune, and a new design is required for every different person.
- **Semi-Custom ASIC** = a **made-to-measure suit** from a fixed catalog of pre-cut cloth panels, where the tailor only chooses sizes and stitches them. Fast and cheaper, but you are limited to available cloth patterns.
- **Programmable ASIC (FPGA)** = a **ready-made shirt** off the rack. You can wear it immediately, but the fit is generic and you cannot alter the underlying fabric.

In the same way, Full Custom ASICs give the **VLSI designer absolute control** to squeeze out the last bit of speed, the smallest possible area, and the lowest possible power — at the cost of **high Non-Recurring Engineering (NRE) cost** and **long Time-To-Market (TTM)**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following metrics define the engineering value of a Full Custom ASIC:

| Metric | Standard Unit | Typical Value Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Minimum feature size (λ)** | **µm or nm** | **0.18 µm → 5 nm (modern)** |
| **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost** | **USD ($)** | **$1M – $500M+** |
| **Time-to-Market (TTM)** | **Months** | **12 – 36 months** |
| **Mask Layers** | **Integer count** | **20 – 50+ masks** |
| **Operating Frequency (f_clk)** | **Hz** | **GHz range** |
| **Power Density** | **W/cm²** | **< 100 W/cm² (package-limited)** |
| **Silicon Area Utilization** | **% of die** | **Optimized manually** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Standard Constants used throughout these notes:**
> - ε₀ = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (vacuum permittivity)
> - ε_ox = **3.9 × ε₀** (relative permittivity of SiO₂)
> - k = **1.380 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
> - q = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** (electronic charge)
> - μ_n (electron mobility in Si) ≈ **1350 cm²/V·s**
> - μ_p (hole mobility in Si) ≈ **480 cm²/V·s**
