# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Where Full Custom Design is Used (and Why)

Full Custom ASICs are the **go-to choice** when the application demands one or more of the following:

- **Extreme speed** (e.g., GHz microprocessors, RF front-ends, SerDes transceivers).
- **Ultra-low power** (e.g., IoT sensor nodes, biomedical implants, hearing-aid chips).
- **Mixed-signal / Analog integration** (e.g., ADCs, DACs, PLLs, image sensors) — because analog cells cannot be abstracted into a standard cell library.
- **Memory arrays** (SRAM, DRAM, ROM, CAM) — bit-cells are hand-crafted for minimum area.
- **Pad cells and I/O drivers** — these are always custom even in semi-custom chips.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Examples:** Intel/AMD CPU datapath cells, NVIDIA GPU shader cores, Qualcomm modem baseband, Samsung LPDDR PHY, Apple A-series neural engine — all are predominantly **Full Custom** at the transistor level.

---

## 2.2 The Full Custom Design Flow (Step-by-Step Logic)

A Full Custom chip goes through the following **structured sequence**:

1. **System Specification** – Define functionality, speed, power, I/O, die size, cost.
2. **Architecture / RTL Design** – High-level behavioral description (often in VHDL/Verilog at the algorithmic level).
3. **Transistor-Level Schematic Design** – Each transistor is sized (W/L) and drawn as a circuit.
4. **Circuit Simulation (SPICE)** – DC, AC, transient analysis to verify performance.
5. **Stick Diagram** – Symbolic plan of diffusion (red), polysilicon (green), metal (blue).
6. **Layout Design** – Manual or mask-level tool generation of the full mask geometry.
7. **DRC (Design Rule Check)** – Verifies λ-rules (e.g., minimum width, spacing, enclosure).
8. **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)** – Confirms drawn layout matches the schematic.
9. **Extraction (R, C, RC)** – Lumped/parasitic extraction for post-layout simulation.
10. **Verification & Tape-out** – Final sign-off; photomask generation sent to fab.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Questions on Full Custom ASICs frequently test the **flow order**, the **purpose of DRC/LVS**, and **why stick diagrams are used** (they abstract away exact dimensions and focus on connectivity + relative placement).

---

## 2.3 Stick Diagrams — The "Geometric Skeleton"

A **stick diagram** is a symbolic, color-coded floorplan that captures the *topology* of a CMOS layout without committing to exact dimensions. It uses three standard colors:

- **🔴 Red** = n⁺ diffusion (NMOS active region)
- **🟢 Green** = p⁺ diffusion (PMOS active region, often shown with a dashed border)
- **🟡 Yellow / Blue / Black** = Polysilicon (gate)
- **🔵 Blue / Other** = Metal 1 (interconnect)
- **⚫ Black dot** = Contact (via) between layers

> [!TIP]
> **Euler Path Rule for Stick Diagrams:** To lay out a CMOS gate (NOT, NAND, NOR, AOI, OAI) using a **single horizontal polysilicon line**, the Eulerian path of the NMOS and PMOS networks must share the same sequence of transistor variables. If they don't, the layout requires **two poly rows** and is larger.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_{ox} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \dfrac{3.9 \cdot \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$ | Gate oxide capacitance per unit area | F/m² |
| 2 | $I_{D,sat} = \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})^2$ | NMOS saturation current (long-channel) | A |
| 3 | $R_{on} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_{TH})}$ | Linear-region on-resistance | Ω |
| 4 | $t_{pd} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{on} \cdot C_L$ | Propagation delay (Elmore) | s |
| 5 | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic switching power | W |
| 6 | $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Static (leakage) power | W |
| 7 | $A_{cell} = (n \cdot \lambda) \times (m \cdot \lambda) \; [n,m \in \mathbb{Z}^+]$ | Cell area in lambda² | m² |
| 8 | $C_{wire} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{metal}} \cdot W_{wire} \cdot L_{wire}$ | Interconnect capacitance (parallel-plate approx.) | F |
| 9 | $R_{wire} = \rho_{metal} \cdot \dfrac{L_{wire}}{W_{wire} \cdot t_{metal}}$ | Interconnect resistance | Ω |
| 10 | $A_{die} = \dfrac{A_{cell,total}}{\eta_{util}}$ | Die area after utilization factor | m² |
| 11 | $\tau_{RC} = R_{wire} \cdot C_{wire}$ | RC delay of a wire | s |

> **Notation Note:** $\vert V_{GS} - V_{TH} \vert$ is the gate overdrive voltage. The utilization factor $\eta_{util}$ is typically **0.6 – 0.8** for Full Custom blocks.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Full Custom design is the **backbone of every modern System-on-Chip (SoC)**. Even when the digital logic uses standard cells, the following are always hand-crafted:
- **SRAM bit-cells** (the smallest possible 6T layout).
- **I/O pad rings** (ESD-protected, high-drive).
- **PLL / DLL analog blocks.**
- **Power distribution network (PDN)** to avoid electromigration.

A well-designed Full Custom block can be **2× to 10× smaller, faster, and lower-power** than its semi-custom equivalent — the trade-off is human design time.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Propagation Delay of a CMOS Inverter (Full Custom Sized)

We derive the **high-to-low propagation delay** $t_{pHL}$ for a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$. This is the **single most important metric** a Full Custom designer optimizes.

**Step 1 — Start with the differential equation at the output node.**

When the input switches from low to high, the NMOS turns on and discharges $C_L$ from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$. KCL at the output gives:

$$
C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{D,n}(V_{out})
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the long-channel NMOS current in saturation (region where $V_{DS} > V_{GS} - V_{TH}$).**

$$
I_{D,n} = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{TH})^2
$$

With $V_{GS} = V_{DD}$ during the discharge, the overdrive is $(V_{DD} - V_{TH})$, which is **constant**. The current is therefore constant during the initial saturation phase.

**Step 3 — Solve the differential equation for the time to reach $V_{DD}/2$.**

$$
C_L \int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{DD}/2} dV_{out} = -\int_0^{t_{pHL}} I_{D,n} \, dt
$$

$$
C_L \left( \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{DD} \right) = -I_{D,n} \cdot t_{pHL}
$$

$$
C_L \cdot \left( -\frac{V_{DD}}{2} \right) = -I_{D,n} \cdot t_{pHL}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $t_{pHL}$ explicitly.**

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,n}}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the current expression.**

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}\,}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The factor of 2 in the denominator from the constant saturation current cancels the ½ in the current equation, leaving a clean $\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)$ in the denominator. This shows that **doubling W halves the delay** — the designer's primary optimization knob.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Dynamic Power Consumption

The **dynamic power** of a CMOS gate switching at frequency $f$ with switching activity $\alpha$ (0 ≤ α ≤ 1) is derived from the energy stored in $C_L$ each switching cycle.

**Step 1 — Energy to charge $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$:**

$$
E_{charge} = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V \cdot C_L \, dV = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Energy dissipated in the PMOS during charging:**

$$
E_{PMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Energy stored on $C_L$ (recovered on next discharge, NOT dissipated):**

$$
E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Total energy dissipated per switching cycle (one full charge + discharge):**

$$
E_{cycle} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by switching events per second ($\alpha \cdot f$) to get power:**

$$
\boxed{\,P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f\,}
$$

**Real-world insight:** $V_{DD}^2$ means that **reducing supply voltage from 1.2 V to 0.8 V cuts dynamic power by 55 %** — this is why Full Custom low-power designs aggressively use **Voltage Scaling**, **Power Gating**, and **Clock Gating**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cell Area Estimation from Lambda Rules

For a CMOS NAND-2 gate in a Full Custom λ-based design rule:

- The pull-down network has **2 NMOS in series** ⇒ requires 4λ height.
- The pull-up network has **2 PMOS in parallel** ⇒ requires 4λ height (matching NMOS).
- Each transistor uses **2λ for source/drain + 2λ for poly** = **4λ wide** minimum.
- A contact is **2λ × 2λ**.

**Step 1 — Width of the cell** (for two parallel branches, 2 poly columns):

$$
W_{cell} = 2 \cdot (2\lambda_{n^+}) + 2\lambda_{poly} + 2\lambda_{spacing} = 8\lambda
$$

**Step 2 — Height of the cell:**

$$
H_{cell} = 2\lambda_{n^+} + 2\lambda_{poly} + 2\lambda_{n^+} = 6\lambda
$$

**Step 3 — Area in λ²:**

$$
A_{NAND2} = 8\lambda \times 6\lambda = 48\lambda^2
$$

**Step 4 — Die area after utilization (assume $\eta_{util} = 0.7$):**

$$
A_{die} = \frac{A_{cell,total}}{\eta_{util}} = \frac{N_{cells} \cdot A_{cell}}{0.7}
$$

**Analytical Insight:** A Full Custom NAND-2 can be drawn in as little as **36 λ²** by sharing diffusion (using **Euler-path** layout), whereas a Standard Cell library NAND-2 typically takes **60 – 80 λ²** — demonstrating the **30–50% area savings** of Full Custom.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Full Custom ASIC Design Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[System Specification] --> B[Architecture and RTL Design]
    B --> C[Transistor Level Schematic]
    C --> D[SPICE Circuit Simulation]
    D --> E[Stick Diagram Layout Planning]
    E --> F[Full Custom Mask Level Layout]
    F --> G[DRC Design Rule Check]
    F --> H[LVS Layout vs Schematic]
    G --> I[RC Parasitic Extraction]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Post Layout Simulation]
    J --> K{Performance Met?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Tape Out to Fab]
    K -- No --> C
    L --> M[Wafer Fabrication]
    M --> N[Packaging and Test]
    N --> O[Production ASIC Chip]
```

**Flow Logic Explained:**
- The loop **C → D → E → F → G → I → J → K** represents the iterative refinement cycle, which is the heart of Full Custom design.
- **DRC and LVS run in parallel** from the same layout; both must pass before extraction.
- The **feedback loop** to Step C (Schematic) when performance is unmet is what makes Full Custom design slow but precise.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Full Custom Subsystems on a Modern SoC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SoC[Modern System on Chip Die]
        CPU[Custom CPU Datapath] 
        GPU[Custom GPU Shader Core]
        SRAM[Hand Crafted SRAM Array]
        PLL[Analog PLL Block]
        ADC[Custom ADC Block]
        IO[Custom IO Pad Ring]
        PDN[Hand Designed Power Grid]
    end
    CPU --- SRAM
    GPU --- SRAM
    CPU --- PLL
    PLL --- ADC
    SoC --- PDN
    SoC --- IO
```

**Architecture Insight:** Even on a "semi-custom" SoC, the **PLL, ADC, SRAM, I/O, and PDN** are always Full Custom. This is why every VLSI engineer must understand Full Custom design rules, even if they primarily do digital standard-cell work.

---

## 4.3 Stick-Diagram Topology Map of a 2-Input NAND Gate

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VDD[VDD Rail Metal 1] --> P1[PMOS A]
    VDD --> P2[PMOS B]
    P1 --> OUT[Output Node Metal 1]
    P2 --> OUT
    OUT --> N1[NMOS A]
    N1 --> N2[NMOS B]
    N2 --> GND[VSS Rail Metal 1]
    A[Input A Poly] --> P1
    A --> N1
    B[Input B Poly] --> P2
    B --> N2
```

**Reading the Diagram:** This topological view shows the **series NMOS / parallel PMOS** arrangement. To build a single-poly-row stick diagram, the Euler path through the graph must traverse the transistors in the same order for both pull-up and pull-down networks. For NAND-2, the order **(A, B)** is shared — hence a **single poly row** layout is feasible.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEVT503, Module 2]`

**Define Full Custom ASIC. List its two main advantages over Semi-Custom ASIC.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Full Custom ASIC** is an Application-Specific Integrated Circuit in which **every transistor and every interconnect is individually designed and laid out at the mask level** to meet specific area, speed, and power targets. There is no pre-designed cell library; the geometry of every diffusion, polysilicon, and metal layer is unique to the chip.

**Two main advantages over Semi-Custom ASIC:**

1. **Higher Performance / Lower Power** — Hand-optimized transistor sizing and layout yields smaller parasitic capacitance and resistance, enabling faster switching and lower dynamic power.

2. **Smaller Die Area** — Custom diffusion sharing (using Euler paths) and minimum-geometry transistors reduce silicon area by 30 – 50 % compared to a fixed standard-cell library implementation, lowering per-unit cost in high volume.

*(Acceptable alternative for advantage 2: ability to integrate analog/mixed-signal blocks that cannot be expressed in a standard cell library.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEVT503, Module 2]`

**What is a stick diagram? Why is it used in Full Custom layout design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stick diagram** is a **color-coded, symbolic topological sketch** of a CMOS layout that shows the relative placement of diffusion regions, polysilicon gates, metal interconnects, and contact cuts **without specifying exact dimensions**.

**It is used because:**

1. **Quick design exploration** — The designer can rapidly try different transistor orderings and Euler paths to find the minimum-area topology before committing to the time-consuming mask-level layout.

2. **Design-rule independent** — Stick diagrams abstract away λ-based design rules, letting the designer focus on **transistor connectivity and functional correctness** first, and geometric constraints later.

3. **Easy communication** — It serves as a clear blueprint that can be handed from the circuit designer to the layout engineer without ambiguity.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCEVT503, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain the **complete Full Custom ASIC design flow** with a neat block diagram. Highlight the role of **DRC and LVS** in the flow. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat stick diagram, explain the **Euler-path-based layout** of a CMOS **NAND-2 gate**. Show how diffusion sharing reduces the cell area. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]**

The Full Custom ASIC design flow consists of the following sequential stages:

1. **System Specification (1 mark):** Functional, speed, power, area, I/O, and cost targets are defined from the market requirement.

2. **Architecture / RTL Design:** The chip behaviour is described using VHDL/Verilog or algorithmic C-models to validate system-level performance.

3. **Transistor-Level Schematic Design (1 mark):** Each cell is drawn as a transistor-level circuit in SPICE. The designer manually sizes W/L for performance.

4. **Circuit Simulation:** DC, AC, and transient analyses confirm timing, noise margins, and gain.

5. **Stick Diagram (1 mark):** Symbolic plan of the layout to optimize topology and transistor ordering.

6. **Full Custom Mask-Level Layout:** The actual λ-rule geometry (diffusion, poly, metal) is drawn by hand or with mask-level tools.

7. **Design Rule Check – DRC (1 mark):** Automated tool verifies that the layout obeys all foundry λ-rules — minimum width, spacing, enclosure, antenna rules. **It checks geometric validity only.**

8. **Layout vs. Schematic – LVS (1 mark):** Compares the drawn layout's netlist against the original schematic. **It checks electrical/functional equivalence** (device count, connectivity, terminal matching).

9. **RC Extraction & Post-Layout Simulation (1 mark):** Parasitic resistance and capacitance are extracted and re-simulated to confirm timing closure.

10. **Tape-out & Fabrication (1 mark):** Final GDSII is sent to the foundry.

**Role of DRC:** Ensures **manufacturability** — the layout can be fabricated on silicon with acceptable yield. **Role of LVS:** Ensures **functional correctness** — the layout implements the intended circuit. Both must pass; they are *complementary*, not redundant.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]**

**Euler-Path Layout of CMOS NAND-2 (2 marks for stick diagram description):**

The NAND-2 gate has:
- **Pull-up network:** Two PMOS in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and output.
- **Pull-down network:** Two NMOS in **series** between output and $V_{SS}$.

**Euler Path Analysis (2 marks):**

An Euler path traverses every edge of a graph exactly once. The transistor order that traverses **both** the PMOS and NMOS networks is **A → B**. Hence a **single horizontal polysilicon line** can be used, with the layout having only one poly row.

**Diffusion Sharing (2 marks):**

The drain diffusion of PMOS-A and the source diffusion of NMOS-A (both connected to the output node) are merged into a **single n-diffusion/p-diffusion region** separated by a single well boundary. Likewise, the source of PMOS-B and drain of NMOS-B share the output node. This eliminates two separate n⁺ regions, saving **4λ × 6λ = 24 λ²** of area.

**Resulting Cell Area:**

- Width = 8λ (two poly columns + spacing + contacts).
- Height = 6λ (one n⁺ + poly + one n⁺ row, mirrored for p⁺).
- **Area = 48 λ²** — significantly less than the **64 λ²** non-shared layout (2 marks).

The Euler-path stick diagram therefore **directly translates** into the minimum-area mask layout.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCEVT503, Module 2]`

**(a)** Compare **Full Custom, Semi-Custom (Standard Cell), and Gate Array** ASIC design styles in terms of NRE cost, time-to-market, performance, and area efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **propagation delay $t_{pHL}$** of a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ in saturation. Show how transistor sizing (W/L) directly impacts the delay. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]**

| Parameter | **Full Custom** | **Standard Cell (Semi-Custom)** | **Gate Array (Semi-Custom)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **NRE Cost** (1 mark) | **Highest** ($1M – $500M); full mask set unique | **Medium** ($100K – $1M); pre-built cell library | **Lowest** ($50K – $300K); pre-fabricated master |
| **Time-to-Market** (1 mark) | **Longest** (12 – 36 months) | **Medium** (3 – 9 months) | **Shortest** (1 – 3 months) |
| **Performance (Speed)** (1 mark) | **Highest** (GHz range) | **Medium-High** | **Medium** (fixed master routing) |
| **Area Efficiency** (1 mark) | **Best** (30 – 50 % smaller die) | **Good** (fixed cell height) | **Worst** (predefined channels waste area) |
| **Power** (1 mark) | **Lowest** (optimized sizing) | **Medium** | **Highest** leakage |
| **Flexibility / Analog** (1 mark) | **Total** (any transistor geometry) | **Limited** (cells are digital) | **Very limited** (master fixed) |
| **Volume Suitability** (1 mark) | **High volume** (to amortize NRE) | **Medium volume** | **Low-to-medium volume / prototyping** |

**Conclusion:** Full Custom is chosen for **highest-performance, high-volume products**; Gate Array for **fast prototyping**; Standard Cell for the **balance** of cost, time, and performance.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]**

**Derivation of $t_{pHL}$ for a CMOS Inverter:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** When input switches from 0 to $V_{DD}$, the NMOS turns ON and the PMOS turns OFF. The output capacitance $C_L$ discharges through the NMOS from $V_{DD}$ towards 0. KCL at the output node gives:

$$
C_L \frac{dV_{out}}{dt} = -I_{D,n}(V_{out})
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** While $V_{out} \geq V_{DD} - V_{TH}$, the NMOS is in **saturation** and the current is approximately constant:

$$
I_{D,n,sat} \approx \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Integrating the KCL from $V_{DD}$ to $V_{DD}/2$ (the 50% switching point) over time interval $[0, t_{pHL}]$:

$$
C_L \int_{V_{DD}}^{V_{DD}/2} dV_{out} = -\int_0^{t_{pHL}} I_{D,n} \, dt
$$

$$
C_L \left( \frac{V_{DD}}{2} - V_{DD} \right) = -I_{D,n} \cdot t_{pHL}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Solving for the delay:

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot I_{D,n}}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting the saturation current:

$$
t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{2 \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,t_{pHL} = \frac{C_L \cdot V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{TH})^2}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Impact of Transistor Sizing (2 marks):**

From the boxed equation, $t_{pHL}$ is **inversely proportional to W/L**. Therefore:

- **Doubling the NMOS width (2W/L)** ⇒ delay **halved** (2× faster).
- **Doubling the load $C_L$** ⇒ delay **doubled**.
- **Reducing $V_{DD}$ by 30 %** (e.g., 1.2 V → 0.84 V) ⇒ delay **increases** because $(V_{DD}-V_{TH})^2$ drops rapidly.

A Full Custom designer exploits this by **upsizing critical-path transistors** to balance delay across the chip — a technique impossible in a fixed standard-cell library.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing DRC and LVS:** DRC = geometry/manufacturability; LVS = electrical/functional equivalence. *Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks per occurrence.*
> 2. **Forgetting the activity factor α** in the dynamic power formula $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. Many students write $C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ only — losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Not showing the constant-current assumption** when deriving $t_{pHL}$. The "constant current in saturation" is the *key step*; skipping it loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Mixing up Full Custom vs. FPGA:** Some students write that Full Custom is "configurable post-fabrication" — that is **FPGA**, not Full Custom. Costly confusion.
> 5. **Euler path answer must mention the shared transistor order** between NMOS and PMOS networks — simply drawing a stick diagram without the path analysis loses 2 marks.
> 6. **No units in numerical answers** — always include **W, s, F, m², etc.** KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Full Custom ASIC = every transistor is hand-designed** at the mask level.
- [x] **Highest performance, smallest die, lowest power** — but **highest NRE**, **longest TTM**.
- [x] Used for **CPU/GPU cores, SRAM, analog blocks, PLLs, I/O, PDN**.
- [x] **Design flow:** Spec → RTL → Schematic → SPICE → Stick Diagram → Layout → DRC → LVS → Extraction → Post-layout Sim → Tape-out.
- [x] **DRC = Design Rule Check (geometry); LVS = Layout vs. Schematic (electrical).**
- [x] **Stick diagram colors:** Red = n⁺, Green (dashed) = p⁺, Yellow = Poly, Blue = Metal 1, Black dot = Contact.
- [x] **Euler path** = shared transistor order between pull-up and pull-down ⇒ single poly row, smaller area.
- [x] **Dynamic power:** $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ — dominated by $V_{DD}^2$.
- [x] **Static power:** $P_{stat} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ — grows exponentially with temperature.
- [x] **Propagation delay:** $t_{pHL} = \dfrac{C_L V_{DD}}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{DD}-V_{TH})^2}$ — **inversely proportional to W/L**.
- [x] **Cell area** measured in $\lambda^2$; typical NAND-2 Full Custom ≈ 48 λ².
- [x] **Full Custom savings:** 30 – 50 % smaller area, 2 – 10× faster than Semi-Custom.
- [x] **Constants to memorize:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \varepsilon_0$, $\mu_n \approx 1350$ cm²/V·s, $\mu_p \approx 480$ cm²/V·s.
- [x] **Utilization factor** $\eta_{util} \approx 0.6$ – $0.8$ when estimating die area from total cell area.
- [x] **Never use `|` inside markdown tables** — use `\vert` or `\mid` (KTU formatting compliance).

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Cell Based ASICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Standard Cell Based ASICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Cell Based ASIC (Cell-Based ASIC / CBIC):** A semi-custom ASIC design methodology in which the entire chip functionality is constructed using a **pre-designed, pre-characterized, and pre-verified library of standard logic cells** (such as NAND, NOR, flip-flops, multiplexers, adders) that share a **fixed cell height** (constant row pitch) but have variable widths. The cells are arranged in tightly packed parallel rows, separated by horizontal/vertical routing channels, with I/O cells placed along the chip periphery and macro blocks (RAM, ROM, PLLs) embedded in designated channels.

The standard cell approach is the **workhorse design style** of the modern VLSI industry because it strikes a pragmatic balance between design effort, time-to-market, area efficiency, and performance. It sits exactly between the two extremes of the ASIC design spectrum:

| Design Style | Effort | Performance | Area | Cost |
|--------------|--------|-------------|------|------|
| Full Custom | Very High | Excellent | Smallest | Highest NRE |
| **Standard Cell** | **Moderate** | **Very Good** | **Compact** | **Moderate NRE** |
| Gate Array | Low | Good | Larger | Lowest NRE |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The LEGO Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Building a City with Pre-Fabricated Skyscrapers**
> Imagine you are an architect tasked with building a new city in **30 days** instead of **3 years**.
> - A **Full Custom ASIC** is like designing every brick, every window, and every elevator from scratch — perfect but impossibly slow.
> - A **Gate Array** is like a pre-built city block where you can only decorate empty lots — fast but inflexible.
> - A **Standard Cell ASIC** is like having a catalog of **pre-engineered skyscrapers** of *identical floor heights* but *varying widths* (1-bay, 2-bay, 4-bay buildings). You stack them side by side in orderly streets, run power/water lines between them, and connect the whole city with roads. Each skyscraper is already certified, fire-tested, and elevator-calibrated.

This analogy captures the essence: **fixed-height, variable-width, pre-verified building blocks** snapped together by automated tools.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of Standard Cells

A standard cell must satisfy a set of rigid geometric and electrical constraints so that automated tools can use it seamlessly:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Constant Cell Height:** All standard cells share the *same vertical pitch* (typically expressed in routing tracks). This guarantees they fit into a uniform row.
> - **Variable Cell Width:** Horizontal dimension scales with logic complexity (an inverter is narrow; an adder is wide).
> - **Fixed Power/Ground Rails:** VDD and VSS rails run along the top and bottom of every cell at fixed positions.
> - **Pre-characterized Timing:** Each cell has pre-computed Look-Up Tables (LUTs) for delay, power, and output load.
> - **Pin Abutment Compatibility:** Cell pins sit on a predefined routing grid pitch.
> - **Eclipse / Pruning Rules:** Internal cell geometry adheres to Design Rule Check (DRC) compatibility.

## 1.4 The Standard Cell Library — The Heart of the Methodology

The library is essentially a database containing three coordinated views of every cell:

1. **Behavioral View:** VHDL/Verilog functional model used during simulation.
2. **Timing View:** Liberty (.lib) format files containing delay, setup/hold, and power characterization.
3. **Physical View:** Layout (GDSII) containing drawn geometries, pin positions, and blockage information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examples of Standard Cells:** Inverter (INV), 2-input NAND (NAND2), 2-input NOR (NOR2), D flip-flop (DFF), scan flip-flop (SDFF), XOR, half-adder, full-adder, AND-OR-Invert (AOI), OR-AND-Invert (OAI), multiplexers (MUX2, MUX4), buffers, clock buffers, etc.

## 1.5 Why Standard Cells Dominate Modern ASICs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard cell based ASICs are used in **over 90% of all commercial digital ICs** today — including microprocessors, SoCs, GPUs, AI accelerators, and network processors — because they enable **automated place-and-route (P&R)** to handle millions of cells with high utilization (typically 70–85%).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How a Standard Cell ASIC is Built

The standard cell design flow follows a strict, well-orchestrated sequence. Understanding each phase is critical for KTU examinations.

### Phase 1: RTL Design
- Engineer writes the hardware description in **Verilog** or **VHDL**.
- Functional simulation is performed against a testbench.
- Output: RTL netlist + behavioral testbench.

### Phase 2: Logic Synthesis
- Synthesis tools (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) map the RTL onto the standard cell library.
- Tool optimizes for one or more objectives: **area, timing, power**.
- Output: **Gate-level netlist** (mapped to standard cells).

### Phase 3: Floorplanning
- The chip outline, core area, I/O pad placement, macro placement, and **standard cell rows** are defined.
- **Rows** are horizontal tracks of constant pitch — the canvas onto which cells are placed.
- Power grid (VDD/VSS rings, straps, rails) is planned.

### Phase 4: Placement
- Standard cells are physically positioned inside the rows.
- Goals: minimize wirelength, balance timing, reduce congestion, control density.
- Algorithms include simulated annealing, quadratic placement, and analytical placement.

### Phase 5: Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)
- A balanced clock distribution network is built using clock buffers/inverters.
- Goal: minimize **clock skew** and **clock latency**.

### Phase 6: Routing
- Connections between placed cells are drawn using metal layers.
- Two phases: **Global routing** (loose assignment) and **Detailed routing** (exact tracks).
- Routing channels between cell rows absorb horizontal/vertical wires.

### Phase 7: Verification
- **DRC (Design Rule Check)**, **LVS (Layout vs. Schematic)**, **STA (Static Timing Analysis)**, **Power analysis**, **Formal verification**, **ERC (Electrical Rule Check)**.

## 2.2 The Standard Cell Architecture — Geometric Anatomy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Row Pitch Concept:** The vertical distance between the bottom of one cell row and the bottom of the next row is called the *row pitch*. The cell height is typically a fraction of this pitch (e.g., 7-track, 9-track, 12-track cells in modern nodes).

Each standard cell row contains:

- **Power Rail (VDD)** at the top of the row.
- **Ground Rail (VSS)** at the bottom of the row.
- **N-well and P-well stripes** inside the cell.
- **Internal transistor geometry** (diffusion, polysilicon, contacts).
- **Pin access points** for routing.

## 2.3 Routing Channels and Feedthrough Cells

When horizontal routing is required across a row, **feedthrough cells** (cells with vertical metal paths passing through them) are inserted to allow signals to traverse the row without disrupting placement.

**Channel Routing Capacity (Classical Model):**
The number of routing tracks available in a channel is fixed by the channel height. The **channel density** (maximum number of nets crossing any horizontal cut) must be ≤ the **channel capacity**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Standard cell ASICs power nearly every modern electronic device: smartphones, automotive ECUs, aerospace avionics, IoT sensors, data center accelerators, and AI training chips (e.g., Google TPU). They enable engineers to focus on **architecture, algorithms, and verification** rather than transistor-level design.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates the essential equations and metrics for standard cell ASIC design.

| Symbol / Concept | Equation / Definition | Units | Notes |
|------------------|----------------------|-------|-------|
| **Cell Utilization** | $U = \dfrac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | dimensionless | Typically 70%–85% |
| **Channel Density** | $d = \max\limits_{x} \left( N_{\text{nets crossing cut at } x} \right)$ | nets | Must be ≤ channel capacity |
| **Channel Capacity** | $C_{\text{ch}} = N_{\text{tracks per row pitch}}$ | tracks | Set by technology node |
| **Row Pitch** | $P_{\text{row}} = H_{\text{cell}} + H_{\text{channel}}$ | µm or nm | Cell height + channel height |
| **Gate Delay (Elmore)** | $t_{d} \approx 0.69 \, R_{\text{eq}} \, C_{\text{L}}$ | seconds | RC delay approximation |
| **Wire Length (Rent's Rule)** | $L = k \, N^{p}$ | µm | $k$, $p$ are empirical constants |
| **Rent's Exponent** | $0.5 \leq p \leq 0.75$ | dimensionless | Higher $p$ = more complex interconnection |
| **Average Fanout** | $F_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{\sum f_{i}}{N_{\text{gates}}}$ | dimensionless | Used in wirelength estimation |
| **Total Wirelength (Donath)** | $L_{\text{total}} \propto N^{2p-1}$ | µm | Donath's hierarchical model |
| **Interconnect Delay** | $t_{\text{int}} \propto R_{\text{wire}} \cdot C_{\text{wire}}$ | seconds | Dominates in deep submicron |
| **Buffer Insertion Delay** | $t_{\text{buf}} = N_{\text{buf}} \cdot t_{\text{gate}}$ | seconds | Optimal for long wires |
| **Clock Skew** | $\Delta t_{\text{skew}} = t_{\text{max}} - t_{\text{min}}$ | seconds | Minimized by CTS |
| **Power Dissipation** | $P = P_{\text{dyn}} + P_{\text{leak}}$ | Watts | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V^{2} f$ |
| **Dynamic Power** | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \, C \, V^{2} \, f$ | Watts | Dominates in active mode |
| **Leakage Power** | $P_{\text{leak}} = V \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | Watts | Dominates in standby |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rent's Rule and Wire Length Estimation:** Rent's Rule is the most commonly tested analytical model in KTU examinations for predicting interconnect complexity in standard cell designs. Remember the exponent range ($0.5 \leq p \leq 0.75$) — it is a high-yield exam fact.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cell Utilization Formula

Cell utilization is the foundational metric used by placement tools to determine how densely cells are packed within a core area. It directly impacts routability, congestion, and timing closure.

**Starting Definition:** Utilization is the ratio of *used* silicon area to *total* available silicon area inside the placement region.

$$U = \frac{A_{\text{used}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$$

**Step-by-Step Expansion:**

1. Identify the area of all standard cells placed:

$$A_{\text{used}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( H_{\text{cell}} \cdot W_{i} \right)$$

where $N$ is the total number of cells, $H_{\text{cell}}$ is the uniform cell height, and $W_{i}$ is the width of the $i^{\text{th}}$ cell.

2. Identify the total placement area:

$$A_{\text{total}} = W_{\text{core}} \cdot H_{\text{core}}$$

where $W_{\text{core}}$ and $H_{\text{core}}$ are the dimensions of the core placement region.

3. Substitute both quantities:

$$U = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( H_{\text{cell}} \cdot W_{i} \right)}{W_{\text{core}} \cdot H_{\text{core}}}$$

4. **Design Rule:** If $U > 85\%$, routability degrades sharply because the router runs out of routing resources. If $U < 60\%$, area is wasted. Target $U \approx 70\% - 80\%$ for most designs.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Rent's Rule for Wire Length Estimation

Rent's Rule is a *power-law empirical relationship* describing how the number of external connections (pins) of a logic block scales with the number of gates inside the block.

**Starting Hypothesis:**

$$T = k \cdot N^{p}$$

where $T$ = number of external terminals (pins), $N$ = number of gates, $k$ = average number of pins per gate, and $p$ = Rent's exponent.

**Deriving Average Wire Length per Net:**

1. Partition the chip into hierarchical blocks of size $N$ gates.
2. The average wire length connecting two random points in a 2D layout is:

$$L_{\text{avg}} = k' \cdot N^{p}$$

3. The total number of nets in the block:

$$N_{\text{nets}} \propto N$$

4. Therefore, the total wire length is:

$$L_{\text{total}} = N_{\text{nets}} \cdot L_{\text{avg}} \propto N \cdot N^{p} = N^{1+p}$$

5. Applying Donath's hierarchical model correction (which accounts for partitioning at multiple levels):

$$L_{\text{total}} \propto N^{2p - 1}$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

$$\begin{aligned}
p &= 0.5 \Rightarrow \text{short wires, low complexity (regular structures)} \\
p &= 0.75 \Rightarrow \text{long wires, high complexity (random logic)}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Elmore Delay Model for RC Interconnect

The Elmore delay model provides a first-order approximation of signal propagation delay through an RC network, critical for understanding why interconnect delay dominates in modern standard cell designs.

**Starting RC Lumped Model:** A wire of resistance $R_{\text{wire}}$ drives a load capacitance $C_{L}$.

The voltage response to a step input has a time constant $\tau = R \cdot C$. The 50% propagation delay is:

$$t_{d} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{\text{eq}} \cdot C_{L}$$

**Distributed RC Model (π-model):** For a wire of length $l$, total resistance $R$, and total capacitance $C$:

$$t_{d} \approx 0.69 \cdot \frac{R \cdot C}{2} + 0.69 \cdot R_{\text{driver}} \cdot (C + C_{L})$$

**Key Insight:** As technology scales down, wire resistance increases (narrower wires) while gate resistance decreases. Hence, interconnect delay grows exponentially with scaling — the *interconnect crisis* that drove the adoption of **buffer insertion** and **multi-layer metal stacks**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Standard Cell vs Gate Array vs Full Custom

> [!NOTE]
> The following analytical comparison is a high-yield KTU topic — students are frequently asked to compare design styles.

| Parameter | Full Custom | Standard Cell | Gate Array |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|------------|
| Cell Geometry | Custom, no rules | Fixed height, variable width | Fixed (pre-fabricated) |
| Pre-characterization | No | Yes | Yes (partial) |
| Mask Cost | Very High | High | Low |
| NRE Cost | Very High | Moderate | Low |
| Per-unit Cost (high vol.) | Lowest | Low | Moderate |
| Performance | Best | Very Good | Good |
| Time to Market | Slowest | Moderate | Fastest |
| Area Efficiency | Best (85–95%) | Good (70–85%) | Poor (50–70%) |
| Custom Transistors Allowed | Yes | No | No |
| Best Use Case | Analog, RF, high-perf CPUs | SoCs, ASICs | Low-volume prototyping |

## 3.5 Worked Example: Wire Length Estimation Using Rent's Rule

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Problem (KTU-style):** A standard cell ASIC contains $N = 100{,}000$ gates. Compute the total estimated wire length using Rent's Rule for $p = 0.6$ and $p = 0.75$. Assume proportionality constant $K = 1.0$ for simplicity.

**Solution for p = 0.6:**

$$L_{\text{total}} \propto N^{2p - 1} = (100{,}000)^{2(0.6) - 1} = (100{,}000)^{0.2}$$

$$(100{,}000)^{0.2} = (10^{5})^{0.2} = 10^{1.0} = 10$$

**Solution for p = 0.75:**

$$L_{\text{total}} \propto N^{2p - 1} = (100{,}000)^{2(0.75) - 1} = (100{,}000)^{0.5}$$

$$(100{,}000)^{0.5} = \sqrt{100{,}000} \approx 316.23$$

**Interpretation:** A higher Rent's exponent ($p = 0.75$) yields **~31× more wirelength** than $p = 0.6$ for the same gate count. This dramatically impacts routing congestion and timing closure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Standard Cell ASIC — Physical Architecture (Top-Down View)

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the top-down physical organization of a standard cell based ASIC chip. Each block represents a logical region of the die.

```mermaid

    IO["PERIPHERAL I/O PADS REGION"]:::io
    CORNER["CORNER CELLS AND POWER TAPS"]:::corner

    MACROS["MACRO BLOCKS RAM ROM PLL"]:::macro
    ROWS["STANDARD CELL ROWS WITH CONSTANT PITCH"]:::rows
    CHANNELS["ROUTING CHANNELS BETWEEN ROWS"]:::channels
    POWERMESH["POWER MESH VDD VSS GRID"]:::power
    FEEDTHROUGH["FEEDTHROUGH CELLS FOR CROSS ROW SIGNALS"]:::feed

    CORE --> IO
    CORE --> CORNER
    CORE --> COREAREA
    COREAREA --> MACROS
    COREAREA --> ROWS

    COREAREA --> FEEDTHROUGH

```

## 4.2 Anatomy of a Single Standard Cell Row

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the internal anatomy of one standard cell row, showing how individual cells (Inverter, NAND2, DFF) share common power rails and are separated by routing tracks.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    VSSRAIL["VSS RAIL BOTTOM OF ROW"]:::vs

    INV["STANDARD CELL INVERTER INVX1"]:::cell

    TRACK2["ROUTING TRACK M2 METAL 2"]:::track

    INV --> PIN1
```

## 4.3 Standard Cell ASIC Design Flow — Sequential Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid diagram captures the complete front-to-back design flow used to transform an RTL description into a fabricated standard cell ASIC.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SPEC["DESIGN SPECIFICATION"]:::startnode
    RTL["RTL DESIGN VERILOG VHDL"]:::rnode
    SIM1["FUNCTIONAL SIMULATION"]:::rnode
    SYNTH["LOGIC SYNTHESIS WITH STANDARD CELL LIBRARY"]:::snode
    GATENET["GATE LEVEL NETLIST"]:::snode
    FLOOR["FLOORPLANNING AND POWER PLANNING"]:::pnode
    PLACE["STANDARD CELL PLACEMENT"]:::pnode
    CTS["CLOCK TREE SYNTHESIS"]:::pnode
    ROUTE["GLOBAL AND DETAILED ROUTING"]:::pnode
    STA["STATIC TIMING ANALYSIS"]:::vnode
    DRC["DRC AND LVS CHECKS"]:::vnode
    POWER["DYNAMIC AND LEAKAGE POWER ANALYSIS"]:::vnode
    TAPEOUT["TAPE OUT GDSII"]:::endnode
    FAB["FABRICATION AND PACKAGING"]:::endnode

    SPEC --> RTL
    RTL --> SIM1
    SIM1 --> SYNTH
    SYNTH --> GATENET
    GATENET --> FLOOR
    FLOOR --> PLACE
    PLACE --> CTS
    CTS --> ROUTE
    ROUTE --> STA
    ROUTE --> DRC
    ROUTE --> POWER
    STA --> TAPEOUT
    DRC --> TAPEOUT
    POWER --> TAPEOUT
    TAPEOUT --> FAB

    classDef rnode fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0e7490,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff

    classDef pnode fill:#16a34a,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef vnode fill:#d97706,stroke:#d97706,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endnode fill:#dc2626,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.4 Standard Cell Library Structure — Multi-View Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates the three coordinated views every standard cell library must maintain: behavioral, timing, and physical.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    BEH["BEHAVIORAL VIEW VHDL VERILOG MODEL"]:::beh
    TIM["TIMING VIEW LIBERTY LIB TIMING POWER"]:::tim
    PHY["PHYSICAL VIEW GDSII LEF DEF LAYOUT"]:::phy
    SIMUSE["USED FOR RTL AND GATE SIMULATION"]:::usecase
    SYNTHUSE["USED FOR SYNTHESIS AND STA"]:::usecase
    APRUSE["USED FOR PLACEMENT AND ROUTING"]:::usecase
    INVEX["INVX1 INVERTER"]:::cell
    NANDEX["NAND2X1 2 INPUT NAND"]:::cell
    DFFEX["DFFX1 D FLIP FLOP"]:::cell
    AOIEX["AOI21X1 AND OR INVERT"]:::cell

    LIB --> BEH
    LIB --> TIM
    LIB --> PHY
    LIB --> INVEX
    LIB --> NANDEX
    LIB --> DFFEX
    LIB --> AOIEX
    BEH --> SIMUSE
    TIM --> SYNTHUSE
    PHY --> APRUSE

    classDef libroot fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#1f3a5f,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
```
