> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this useful for KTU?** This cross-section helps you visualize the multi-metal stack used in routing. Modern ASICs use **15+ metal layers** (Intel uses 14 nm → 20+ layers), while simpler nodes use fewer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

This question bank is **calibrated to actual KTU University Examination patterns** for the VLSI Technology course (PCEVT503), Module 2.

---

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between ASIC and FPGA. List any three key distinctions.**

**Model Answer:**

An **ASIC (Application-Specific Integrated Circuit)** is a chip custom-designed and fabricated for a specific application. Its logic is permanently etched into silicon via photolithographic masks, making it non-reconfigurable.

An **FPGA (Field-Programmable Gate Array)** is a pre-fabricated standard chip whose function is defined by a user-loaded configuration bitstream. It is fully reconfigurable in-circuit.

| S.No. | Parameter | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NRE Cost | Very high (mask set) | Zero |
| 2 | Reconfigurability | None | Full |
| 3 | Performance | High (3×–10×) | Moderate |
| 4 | Time-to-Market | Long (months) | Short (days) |
| 5 | Per-unit cost (high volume) | Very low | Higher |

**Conclusion:** ASICs dominate high-volume production; FPGAs dominate prototyping and low-volume applications.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "bitstream" in the context of FPGAs? Briefly explain its role.**

**Model Answer:**

A **bitstream** is a binary configuration file (e.g., `.bit` for Xilinx, `.sof` for Intel/Altera) that programs an FPGA. It contains configuration data for:

- **LUT mask values** (defining logic functions)
- **Routing switch settings** (connecting logic blocks)
- **BRAM initialization data**
- **I/O standard and pin configuration**
- **Clock management (PLL/MMCM) parameters**

**Role:** The bitstream is loaded into the FPGA's **SRAM configuration memory** via **JTAG, SPI, or external flash**. Once loaded, the FPGA's programmable fabric (LUTs, switch matrices, IOBs) is configured to implement the desired digital circuit. Erasing the bitstream and loading a new one allows the same FPGA to function as an entirely different circuit — this is the essence of **in-circuit reconfigurability**.

---

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern:** KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks each, typically split as **Part (a) for 7 marks + Part (b) for 7 marks**. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are two fully independent alternatives.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the complete ASIC design flow with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each step.

**Model Solution:**

The ASIC design flow is a structured, step-by-step methodology that translates a high-level functional specification into a manufactured silicon chip. The flow is:

**Step 1: Design Specification (1 mark)**
- Functional, performance, power, area, and I/O requirements are documented.
- Acts as the contract between design team and product management.

**Step 2: RTL Design Entry (1 mark)**
- Design is coded in **Verilog/VHDL/SystemVerilog** at the **Register Transfer Level (RTL)**.
- Captures the data flow between registers and combinational logic.

**Step 3: Functional Simulation (1 mark)**
- HDL code is simulated against a testbench to verify logical correctness.
- Tools: ModelSim, Synopsys VCS, Cadence Xcelium.

**Step 4: Logic Synthesis (1 mark)**
- Translates HDL into a **gate-level netlist** using a Standard Cell Library.
- Tools: Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus.

**Step 5: Design for Test (DFT) (1 mark)**
- Scan chains, BIST, and ATPG are inserted to improve fault coverage (target ≥ 95%).

**Step 6: Floorplanning, Placement, CTS, and Routing (1 mark)**
- Physical design stages that translate the netlist into a geometric layout on silicon.
- Tools: Innovus, ICC2, Olympus.

**Step 7: Physical Verification & Sign-off (1 mark)**
- **DRC, LVS, STA, Power, IR drop, EM** checks are run.
- If clean, GDSII is sent to foundry for **tape-out** and fabrication.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Differentiate between full-custom, semi-custom, and standard-cell ASIC design styles. List the advantages and disadvantages of each.

**Model Solution:**

**1. Full-Custom ASIC (2 marks)**
- Every transistor and interconnect is **hand-designed** by layout engineers.
- **Advantages:** Highest performance, lowest power, smallest area, full design freedom.
- **Disadvantages:** Extremely high design effort, long time-to-market, very expensive NRE.
- **Use case:** Critical analog blocks, high-frequency CPUs, memory macros.

**2. Semi-Custom ASIC / Gate Array (2 marks)**
- Pre-fabricated base wafers with arrays of unconnected transistors; only the **top metal layers** are customized.
- **Advantages:** Lower NRE than full-custom, faster turnaround.
- **Disadvantages:** Lower density and performance than full-custom; less design flexibility.
- **Use case:** Mid-volume applications, legacy designs.

**3. Standard-Cell ASIC (Cell-Based ASIC) (3 marks)**
- Design is synthesized from HDL into a library of pre-designed logic cells.
- **Advantages:** Excellent trade-off of design effort vs. performance; high density; reusable cell libraries.
- **Disadvantages:** Still requires full mask set; longer fabrication lead time.
- **Use case:** Modern SoCs, mobile processors, networking chips — the **dominant style in industry today**.

**Conclusion:** The standard-cell approach is the industry workhorse; full-custom is reserved for performance-critical blocks; gate arrays are largely obsolete.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]

#### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the FPGA design flow with a neat block diagram. Compare it with the ASIC design flow.

**Model Solution:**

**FPGA Design Flow (5 marks):**

1. **Design Entry (1 mark):** HDL (Verilog/VHDL) or High-Level Synthesis (C/C++ → RTL) entry.
2. **Functional Simulation (0.5 mark):** Verifies logic correctness via testbench.
3. **Logic Synthesis (0.5 mark):** HDL → vendor-specific netlist.
4. **Technology Mapping (1 mark):** Maps netlist onto FPGA primitives (LUTs, BRAM, DSP, PLL).
5. **Placement (0.5 mark):** Places mapped logic onto the FPGA's CLB grid.
6. **Routing (0.5 mark):** Connects placed blocks using switch matrices and routing channels.
7. **Static Timing Analysis (0.5 mark):** Verifies Fmax is met at target PVT.
8. **Bitstream Generation (0.5 mark):** Generates the configuration bitstream (.bit).
9. **Programming (0.5 mark):** Downloads bitstream to FPGA via JTAG/Flash.

**Comparison with ASIC (2 marks):**

| Step | ASIC | FPGA |
|---|---|---|
| Synthesis | To standard cells | To vendor primitives |
| Physical Design | Full custom | Vendor place-and-route |
| Sign-off | DRC, LVS, STA, Power | Post-route STA |
| Output | GDSII → Fabrication | Bitstream → Download |
| Time | Months | Hours-Days |
| Cost | High NRE, low unit | Zero NRE, higher unit |

#### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the internal architecture of an FPGA. Describe the role of LUTs, CLBs, switch matrices, and BRAMs.

**Model Solution:**

**Internal FPGA Architecture (7 marks):**

An FPGA consists of a 2D array of **Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs)**, **programmable interconnect**, **I/O blocks**, and **dedicated hard-IP blocks** (BRAM, DSP, PLL/MMCM, transceivers).

**1. Look-Up Tables (LUTs) (2 marks)**
- A $k$-input LUT implements **any boolean function of $k$ variables** by storing the truth table in SRAM.
- Example: A 4-input LUT (4-LUT) has $2^4 = 16$ SRAM cells.
- The output is the addressed truth-table entry based on the input combination.
- **Role:** Implement combinational logic in the FPGA.

**2. Configurable Logic Blocks (CLBs) (2 marks)**
- Each CLB contains **multiple slices**, each with LUTs, flip-flops, and carry logic.
- CLBs are the basic building blocks of logic in the FPGA fabric.

**3. Switch Matrices / Programmable Interconnect (1.5 marks)**
- A grid of **programmable switches** (pass transistors / muxes) that route signals between CLBs.
- Configured by the bitstream to create any desired wire connection.

**4. Block RAM (BRAM) (1 mark)**
- Dedicated on-chip memory blocks (typically 18 Kb or 36 Kb each).
- Configurable as single/dual port, FIFO, etc.

**5. Other Hard Blocks (0.5 mark)**
- **DSP slices** (multipliers, MAC units), **PLL/MMCM** (clock synthesis), **transceivers** (high-speed serial I/O).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse FPGA **bitstream** with microcontroller **firmware**. The bitstream *physically configures hardware*; firmware is *software* executed by a processor.
> 2. **Do not** state that FPGA is "faster than ASIC." It is the opposite — ASIC outperforms FPGA.
> 3. **Do not** omit **DFT** when describing the ASIC flow — it carries marks.
> 4. **Do not** say ASICs are "always cheaper" — the answer depends on **production volume** (NRE amortization).
> 5. **Hold time violations** are often tested as a trick question — they are frequency-independent.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ASIC** = custom-fabricated, high NRE, low per-unit cost, high performance, non-reconfigurable.
- **FPGA** = pre-fabricated, zero NRE, moderate per-unit cost, moderate performance, fully reconfigurable.
- **ASIC design flow** ends with **GDSII → fabrication (tape-out)**.
- **FPGA design flow** ends with **bitstream → programming (download)**.
- The **bitstream** is the FPGA's hardware-configuration file (not firmware).
- **LUTs** in FPGAs are SRAM-based truth tables implementing combinational logic.
- **CLBs** = slices containing LUTs + flip-flops + carry logic; the basic FPGA logic tile.
- **BRAMs** = dedicated on-chip memory blocks in FPGAs.
- **Standard-cell ASIC** is the dominant industry design style.
- **DFT** (scan, BIST, ATPG) is mandatory for ASIC mass-production testability.
- **Hold time** violations are *frequency-independent* — a classic KTU trick.
- **Setup slack** = $T_{clk} - T_{cq} - T_{logic} - T_{setup} - T_{skew} \geq 0$.
- **Hold slack** = $T_{cq} + T_{logic} - T_{hold} - T_{skew} \geq 0$.
- **Dynamic power** = $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ — voltage has a **quadratic** effect.
- **Rent's Rule** ($T = k N^p$) is widely used for **wire-length estimation** in floorplanning.
- **Rent's exponent** $p$ typically ranges from **0.5 to 0.75** in practice.
- **Yield model** $Y = e^{-D \cdot A_{die}}$ — larger die area → exponentially lower yield.
- **FPGA = prototyping and low-volume; ASIC = high-volume production.**
- **JTAG** is the standard interface for downloading bitstreams to FPGAs.
- **GDSII** is the standard layout file format sent to foundries for ASIC fabrication.
- **DRC** = Design Rule Check; **LVS** = Layout vs. Schematic; **STA** = Static Timing Analysis.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Top-Down and Bottom-Up Design Methodologies, Logical and Physical Design, Speed, Power and Area Considerations in VLSI Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Top-Down and Bottom-Up Design Methodologies, Logical and Physical Design, Speed, Power and Area Considerations in VLSI Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 VLSI Design Methodologies — The Two Fundamental Approaches

VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) design is the process of creating an integrated circuit (IC) by combining **millions of MOSFETs** onto a single silicon die. Because of this immense complexity, the design must be **systematically structured** using either of two opposing philosophical approaches — **Top-Down** or **Bottom-Up**.

### 🔹 Top-Down Design Methodology (The Architect's View)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus-Aligned):**
> *Top-Down design is a hierarchical decomposition strategy in which the VLSI system is first specified at the highest level of abstraction (system behaviour), and is then progressively refined, partitioned, and decomposed into smaller, less complex sub-modules until a level is reached that can be directly implemented using primitive cells (transistors, logic gates, or standard cells).*

**Intuitive Analogy — "Building a Skyscraper":**
Imagine an architect designing a 50-storey building. They do *not* start by ordering bricks. They first draw the building's exterior (system-level), then plan each floor (behavioural), then each room (RTL), and finally decide on the wall, plumbing, and electrical materials (gate/transistor level). The VLSI top-down flow follows this identical philosophy — **"big picture first, details last."**

**Key Hierarchical Levels in Top-Down VLSI Design:**

| Abstraction Level | What the Designer Sees | Tool Domain |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **System Level** | Algorithm, data flow, system specs | MATLAB, C/C++ |
| **Behavioural Level** | Functional behaviour, no timing | SystemVerilog, VHDL |
| **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** | Data movement between registers | Verilog, VHDL |
| **Logic (Gate) Level** | Boolean equations, logic gates | Synthesis tools |
| **Transistor (Circuit) Level** | MOSFETs, capacitors, resistors | SPICE |
| **Layout (Physical) Level** | Geometric masks, polygons | Cadence, Synopsys |

### 🔹 Bottom-Up Design Methodology (The Craftsman's View)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> *Bottom-Up design is a constructive assembly strategy in which primitive design elements (transistors, standard cells, or pre-characterized IP blocks) are first designed, characterized, and verified at the lowest level, and are then progressively combined, interconnected, and abstracted upwards to build more complex functional blocks and eventually the complete system.*

**Intuitive Analogy — "Lego Construction":**
A child does not begin a Lego castle with a plan of the whole city. They start with **one brick**, then attach another, then another — constructing a wall, then a tower, and finally the complete castle. Bottom-up VLSI design is identical: **start with the transistor, build the gate, build the register, build the processor.**

**Typical Bottom-Up Construction Sequence:**
1. Design a single MOSFET (Level 1)
2. Build an inverter from MOSFETs (Level 2)
3. Compose NAND/NOR gates (Level 3)
4. Build flip-flops, multiplexers (Level 4)
5. Build ALUs, register files (Level 5)
6. Integrate into a complete SoC (Level 6)

### 🔹 Why Both? The Hybrid Reality

> [!NOTE]
> Modern VLSI design (e.g., for chips like Snapdragon or Apple M-series) uses a **HYBRID approach** — top-down for behavioural/RTL design, but bottom-up for IP-block reuse and physical cell library construction. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction in Part A questions.

---

## 1.2 Logical Design vs Physical Design

The VLSI design flow is canonically divided into two **complementary halves** along the famous **Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart**.

### 🔹 Logical Design (The "What & How it Computes" Half)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Logical design is the front-end phase of VLSI implementation concerned with defining the functional and structural behaviour of the circuit — from system specification down to a technology-mapped gate-level netlist — without concerning itself with the geometric placement of those gates on silicon.*

**Logical Design Stages:**
- System specification & behavioural modelling
- RTL design (data-path + control logic)
- Functional simulation
- Logic synthesis (RTL → Generic Gates → Technology-Mapped Gates)
- Formal verification
- Generation of a **gate-level netlist** (the final output)

### 🔹 Physical Design (The "Where & How it's Built" Half)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** *Physical design is the back-end phase of VLSI implementation that transforms the synthesised gate-level netlist into a geometric layout (GDSII) suitable for mask fabrication, by determining the exact placement of every standard cell, macro, and routing interconnect on the silicon die.*

**Physical Design Stages:**
1. **Partitioning** — divide the design into sub-blocks
2. **Floorplanning** — arrange blocks on the die
3. **Placement** — assign exact cell locations
5. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — distribute the clock with low skew
6. **Routing** — connect cells using metal layers
7. **Static Timing Analysis (STA)** — verify timing closure
8. **Design Rule Check (DRC) & Layout vs Schematic (LVS)**
9. **Tape-out** (GDSII generation)

### 🔹 The Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart (KTU High-Yield Diagram)

The Y-chart is a **mandatory KTU topic** as it concisely represents all three design domains (behavioural, structural, geometric) at all abstraction levels.

---

## 1.3 Speed, Power, and Area — The VLSI Trinity

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental VLSI Trade-off:** *Every VLSI design must simultaneously optimise three competing metrics — **Speed (Performance)**, **Power Dissipation**, and **Silicon Area (Cost)**. The central challenge of VLSI design is achieving the optimal trade-off among these three, as improvements in one often degrade the others. This is popularly called the **"VLSI Design Triangle"** or **"PPA Triad"**.*

| Metric | Symbol | Units | Primary Knob |
|--------|--------|-------|--------------|
| **Speed / Delay** | $t_p$ | seconds (ps, ns) | $V_{DD}$, transistor sizing, $C_L$ |
| **Power** | $P_{total}$ | Watts (W, mW, µW) | $V_{DD}$, activity factor, clock freq. |
| **Area** | $A$ | $\mu m^2$ | Gate count, cell height, metal density |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently treat these as independent quantities. **They are not.** For example, doubling the transistor width ($W$) **doubles the drive current** (faster speed) but **doubles the load capacitance** and **quadruples the dynamic power** (worse power and area). Always state the *trade-off direction* explicitly in your answers.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Top-Down Design — The Operational Breakdown

The top-down flow follows a strict **divide-and-conquer** discipline. Let us examine each step:

### Step 1: System Specification
- The design team writes a **natural-language and mathematical specification** of what the chip must do.
- Inputs: market requirement, target application, performance budget.
- Outputs: **functional spec, I/O list, throughput/latency targets, power budget, area budget**.

### Step 2: Behavioural Modelling
- The specification is converted into an **executable behavioural model** in HDLs (Verilog/SystemVerilog/VHDL).
- The model is **algorithm-only** — no clock, no registers, no gates.
- Used to validate functionality via simulation.

### Step 3: RTL (Register-Transfer Level) Design
- The behaviour is split into a **data-path** (combinational ALUs, registers) and a **control unit** (FSM).
- A **clock** is now introduced; data transfer between registers is explicit.
- This is the level at which **synthesis tools** begin operation.

### Step 4: Functional Simulation
- The RTL is simulated with **test-benches** to verify correctness against the specification.
- Bugs found here cost **~10× less** to fix than bugs found post-layout.

### Step 5: Logic Synthesis
- A **synthesis tool** (e.g., Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus) converts RTL into a **gate-level netlist** mapped to a specific **Standard Cell Library** of a target foundry (TSMC, Intel, GlobalFoundries).
- Performs optimisations: **constant propagation, dead-code elimination, technology mapping, retiming**.

### Step 6: Gate-Level Netlist
- The final output of logical design — a **structural netlist** of interconnected standard cells (NAND, NOR, DFF, etc.) with associated timing and power models.
- This is the **handoff** to the physical design team.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Top-Down Dominates Industry:** It allows **independent verification at each level**, enables **team parallelisation** (different engineers work on different sub-modules), and provides **early architectural feedback** before committing to silicon.

---

## 2.2 Bottom-Up Design — The Operational Breakdown

The bottom-up flow is **constructive** and relies on a **Library-Driven** methodology:

### Step 1: Cell Library Development
- Designers (or foundry IP teams) design **primitive cells** — inverters, NAND, NOR, flip-flops, multiplexers, adders.
- Each cell is **fully characterised** for: area, propagation delay, input capacitance, power consumption, drive strength.
- Cells are stored in a **Standard Cell Library (.lib file in Liberty format)**.

### Step 2: Logic Cell Assembly
- Higher-level functional blocks (e.g., a 32-bit adder, a barrel shifter) are built by **interconnecting** these primitive cells.
- Each block is independently simulated and verified.

### Step 3: Sub-System Integration
- Sub-systems (e.g., cache controller, DMA engine) are assembled from logic blocks.
- Inter-block interfaces are defined (buses, handshaking protocols).

### Step 4: Full-Chip Assembly
- All sub-systems are integrated onto a single die.
- The chip is verified as a whole.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** *Top-Down* emphasises *decomposition and abstraction* (problem → smaller problems). *Bottom-Up* emphasises *composition and reuse* (primitive cells → complex systems). Modern EDA flows are **bi-directional** — RTL is synthesised top-down, but the cell library itself is designed bottom-up.

---

## 2.3 Detailed Logical Design Flow

The logical design is the **front-end** of VLSI design. Here is the canonical step-by-step expansion:

1. **Specification Capture** — natural-language + timing diagrams
2. **High-Level Modelling** — C/C++/SystemC, MATLAB
3. **RTL Coding** — Verilog/SystemVerilog/VHDL
4. **RTL Simulation** — testbench-driven, waveform analysis
5. **Synthesis (Generic)** — RTL → Boolean equations
6. **Technology Mapping** — Boolean equations → Available standard cells
7. **Optimisation** — area, timing, power-driven
8. **DFT Insertion** — scan chains, BIST, JTAG
9. **Formal Equivalence Checking** — RTL vs Netlist
10. **Static Timing Analysis (Pre-Layout)** — using wire-load models
11. **Netlist Handoff** to Physical Design

---

## 2.4 Detailed Physical Design Flow

The physical design is the **back-end** of VLSI design. It is the most computationally intensive phase.

1. **Data Preparation** — import netlist, .lib, technology file (LEF/DEF)
2. **Partitioning** — divide the design into manageable blocks (helps with hierarchical design closure)
3. **Floorplanning** — place I/O pads, macro blocks, define die/core area
4. **Power Planning** — design power rails (VDD/VSS), create power mesh
5. **Placement** — assign exact locations to all standard cells
6. **Pre-CTS Optimisation** — buffer insertion, gate sizing for timing
7. **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)** — build a balanced clock distribution network (H-tree, fishbone)
8. **Post-CTS Optimisation** — hold-time fixing, skew minimisation
9. **Global Routing** — assign routes to metal layers
10. **Detailed Routing** — exact geometric wire paths
11. **Post-Route Optimisation** — wire sizing, via doubling
12. **Sign-off Verification** — DRC, LVS, Antenna, ERC
13. **Static Timing Analysis (Post-Layout)** — with extracted RC parasitics
14. **Tape-out (GDSII)** — final layout database sent to foundry

---

## 2.5 Speed Considerations in VLSI Design

**Speed** in VLSI is fundamentally limited by **propagation delay** of logic gates and **interconnect delay** of wires.

### Propagation Delay (First-Order RC Model)
For a CMOS inverter driving a load capacitance $C_L$ through an equivalent on-resistance $R_{eq}$:

$$t_p = 0.69 \times R_{eq} \times C_L$$

where:
- $t_p$ = propagation delay (seconds)
- $R_{eq}$ = average on-resistance of PMOS/NMOS ($R_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{\mu C_{ox} (W/L) (V_{DD} - V_{th})}$)
- $C_L$ = total load capacitance (F)

### Critical Path
The **longest combinational path** between two registers — sets the **maximum clock frequency**:

$$f_{max} = \frac{1}{T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{setup}}$$

where $t_{cq}$ is the clock-to-Q delay of the launch flop and $t_{setup}$ is the setup time of the capture flop.

### Setup and Hold Constraints
- **Setup time** ($t_{setup}$): data must be stable *before* the clock edge.
- **Hold time** ($t_{hold}$): data must be stable *after* the clock edge.

---

## 2.6 Power Considerations in VLSI Design

Total power dissipation in a CMOS circuit has **two main components**:

### Static Power (Leakage Power)
$$P_{static} = V_{DD} \times I_{leak}$$

where $I_{leak}$ includes:
- **Sub-threshold leakage** ($I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} (W/L) V_T^2 e^{(V_{gs} - V_{th})/nV_T}$)
- **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage**
- **Reverse-biased junction leakage**

### Dynamic Power (Switching Power)
$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor (0 to 1)
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = clock frequency

### Short-Circuit Power
$$P_{SC} = \frac{\beta}{12} \cdot (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \cdot \tau \cdot f$$

where $\tau$ is the rise/fall time and $\beta = \mu C_{ox} (W/L)$.

### Total Power
$$P_{total} = P_{static} + P_{dynamic} + P_{SC}$$

---

## 2.7 Area Considerations in VLSI Design

Area in VLSI is measured at multiple levels:
- **Gate level**: number of equivalent 2-input NAND gates
- **Transistor level**: $(W \times L)$ sum of all active devices
- **Layout level**: bounding-box die area in $\mu m^2$

A useful **figure of merit** combining all three metrics is the **Power-Delay Product (PDP)**:

$$PDP = P_{dynamic} \times t_p \;\; [Joules]$$

And the more rigorous **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)**:

$$EDP = PDP \times t_p \;\; [Joule \cdot seconds]$$

---

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **exam-day cheat sheet**. Memorise these equations and the boundary conditions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Knob (Design Variable) |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------|
| 1 | CMOS Inverter Delay | $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$ | seconds | $W/L$, $V_{DD}$, $C_L$ |
| 2 | Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Watts | $V_{DD}$, $f$, $C_L$ |
| 3 | Static / Leakage Power | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Watts | $V_{DD}$, $V_{th}$ |
| 4 | Short-Circuit Power | $P_{SC} = \dfrac{\beta}{12} (V_{DD} - 2V_{th})^3 \tau f$ | Watts | $V_{DD}$, $V_{th}$, $\tau$ |
| 5 | Total Power | $P_{tot} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{SC}$ | Watts | — |
| 6 | Power-Delay Product | $PDP = P_{dyn} \cdot t_p$ | Joules | Combined FOM |
| 7 | Energy-Delay Product | $EDP = PDP \cdot t_p$ | J·s | Combined FOM |
| 8 | Max Clock Frequency | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{setup}}$ | Hz | $t_{cq}$, $t_{setup}$ |
| 9 | On-Resistance | $R_{eq} = \dfrac{1}{\mu C_{ox} (W/L)(V_{DD} - V_{th})}$ | Ohms | $W/L$, $V_{DD}$ |
| 10 | Sub-threshold Leakage | $I_{sub} = \mu C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} V_T^2 e^{(V_{gs} - V_{th})/nV_T}$ | Amps | $V_{th}$ |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smartphone SoCs** use **multi-Vt cells** (LVT for critical path, HVT for leakage) to optimise PDP.
- **IoT edge nodes** operate at **near-threshold voltage** ($\sim$0.4 V) to push $P_{dyn} \propto V_{DD}^2$ down by an order of magnitude.
- **GPUs / AI accelerators** trade area for speed using **wide superscalar pipelines** (deeper pipelining → higher $f_{max}$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Dynamic Power Dissipation in CMOS

This is a **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in KTU board exams. We derive $P_{dynamic}$ from first principles using the energy stored in the load capacitor.

### Given
- A CMOS inverter drives a load capacitance $C_L$.
- Supply voltage = $V_{DD}$.
- Switching activity factor = $\alpha$ (probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per clock cycle).
- Clock frequency = $f$.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Energy required to charge $C_L$ from 0 to $V_{DD}$.**

The instantaneous power delivered by the supply is $p(t) = V_{DD} \cdot i(t)$, where the current charging the capacitor is $i(t) = C_L \cdot \dfrac{dv}{dt}$.

The total energy drawn from the supply during the $0 \to V_{DD}$ transition is:

$$E_{charge} = \int_0^{T_{rise}} V_{DD} \cdot i(t) \, dt = \int_0^{V_{DD}} V_{DD} \cdot C_L \, dv$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$E_{charge} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot [v]_0^{V_{DD}} = V_{DD} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD} = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 2: Energy stored in the capacitor at the end of charging.**

The energy stored on a charged capacitor is:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 3: Energy dissipated in the PMOS during charging.**

By conservation of energy, the energy lost as heat in the PMOS resistance during the $0 \to V_{DD}$ transition is:

$$E_{PMOS} = E_{charge} - E_{stored} = C_L V_{DD}^2 - \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 4: Energy dissipated in the NMOS during discharging ($V_{DD} \to 0$).**

During discharge, the supply delivers **no energy** (the current flows from $C_L$ to ground through the NMOS). The full $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ stored in the capacitor is dissipated as heat in the NMOS:

$$E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 5: Total energy dissipated per FULL switching cycle ($0 \to 1 \to 0$).**

$$E_{cycle} = E_{PMOS} + E_{NMOS} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 + \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = C_L V_{DD}^2$$

**Step 6: Energy per clock period.**

If the clock has frequency $f$, then $f$ switching events occur per second. The average dynamic power (energy per second) is:

$$P_{dynamic} = E_{cycle} \cdot f = C_L V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 7: Incorporate the switching activity factor $\alpha$.**

Not every node switches every cycle. Let $\alpha$ be the probability of a $0 \to 1$ transition per clock cycle. The dynamic power is then:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f}$$
> **Critical Insight:** Power has a **QUADRATIC dependence on $V_{DD}$**. This is the *single most important lever* for low-power design — halving $V_{DD}$ reduces power by **4×** (at the cost of a slower gate).

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: CMOS Inverter Propagation Delay

### Given
- A CMOS inverter with NMOS W/L ratio = $(W/L)_n$ and PMOS typically $(W/L)_p = 2.5 \cdot (W/L)_n$ for symmetric drive.
- Load capacitance $C_L$ at the output.
- Supply $V_{DD}$, threshold voltage $V_{th}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1: Average on-resistance of the transistor in saturation.**

In saturation, the drain current of a MOSFET is:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

For a switching transition, the average current is approximated by the **effective on-resistance** $R_{eq}$, which (in the linear/saturation transition) is given by:

$$R_{eq} = \frac{1}{\mu C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{DD} - V_{th})}$$

**Step 2: Output node RC time constant.**

The output node is modelled as a first-order RC low-pass filter with time constant:

$$\tau = R_{eq} \cdot C_L$$

**Step 3: Step response of the RC circuit.**

When the input switches, the output voltage evolves as:

$$v_{out}(t) = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

**Step 4: Definition of propagation delay $t_p$.**

Propagation delay is measured between the **50% point of the input** and the **50% point of the output** (i.e., $v_{out} = 0.5 V_{DD}$). Substituting:

$$0.5 V_{DD} = V_{final} + (V_{initial} - V_{final}) \cdot e^{-t_p/\tau}$$

For a high-to-low transition: $V_{initial} = V_{DD}$, $V_{final} = 0$:

$$0.5 V_{DD} = 0 + (V_{DD} - 0) \cdot e^{-t_{PHL}/\tau}$$

$$0.5 = e^{-t_{PHL}/\tau}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln(0.5) = -\frac{t_{PHL}}{\tau}$$

$$t_{PHL} = \tau \cdot \ln(2) = 0.693 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$$

Similarly, $t_{PLH} = 0.693 \cdot R_{eq,p} \cdot C_L$. The average propagation delay is:

$$t_p = \frac{t_{PHL} + t_{PLH}}{2} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,avg} \cdot C_L$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Result:**
> $$\boxed{t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L}$$
> **Insight:** Delay is **linear in $C_L$** and **inversely proportional to $W/L$**. To halve delay, double the transistor width (but at the cost of doubling $C_{in}$ and quadrupling $P_{dyn}$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Direction Between PPA Metrics

This is the **single most important analytical table** for KTU exams. You must be able to state, for any design move, which way each of the three metrics moves.

| Design Action | Speed ($t_p$) | Power ($P$) | Area ($A$) | Trade-off Verdict |
|---------------|---------------|-------------|------------|-------------------|
| Increase $V_{DD}$ | **Faster** ($\downarrow t_p$) | **Worse** ($\uparrow P \propto V_{DD}^2$) | Unchanged | Power–Speed trade-off |
| Increase transistor width $W$ | **Faster** ($\downarrow t_p$) | **Worse** ($\uparrow C_{in}$, $\uparrow P$) | **Worse** ($\uparrow A$) | All three worsen (except speed) |
| Reduce $C_L$ (smaller fan-out) | **Faster** | **Better** ($\downarrow P$) | Unchanged | Win-Win |
| Use HVT (high-$V_{th}$) cells | Slower | **Better** (low leakage) | Unchanged | Speed–Power trade-off |
| Increase pipeline depth | **Faster** (higher $f_{max}$) | Better (lower $V_{DD}$ possible) | Slightly worse (more flops) | Generally beneficial |
| Clock gating | Unchanged (on critical path) | **Much better** (zero $\alpha$) | Unchanged | Power-only win |
| Power gating (sleep transistors) | Slow wake-up | **Much better** ($I_{leak} \to 0$) | Worse (extra transistors) | Power–Area trade-off |
| Voltage scaling (DVS) | Slower | **Much better** ($\propto V_{DD}^2$) | Unchanged | Power–Speed trade-off |
| Use adiabatic logic | Slower | **Much better** | Worse | Power–Area trade-off |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Trap:** When asked *"How do you reduce power?"*, students answer *"Reduce $V_{DD}$"* and stop. **This is incomplete.** You must also state the *consequence* — *"this reduces power quadratically but increases delay linearly (slower speed)."* Always write the trade-off explicitly.

---

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

**Problem:** A CMOS chip operates at $V_{DD} = 1.2$ V, clock frequency $f = 500$ MHz, total switched capacitance $C_L = 10$ nF, and activity factor $\alpha = 0.1$. Compute the dynamic power.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Write the formula.

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the numerical values.

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.1 \times (10 \times 10^{-9}) \times (1.2)^2 \times (500 \times 10^6)$$

**Step 3:** Compute $V_{DD}^2$.

$$V_{DD}^2 = 1.2^2 = 1.44 \; V^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the product.

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.44 \times 500 \times 10^6$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.1 \times 10^{-8} \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^8$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.1 \times 1.44 \times 5 \times 10^{0}$$

$$P_{dynamic} = 0.1 \times 7.2 = 0.72 \; W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $P_{dynamic} = 720$ mW. Notice the quadratic dependence — if $V_{DD}$ is dropped from 1.2 V to 0.8 V, the power would reduce to $P_{new} = 0.72 \times (0.8/1.2)^2 = 0.32$ W, a **55% reduction**.


> [!NOTE]
> **Three axes of the Y-chart:** *Behavioural* (what the system does), *Structural* (how it is composed of sub-modules), *Geometric* (how it is laid out on silicon). The **rings** (System → Algorithm → RTL → Logic → Circuit → Layout) represent the **abstraction levels**. A design moves *outward along the radial axis* during top-down decomposition and *inward* during bottom-up composition.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Physical Design Flow (Back-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1: Netlist Handoff from Logic Design"]:::phase1
    step2["Step 2: Partitioning into Sub Blocks"]:::phase1
    step3["Step 3: Floorplanning of Macros and IO"]:::phase2
    step4["Step 4: Power Planning and Power Mesh"]:::phase2
    step5["Step 5: Placement of Standard Cells"]:::phase3
    step6["Step 6: Pre CTS Optimisation"]:::phase3
    step7["Step 7: Clock Tree Synthesis CTS"]:::phase4
    step8["Step 8: Global and Detailed Routing"]:::phase4
    step9["Step 9: Post Route Optimisation"]:::phase5
    step10["Step 10: Sign Off DRC LVS STA"]:::phase5
    step11["Step 11: Tape Out GDSII to Foundry"]:::phase5

    step1 --> step2
    step2 --> step3
    step3 --> step4
    step4 --> step5
    step5 --> step6
    step6 --> step7
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9
    step9 --> step10
    step10 --> step11

    classDef phase1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#0891b2,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef phase2 fill:#0891b2,stroke:#06b6d4,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef phase3 fill:#06b6d4,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef phase4 fill:#22d3ee,stroke:#67e8f9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef phase5 fill:#67e8f9,stroke:#22d3ee,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading Guide:** This is the canonical **physical design flow** tested in KTU exams. Note that steps 1–2 are **planning**, steps 3–4 are **macros/IO/power**, steps 5–6 are **cell-level**, steps 7–8 are **timing-critical** (clock + routing), and steps 9–11 are **sign-off and tape-out**.

---

## 4.5 Diagram 5 — The VLSI PPA Triad (Speed–Power–Area Triangle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["SPEED / PERFORMANCE"]:::corner1
    nodeB["POWER DISSIPATION"]:::corner2
    nodeC["SILICON AREA / COST"]:::corner3
    nodeD["Higher VDD"]:::action
    nodeE["Larger W over L"]:::action
    nodeF["Deeper Pipelining"]:::action
    nodeG["Lower VDD"]:::action
    nodeH["Clock Gating"]:::action
    nodeI["Multi Vt Cells"]:::action
    nodeJ["Smaller Cells"]:::action
    nodeK["Higher Density Layout"]:::action
    nodeL["Shared Resources"]:::action

    nodeA --- nodeB
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeA --- nodeC
    nodeA -.- nodeD
    nodeA -.- nodeE
    nodeA -.- nodeF
    nodeB -.- nodeG
    nodeB -.- nodeH
    nodeB -.- nodeI
    nodeC -.- nodeJ
    nodeC -.- nodeK
    nodeC -.- nodeL

    classDef corner1 fill:#dc2626,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef corner2 fill:#16a34a,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef corner3 fill:#2563eb,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:3px,color:#ffffff
    classDef action fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

**Reading Guide:** The triangle's three vertices represent the three metrics. The dotted lines list **design actions** that improve the *closest* vertex. **No action improves all three simultaneously** — this is the essence of the VLSI trade-off.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between Top-Down and Bottom-Up design methodologies in VLSI. State one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Top-Down Design | Bottom-Up Design |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Direction | System → Modules → Cells | Cells → Modules → System |
| Starting point | High-level specification | Primitive cells / libraries |
| Primary activity | Decomposition & abstraction | Composition & integration |
| Industry usage | RTL/logic design phase | Library/IP-block development |
| Advantage | Allows early architectural exploration, team parallelisation, and design reusability | Enables full characterisation of low-level cells, accurate timing/power models, and reliable system construction |

*(Word limit: ~100 words. 3 marks.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Q: List the various phases of the physical design flow. Why is clock tree synthesis (CTS) considered a critical step?**

**Model Answer:**

The phases of physical design are: (1) Partitioning, (2) Floorplanning, (3) Power Planning, (4) Placement, (5) Pre-CTS Optimisation, (6) **Clock Tree Synthesis (CTS)**, (7) Routing, (8) Post-Route Optimisation, (9) Sign-off (DRC/LVS/STA), (10) Tape-out.

CTS is critical because the **clock signal** must reach every sequential element (flip-flop/latch) on the die with **minimum skew** (difference in arrival times) and **minimum latency**. Any clock skew directly degrades setup/hold timing margins and limits the maximum operating frequency $f_{max}$. Poor CTS is the single most common cause of post-layout timing failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Explain the Top-Down design approach in VLSI with a neat block diagram. List the major abstraction levels and the corresponding EDA tools used at each level.**

**Model Solution:**

The **Top-Down design methodology** is a *decomposition-based* approach where the VLSI system is progressively refined from a high-level specification down to the physical layout. The approach is shown in the diagram in Section 4.1.

**Major Abstraction Levels (with EDA tools):**

1. **System Specification Level** — defines the chip's functionality, performance, power, and area budgets. *Tools*: requirement management tools, datasheets, MATLAB/SystemC for algorithm validation.
2. **Behavioural Level** — algorithmic, untimed model. *Tools*: SystemC, MATLAB, high-level synthesis (HLS) tools like Catapult HLS.
3. **Register-Transfer Level (RTL)** — clocked, register-based description. *Tools*: Verilog/VHDL editors (Verilator, ModelSim, QuestaSim), commercial simulators (VCS, Xcelium).
4. **Logic / Gate Level** — Boolean equations mapped to technology cells. *Tools*: Synopsys Design Compiler, Cadence Genus (synthesis); Formality (equivalence checking).
5. **Transistor / Circuit Level** — SPICE-level analysis. *Tools*: HSPICE, FineSim, ngspice.
6. **Physical / Layout Level** — geometric polygons. *Tools*: Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2 (P&R); Calibre (DRC/LVS); PrimeTime (STA).

**Advantages (3 key points for full marks):**
- Enables *early architectural exploration* (you can change algorithms cheaply at high level).
- Allows *team parallelisation* — different sub-modules are designed simultaneously.
- Provides *multi-level verification* — bugs caught early are 10×–100× cheaper to fix.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: With neat diagrams, explain Logical Design and Physical Design. State clearly the input, output, and the EDA tools used at each stage.**

**Model Solution:**

**Logical Design (Front-End):**

- **Input:** System specification (natural language + algorithmic model).
- **Process:** RTL coding → Functional simulation → Logic synthesis → Technology mapping → DFT insertion → Formal verification.
- **Output:** A **gate-level netlist** in Verilog/VHDL format, mapped to a target Standard Cell Library.
- **Tools:** Synopsys VCS (simulation), Design Compiler (synthesis), Formality (equivalence checking), Tessent (DFT).
- **Key concern:** Functional correctness, synthesis-time area/timing/power estimates.

**Physical Design (Back-End):**

- **Input:** Gate-level netlist + .lib (timing/power library) + .lef (technology file) + floorplan constraints.
- **Process:** Partitioning → Floorplanning → Power planning → Placement → CTS → Routing → Sign-off verification.
- **Output:** **GDSII** layout database, ready for mask fabrication at a foundry.
- **Tools:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys IC Compiler II, Mentor Olympus; Calibre for DRC/LVS, PrimeTime for post-layout STA.
- **Key concern:** Timing closure, DRC/LVS-clean layout, manufacturability, signal integrity, IR drop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently confuse the **gate-level netlist** as the output of *physical design*. **It is not** — the netlist is the output of *logical design* and the **input** to *physical design*. The *output* of physical design is the **GDSII layout**. This single error costs 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Derive the expression for dynamic power dissipation in a CMOS circuit. State clearly the dependence on supply voltage and frequency, and explain why dynamic power dominates in modern high-performance chips.**

**Model Solution:**

The detailed step-by-step derivation is given in **Section 3.1**. The result is:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

**Dependence Analysis:**

- **Quadratic dependence on $V_{DD}$:** $P_{dynamic} \propto V_{DD}^2$. Halving $V_{DD}$ (e.g., 1.2 V → 0.6 V) reduces dynamic power by **4×**. This is the most powerful knob in low-power design.
- **Linear dependence on $f$:** $P_{dynamic} \propto f$. Doubling the clock frequency doubles the dynamic power (but improves performance). Hence the figure of merit *power per unit performance* is $\propto V_{DD}^2$.
- **Linear dependence on $C_L$:** Reducing load capacitance via shorter wires, smaller fan-out, and smaller gate sizing reduces power.
- **Linear dependence on $\alpha$:** Activity factor. Techniques like **clock gating** set $\alpha \to 0$ for idle blocks.

**Why Dynamic Power Dominates in Modern Chips:**

In sub-100 nm CMOS technologies (e.g., 7 nm, 5 nm), $V_{DD}$ has been scaled down to ~0.7–1.0 V, drastically reducing *per-transistor* dynamic energy. However:
1. Modern chips integrate **billions of transistors**, so the *total switched capacitance* $C_L$ is enormous.
2. Clock frequencies of multi-GHz processors mean $f$ is very high.
3. The product $\alpha \cdot C_L \cdot f$ is huge, making dynamic power the **dominant** term (typically 60–80% of $P_{total}$) in high-performance designs.
4. Leakage is a concern but is mitigated by HVT cells and power gating.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Q: Discuss the Speed–Power–Area (SPA) trade-off in VLSI design. With a suitable example, show how a design decision to improve one metric adversely affects the other two.**

**Model Solution:**

The SPA trade-off is the **fundamental design challenge** in VLSI — every decision to optimise one metric (Speed, Power, or Area) typically degrades the other two. (Refer to the analytical matrix in Section 3.3.)

**Detailed Example — Doubling the transistor width $W$:**

Consider a CMOS inverter with width $W$ driving a load $C_L$. Original values:
- $W = 1 \;\mu m$, $L = 0.1 \;\mu m$, $C_L = 10$ fF, $V_{DD} = 1$ V, $f = 1$ GHz, $\alpha = 0.1$.

**Metric 1 — Speed (Delay):**
$t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$, where $R_{eq} \propto 1/W$. Doubling $W$ halves $R_{eq}$, so $t_p$ is **halved** (faster). ✓ *Speed improves.*

**Metric 2 — Power:**
- Input capacitance $C_{in} \propto W$, so the inverter now presents **double** the input load to the previous stage, doubling its $C_L$ and hence its dynamic power.
- Total $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ approximately **doubles**. ✗ *Power worsens.*

**Metric 3 — Area:**
- Layout area of the transistor $\propto W \cdot L$, so area **doubles**. ✗ *Area worsens.*

**Conclusion:** Doubling $W$ *improved* speed by 2×, but *doubled* both power consumption and silicon area. The VLSI designer's role is to choose the **optimal $W$** that satisfies all three constraints simultaneously — this is the heart of the **gate-sizing** optimisation problem solved by EDA tools.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** When asked "explain the SPA trade-off," students often list *three separate definitions* of speed, power, and area. **This is not the answer.** The question demands that you describe a **decision** and show its *adverse effect on at least one other metric*. Always provide a **concrete numerical example** with substitution.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-night revision checklist before the KTU exam.**

### 🔹 Top-Down vs Bottom-Up
- **Top-Down:** System → Sub-systems → Cells (decomposition). Used for RTL/logic design.
- **Bottom-Up:** Cells → Sub-systems → System (composition). Used for cell library and IP design.
- Modern flow is **hybrid** — top-down for behaviour, bottom-up for primitives.

### 🔹 Logical Design
- **Output:** Gate-level netlist (technology-mapped, timing-annotated).
- **Tools:** Synopsys DC, Cadence Genus, VCS, ModelSim.
- **Phases:** Spec → Behaviour → RTL → Simulation → Synthesis → Netlist.

### 🔹 Physical Design
- **Output:** GDSII layout.
- **Tools:** Cadence Innovus, Synopsys ICC2, Mentor Olympus, Calibre.
- **Phases:** Partition → Floorplan → Power plan → Placement → CTS → Route → Sign-off.
- **CTS is critical** — controls clock skew and $f_{max}$.

### 🔹 Gajski-Kuhn Y-Chart
- **Three domains:** Behavioural, Structural, Geometric.
- **Rings (levels):** System, Algorithm, RTL, Logic, Circuit, Layout.
- **Radial movement:** represents abstraction; **angular movement:** represents a change of design domain.

### 🔹 Speed
- $t_p = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq} \cdot C_L$.
- $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{clk} - t_{cq} - t_{setup}}$.
- Speed-up via: $W$↑, $V_{DD}$↑, $C_L$↓, pipelining.

### 🔹 Power
- $P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ — **quadratic in $V_{DD}$**.
- $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$.
- $P_{total} = P_{dyn} + P_{stat} + P_{SC}$.
- Reduction via: $V_{DD}$↓, $\alpha$↓ (clock gating), $f$↓, multi-Vt, power gating.

### 🔹 Area
- Measured in $\mu m^2$, gate equivalents, or transistor count.
- Reduced by: cell sharing, resource multiplexing, higher integration, smaller feature size.
- Increased by: larger $W$, more pipeline stages, redundant circuits (e.g., ECC), power-gating transistors.

### 🔹 Figures of Merit
- **PDP** = $P \cdot t_p$ [Joules] — energy per operation.
- **EDP** = $PDP \cdot t_p$ [J·s] — penalises slow designs more.

### 🔹 Golden Rules to Memorise
1. Power is **quadratic** in $V_{DD}$, **linear** in $f$ and $C_L$.
2. Delay is **linear** in $C_L$, **inversely proportional** to $W/L$.
3. There is **no free lunch** — every optimisation degrades at least one PPA metric.
4. The **netlist** is the boundary between logical (front-end) and physical (back-end) design.
5. **CTS** is the most timing-critical step in physical design.

> **Good luck with your KTU VLSI Technology exam — focus on the trade-offs, not just the formulas!** 🚀

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CMOS Inverters - DC Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** CMOS Inverters - DC Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **CMOS Inverter** (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Inverter) is the fundamental logic gate of modern digital VLSI circuits, constructed by the series connection of a **p-channel MOSFET (PMOS)** pull-up network and an **n-channel MOSFET (NMOS)** pull-down network, sharing a common gate input and a common drain output. The **DC Characteristics** describe the steady-state, time-independent behavior of this inverter—specifically how the output voltage ($V_{out}$) responds to a varying DC input voltage ($V_{in}$) under no-load (quasi-static) conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** The DC characteristic analysis is rooted in the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** curve, the **switching threshold**, and the **noise margins**, which are the cornerstones of robust digital design under the 2024 NEP-aligned outcome-based framework.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **two-person seesaw** in a playground where two children of equal strength sit on opposite ends.

- **Child A (PMOS)** is positioned to **lift** the seesaw (raise output to $V_{DD}$).
- **Child B (NMOS)** is positioned to **push down** the seesaw (lower output to $0\text{ V}$, i.e., ground).

Both children receive the **same command simultaneously**:
- When the command says **"push down"** ($V_{in} = V_{DD} = \text{High}$): Child A steps off (PMOS turns OFF), and Child B pushes hard (NMOS turns ON) → the seesaw hits the ground → $V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$ (Logic 0).
- When the command says **"stay neutral"** ($V_{in} = 0\text{ V}$): Child B steps off (NMOS turns OFF), and Child A pushes up (PMOS turns ON) → the seesaw rises to the top → $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (Logic 1).

The **smooth transition** between these two states is what we characterize as the DC behavior of the CMOS inverter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Conceptual Insight:** In steady state, the CMOS inverter theoretically draws **zero static current** from the supply rail because one transistor is always OFF. Power is consumed only during the brief switching transition. This is the reason CMOS dominates modern low-power VLSI design.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics (per KTU 2024 VLSI syllabus) are used throughout this module:

- **$V_{DD}$:** Positive supply voltage (typically **$3.3\text{ V}$** in conventional CMOS, scaling down to **$1.2\text{ V}$** or **$0.8\text{ V}$** in advanced nanometer nodes).
- **$V_{Tn}$:** Threshold voltage of the NMOS transistor (typically **$0.4\text{ V}$ to $0.7\text{ V}$**).
- **$V_{Tp}$:** Threshold voltage of the PMOS transistor (negative value, typically **$-0.4\text{ V}$ to $-0.7\text{ V}$**, often quoted in magnitude as **$|V_{Tp}|$**).
- **$k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$:** Process transconductance parameter for NMOS.
- **$k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$:** Process transconductance parameter for PMOS.
- **$\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2\text{ to }3$:** Electron-to-hole mobility ratio (this dictates the sizing rule $W_p/L_p \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$ times $W_n/L_n$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown – The Five Regions of the VTC

The DC analysis of the CMOS inverter requires partitioning the input voltage sweep ($0 \leq V_{in} \leq V_{DD}$) into **five distinct operating regions**, governed by the saturation conditions of both transistors:

| Region | $V_{in}$ Range | NMOS State | PMOS State | $V_{out}$ Behavior |
|--------|---------------|------------|------------|--------------------|
| **R1** | $0 \leq V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ | Cutoff (OFF) | Linear (Triode) | $V_{out} \approx V_{DD}$ (HIGH) |
| **R2** | $V_{Tn} \leq V_{in} < V_{in}^{\ast}$ | Saturation | Linear (Triode) | $V_{out}$ drops from $V_{DD}$ toward midpoint |
| **R3** | $V_{in} = V_{in}^{\ast}$ | Saturation | Saturation | $V_{out} = V_M$ (switching threshold) |
| **R4** | $V_{in}^{\ast} < V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Linear (Triode) | Saturation | $V_{out}$ drops sharply toward $0\text{ V}$ |
| **R5** | $V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ | Linear (Triode) | Cutoff (OFF) | $V_{out} \approx 0\text{ V}$ (LOW) |

### 2.2 Why These Regions Exist — The Physical Logic

- **Why does the NMOS turn ON only after $V_{in} \geq V_{Tn}$?** Because the gate-to-source voltage ($V_{GS,n} = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$) must exceed the built-in threshold to create an inversion layer (channel) of electrons beneath the gate oxide.
- **Why does the PMOS turn OFF only after $V_{in} > V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$?** Because $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$ must drop below $|V_{Tp}|$ for the channel to vanish. The PMOS is ON as long as $V_{SG,p} > |V_{Tp}|$, i.e., $V_{in} < V_{DD} - |V_{Tp}|$.
- **Why does the output swing rail-to-rail?** Because when one device is fully OFF, its channel resistance is effectively infinite, so the full $V_{DD}$ drops across it (no current path means no $IR$ drop in the ON device).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description / Condition | Units |
|---|---------|------------------------|-------|
| 1 | $k_n = \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_n$ | NMOS transconductance parameter | $\text{A/V}^2$ |
| 2 | $k_p = \mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_p$ | PMOS transconductance parameter | $\text{A/V}^2$ |
| 3 | $k_r = k_n / k_p$ | Beta ratio (asymmetry factor) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$ | High output level (theoretical) | Volts |
| 5 | $V_{OL} = 0$ | Low output level (theoretical) | Volts |
| 6 | $I_{Dn} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})^2$ | NMOS saturation current | Amps |
| 7 | $I_{Dp} = \tfrac{1}{2} k_p (V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$ | PMOS saturation current | Amps |
| 8 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert + V_{Tp})}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Switching threshold (with $V_{Tp}$ negative) | Volts |
| 9 | $V_M = \dfrac{V_{Tn} + \sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$ | Switching threshold (using magnitudes) | Volts |
| 10 | $V_{IL} = \dfrac{2 V_{out} + V_{Tn} - V_{DD} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert \sqrt{\tfrac{k_n}{k_p}} \cdot \text{(...)}}{...}$ | Input LOW voltage (max LOW) | Volts |
| 11 | $V_{IH} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_n/k_p}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + \sqrt{k_n/k_p}}$ | Input HIGH voltage (min HIGH) | Volts |
| 12 | $NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH}$ | High noise margin | Volts |
| 13 | $NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL}$ | Low noise margin | Volts |
| 14 | $P_{DC} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{static}$ | Static power dissipation (≈ 0 for CMOS) | Watts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The switching threshold $V_M$ determines the **trip point** of any cascaded gate. Noise margins ($NM_H$, $NM_L$) quantify the **robustness** of digital logic against voltage noise, coupling, and supply variations—directly impacting yield and reliability in fabricated ICs.

### 2.4 Special Condition: The Symmetric Inverter

If the designer sizes the transistors such that $k_n = k_p$ (i.e., $\sqrt{k_r} = 1$), the switching threshold simplifies to:

$$V_M \bigg\vert_{k_n = k_p} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

This is the **most desirable condition** for digital design because:
1. $V_M$ sits exactly at the midpoint, giving **equal noise margins** above and below.
2. The VTC curve becomes **anti-symmetric** about the point $(V_M, V_M)$.
3. Dynamic switching occurs at the **steepest possible point** of the curve.

Since $\mu_n \approx 2\mu_p$ to $3\mu_p$, to enforce $k_n = k_p$, the designer must make:

$$\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3$$

This explains the **canonical sizing rule** in CMOS standard cell libraries: **PMOS is made 2× to 3× wider than NMOS**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Switching Threshold $V_M$

**Starting Assumption:** At $V_{in} = V_M$, both transistors operate in **saturation**. The node equation requires that the currents balance ($I_{Dn} = -I_{Dp}$, since PMOS current flows opposite to drain convention):

$$I_{Dn,\text{sat}} = -I_{Dp,\text{sat}}$$

**Step 1 — Write the saturation current equations.**

For NMOS in saturation:

$$I_{Dn} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{GS,n} - V_{Tn})^2$$

For PMOS in saturation (with proper sign convention):

$$I_{Dp} = \dfrac{1}{2} k_p (V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the gate-source voltages in terms of $V_{in}$ and $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{GS,n} = V_{in} - 0 = V_{in}$$

$$V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in}$$

At the switching threshold, $V_{out} = V_M = V_{in}$, so:

$$V_{GS,n} = V_M, \quad V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_M$$

**Step 3 — Equate the magnitudes.**

$$\dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \dfrac{1}{2} k_p (V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ cancels from both sides.

**Step 4 — Take the square root of both sides.**

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,(V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$$

**Step 5 — Isolate $V_M$.**

Begin by expanding the right-hand side:

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{Tn} = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} - \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

Collect $V_M$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$\sqrt{k_n}\,V_M + \sqrt{k_p}\,V_M = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{Tn} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

Factor $V_M$ on the left:

$$V_M (\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}) = \sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{Tn} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{Tp}\vert$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_M$.**

$$\boxed{V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_p}\,V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n}\,V_{Tn} - \sqrt{k_p}\,\vert V_{Tp}\vert}{\sqrt{k_n} + \sqrt{k_p}}}$$

Dividing numerator and denominator by $\sqrt{k_p}$:

$$V_M = \dfrac{V_{DD} + \sqrt{k_n/k_p}\,V_{Tn} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert}{1 + \sqrt{k_n/k_p}}$$

Letting $k_r = k_n / k_p$ (so $\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{k_n/k_p}$):

$$\boxed{V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_r}\,V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}}$$

**Verification (Symmetric Case):** If $k_r = 1$ and $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$, then:

$$V_M = \dfrac{1 \cdot V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - V_{Tn})}{1 + 1} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} \quad \checkmark$$

### 3.2 Derivation of $V_{IL}$ (Unity-Gain Point on Falling Edge)

The point $V_{IL}$ is defined as the input voltage at which $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$. At this point, the NMOS is in **saturation** and the PMOS is in **linear (triode)**.

**Step 1 — PMOS triode current expression.**

$$I_{Dp} = k_p \left[ (V_{SG,p} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) V_{SD,p} - \dfrac{V_{SD,p}^2}{2} \right]$$

With $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{IL}$ and $V_{SD,p} = V_{DD} - V_{out}$:

$$I_{Dp} = k_p \left[ (V_{DD} - V_{IL} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)(V_{DD} - V_{out}) - \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_{out})^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Equate to NMOS saturation current (magnitude).**

$$\dfrac{1}{2} k_n (V_{IL} - V_{Tn})^2 = I_{Dp}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the unity-gain slope condition.**

Differentiating both sides with respect to $V_{in}$ and setting $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ yields the closed-form expression (derived in standard VLSI texts):

$$V_{IL} = \dfrac{2 V_{out} + V_{Tn} - V_{DD} - (V_{DD} - V_{out} + \vert V_{Tp}\vert)\sqrt{k_p/k_n}}{...}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU exam purposes**, students are expected to **derive** the expression symbolically and then **substitute numerical values**. The closed-form final expression (presented in Section 2.3) is what is typically required.

### 3.3 Derivation of $V_{IH}$ (Unity-Gain Point on Rising Edge)

By symmetry (mirror image), $V_{IH}$ is found when the NMOS is in **linear (triode)** and the PMOS is in **saturation**. Applying the analogous procedure:

**Step 1 — Equate the currents.** With $V_{GS,n} = V_{IH}$, $V_{DS,n} = V_{out}$:

$$\dfrac{1}{2} k_p (V_{DD} - V_{IH} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2 = k_n \left[ (V_{IH} - V_{Tn})V_{out} - \dfrac{V_{out}^2}{2} \right]$$

**Step 2 — Apply $\dfrac{dV_{out}}{dV_{in}} = -1$ and solve.**

After algebraic manipulation:

$$\boxed{V_{IH} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_n/k_p}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + \sqrt{k_n/k_p}}}$$

**Verification (Symmetric Case $k_n = k_p$, $V_{Tn} = \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}) + V_{Tn}}{2} = \dfrac{V_{DD}}{2} = V_M$$

This confirms the **anti-symmetric** property of the symmetric inverter.

### 3.4 Noise Margin Computation

Once $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ are obtained, the noise margins are **direct subtractions**:

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IH}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{IL} - 0 = V_{IL}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Larger noise margins mean a more **robust** logic gate—less susceptible to false switching due to noise, crosstalk, ground bounce, or supply droop. KTU 2024 outcomes strongly emphasize this metric under the CO (Course Outcome) on **digital design robustness**.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.6\text{ V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.7\text{ V}$, $k_n / k_p = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\sqrt{k_r}$:**

$$\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{2.5} \approx 1.581$$

**Step 2 — Compute $V_M$:**

$$V_M = \dfrac{(1.581)(0.6) + (3.3 - 0.7)}{1 + 1.581} = \dfrac{0.9486 + 2.6}{2.581} = \dfrac{3.5486}{2.581} \approx 1.375\text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $V_{IH}$:**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{(1.581)(3.3 - 0.7) + 0.6}{2.581} = \dfrac{(1.581)(2.6) + 0.6}{2.581} = \dfrac{4.1106 + 0.6}{2.581} = \dfrac{4.7106}{2.581} \approx 1.825\text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $V_{IL}$ (using triode-region derivation):**

$$V_{IL} \approx 0.96\text{ V (calculated via full triode current balance)}$$

**Step 5 — Compute noise margins:**

$$NM_H = V_{DD} - V_{IH} = 3.3 - 1.825 = 1.475\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - 0 = 0.96\text{ V}$$

**Observation:** $NM_H > NM_L$ because $k_n > k_p$ (asymmetric design). To balance them, increase $(W/L)_p$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the switching threshold voltage $V_M$ of a CMOS inverter. Derive its expression in terms of $V_{Tn}$, $V_{Tp}$, $V_{DD}$, and the beta ratio $k_r = k_n/k_p$.**

**Model Answer:**

The switching threshold $V_M$ is defined as the input voltage $V_{in}$ at which the output voltage $V_{out}$ equals $V_{in}$, i.e., the point on the VTC curve where $V_{out} = V_{in}$. At this point, both the NMOS and PMOS transistors operate in saturation.

Equating the saturation currents ($I_{Dn} = -I_{Dp}$):

$$\dfrac{1}{2}k_n(V_M - V_{Tn})^2 = \dfrac{1}{2}k_p(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^2$$

Taking the square root and solving for $V_M$:

$$V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_r}\,V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$$

where $k_r = k_n/k_p$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**What are noise margins in a CMOS inverter? Define $NM_H$ and $NM_L$.**

**Model Answer:**

Noise margins quantify the ability of a logic gate to tolerate voltage noise without producing erroneous logic levels. They are defined using the unity-gain points $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$ on the VTC curve.

$$NM_H = V_{OH} - V_{IH} = V_{DD} - V_{IH}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - V_{OL} = V_{IL} - 0 = V_{IL}$$

**$NM_H$** (High Noise Margin) is the range of voltages at the input that are still recognized as valid logic HIGH.
**$NM_L$** (Low Noise Margin) is the range of voltages at the input that are still recognized as valid logic LOW. Larger noise margins indicate a more robust design.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a CMOS inverter. Identify and describe **all five regions** of operation as $V_{in}$ varies from $0$ to $V_{DD}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 2.5\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.6\text{ V}$, and $k_n/k_p = 3$, calculate: **(i)** the switching threshold $V_M$, **(ii)** the input high voltage $V_{IH}$, and **(iii)** the noise margins $NM_H$ and $NM_L$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The CMOS inverter consists of a PMOS (source at $V_{DD}$, gate at $V_{in}$, drain at $V_{out}$) connected in series with an NMOS (source at GND, gate at $V_{in}$, drain at $V_{out}$).

**Region 1 ($0 \leq V_{in} < V_{Tn}$):** $V_{GS,n} = V_{in} < V_{Tn}$ → NMOS is OFF (cutoff). $V_{SG,p} = V_{DD} - V_{in} > \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ → PMOS is ON, operating in the linear (triode) region. Since no current flows, the voltage drop across PMOS is zero, so $V_{out} = V_{DD}$ (Logic HIGH).

**Region 2 ($V_{Tn} \leq V_{in} < V_{in}^{\ast}$):** NMOS turns ON and enters saturation (since $V_{DS,n} = V_{out}$ is still high). PMOS remains in the triode region. As $V_{in}$ increases, $V_{out}$ begins to fall.

**Region 3 ($V_{in} = V_M$):** Both transistors are in saturation. This is the high-gain transition point where $V_{out} = V_{in} = V_M$. Maximum current flows momentarily.

**Region 4 ($V_{in}^{\ast} < V_{in} \leq V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):** NMOS enters the linear (triode) region. PMOS remains in saturation. $V_{out}$ falls sharply.

**Region 5 ($V_{in} > V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert$):** $V_{SG,p} < \vert V_{Tp}\vert$ → PMOS is OFF. NMOS is fully ON in triode. $V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$ (Logic LOW).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 2.5\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.5\text{ V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.6\text{ V}$, $k_r = k_n/k_p = 3$.

**(i) Switching Threshold $V_M$:**

$$\sqrt{k_r} = \sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$$

$$V_M = \dfrac{(1.732)(0.5) + (2.5 - 0.6)}{1 + 1.732} = \dfrac{0.866 + 1.9}{2.732} = \dfrac{2.766}{2.732} \approx 1.013\text{ V}$$

**(ii) Input High Voltage $V_{IH}$:**

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_r}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert) + V_{Tn}}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}} = \dfrac{(1.732)(2.5 - 0.6) + 0.5}{2.732}$$

$$V_{IH} = \dfrac{(1.732)(1.9) + 0.5}{2.732} = \dfrac{3.2908 + 0.5}{2.732} = \dfrac{3.7908}{2.732} \approx 1.388\text{ V}$$

**(iii) Noise Margins:**

For this problem, $V_{IL}$ is computed from the standard derivation. Using the iterative result for $k_r = 3$:

$$V_{IL} \approx 0.74\text{ V}$$

$$NM_H = V_{DD} - V_{IH} = 2.5 - 1.388 = 1.112\text{ V}$$

$$NM_L = V_{IL} - 0 = 0.74\text{ V}$$

**Conclusion:** The asymmetric noise margins indicate that the inverter is biased toward the NMOS (faster LOW-to-HIGH transition). To balance them, the PMOS width should be increased.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** What is the significance of the **beta ratio** $k_r = k_n/k_p$ in CMOS inverter design? Explain how it affects the switching threshold and the symmetry of the VTC curve. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a CMOS inverter with $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.4\text{ V}$, and $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.5\text{ V}$ such that the switching threshold is exactly at $V_M = 0.9\text{ V}$. Calculate the required beta ratio and the corresponding $(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n$ ratio assuming $\mu_n/\mu_p = 2.5$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

The beta ratio $k_r = k_n/k_p$ is a **design parameter** that controls the relative drive strengths of the NMOS and PMOS transistors. It directly influences:

1. **Switching Threshold ($V_M$):** A larger $k_r$ (stronger NMOS) shifts $V_M$ **downward**; a smaller $k_r$ (stronger PMOS) shifts $V_M$ **upward**.

2. **VTC Symmetry:** If $k_r = 1$, the VTC is **anti-symmetric** about $(V_M, V_M)$, yielding $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ and equal noise margins. If $k_r \neq 1$, the curve becomes asymmetric, and the trip point skews toward one supply rail.

3. **Noise Margins:** The relationship $V_{IH} > V_M > V_{IL}$ holds always, but the *distance* of $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ from $V_M$ depends on $k_r$.

4. **Design Trade-off:** Designers typically size PMOS wider to compensate for the lower hole mobility ($\mu_p \approx \mu_n/2.5$), resulting in $k_r \approx 1$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$, $V_{Tn} = 0.4\text{ V}$, $\vert V_{Tp}\vert = 0.5\text{ V}$, $V_M = 0.9\text{ V}$, $\mu_n/\mu_p = 2.5$.

**Step 1 — Apply the $V_M$ formula and solve for $k_r$.**

$$V_M = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_r}\,V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$$

$$0.9 = \dfrac{\sqrt{k_r}\,(0.4) + (1.8 - 0.5)}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}} = \dfrac{0.4\sqrt{k_r} + 1.3}{1 + \sqrt{k_r}}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply.**

$$0.9(1 + \sqrt{k_r}) = 0.4\sqrt{k_r} + 1.3$$

$$0.9 + 0.9\sqrt{k_r} = 0.4\sqrt{k_r} + 1.3$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $\sqrt{k_r}$.**

$$0.9\sqrt{k_r} - 0.4\sqrt{k_r} = 1.3 - 0.9$$

$$0.5\sqrt{k_r} = 0.4$$

$$\sqrt{k_r} = 0.8$$

**Step 4 — Compute $k_r$.**

$$k_r = (0.8)^2 = 0.64$$

**Step 5 — Find $(W/L)_p / (W/L)_n$.**

Since $k_r = k_n/k_p$:

$$\dfrac{k_n}{k_p} = \dfrac{\mu_n(W/L)_n}{\mu_p(W/L)_p} = 0.64$$

$$\dfrac{(W/L)_p}{(W/L)_n} = \dfrac{\mu_n}{0.64 \cdot \mu_p} = \dfrac{2.5}{0.64} \approx 3.91$$

**Conclusion:** The PMOS must be approximately **3.91 times wider** than the NMOS to achieve $V_M = 0.9\text{ V}$ at $V_{DD} = 1.8\text{ V}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign error on $V_{Tp}$:** $V_{Tp}$ is **negative** for PMOS. Always use $\vert V_{Tp}\vert$ in formulas, or insert $V_{Tp}$ with its negative sign explicitly. Forgetting this yields an incorrect $V_M$.
> 2. **Forgetting to take the square root of $k_r$:** When equating currents and taking the square root, students often write $\sqrt{k_n} = \sqrt{k_p}$, which is wrong. The correct relation is $\sqrt{k_n}(V_M - V_{Tn}) = \sqrt{k_p}(V_{DD} - V_M - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)$.
> 3. **Ignoring mobility ratio:** In numerical problems, students sometimes assume $k_n = k_p$ without justification. If not stated otherwise, use $\mu_n/\mu_p = 2$ to $3$.
> 4. **Mixing up $V_{IL}$ and $V_{IH}$:** $V_{IL}$ is the **maximum** input that is still recognized as LOW. $V_{IH}$ is the **minimum** input that is still recognized as HIGH. Confusing these gives negative noise margins.
> 5. **Skipping the VTC region sketch:** KTU examiners reward explicit labeling of all five operating regions in part-(a) answers. A bare diagram without region identification loses 2–3 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **CMOS Inverter Topology:** PMOS pull-up (source at $V_{DD}$) + NMOS pull-down (source at GND), common gate input, common drain output.
- [x] **Five Operating Regions:** (R1) NMOS-OFF/PMOS-Triode, (R2) NMOS-Sat/PMOS-Triode, (R3) Both-Sat, (R4) NMOS-Triode/PMOS-Sat, (R5) NMOS-Triode/PMOS-OFF.
- [x] **Rail-to-Rail Swing:** $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0\text{ V}$ (ideal CMOS).
- [x] **Zero Static Power:** CMOS draws no DC current in steady state since one device is always OFF.
- [x] **Switching Threshold Formula:** $V_M = [\sqrt{k_r}\,V_{Tn} + (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)] / (1 + \sqrt{k_r})$.
- [x] **Symmetric Design:** $k_r = 1$ gives $V_M = V_{DD}/2$ and equal noise margins.
- [x] **Mobility Compensation:** $(W/L)_p \approx (\mu_n/\mu_p)(W/L)_n \approx 2\text{ to } 3 \times (W/L)_n$.
- [x] **Noise Margins:** $NM_H = V_{DD} - V_{IH}$, $NM_L = V_{IL} - 0$.
- [x] **Robust Design Goal:** Maximize both $NM_H$ and $NM_L$ for noise immunity; balance them for symmetric trip behavior.
- [x] **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic directly serves **CO3** (Analyze CMOS inverter characteristics) and **CO4** (Design VLSI circuits for specified DC performance).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# ⚡ MODULE 3: SWITCHING CHARACTERISTICS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 📘 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Switching Characteristics** of a MOSFET refer to the **dynamic (time-dependent) response** of the device and the CMOS logic gate when the input signal transitions between logic levels. They describe how fast the transistor can turn ON and turn OFF, governed by the charging and discharging of **intrinsic and parasitic capacitances** through the nonlinear channel resistance of the MOSFET.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCEVT503):** Switching characteristics cover the **transient behavior of MOSFETs**, the propagation of signals through a CMOS inverter, and the resulting **delay, rise/fall times, and dynamic power consumption** that determine the speed of digital VLSI circuits.

The switching characteristics of a MOSFET (and CMOS inverter) are quantified using the following time-domain parameters:

- **Rise Time ($t_r$)**: Time taken for the output to transition from **10% to 90%** of its final value.
- **Fall Time ($t_f$)**: Time taken for the output to transition from **90% to 10%** of its final value.
- **Propagation Delay ($t_p$)**: Time between the **50% point of the input transition** and the **50% point of the output transition**.
  - $t_{pLH}$ → Low-to-High output transition (PMOS charging)
  - $t_{pHL}$ → High-to-Low output transition (NMOS discharging)
- **Contamination Delay ($t_cd}$)**: The minimum time after which the output starts to change.
- **Propagation Delay Average ($t_p$)**: $\displaystyle t_p = \frac{t_{pLH} + t_{pHL}}{2}$

---

### 🌉 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Water Tap and Bucket System**
>
> Imagine a **water tap (MOSFET)** filling a **bucket (load capacitance $C_L$)**:
>
> - The tap cannot fill the bucket **instantly** because water must overcome the tap's **resistance ($R_{eq}$)** and the bucket's **inertia** (capacitance).
> - When you **open the tap (turn ON the transistor)**, water flows slowly, and the bucket level (output voltage) rises gradually. This is the **rise time / turn-on transient**.
> - When you **shut the tap (turn OFF)**, the bucket drains slowly through a small leak. This is the **fall time / turn-off transient**.
> - The **delay** is the time between you opening the tap and water actually starting to fill the bucket — caused by the **resistance-capacitance (RC) charging time**.
>
> In VLSI, the "tap resistance" is the **ON-resistance of the MOSFET ($R_{on}$)**, the "bucket" is the **load capacitance ($C_L$)**, and the "water level" is the **output voltage**. The product **$R_{on} \cdot C_L$** is the fundamental **delay unit** of every digital gate.

---

### 🎯 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Values Used in VLSI Switching Analysis:**
>
> - **Permittivity of free space:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14} \text{ F/cm}$
> - **Permittivity of SiO₂:** $\varepsilon_{ox} = 3.9 \times \varepsilon_0 = 3.45 \times 10^{-13} \text{ F/cm}$
> - **Gate oxide capacitance per unit area:** $\displaystyle C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}}$
> - **Load capacitance ($C_L$)**: Typically in the range of **fF to pF** for modern CMOS nodes.
> - **Operating voltage ($V_{DD}$)**: **0.7 V – 3.3 V** depending on technology node.
> - **Propagation delay ($t_p$)**: Ranges from **nanoseconds (older nodes)** to **picoseconds (modern FinFET nodes)**.
