A typical NAND-2 standard cell width is $W_{cell} = 14\lambda$ to $20\lambda$.

## 3.2 Derivation — Parasitic Capacitance from Layout Geometry

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** *A poly gate of width $W = 4\lambda$ and minimum channel length $L = 2\lambda$ has a $2\lambda$ extension over the source/drain diffusion in a $0.5\ \mu m$ process. The gate oxide is $t_{ox} = 10\ nm$. Compute the gate-overlap parasitic capacitance per side.*

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units.**

$$W = 4\lambda = 4 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 1.0\ \mu m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ m$$

$$X_{min} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 0.5\ \mu m = 0.5 \times 10^{-6}\ m$$

**Step 2 — Compute the gate oxide capacitance per unit area.**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0}{t_{ox}}$$

Using $\varepsilon_r = 3.9$ (SiO₂) and $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ F/m$:

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{10 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{1.0 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.453 \times 10^{-3}\ F/m^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute the overlap area per side.**

$$A_{ov} = W \cdot X_{min} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6} = 5.0 \times 10^{-13}\ m^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overlap capacitance per side.**

$$C_{ov,side} = C_{ox} \cdot A_{ov} = 3.453 \times 10^{-3} \times 5.0 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$C_{ov,side} = 1.727 \times 10^{-15}\ F = 1.727\ fF$$

**Step 5 — Compute the *total* overlap capacitance (both source and drain sides).**

$$C_{ov,total} = 2 \cdot C_{ov,side} = 2 \times 1.727 = 3.453\ fF$$

> **Insight for KTU:** Even in a single minimum-size transistor, the *layout-induced* gate overlap capacitance is on the order of femtofarads. In a million-transistor VLSI chip, these accumulate to nano- and pico-farad totals that dominate dynamic power dissipation:

$$P_{dynamic} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$$

This is the **direct link between layout rules and power consumption**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Each Rule on Circuit Behavior

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 3.1 — How a Violation of Each Rule Manifests in a Real Chip**

| Rule Violated | Fabrication Failure | Electrical Symptom | Detection Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimum poly width | Poly line breaks → open circuit | Floating gate, no drive | Continuity test (STUCK-AT) |
| Minimum poly spacing | Adjacent poly lines merge | Gate-to-gate short, always-ON transistor | I/O leakage test |
| Minimum contact enclosure | Metal does not fully cover contact cut | High contact resistance, intermittent open | Parametric yield test |
| Minimum n-diff / p-diff spacing | n-p junction breakdown | High leakage, latch-up | I_ddq test |
| Active-to-poly extension | Source/drain not formed under gate | Threshold voltage shift, weak drive | Ring-oscillator frequency |
| Minimum metal width | Electromigration opens line | Open interconnect over time | Burn-in test |

## 3.4 Derivation — Scaling of Lambda Rules Across Process Nodes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example:** *A $0.5\ \mu m$ process has $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$. If the foundry migrates to a $0.13\ \mu m$ process, what is the new $\lambda$? Express the rule "minimum poly width = $2\lambda$" in absolute microns for both processes.*

**Step 1 — Old process lambda.**

$$\lambda_{old} = \frac{L_{min,old}}{2} = \frac{0.5\ \mu m}{2} = 0.25\ \mu m$$

**Step 2 — New process lambda.**

$$\lambda_{new} = \frac{L_{min,new}}{2} = \frac{0.13\ \mu m}{2} = 0.065\ \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Old poly width.**

$$W_{poly,old} = 2\lambda_{old} = 2 \times 0.25 = 0.50\ \mu m$$

**Step 4 — New poly width.**

$$W_{poly,new} = 2\lambda_{new} = 2 \times 0.065 = 0.13\ \mu m$$

> **Conclusion:** The same $\lambda$-rule ($2\lambda$) yields a poly width that scales linearly with the process node. The *layout artwork remains unchanged* in $\lambda$ units, but the physical dimensions shrink by a factor of:

$$\text{Scaling factor} = \frac{\lambda_{new}}{\lambda_{old}} = \frac{0.065}{0.25} = 0.26 \approx \tfrac{1}{4}$$

This is the *portability* advantage of lambda-based design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Design Rule Verification

The design-rule check (DRC) is the automated enforcement engine. The following Mermaid block describes the relationship between the layout, the design rule database, and the verification tool flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Layout GDS2 File] --> B[Layout Extraction Tool]
    B --> C[Layer Recognition Engine]
    C --> D[Geometry Database]
    E[Design Rule Manual] --> F[Rule Compiler]
    F --> G[Compiled Rule Set]
    D --> H[DRC Engine]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Rule Check Pass}
    I -->|Yes| J[Layout Approved for Fabrication]
    I -->|No| K[Generate Violation Report]
    K --> L[Layout Designer]
    L --> M[Edit Layout]
    M --> A
    H --> N[Parasitic Extraction LPE]
    N --> O[Post-Layout Simulation]
    O --> P{Circuit Performance OK}
    P -->|Yes| J
    P -->|No| L
```

**Explanation of flow:**

1. The **GDS2 file** (industry-standard layout interchange format) is read by an extraction tool.
2. The **layer recognition engine** classifies each polygon into its mask layer (poly, metal, contact, etc.).
3. The **geometry database** is built (a list of vertices and edges per layer).
4. The **DRC engine** compares every pair of polygons on the same and different layers against the **compiled rule set**.
5. If a rule is violated, a violation report is generated and the loop continues.
6. If all rules pass, the layout undergoes **parasitic extraction (LPE)** and **post-layout simulation** to verify electrical performance.

## 4.2 Schematic of a Standard-Cell Layout (NAND-2 CMOS)

The following diagram shows a stylized **layout view** of a 2-input CMOS NAND standard cell, annotated with the design rules.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph VDDRail[VDD Power Rail - Top]
        VDD[VDD Metal1 Horizontal]
    end
    subgraph PMOSBlock[PMOS Pull-up Network]
        P1[P-diff Active Region]
        P2[P-diff Active Region]
        GP1[Poly Gate 1]
        GP2[Poly Gate 2]
    end
    subgraph NMOSBlock[NMOS Pull-down Network]
        N1[N-diff Active Region]
        N2[N-diff Active Region]
        GN1[Poly Gate 1]
        GN2[Poly Gate 2]
    end
    subgraph GNDRail[GND Power Rail - Bottom]
        GND[GND Metal1 Horizontal]
    end
    C1[Contact Cut to VDD]
    C2[Contact Cut to Output]
    C3[Contact Cut to GND]
    C4[Contact Cut Input A]
    C5[Contact Cut Input B]
    VDD --> C1
    P1 --> C1
    P2 --> C2
    N1 --> C2
    N2 --> C3
    GND --> C3
    GP1 --> C4
    GN1 --> C4
    GP2 --> C5
    GN2 --> C5
```

**Reading the schematic:**

- Two p-diff regions form the **PMOS pair** (parallel). They share a common **VDD** rail at the top.
- Two n-diff regions form the **NMOS pair** (series). They share a common **GND** rail at the bottom.
- The **poly gates** (red vertical lines) extend continuously from the p-diff down through the n-diff, providing the gate connections for both PMOS and NMOS at the same input.
- The **output node** is the metal line connecting the *inner* p-diff and the *inner* n-diff drain regions through contact cuts.

## 4.3 Design Rule Categories — Mermaid Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Layout Design Rules] --> B[Width Rules]
    A --> C[Spacing Rules]
    A --> D[Overlap Rules]
    A --> E[Extension Rules]
    A --> F[Area Rules]
    B --> B1[Min Poly Width 2 lambda]
    B --> B2[Min Metal Width 3 lambda]
    C --> C1[Poly to Poly 2 lambda]
    C --> C2[Metal to Metal 3 lambda]
    C --> C3[Ndiff to Pdiff 4 lambda]
    D --> D1[Contact Enclosure 1 lambda]
    D --> D2[Via Enclosure 1 lambda]
    E --> E1[Active Extension under Poly 2 lambda]
    F --> F1[Contact Area 4 lambda squared]
```

## 4.4 Lambda Rule Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Node] -->|L_min in micrometers| B[Lambda parameter]
    B -->|lambda equals L min over 2| C[Universal Set of Geometric Rules]
    C --> D[Width Rules]
    C --> E[Spacing Rules]
    C --> F[Overlap Rules]
    C --> G[Extension Rules]
    D --> H[Layout dimensions in lambda units]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H -->|Convert to microns| I[Process Specific Layout]
    I --> J[DRC Verification]
    J -->|Pass| K[Fabrication Mask Set]
    J -->|Fail| L[Edit and Recheck]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define layout design rules. Why are they necessary in VLSI design?**

**Model Answer:**

Layout design rules are a set of geometric constraints that specify the minimum allowable dimensions, spacings, overlaps, and extensions of features on each mask layer of an integrated circuit. They serve as a **communication interface** between the circuit designer and the fabrication process engineer.

They are necessary because:

1. **Process variability:** Photolithography, etching, and dopant diffusion are statistical processes with finite resolution. Rules guarantee robustness against worst-case variation.
2. **Yield maximization:** A layout that violates rules produces low-quality or non-functional chips, lowering the foundry's yield and raising cost.
3. **Design portability:** Lambda-based rules allow the same layout artwork to be migrated to a new process node by simply rescaling $\lambda$.
4. **DRC automation:** They enable automated verification using tools like Cadence Diva, Synopsys IC Validator, and Mentor Calibre.

> **Mark Distribution:** Definition (1.5 marks), Any two reasons (0.75 + 0.75 marks).

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Differentiate between $\lambda$-based and micron-based design rules. Which is more common in modern foundries and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | $\lambda$-based | Micron-based |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of expression** | Multiples of $\lambda$ (e.g., $2\lambda$, $3\lambda$) | Absolute micrometers (e.g., $0.18\ \mu m$) |
| **Portability** | High — same artwork works for any process by rescaling $\lambda$ | Low — must be redrawn for each new process |
| **Precision** | Less precise (rounded to integer $\lambda$ multiples) | Highly precise, matches actual process limits |
| **Used by** | Academic layouts, educational kits (e.g., MOSIS) | Modern commercial foundries (TSMC, Intel, Samsung, GlobalFoundries) |
| **Computation** | Simple — multiply $\lambda$ | Direct — no scaling needed |

**Micron-based rules are more common in modern foundries** because they precisely track the actual process limits (e.g., minimum poly width = $0.18\ \mu m$ exactly, not $3\lambda = 0.195\ \mu m$ in a $\lambda = 0.065\ \mu m$ node). Modern processes are highly non-uniform in their layer-specific limits, and absolute rules provide the granularity needed.

> **Mark Distribution:** Table differences (2 marks), Justification of micron rules (1 mark).

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

### Question A: Design Rule Application to CMOS Inverter Layout

**(a)** *With the help of a clear diagram, explain the $\lambda$-based layout of a CMOS inverter. List the standard $\lambda$-rules for the following: minimum poly width, poly-to-poly spacing, n-diff to p-diff spacing, contact cut size, contact enclosure, and active extension under polysilicon. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For a $0.5\ \mu m$ CMOS process with $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$, compute the layout area of a CMOS inverter standard cell whose poly gate is $W = 8\lambda$ wide and the active regions are sized as: n-diff width $= 6\lambda$, p-diff width $= 12\lambda$ (for matched drive strength), n-diff to p-diff spacing $= 4\lambda$, poly-to-poly spacing $= 2\lambda$. Include power-rail margin of $2\lambda$ on top and bottom. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Stick diagram to layout translation of a CMOS inverter:**

The CMOS inverter consists of one PMOS (top) and one NMOS (bottom) sharing a common gate poly line and a common output node.

**Step 1 — Active region definition.**  
A single p-diff region sits in the upper half of the cell; a single n-diff region sits in the lower half. They are separated horizontally by a minimum spacing of $4\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Poly gate definition.**  
A vertical poly line crosses both active regions. The poly width is $2\lambda$. The poly extends $2\lambda$ beyond each side of the active region to satisfy the active-extension rule.

**Step 3 — Contacts and metal.**  
- A VDD contact sits at the top of the p-diff (surrounded by metal-1).
- A GND contact sits at the bottom of the n-diff.
- An OUTPUT contact sits at the right edge, shared between the inner p-diff and n-diff.
- INPUT is the poly line itself (no contact needed if accessible from above, or with a poly contact if routed horizontally).

**Standard $\lambda$-Rules (SCMOS Submicron Rules):**

| Rule | Value |
|---|---|
| Minimum poly width | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum poly-to-poly spacing | $2\lambda$ |
| Minimum n-diff to p-diff spacing | $4\lambda$ |
| Contact cut size | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ |
| Contact cut metal-1 enclosure | $1\lambda$ |
| Contact cut active enclosure | $1\lambda$ |
| Active extension under poly | $2\lambda$ |

#### Model Solution for (b)

**Given:** $\lambda = 0.25\ \mu m$, $W_{poly} = 8\lambda$, $W_{n\text{-}diff} = 6\lambda$, $W_{p\text{-}diff} = 12\lambda$, $S_{n\text{-}p} = 4\lambda$, $S_{poly} = 2\lambda$, top/bottom rail margin $= 2\lambda$ each.

**Step 1 — Cell height (vertical direction).**

The cell height is determined by the n-diff width, p-diff width, and the spacing between them:

$$H_{cell} = W_{p\text{-}diff} + S_{n\text{-}p} + W_{n\text{-}diff} + \text{top margin} + \text{bottom margin}$$

$$H_{cell} = 12\lambda + 4\lambda + 6\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 26\lambda$$

Converting to microns:

$$H_{cell} = 26 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 6.5\ \mu m$$

**Step 2 — Cell width (horizontal direction).**

The cell width is determined by the poly gate width plus the extensions and side margins:

$$W_{cell} = \text{left margin} + \text{left poly extension} + W_{poly} + \text{right poly extension} + \text{right margin}$$

Taking standard left/right margin of $2\lambda$ and poly-extension of $2\lambda$ beyond the active region on each side:

$$W_{cell} = 2\lambda + 2\lambda + 8\lambda + 2\lambda + 2\lambda = 16\lambda$$

Converting to microns:

$$W_{cell} = 16 \times 0.25\ \mu m = 4.0\ \mu m$$

**Step 3 — Cell area.**

$$A_{cell} = W_{cell} \times H_{cell} = 4.0\ \mu m \times 6.5\ \mu m = 26.0\ \mu m^2$$

In $\lambda^2$ units (for the alternative answer):

$$A_{cell} = 16\lambda \times 26\lambda = 416\lambda^2$$

> **Mark Distribution for (b):** Cell height derivation (2.5 marks), Cell width derivation (2.5 marks), Area calculation (2 marks).

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

### Question B: Stick Diagram to Layout Translation with Parasitics

**(a)** *Draw the stick diagram of a CMOS 2-input NAND gate. Identify every mask layer used and justify the choice of each. Convert the stick diagram into a $\lambda$-based layout using the following rules: minimum poly width $= 2\lambda$, poly-to-poly spacing $= 2\lambda$, n-diff to p-diff spacing $= 4\lambda$, contact size $= 2\lambda \times 2\lambda$, contact metal enclosure $= 1\lambda$, contact active enclosure $= 1\lambda$. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *For the NAND gate, the gate oxide thickness is $t_{ox} = 9\ nm$, the supply voltage is $V_{DD} = 3.3\ V$, and the channel length is $L = 2\lambda$ where $\lambda = 0.5\ \mu m$. Each NMOS has $W = 6\lambda$ and each PMOS has $W = 6\lambda$. The poly extension over the source/drain is $X_{min} = 2\lambda$. Compute:*
- *(i) The channel resistance of a single NMOS in saturation if $\mu_n C_{ox} = 50\ \mu A/V^2$ and $V_{GS} - V_{th} = 1.0\ V$. (3.5 marks)*
- *(ii) The total gate overlap capacitance on both the source and drain sides. (3.5 marks)*

#### Model Solution for (a)

**Stick diagram of CMOS NAND-2:**

The NAND-2 has:
- **Two PMOS in parallel** (pull-up network)
- **Two NMOS in series** (pull-down network)

**Mask layer identification:**

| Stick Color | Mask Layer | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Green horizontal | n-diff | Forms source/drain of the two series NMOS |
| Yellow/Brown horizontal | p-diff | Forms source/drain of the two parallel PMOS |
| Red vertical | Polysilicon | Forms the two gate electrodes (inputs A and B) |
| Black square | Contact cut | Connects metal-1 to poly, n-diff, or p-diff |
| Blue horizontal | Metal-1 | Connects VDD rail, GND rail, output node, and the middle NMOS-to-PMOS drain connection |

**Layout conversion rules applied:**

- Poly lines for A and B are spaced $\geq 2\lambda$ apart.
- The two p-diff regions are separated by $\geq 4\lambda$ from the n-diff.
- Each contact cut is $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$, with $1\lambda$ metal-1 and $1\lambda$ active enclosure on all sides.
- The VDD rail sits across the top of the p-diff; the GND rail sits across the bottom of the n-diff.

#### Model Solution for (b)(i)

**Step 1 — Convert dimensions to SI.**

$$W = 6\lambda = 6 \times 0.5\ \mu m = 3.0\ \mu m = 3.0 \times 10^{-6}\ m$$

$$L = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.5\ \mu m = 1.0\ \mu m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ m$$

**Step 2 — Apply channel resistance formula in saturation.**

$$R_{channel} = \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})}$$

$$R_{channel} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} \times 1.0}$$

$$R_{channel} = \frac{1}{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.0 \times 1.0}$$

$$R_{channel} = \frac{1}{150 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R_{channel} = \frac{1}{1.5 \times 10^{-4}} = 6666.67\ \Omega \approx 6.67\ k\Omega$$

#### Model Solution for (b)(ii)

**Step 1 — Compute $C_{ox}$.**

$$C_{ox} = \frac{\varepsilon_{ox}}{t_{ox}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}{9 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$C_{ox} = \frac{3.453 \times 10^{-11}}{9.0 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.837 \times 10^{-3}\ F/m^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute overlap area per side.**

$$X_{min} = 2\lambda = 2 \times 0.5\ \mu m = 1.0\ \mu m = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ m$$

$$A_{ov,side} = W \cdot X_{min} = 3.0 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-6} = 3.0 \times 10^{-12}\ m^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute overlap capacitance per side.**

$$C_{ov,side} = C_{ox} \cdot A_{ov,side} = 3.837 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.0 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$C_{ov,side} = 1.151 \times 10^{-14}\ F = 11.51\ fF$$

**Step 4 — Compute total overlap capacitance (source + drain).**

$$C_{ov,total} = 2 \times C_{ov,side} = 2 \times 11.51 = 23.02\ fF$$

> **Mark Distribution for (b):** Part (i) 3.5 marks (correct formula 1.5, substitution 1, final answer 1); Part (ii) 3.5 marks ($C_{ox}$ 1 mark, area 1, per-side cap 1, total cap 0.5).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing units:** Students often substitute $\lambda$ directly into a formula that expects microns. Always convert $\lambda$ to micrometers or meters *first*, then plug in.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 for total overlap:** The poly overlaps the source *and* the drain. The exam frequently asks for the *total* — students write only the per-side value and lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing $W$ and $L$ in the $R_{channel}$ formula:** The aspect ratio is $W/L$, *not* $L/W$. Drawing the transistor cross-section correctly on the answer sheet earns an extra mark.
> 4. **Omitting enclosure rules in layout diagrams:** A layout diagram missing the $1\lambda$ metal enclosure around a contact is technically wrong — the contact would not be reliably manufacturable. Draw a $4\lambda \times 4\lambda$ metal square around a $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ contact cut.
> 5. **Calling the metal layer "Aluminium" in an exam:** It is now typically **copper** in sub-$0.25\ \mu m$ processes. Use "metal-1" or "first-level interconnect" in the answer.
> 6. **Neglecting to identify stick-diagram colors:** Examiners award marks for the *colour code* of each layer. Always write the color (green for n-diff, red for poly, blue for metal-1, black for contacts).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day**

- ☐ **Definition:** Layout design rules are geometric constraints ensuring manufacturability, yield, and reliability of VLSI chips.
- ☐ **Two schemes:** Lambda-based (portable, integer multiples) vs. micron-based (process-specific, modern foundries use this).
- ☐ **Five rule types:** Width, Spacing, Overlap/Enclosure, Extension, Area.
- ☐ **Channel length relation:** $L_{min} = 2\lambda$ — this defines the lambda for any process.
- ☐ **Conversion:** $d_{abs}(\mu m) = N_{\lambda} \cdot \lambda$.
- ☐ **Standard $\lambda$-rules to memorize:** poly width = $2\lambda$, poly spacing = $2\lambda$, n-diff to p-diff spacing = $4\lambda$, contact size = $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$, contact enclosure = $1\lambda$, active extension = $2\lambda$.
- ☐ **Color codes:** green = n-diff, yellow = p-diff, red = poly, blue = metal-1, magenta = metal-2, black = contact/via.
- ☐ **Parasitic formulas:** $C_{ov} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot X_{min}$ and $C_{diff} = C_j A + C_{jsw} P$ and $R_{metal} = R_{\square} \cdot L_m / W_m$.
- ☐ **Channel resistance:** $R_{ch} = 1 / \left( \mu_n C_{ox} \tfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th}) \right)$ in saturation.
- ☐ **Standard cell height formula:** $H_{cell} = W_{p\text{-}diff} + S_{n\text{-}p} + W_{n\text{-}diff} + \text{rail margins}$.
- ☐ **Scaling:** Halving $\lambda$ halves every dimension — same artwork, new process.
- ☐ **Failure modes:** Width violation → open; spacing violation → short; enclosure violation → high R; area violation → contact failure.
- ☐ **DRC tools:** Cadence Diva / Assura, Synopsys IC Validator, Mentor Calibre — they enforce rules before fabrication.
- ☐ **Why micron rules dominate today:** Modern processes have non-uniform layer-specific limits that integer $\lambda$ rules cannot capture.
- ☐ **Link to power:** $P_{dynamic} = \alpha C V^2 f$ — layout-driven $C$ directly determines chip power consumption.
- ☐ **Link to timing:** Wider metal → lower $R$ but higher $C$ → layout is an RC trade-off optimization.
- ☐ **Stick diagram ↔ layout:** Stick diagrams are *non-scaled*; layouts are *scaled* to $\lambda$ and checked by DRC.
- ☐ **Body ties:** Every standard cell needs an n-diff to GND (NMOS body) and a p-diff to VDD (PMOS body) tie to prevent latch-up — this is enforced by the n-diff to p-diff spacing rule and the body-contact enclosure rule.
- ☐ **Substrate/well contacts:** Must be at least every $20\lambda$ to $50\lambda$ along the cell row to maintain the body potential.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stick Diagram and Layout of CMOS Inverter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Stick Diagram and Layout of CMOS Inverter ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Stick Diagram** is an abstract, symbolic, two-dimensional representation of a VLSI CMOS circuit layout in which different mask layers of a CMOS process are denoted by **monochromatic lines (sticks)** of standardized **color codes**. It captures relative geometric placement of transistors and interconnects *without* exact dimensions, allowing rapid topological verification of circuit-to-layout mapping. A **Layout (Mask Layout / Physical Design)** is the precise, fully-dimensioned geometric representation of every mask layer in a CMOS process, drawn to scale using **design rules (λ-based or micron-based)**. It is the final artwork that goes for photomask fabrication. For a **CMOS Inverter**, the stick diagram and layout together define the geometry of: - The **nMOS pull-down** transistor (in p-substrate or p-well) - The **pMOS pull-up** transistor (in n-well) - The shared **polysilicon gate** driven by the input signal **A** - The **metal interconnect** tying both drains together to form output **Y** - The **VDD** rail (top, pMOS source) and **VSS** rail (bottom, nMOS source) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Stick diagrams and layout design of basic CMOS gates (NOT, NAND, NOR) using **Euler's rule** and **λ-based design rules** form the most repeated 14-mark question in KTU VLSI ESE. Mastering the CMOS inverter is the foundation for every subsequent gate. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Blueprint Painter's Toolkit" Imagine you are an **architect sketching a two-story house** on a single sheet of tracing paper: - **Stick Diagram = Rough Sketch** : You use **colored pencils** — *red* for bricks, *blue* for windows, *green* for plumbing, *yellow* for electrical wiring. You don't worry yet about exact brick thickness or window width; you just ensure pipes reach the kitchen and wires reach the switches. Any topological error (a wire crossing a pipe) is caught immediately. - **Layout = Engineer's Final Blueprint** : The same architect now draws to *exact scale* — every wall is 230 mm thick, every door is 900 mm wide — so the contractor can build it. Mistakes here cost money. In VLSI: - **Stick Diagram** uses the *color code* (red/green/blue/black/yellow) to abstractly represent mask layers. - **Layout** uses *precise λ-multiples* (e.g., 2λ, 3λ) to define physical dimensions. ### 1.3 Standard Color Code for Stick Diagrams (Must Memorize for KTU) | Mask Layer (Formal Name) | Stick Diagram Color | Stick Diagram Symbol | |---|---|---| | n-type diffusion (n-diff) / n+ | **Green** | Solid green line | | p-type diffusion (p-diff) / p+ | **Yellow / Orange** | Solid yellow line | | Polysilicon (Poly / Gate) | **Red** | Solid red line (drawn over diffusion to form transistor) | | Metal 1 (Interconnect) | **Blue** | Solid blue line | | Contact (Cut / Via) | **Black** | Solid black square `■` | | p-substrate / bulk | **(implicit, no stick)** | — | | n-well | **Brown dashed boundary** | Dashed outline around pMOS | > [!NOTE] > **Mnemonic Trick for KTU** : "**R**ed **G**ate, **G**reen **n**, **Y**ellow **p**, **B**lue **M**etal, **B**lack **C**ut." → **R-G-Y-B-B** → *"Raja-Gopi-Yellam-Bahubali-Bomma"* (a fun Kerala-flavored mnemonic!). Every KTU ESE paper expects you to write the color code in Module 3 questions. ### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Minimum feature size (λ, lambda)** : the smallest resolvable dimension in a process. For a 0.5 µm process, **λ = 0.25 µm** ; for 0.18 µm process, **λ = 0.09 µm**. - **Channel length (L)** : always **≥ 2λ** (in classical λ-rules). - **Inverter β-ratio (Z_p / Z_n)** : typically **2 to 3** (i.e., pMOS is 2×–3× wider than nMOS to match rise/fall times). - **Minimum contact size** : **2λ × 2λ**. - **Poly-to-diffusion spacing** : **1λ** minimum. - **Diffusion-to-diffusion spacing (same type)** : **3λ** minimum. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why We Need Stick Diagrams *Before* Layout A VLSI designer must verify three things early in the design flow: 1. **Logical Connectivity** : Are the right terminals connected? (poly gate to input, drains tied to output). 2. **No topological short-circuits** : Is metal accidentally crossing poly without a contact? 3. **Wells and diffusions are correctly typed** : Is the pMOS truly in n-well with p+ source/drain? Stick diagrams answer all three *without* doing tedious full-mask layout, saving **~70% of design time** in the conceptual phase (a KTU viva favorite). ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Building a CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram The CMOS Inverter consists of: - **One nMOS** (pull-down) with source at **VSS**, drain at **Y**, gate at **A**. - **One pMOS** (pull-up) with source at **VDD**, drain at **Y**, gate at **A**. Logical to physical mapping: | Net | Layer (Stick) | Color | Reasoning | |---|---|---|---| | Input **A** | Polysilicon gate (continuous, over both diffusions) | **Red** | Gates of nMOS and pMOS must be tied → single red poly stripe | | nMOS source → VSS | n+ diffusion (green) + contact (black) → metal (blue) | Green + Black + Blue | nMOS source is grounded through metal | | pMOS source → VDD | p+ diffusion (yellow) + contact → metal | Yellow + Black + Blue | pMOS source tied to VDD | | nMOS drain & pMOS drain → **Y** | Both diffusions + contact + metal | Green + Yellow + Black + Blue | Drains are tied together as the output node | | nMOS body | p-substrate (implicit) | — | Connects to VSS (taps) | | pMOS body | n-well | Brown dashed | Must enclose p+ regions, connects to VDD | ### 2.3 Euler's Rule (THE KTU Must-Know Theorem) A stick diagram is a **planar graph**. For any connected planar graph: $$E - V + N = 1$$ where: - **E** = number of **edges** (sticks / line segments), - **V** = number of **vertices** (junctions / dots), - **N** = number of connected components (for a single stick diagram, **N = 1**). > [!NOTE] > **Extended Statement** : For a stick diagram with **F** faces (bounded regions, e.g., source, drain, channel, well taps), Euler's rule becomes: > $$E - V + F = 1 - C$$ > where **C** is the number of connected components. For most stick diagrams of single gates, **C = 1**, so **E − V + F = 0** (i.e., **E − V = −F**). KTU students often confuse these two forms; remember the version with **N = 1** is the one used in Module 3. **Application in KTU** : Given number of sticks (transistors, connections), we can count minimum contacts/junctions required. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready Table) | # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units | KTU Marks Weight | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Euler's rule (planar graph) | $E - V + N = 1$ | dimensionless | **2 marks** (frequent in 3-mark Q) | | 2 | Channel length (nMOS/pMOS) | $L = 2\lambda$ | length | **1 mark** | | 3 | nMOS width for symmetric inverter | $W_n = 2\lambda$ | length | **2 marks** | | 4 | pMOS width for symmetric inverter | $W_p = (2 \text{ to } 3)\,W_n$ | length | **2 marks** | | 5 | Beta ratio | $\beta_r = \dfrac{Z_{p}/L_{p}}{Z_{n}/L_{n}}$ | dimensionless | **2 marks** | | 6 | Minimum contact size | $2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ | length² | **1 mark** | | 7 | Poly to n+ / p+ spacing | $\geq 1\lambda$ | length | **1 mark** | | 8 | n+ to n+ spacing (same net) | $\geq 3\lambda$ | length | **1 mark** | | 9 | Metal width (min) | $3\lambda$ | length | **1 mark** | | 10 | Polysilicon width over channel (gate) | $2\lambda$ | length | **1 mark** | | 11 | Number of masks for CMOS n-well process | **n-well, p+ (pMOS), n+ (nMOS), poly, contact, metal1, n-active, p-active** = **8–10 masks** | count | **2 marks** | | 12 | λ-scaling relationship | $F \propto \lambda^2$ (area scales with λ²) | area | **1 mark** | > **CRITICAL EXAM TIP** : KTU frequently gives a 3-mark question like *"For a stick diagram with E = 12 and V = 9, verify if it satisfies Euler's rule."* The answer is **12 − 9 + N = 1 → N = −2**, which is invalid (cannot be negative); hence the diagram is **not topologically realizable** as drawn. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - Stick diagrams are used in **academic coursework and early-stage floorplanning** to verify circuit topology *before* full-custom layout (which takes days to weeks for a single block). - The **λ-based design rules** used in stick diagrams were originally developed by **Mead and Conway (1980)** and remain the *universal language* of teaching VLSI design worldwide. - Modern industry (Intel, TSMC, Samsung) uses **micron-based or nanometer-based design rules** at advanced nodes (3 nm, 5 nm), but the **topology philosophy** (color-coded abstraction, Euler verification) is still embedded in tools like Cadence Virtuoso and Synopsys Custom Compiler. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: CMOS Inverter Component-by-Component | Step | What is drawn | Layer | Color | Coordinates (relative) | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | nMOS active region (n+) | n-diffusion | Green | Horizontal green stick, length ≈ 6λ (source + channel + drain) | | 2 | pMOS active region (p+) inside n-well | p-diffusion | Yellow | Horizontal yellow stick, parallel to and above the green stick | | 3 | Polysilicon gate (input A) crossing both diffusions | Polysilicon | Red | Vertical red line (2λ wide) crossing both sticks, length spans 2λ above and below | | 4 | nMOS source → VSS contact | Contact + Metal | Black square on green + blue line going down | Blue line down to bottom rail | | 5 | pMOS source → VDD contact | Contact + Metal | Black square on yellow + blue line going up | Blue line up to top rail | | 6 | Both drains connected together → output Y | Metal | Blue | Horizontal blue line between drain contacts of nMOS and pMOS | | 7 | n-well enclosing pMOS region | n-well | Brown dashed | Dashed rectangle around pMOS | | 8 | Substrate contact (p+) for body of nMOS | p+ diffusion + contact | Yellow + Black | Near VSS rail | ### 3.2 Detailed Derivation — Stick Diagram of CMOS Inverter We start with the circuit schematic and translate it to a stick diagram in 8 steps. **Step 1: Identify nets and assign layers** For the CMOS inverter: - Input **A** → Polysilicon (red). - Output **Y** → Metal (blue) connecting both drains. - VDD → Metal (blue) rail. - VSS → Metal (blue) rail. **Step 2: Draw the nMOS active region** A green stick represents the **n+ diffusion** for the nMOS transistor. It will host source (S_n), channel (under poly gate), and drain (D_n). **Step 3: Draw the pMOS active region inside an n-well** A yellow stick represents the **p+ diffusion** for the pMOS transistor. It must be fully enclosed in a brown-dashed **n-well** to isolate it from the p-substrate. **Step 4: Draw the polysilicon gate (red)** A vertical red stick crosses **both** the green and yellow diffusions. The two transistors share this single gate (input **A**). The crossing of poly-over-diffusion *automatically creates* a transistor (channel is the diffusion region under the poly). **Step 5: Place contacts (black squares)** Contacts are needed at every metal-to-silicon (or metal-to-poly) junction: - **C1** : VSS metal → nMOS source (n+). - **C2** : VDD metal → pMOS source (p+). - **C3** : Output metal Y → nMOS drain. - **C4** : Output metal Y → pMOS drain. - **C5** (optional) : Input A from metal to poly (if A is brought in on metal). **Step 6: Wire the rails** - The bottom blue line is **VSS** (ground). - The top blue line is **VDD**. - The middle blue line is the output **Y**. **Step 7: Add well/substrate taps** A **substrate contact** (p+ in p-substrate) tied to VSS prevents latch-up. An **n-well contact** (n+ inside n-well) tied to VDD prevents latch-up. These are mandatory in real layouts and are often asked in KTU viva. **Step 8: Verify with Euler's rule** Count edges (E) and vertices (V): For the canonical CMOS inverter stick diagram: - **E = 12** (3 vertical segments of metal, 2 diffusions, 1 poly, 2 drain connectors, 2 source connectors, 2 supply connectors = 12; textbook figure may give **E = 11** depending on counting convention). - **V = 9** (4 contacts + 2 transistor cross points + 3 supply rail joints = 9). - **N = 1**. Check: $E - V + N = 12 - 9 + 1 = 4$ (face count F = 4) → **Satisfies Euler's rule with 4 bounded regions** (source, channel, drain, output area). > **Note on KTU Marking** : If the count comes out inconsistent, students lose 1 mark. **Always recount contacts as vertices** but **NOT transistors** (transistors are crossings, sometimes counted, sometimes not — follow your module textbook's convention). ### 3.3 Detailed Derivation — Layout of CMOS Inverter (Lambda-Based Rules) The **layout** converts the stick diagram into a fully-dimensioned drawing where every edge has a width and every spacing is a multiple of **λ**. **Step 1: Define the wells and active regions** - Draw the **n-well** boundary as a brown rectangle. The pMOS will be inside. - Draw the **p-substrate** region (rest of chip). The nMOS will be inside. - For symmetric rise/fall drive strength, take: $$\frac{W_p}{W_n} = 2 \quad \text{(minimum recommended for equal noise margins)}$$ - Choose $W_n = 2\lambda$ and $W_p = 4\lambda$ (so pMOS is 2× wider than nMOS). **Step 2: Draw the active area (n+ and p+)** - The **n+ active region** is a green rectangle of size $W_n \times L_n = 2\lambda \times 2\lambda$ for the channel, plus source/drain extensions. - The **p+ active region** is a yellow rectangle of size $W_p \times L_p = 4\lambda \times 2\lambda$. - Minimum spacing between **n+ and p+ active areas** = **3λ** (to prevent punch-through). **Step 3: Draw the polysilicon gate** - A **red rectangle** of width **2λ** (the gate length) crosses both active regions. - Polysilicon must extend at least **2λ** beyond the active area on each side (so it can serve as a routing stub). - The poly gate must be at least **1λ** away from any other poly or active region on its parallel sides. **Step 4: Place contacts (cut masks)** - Each contact is a **2λ × 2λ** black square, surrounded by **1λ** of silicon/metal overlap on all sides. - Place contacts at: - nMOS source (to VSS metal). - nMOS drain (to Y metal). - pMOS source (to VDD metal). - pMOS drain (to Y metal). - Substrate tap and n-well tap (for latch-up immunity). **Step 5: Draw the metal interconnects (blue)** - Metal-1 minimum width = **3λ**. - Metal-1 minimum spacing = **3λ**. - **Y** output runs horizontally between the two drain contacts. - **VDD** runs horizontally at the top. - **VSS** runs horizontally at the bottom. **Step 6: Apply design-rule checks (DRC)** Verify every spacing and width against the λ-rule table. A canonical λ-rule checklist for CMOS inverter: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Channel length: } & L = 2\lambda \\ \text{nMOS width: } & W_n = 2\lambda \\ \text{pMOS width: } & W_p = 4\lambda \\ \text{Poly width: } & 2\lambda \\ \text{Poly to active spacing: } & \geq 1\lambda \\ \text{Contact size: } & 2\lambda \times 2\lambda \\ \text{Contact enclosure (silicon): } & \geq 1\lambda \\ \text{Metal width: } & \geq 3\lambda \\ \text{Metal spacing: } & \geq 3\lambda \\ \text{n+ to n+ spacing: } & \geq 3\lambda \\ \text{p+ to p+ spacing: } & \geq 3\lambda \\ \text{n+ to p+ spacing: } & \geq 2\lambda \\ \text{n-well to n+ spacing: } & \geq 3\lambda \\ \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7: Compute area (KTU 3-mark favorite)** For a typical CMOS inverter layout with the dimensions above, the bounding-box area scales as: $$A_{\text{inverter}} \approx (W_p + W_n + 2 \times 3\lambda) \times (L + 6\lambda) \quad [\lambda^2]$$ Substituting $W_p = 4\lambda$, $W_n = 2\lambda$, $L = 2\lambda$: $$\begin{aligned} A_{\text{inverter}} &\approx (4\lambda + 2\lambda + 6\lambda) \times (2\lambda + 6\lambda) \\ &= (12\lambda) \times (8\lambda) \\ &= 96\lambda^2 \end{aligned}$$ This is a **standard KTU answer** for "estimate the area of CMOS inverter layout in λ²." **Step 8: Mask count verification** For an n-well CMOS process, the number of mask layers required is: | Mask # | Layer | Purpose | |---|---|---| | 1 | n-well | Defines pMOS body region | | 2 | Active (n+ / p+) | Defines source/drain implants | | 3 | Polysilicon | Gate | | 4 | n+ select | nMOS source/drain implant | | 5 | p+ select | pMOS source/drain implant | | 6 | Contact (cut) | Opens contacts | | 7 | Metal-1 | Interconnect | | 8 | Pad / Passivation | Bonding pads | **Total = 8 masks minimum** (advanced processes may add Metal-2, Via, etc., taking it to 10–12). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram (Color-Coded Topology) The stick diagram below shows the canonical CMOS inverter with all layers. (Note: Mermaid does not natively support color-coded sticks like a real VLSI textbook, so the diagram below is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** showing the connectivity and layer assignments; in a written KTU exam, the student should draw the color-coded version by hand.) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph nwell[Brown Dashed n-well Boundary] pmos["YELLOW p+ diffusion strip
pMOS Active Region"] polysilicon["RED Polysilicon Gate
Input A (vertical stripe)"] pmosSource["YELLOW p+ Source contact
BLACK square"] pmosDrain["YELLOW p+ Drain contact
BLACK square"] end subgraph psub[p-substrate] nmos["GREEN n+ diffusion strip
nMOS Active Region"] nmosSource["GREEN n+ Source contact
BLACK square"] nmosDrain["GREEN n+ Drain contact
BLACK square"] end vdd["BLUE Metal rail VDD"] vss["BLUE Metal rail VSS"] ymetal["BLUE Metal output Y"] inputA["BLUE Metal input A"] vdd --- pmosSource pmosSource --- pmos pmos --- pmosDrain pmosDrain --- ymetal ymetal --- nmosDrain nmosDrain --- nmos nmos --- nmosSource nmosSource --- vss polysilicon -.crosses.-> pmos polysilicon -.crosses.-> nmos inputA --- polysilicon ``` **How to read this Mermaid block in the KTU context** : - **Solid lines (---)** represent **same-layer connections** (e.g., metal-to-metal or diffusion continuity). - **Dotted crossing lines (-.crosses.->)** represent **transistor formation points** where poly crosses diffusion. - Each node is **labeled with the COLOR + LAYER + FUNCTION** in plain uppercase text (no bold, no italics, no markdown inside labels — Mermaid-safe). - In the actual KTU answer script, the student should reproduce this as a **hand-drawn color-coded stick figure** using a ballpoint pen and refer to the color code explicitly. ### 4.2 CMOS Inverter Layout — Top View (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph layout[CMOS Inverter Layout Top View] direction TB vddRail["BLUE Metal VDD rail
top horizontal stripe"] wellTap["BLACK n+ contact inside n-well
to VDD via blue metal"] pmosActive["YELLOW p+ active region
width 4 lambda"] pmosGate["RED Polysilicon gate
width 2 lambda vertical"] nmosActive["GREEN n+ active region
width 2 lambda"] nmosGate[RED Polysilicon continues] pmosDrainC["BLACK contact to Y metal"] yMetal["BLUE Metal output Y
middle horizontal stripe"] nmosDrainC["BLACK contact to Y metal"] subTap["YELLOW p+ substrate tap
BLACK contact to VSS"] vssRail["BLUE Metal VSS rail
bottom horizontal stripe"] end vddRail --- wellTap vddRail --- pmosActive pmosActive --- pmosDrainC pmosDrainC --- yMetal yMetal --- nmosDrainC nmosDrainC --- nmosActive nmosActive --- subTap subTap --- vssRail pmosGate -.crosses.-> pmosActive pmosGate -.crosses.-> nmosActive ``` ### 4.3 Layer Cross-Section Schematic (Stick Diagram → Physical Cross-Section) The cross-section (viewing the chip from the side) shows why stick diagrams map to physical reality. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph xsection[Cross-Section View of CMOS Inverter] metal1["BLUE Metal-1 layer
Y output wire"] contact["BLACK Contact plug
2 lambda by 2 lambda"] nplus["GREEN n+ region
drain of nMOS"] pplus["YELLOW p+ region
drain of pMOS"] gateox["Thin Gate Oxide
approx 10 to 100 Angstrom"] poly["RED Polysilicon gate
Input A"] nwell["n-well region
contains pMOS"] psub["p-substrate
contains nMOS"] end metal1 --- contact contact --- nplus contact --- pplus pplus --- nwell nplus --- psub gateox --- nplus gateox --- pplus poly --- gateox ``` **Interpretation** : - The **RED poly gate** sits on top of the **gate oxide (SiO₂)**. - The **GREEN n+** and **YELLOW p+** are on either side of the gate — these are the source/drain formed by ion implantation. - The **BLACK contact** drops the **BLUE metal** down to the silicon. - The **n-well** (brown, dashed in stick diagram) encloses the pMOS to give it a p-body to operate in (since pMOS needs n-body). ### 4.4 Euler's Rule Verification Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TB start["Start: Given stick diagram"] countE["Count E = number of sticks edges"] countV["Count V = number of junctions vertices"] countN["Count N = connected components"] apply["Apply Euler: E minus V plus N = 1"] check{"Equals 1?"} valid["Diagram is topologically valid"] invalid["Redesign: Add or remove sticks"] start --> countE start --> countV start --> countN countE --> apply countV --> apply countN --> apply apply --> check check -- Yes --> valid check -- No --> invalid ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *List the color codes used in stick diagrams for the following layers: (a) n-diffusion, (b) p-diffusion, (c) Polysilicon, (d) Metal, and (e) Contact. Why is stick diagram representation preferred before full layout design?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Layer | Color | |---|---| | n-diffusion (n+) | **Green** | | p-diffusion (p+) | **Yellow** | | Polysilicon (gate) | **Red** | | Metal-1 | **Blue** | | Contact (cut/via) | **Black** | Stick diagrams are preferred before full layout because they: 1. Enable **rapid topological verification** of the circuit connectivity. 2. Allow **early detection of design errors** (shorts, opens, missing contacts) *without* wasting time on full mask layout. 3. Provide a **compact, dimension-free abstraction** that focuses on *form* rather than *exact size*, which is ideal for conceptual design and teaching. 4. Can be **validated using Euler's rule** to check if the topology is planar and realizable. *(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for color codes, 2 marks for justification.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *State Euler's rule for stick diagrams. A CMOS inverter stick diagram has 11 edges and 9 vertices. Verify if it satisfies Euler's rule. If not, suggest the correction.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Euler's Rule (1 mark):** For a connected planar stick diagram, $$E - V + N = 1$$ where *E* = number of edges (sticks), *V* = number of vertices (junctions), *N* = number of connected components (typically **N = 1** for a single stick diagram). **Verification (1 mark):** Given *E* = 11, *V* = 9, *N* = 1. $$E - V + N = 11 - 9 + 1 = 3$$ This equals 3, **not 1**. Therefore, **Euler's rule is NOT satisfied** with the given count (or, if a single connected diagram, the count is invalid for *E − V + N = 1*). **Correction (1 mark):** The diagram must be redrawn to give *E − V + N = 1*, i.e., *E = V* (when N = 1). Either remove 2 sticks or add 2 junctions/vertices to satisfy the rule. Alternatively, the *E* and *V* counts are wrong; recount after redrawing. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall** : Many students write *"Euler's rule is E − V + F = 2"* (which is for *simple polyhedra*). The **correct form for stick diagrams** is **E − V + N = 1** (for a connected planar graph). Writing the polyhedral form will cost you **1 mark** in KTU valuation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern) > KTU 2024 Scheme Part-B question structure: One question of 14 marks with sub-parts (typically 7 + 7 marks) and an **internal choice** between two alternatives. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — CMOS Inverter Stick Diagram + Layout** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** *(a)* Draw the **stick diagram of a CMOS inverter** with proper color codes. Label all the layers (n-diffusion, p-diffusion, poly, metal, contacts) and identify the input, output, VDD, and VSS nets. Verify the diagram using Euler's rule. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Draw the **physical layout (mask layout)** of the CMOS inverter using λ-based design rules. Show the dimensions of nMOS and pMOS (assume β-ratio = 2), mark all design rules (minimum widths, spacings, contact sizes), and estimate the total layout area in λ². **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (2 marks): Draw the stick diagram.** - Draw **one vertical red poly stripe** (input **A**) crossing two horizontal diffusions. - Draw **one green horizontal stick** (nMOS active region) below. - Draw **one yellow horizontal stick** (pMOS active region) above, **enclosed in a brown-dashed n-well**. - The **left end of green** = nMOS source, **right end of green** = nMOS drain. - The **left end of yellow** = pMOS source, **right end of yellow** = pMOS drain. - **Connect both drains** with a horizontal blue line = **output Y**. - **Connect nMOS source** to a blue VSS rail (bottom). - **Connect pMOS source** to a blue VDD rail (top). - Place **black contact squares** at all 4 source/drain junctions (4 marks for correct drawing). **Step 2 (2 marks): Label all nets.** | Net | Layer | Color | |---|---|---| | Input A | Poly | Red | | Output Y | Metal | Blue | | VDD | Metal | Blue | | VSS | Metal | Blue | | nMOS body | p-substrate | Implicit | | pMOS body | n-well | Brown dashed | **Step 3 (1 mark): Euler's rule verification.** Count for the canonical inverter: *E* = 12, *V* = 9, *N* = 1. $$E - V + N = 12 - 9 + 1 = 4 \neq 1$$ For the *planar face-counting* version: $E - V + F = 2$ (Euler's polyhedron formula for a planar embedding), giving *F* = 4 bounded faces. For the **stick-diagram form**, the convention used in KTU Module 3 textbook is **E − V + N = 1** which holds if we redefine *E* and *V* appropriately. **Euler's rule is satisfied for the standard CMOS inverter stick diagram** as per textbook convention. (1 mark for stating the rule correctly and the count.) **Step 4 (2 marks): Add well/substrate taps** for latch-up prevention (yellow p+ tap to VSS near nMOS, n+ tap to VDD inside n-well). --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark): Choose transistor dimensions.** For β-ratio = 2, with $L_n = L_p = 2\lambda$: - $W_n = 2\lambda$ → $W_p = 2 \times 2\lambda = 4\lambda$. **Step 2 (3 marks): Draw layout layers in order.** Draw in this order (bottom to top in physical terms, but any order is acceptable in exam): 1. **n-well** (brown dashed) — top portion of layout. 2. **Active areas** (green n+ on bottom, yellow p+ on top inside n-well). 3. **Polysilicon gate** (red) — vertical 2λ stripe crossing both active areas. 4. **n+ select / p+ select** (implicit, marks the implant type). 5. **Contacts** (black 2λ × 2λ squares at all four source/drain terminals). 6. **Metal-1** (blue, 3λ wide minimum) for VDD, VSS, Y, and input A. **Step 3 (2 marks): Mark all design rules.** | Rule | Value | |---|---| | Channel length L | 2λ | | nMOS width W_n | 2λ | | pMOS width W_p | 4λ | | Poly width | 2λ | | Poly to active (parallel) | 1λ | | Contact size | 2λ × 2λ | | Contact enclosure | 1λ | | Metal width | 3λ | | Metal spacing | 3λ | | n+ to n+ (same net) | 3λ | | p+ to p+ (same net) | 3λ | | n+ to p+ (different net) | 2λ | | n-well overlap of p+ | 2λ | **Step 4 (1 mark): Estimate area.** $$A = (W_p + W_n + 2 \times 3\lambda_{\text{sep}}) \times (L + 6\lambda_{\text{drain+contact}})$$ $$\begin{aligned} A &= (4\lambda + 2\lambda + 6\lambda) \times (2\lambda + 6\lambda) \\ &= 12\lambda \times 8\lambda \\ &= 96\lambda^2 \end{aligned}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Euler's Rule + NAND Gate (Alternative Internal Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 3 — Alternative]** *(a)* State and explain **Euler's rule** for stick diagrams. A 2-input NAND gate stick diagram has 18 edges, 14 vertices, and 1 connected component. Verify Euler's rule and identify the number of bounded faces. **(7 marks)** *(b)* Draw the **stick diagram of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate** with proper color codes. Mark all input/output nets, VDD, VSS, and identify the number of transistors and contacts used. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (a) — 7 marks:** **Euler's Rule (2 marks):** For a connected planar graph representing a stick diagram: $$E - V + F = 1 + C$$ where: - *E* = number of edges (sticks), - *V* = number of vertices (junctions/dots), - *F* = number of faces (bounded regions), - *C* = number of connected components. For a single stick diagram, *C* = 1, so: $$E - V + F = 2 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad E - V + N = 1 \text{ (depending on convention)}$$ **Verification (3 marks):** Given *E* = 18, *V* = 14, *C* = 1. $$E - V + F = 2 \implies F = 2 - E + V = 2 - 18 + 14 = -2$$ This gives a **negative face count**, which is **impossible** in a planar graph. The stick diagram as counted is **NOT topologically realizable** with the given *E* and *V*. The student must recount or redraw. Alternatively, applying $E - V + N = 1$ with $N = 1$: $$E - V + 1 = 1 \implies E = V$$ But here $E = 18 \neq V = 14$, so **Euler's rule is violated**. The diagram needs 4 more vertices (junctions) to be valid. **Realization (2 marks):** A 2-input CMOS NAND has 4 transistors (2 pMOS in parallel + 2 nMOS in series). Its actual stick diagram has approximately: - *E* ≈ 22–24 sticks (active regions, poly, metal rails, interconnects). - *V* ≈ 16–18 vertices (contacts, junctions, transistor crossings). **Model Answer (b) — 7 marks:** The 2-input CMOS NAND has: - **2 pMOS** in parallel (both sources at VDD, both drains tied to output Y, gates at A and B). - **2 nMOS** in series (nMOS-A drain = nMOS-B source, nMOS-A source = VSS, nMOS-B drain = Y, gates at A and B). **Stick diagram (full marks for correct drawing):** 1. Two **green n+ strips** (one for each nMOS), placed in series with a shared diffusion node. 2. Two **yellow p+ strips** in parallel, both enclosed in a brown n-well. 3. Two **red poly gates** (input A and input B) crossing the four active regions → 4 transistors. 4. **Black contacts** at VDD (2 pMOS sources), VSS (nMOS-A source), Y (pMOS drains joined + nMOS-B drain), and the intermediate node (nMOS-A drain = nMOS-B source). 5. **Blue metal** for VDD rail, VSS rail, output Y, and input nets A and B. 6. **Substrate and well taps** for latch-up. | Component | Count | |---|---| | Transistors | **4** (2 pMOS + 2 nMOS) | | Black contacts | **≥ 6** (4 source/drain + 2 taps) | | Color-coded layers used | Green, Yellow, Red, Blue, Black, Brown-dashed | --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision — Read this 3 times before the exam.** - ✅ **Color Code (MUST memorize verbatim)** : n+ = **Green**, p+ = **Yellow**, Poly = **Red**, Metal = **Blue**, Contact = **Black**, n-well = **Brown dashed**. - ✅ **CMOS Inverter has 2 transistors** : 1 nMOS (pull-down, in p-substrate) + 1 pMOS (pull-up, in n-well). - ✅ **Single red poly gate** = shared input A, crosses both diffusions. - ✅ **Output Y** = blue metal connecting the two drains. - ✅ **β-ratio** for symmetric inverter = 2 to 3 (pMOS is 2×–3× wider than nMOS). - ✅ **Channel length L = 2λ** (minimum). - ✅ **Contact size = 2λ × 2λ** with **1λ enclosure** on all sides. - ✅ **Metal minimum width = 3λ**, spacing = 3λ. - ✅ **Euler's rule for stick diagrams** : $E - V + N = 1$ (for connected planar graph) or equivalently $E - V + F = 2$ (Euler's polyhedron formula). - ✅ **Standard CMOS process requires ≥ 8 masks** (n-well, active, n+ select, p+ select, poly, contact, metal-1, pad). - ✅ **Typical CMOS inverter layout area ≈ 96λ²** (for β-ratio = 2). - ✅ **Latches** (latch-up prevention) : **p+ substrate tap → VSS** near nMOS, **n+ well tap → VDD** inside n-well. **Always add these in your layout.** - ✅ **Poly is drawn over diffusion** in stick diagrams; the crossing *is* the transistor. - ✅ **Verify Euler's rule** at the end of every stick diagram problem to ensure topological validity. - ✅ **KTU 14-mark question always asks for either Euler's verification OR a layout with β-ratio.** Practice both. - ✅ **Don't confuse Euler's rule forms** : $E - V + F = 2$ is for *polyhedra* / planar embeddings; **$E - V + N = 1$** is the **KTU Module 3 form** for stick diagrams. Use the latter unless the question specifies otherwise. - ✅ **Mnemonic for design rules** : *"Two is the gate, Three is the spacing, One is enclosure, Poly is the king."* ---

topics.title: Two Input NAND and NOR Gates, MOSFET Logic Design - Pass Transistor Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Two Input NAND and NOR Gates, MOSFET Logic Design - Pass Transistor Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 CMOS Two-Input NAND Gate

A **CMOS Two-Input NAND Gate** is a complementary static CMOS logic gate constructed using two p-channel MOSFETs (PMOS) arranged in **parallel** in the Pull-Up Network (PUN) and two n-channel MOSFETs (NMOS) arranged in **series** in the Pull-Down Network (PDN). It implements the Boolean function $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Static CMOS implementation of 2-input NAND and NOR gates is a core Module 3 topic. Mastery of the pull-up/pull-down network duality and transistor sizing is mandatory for ESE questions.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Think of the CMOS NAND gate as a **two-person safety lock on a bank vault door**. The vault door (output node) opens (pulls down to GND) only if **both** security guards (NMOS transistors) give their independent signals. The vault closes (pulls up to $V_{DD}$) if **even one** guard flips a switch (one PMOS conducts). The "series" NMOS represents *both* guards required simultaneously, while the "parallel" PMOS represents *either one* is sufficient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – Pull-Up Network (PUN):** The PMOS network connecting the output node to $V_{DD}$. It conducts when the output is logic HIGH.
>
> **Definition – Pull-Down Network (PDN):** The NMOS network connecting the output node to GND. It conducts when the output is logic LOW.

### 1.2 CMOS Two-Input NOR Gate

A **CMOS Two-Input NOR Gate** is the dual of the NAND gate, using two PMOS in **series** in the PUN and two NMOS in **parallel** in the PDN. It implements the Boolean function $F = \overline{A + B}$.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Visualize the NOR gate as **two fire alarms in parallel connected to a single sprinkler**. Either alarm alone (any input HIGH) is enough to trigger the sprinkler (output goes LOW). Only when **both** alarms are off (both inputs LOW) does the sprinkler remain idle (output remains HIGH).

### 1.3 MOSFET Logic Design – Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)

**Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** is a non-restoring MOS logic family in which the MOS transistor is used as a **voltage-controlled switch** to pass logic signals from input to output, rather than as a current-source amplifier (as in static CMOS). The transistor acts as a "pass gate" controlled by a clock or control signal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition – PTL:** A logic design style that routes input signals through MOS transistors to the output node, exploiting the source/drain symmetry of MOSFETs to implement logic functions with **fewer transistors** than static CMOS.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):**
Imagine a **railway signal-switching yard**. A single NMOS or PMOS transistor is like a movable track-switch (controlled by a gate signal) that either connects an input train (data signal) onto the main rail (output node) or disconnects it. You can build complex routing networks with fewer switches (transistors) than building entirely new tracks (static gates) for every function.

**Standard Metrics in PTL Design:**
- **Threshold Voltage Drop ($V_{T0}$):** The maximum $V_{DS}$ loss in an NMOS pass transistor when passing a logic HIGH; equals approximately **0.7 V** for a 0.5 $\mu$m process.
- **Static Power Dissipation:** Reduced by ~30–50% compared to static CMOS.
- **Transistor Count Ratio:** Typically **n+1** for an n-input PTL function versus **2n** for static CMOS.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 CMOS Two-Input NAND Gate – Operational Analysis

**Circuit Topology:**
- PMOS transistors (Mp1, Mp2): **Source** $\rightarrow$ $V_{DD}$, **Drains** tied to output node. **Gates** tied to inputs A and B respectively.
- NMOS transistors (Mn1, Mn2): **Source** $\rightarrow$ GND, **Drains** tied to output node (or to each other forming a series chain). **Gates** tied to inputs A and B.

**Truth-Table Logic Breakdown:**

| A | B | Mp1 (A) | Mp2 (B) | Mn1 (A) | Mn2 (B) | Output F = A·B | F̄ |
|---|---|---------|---------|---------|---------|---------------|----|
| 0 | 0 | ON | ON | OFF | OFF | 1 | **1** |
| 0 | 1 | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 0 | **1** |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | 0 | **1** |
| 1 | 1 | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 1 | **0** |

**Why NAND? – The "How" Behind the Logic:**
- When **at least one input is LOW** $\rightarrow$ at least one PMOS turns ON $\rightarrow$ output is strongly pulled to $V_{DD}$.
- When **both inputs are HIGH** $\rightarrow$ both PMOS turn OFF, both NMOS turn ON $\rightarrow$ output is strongly pulled to GND.
- Series NMOS in PDN is **AND**-like (both must conduct for discharge).
- Parallel PMOS in PUN is **OR**-like (either can pull up to $V_{DD}$).
- The combination yields NAND.

### 2.2 CMOS Two-Input NOR Gate – Operational Analysis

**Circuit Topology:**
- PMOS (Mp1, Mp2): **Series** chain from $V_{DD}$ to output.
- NMOS (Mn1, Mn2): **Parallel** paths from output to GND.

**Truth-Table Logic Breakdown:**

| A | B | PUN (series PMOS) | PDN (parallel NMOS) | Output F̄ |
|---|---|-------------------|---------------------|-----------|
| 0 | 0 | ON–ON (conducts) | OFF–OFF | **1** |
| 0 | 1 | ON–OFF (blocked) | OFF–ON (conducts) | **0** |
| 1 | 0 | OFF–ON (blocked) | ON–OFF (conducts) | **0** |
| 1 | 1 | OFF–OFF (blocked) | ON–ON (conducts) | **0** |

**Why NOR? – The "How" Behind the Logic:**
- Series PMOS in PUN: **AND** condition required for pull-up.
- Parallel NMOS in PDN: **OR** condition sufficient for pull-down.
- The dual combination yields NOR.

### 2.3 Transistor Sizing – Matching the Reference Inverter

For equal drive strength to a unit inverter (NMOS width $W_n$, PMOS width $W_p = 2W_n$):

**For 2-Input NAND:**
- The series NMOS chain has effective resistance $2R_n$. To restore $R_n$, each NMOS width becomes $2W_n$.
- The parallel PMOS gives $R_p/2$. To restore $R_p$, each PMOS width becomes $2W_p = 4W_n$.

**For 2-Input NOR:**
- The series PMOS chain needs each PMOS to be $2W_p = 4W_n$ wide.
- The parallel NMOS gives $R_n/2$, so each NMOS width becomes $W_n$ (no change).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transistor Sizing Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):**
> - 2-input NAND: PMOS = $4W_n$ each, NMOS = $2W_n$ each.
> - 2-input NOR: PMOS = $4W_n$ each, NMOS = $2W_n$ each.
> - 2-input NAND uses **4 transistors** but consumes **silicon area similar to 3 inverters**.
> - 2-input NOR is **worse** in area because each of the 2 series PMOS must be $2\times$ wider.

### 2.4 Pass Transistor Logic (PTL) – Deep Theory

**Operating Principle:**
A single NMOS transistor with gate tied to a control signal C acts as a switch:
- When $C = V_{DD}$ $\rightarrow$ NMOS turns ON $\rightarrow$ connects input $A$ to output $Y$.
- When $C = 0$ $\rightarrow$ NMOS turns OFF $\rightarrow$ output is high-impedance (floating).

**Voltage Degradation Problem:**
When passing a logic **1** ($V_{DD}$) through an NMOS pass transistor, the output node can charge only up to:
$$V_{Y,\text{max}} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$$

Because the NMOS turns OFF when $V_{GS} < V_{Tn}$, i.e., when $V_Y - 0 < V_{Tn}$.

When passing a logic **0** through PMOS pass transistor:
$$V_{Y,\text{min}} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$$

**Solution: Transmission Gate (TG)**
A parallel combination of NMOS + PMOS controlled by complementary clocks C and C̄ solves the rail-to-rail issue by combining:
- NMOS passes strong **0** (good low).
- PMOS passes strong **1** (good high).

### 2.5 Variants of Pass Transistor Logic

1. **Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL):** Uses transmission gates + inverters; fully differential.
2. **Double Pass Transistor Logic (DPL):** Uses two parallel TGs; more robust, no static power.
3. **Swing Restored Pass Transistor Logic (SRPL):** Adds a weak cross-coupled feedback inverter for full swing.
4. **Level Restoring Pass Transistor Logic (LEAP):** PMOS feedback ensures full output swing.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---------|-------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | 2-input NAND output function | Boolean |
| 2 | $F = \overline{A + B}$ | 2-input NOR output function | Boolean |
| 3 | $V_{Y,\text{max (NMOS)}} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ | Max output when NMOS passes logic 1 | **Volts (V)** |
| 4 | $V_{Y,\text{min (PMOS)}} = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$ | Min output when PMOS passes logic 0 | **Volts (V)** |
| 5 | $W_{p,\text{NAND}} = 4W_n$ (each) | PMOS sizing for 2-input NAND | **meters** |
| 6 | $W_{n,\text{NAND}} = 2W_n$ (each) | NMOS sizing for 2-input NAND | **meters** |
| 7 | $W_{p,\text{NOR}} = 4W_n$ (each) | PMOS sizing for 2-input NOR | **meters** |
| 8 | $W_{n,\text{NOR}} = 2W_n$ (each) | NMOS sizing for 2-input NOR | **meters** |
| 9 | $I_{D,\text{sat}} = \dfrac{\mu_n C_{ox}}{2} \dfrac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{Tn})^{2}$ | Saturation drain current (NMOS) | **Amperes (A)** |
| 10 | $N_{\text{tr,PTL}} \approx n + 1$ | Transistor count for n-input PTL function | **count** |
| 11 | $N_{\text{tr,CMOS}} = 2n$ | Transistor count for n-input static CMOS | **count** |
| 12 | $R_{\text{eq,series}} = R_{n1} + R_{n2} + \dots$ | Series resistance addition | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** |
| 13 | $R_{\text{eq,parallel}} = \dfrac{R_1 R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | Parallel resistance | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** |
| 14 | $C_{\text{gate}} = C_{ox} \cdot W \cdot L$ | Gate capacitance of MOSFET | **Farads (F)** |
| 15 | $t_{pLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L$ | Pull-up delay (inverter model) | **seconds (s)** |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Static CMOS NAND/NOR** forms the **AND/OR plane** of every PLA, ROM, and standard-cell library in any commercial ASIC (e.g., Intel, AMD, Qualcomm Snapdragon cores).
- **PTL** is heavily used in **arithmetic units (adders, multipliers)**, **multiplexers**, and **XOR/XNOR** implementations where transistor-count reduction gives **20–30% speed-up and lower power**.
- Modern FPGAs (Xilinx 7-series, Altera Cyclone) implement **6-input LUTs** using PTL/pass-gate multiplexers.
- **CPL/DPL** is used in **biCMOS and ultra-low-power IoT processors** (sub-threshold operation).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: NMOS Pass-Transistor Output Voltage Limit

**Problem:** Show that an NMOS pass transistor cannot pass a full logic HIGH ($V_{DD}$) to the output node.

**Step 1 – Setup:**
Consider an NMOS transistor with:
- Drain = input $A = V_{DD}$
- Gate = control $C = V_{DD}$
- Source = output node $Y$
- Body = GND

Initially $Y = 0$. As current flows, the source voltage $V_Y$ rises.

**Step 2 – Transistor Operating Region:**
As $V_Y$ rises, $V_{GS} = V_C - V_Y = V_{DD} - V_Y$ decreases. The transistor leaves saturation and enters the **triode region** when $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{Tn}$, i.e., when $V_{DD} - V_Y < (V_{DD} - V_Y) - V_{Tn}$ (always true once in triode).

**Step 3 – Cut-Off Condition:**
The transistor turns OFF the moment:
$$V_{GS} = V_{Tn}$$

Substituting $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_Y$:
$$V_{DD} - V_Y = V_{Tn}$$

**Step 4 – Solve for Output Voltage:**
$$V_Y = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$$

**Step 5 – Final Expression:**
$$\boxed{V_{Y,\text{max}} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}}$$

For a standard 0.5 $\mu$m CMOS process, $V_{Tn} \approx 0.7$ V and $V_{DD} = 5$ V, hence:
$$V_{Y,\text{max}} = 5 - 0.7 = 4.3 \text{ V}$$

This degraded HIGH is **insufficient to drive the next CMOS stage reliably**, which is why a transmission gate is used.

### 3.2 Derivation: 2-Input NAND Transistor Sizing

**Step 1 – Reference Inverter Sizing:**
Let the unit NMOS width be $W_n$ with channel length $L$. The matching PMOS has width $W_p = 2W_n$. Effective pull-up resistance: $R_p$. Pull-down resistance: $R_n$.

**Step 2 – Series NMOS in PDN:**
For 2 series NMOS, the total effective resistance becomes:
$$R_{n,\text{total}} = R_{n1} + R_{n2} = 2R_n$$

To restore $R_n$ total, the resistance of each transistor must be halved, which (since $R \propto 1/W$) means **doubling the width**:
$$W_{n,\text{NAND}} = 2W_n \text{ (each)}$$

**Step 3 – Parallel PMOS in PUN:**
Two PMOS in parallel:
$$R_{p,\text{total}} = \dfrac{R_{p1} R_{p2}}{R_{p1} + R_{p2}} = \dfrac{R_p}{2}$$

To restore total $R_p$, each PMOS resistance must be $2R_p$, which means **doubling the width**:
$$W_{p,\text{NAND}} = 2W_p = 4W_n \text{ (each)}$$

**Step 4 – Final Width Table:**

| Transistor | Width | Effective Resistance |
|------------|-------|----------------------|
| PMOS (each) | $4W_n$ | $2R_p$ |
| NMOS (each) | $2W_n$ | $R_n$ |
| Total PUN | – | $R_p$ (matches inverter) |
| Total PDN | – | $R_n$ (matches inverter) |

### 3.3 Derivation: 2-Input NOR Sizing

**Step 1 – Series PMOS in PUN:**
$$R_{p,\text{total}} = 2R_p \implies W_{p,\text{NOR}} = 4W_n \text{ (each)}$$

**Step 2 – Parallel NMOS in PDN:**
$$R_{n,\text{total}} = \dfrac{R_n}{2} \implies W_{n,\text{NOR}} = W_n \text{ (each)}$$

**Step 3 – Comparison Table:**

| Gate | PMOS Width | NMOS Width | Total Area (relative) |
|------|------------|------------|------------------------|
| Inverter | $2W_n$ | $W_n$ | 1× |
| 2-input NAND | $4W_n \times 2 = 8W_n$ | $2W_n \times 2 = 4W_n$ | 4× |
| 2-input NOR | $4W_n \times 2 = 8W_n$ | $W_n \times 2 = 2W_n$ | 4× |

### 3.4 Derivation: NMOS PTL Implementing an AND Function

**Problem:** Implement $F = A \cdot B$ using a single NMOS pass transistor.

**Step 1 – Transistor Configuration:**
- Drain of NMOS = input $A$
- Gate of NMOS = control $B$
- Source of NMOS = output $F$

**Step 2 – Truth-Table Analysis:**

| A | B | NMOS State | Output F | Ideal $A \cdot B$ |
|---|---|------------|----------|-------------------|
| 0 | 0 | OFF | Z (high-Z) | 0 |
| 0 | 1 | ON | F = A = 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0 | OFF | Z | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | ON | F = A = 1, but degrades to $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$ | 1 |

**Step 3 – Why It Is Imperfect:**
The 0-1 case (A=0, B=1) and 1-1 case (A=1, B=1) both require careful handling. To overcome the high-Z and degraded high, an SRPL (Swing Restored PTL) feedback inverter is added at the output.

**Step 4 – Final Expression for Output Swing:**
$$V_{F,\text{max}} = \min(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}, V_{DD}) = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: PTL vs Static CMOS Comparison

| Parameter | Static CMOS (NAND) | PTL (NMOS only) | Transmission Gate PTL |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------|------------------------|
| Transistor Count (2-input) | 4 | 1 + 1 (control) | 2 + 2 (inverter) |
| Output Swing | Full rail-to-rail | Degraded (logic 1) | Full rail-to-rail |
| Static Power | Near zero | Medium (leakage) | Near zero (DPL) |
| Delay | Moderate | Fast (low $C_{out}$) | Fast |
| Noise Margin | High | Low | High |
| Best Use Case | General logic | MUX, XOR | Adder cells, XOR |

### 3.6 Propagation Delay Comparison Derivation

**Step 1 – Inverter Delay Reference:**
$$t_{p,\text{inv}} = 0.69 R_{eq} C_L$$

**Step 2 – NAND Delay (worst case A=B=1→0):**
Discharge through 2 series NMOS: $R_{eq} = 2R_n$. Therefore:
$$t_{pHL,\text{NAND}} = 0.69 (2R_n) C_L = 2 t_{p,\text{inv}}$$

**Step 3 – NOR Delay (worst case A=1→0, B=0):**
Discharge through 1 NMOS but to a larger PMOS load. With sizing matched, the worst-case is:
$$t_{pHL,\text{NOR}} \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2 \times t_{p,\text{inv}}$$

**Step 4 – Key Conclusion:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the same area, **NAND is faster than NOR** because its worst-case path has a smaller PMOS stack. This is why NAND is preferred in CMOS standard cell libraries.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

Define a CMOS transmission gate. Why is it preferred over a single NMOS pass transistor for implementing PTL functions?

**Model Answer:**

A **CMOS transmission gate (TG)** is a parallel combination of one NMOS and one PMOS transistor with their source/drain terminals tied together and gates driven by complementary control signals (C and C̄).

**Why preferred over single NMOS pass transistor:**
1. **Full rail-to-rail output swing:** TG passes both logic 0 (via NMOS) and logic 1 (via PMOS) without threshold drop.
2. **Single NMOS degradation:** An NMOS pass transistor can only charge the output up to $V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$, resulting in a degraded HIGH.
3. **Lower on-resistance:** Parallel NMOS + PMOS reduces effective channel resistance, improving speed.
4. **Bidirectional signal flow:** Both source and drain can act as input or output.
5. **Symmetric performance:** Equal rise and fall times; no skew between 0→1 and 1→0 transitions.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

List the transistor sizing requirements (W/L ratios) for a 2-input CMOS NAND gate to match the drive strength of a reference inverter with NMOS width $W_n$ and PMOS width $2W_n$.

**Model Answer:**

For a 2-input CMOS NAND gate with **equivalent pull-up/pull-down resistance** to the unit inverter:

**Pull-Up Network (PMOS in parallel):**
- Two PMOS in parallel give effective resistance $R_p/2$.
- To restore $R_p$, each PMOS resistance must be $2R_p$, hence each PMOS width = $2 \times 2W_n = 4W_n$.

**Pull-Down Network (NMOS in series):**
- Two NMOS in series give total resistance $2R_n$.
- To restore $R_n$, each NMOS width must be doubled: each NMOS width = $2W_n$.

**Final Sizing Table:**

| Transistor | Width | Effective R |
|------------|-------|-------------|
| PMOS (each) | $4W_n$ | $2R_p$ |
| NMOS (each) | $2W_n$ | $R_n$ |

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – KTU Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Draw the schematic circuit of a 2-input CMOS NAND gate. Explain its operation with the help of a truth table. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)? Explain the voltage degradation problem in NMOS PTL. How is it solved using a transmission gate? Derive the maximum output voltage expression. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) 2-Input CMOS NAND Gate Schematic & Operation:**

**Schematic Diagram (Transistor-Level Description):**
- **Pull-Up Network (PUN):** Two PMOS transistors (Mp1, Mp2) in **parallel** between $V_{DD}$ and output node F. Gate of Mp1 = A, gate of Mp2 = B.
- **Pull-Down Network (PDN):** Two NMOS transistors (Mn1, Mn2) in **series** between output F and GND. Gate of Mn1 = A, gate of Mn2 = B.

**Truth Table:**

| A | B | PUN | PDN | F = $\overline{A \cdot B}$ |
|---|---|-----|-----|---------------------------|
| 0 | 0 | Mp1 ON, Mp2 ON | Both OFF | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | Mp1 ON | Mn2 ON | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | Mp2 ON | Mn1 ON | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | Both OFF | Both ON | 0 |

**Operation Explanation:**
- When **A = 0**: Mp1 turns ON (PMOS conducts for low gate), charging output F to $V_{DD}$.
- When **B = 0**: Mp2 turns ON, charging F to $V_{DD}$.
- Only when **A = B = 1** are both PMOS OFF, and both NMOS turn ON, discharging F to GND.
- This implements $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.

**(b) Pass Transistor Logic – Voltage Degradation & TG Solution:**

**Definition:** PTL is a logic design style where MOS transistors are used as switches to route input signals to the output node, reducing transistor count compared to static CMOS.

**Voltage Degradation in NMOS PTL:**
When a logic HIGH ($V_{DD}$) is passed through an NMOS pass transistor, the output node can only charge to:
$$V_{Y,\text{max}} = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$$

**Derivation:** [Refer Section 3.1] The NMOS turns OFF when $V_{GS} = V_{Tn}$. With gate at $V_{DD}$ and source rising to $V_Y$, the device switches off when:
$$V_{DD} - V_Y = V_{Tn} \implies V_Y = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$$

For $V_{DD} = 5$ V and $V_{Tn} = 0.7$ V: $V_{Y,\text{max}} = 4.3$ V, which fails to drive the next stage.

**Solution – Transmission Gate:**
A transmission gate is a parallel combination of NMOS + PMOS with complementary control signals C and C̄. 
- NMOS passes strong **0** (low resistance for LOW).
- PMOS passes strong **1** (low resistance for HIGH).
- Combined, full rail-to-rail transfer is achieved.

**Conclusion:** TGs eliminate the $V_{Tn}$ drop, providing complete logic levels at the output, which is essential for cascading multiple PTL stages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**(a)** Design a 2-input CMOS NOR gate. Derive the transistor sizing required to match the reference inverter's drive strength. Compare the NAND and NOR gates in terms of area, delay, and preferred use. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the various Pass Transistor Logic variants (PTL, CPL, DPL, SRPL, LEAP) with their merits and demerits. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) 2-Input CMOS NOR Gate Design & Sizing:**

**Schematic:**
- PUN: Two PMOS in **series** (Mp1–Mp2) from $V_{DD}$ to F.
- PDN: Two NMOS in **parallel** (Mn1, Mn2) from F to GND.

**Sizing Derivation:**

For matching reference inverter ($W_n$ NMOS, $2W_n$ PMOS):
- Series PMOS chain: total $R_p = 2R_p \implies$ each PMOS = $2 \times 2W_n = 4W_n$.
- Parallel NMOS: total $R_n = R_n/2 \implies$ each NMOS = $W_n$ (no change).

**Final Widths:**

| Gate | PMOS each | NMOS each | Total Width |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------------|
| NOR | $4W_n$ | $W_n$ | $2 \cdot 4W_n + 2 \cdot W_n = 10W_n$ |
| NAND | $4W_n$ | $2W_n$ | $2 \cdot 4W_n + 2 \cdot 2W_n = 12W_n$ |

**Comparison NAND vs NOR:**

| Parameter | 2-Input NAND | 2-Input NOR |
|-----------|--------------|--------------|
| Area (relative) | Larger (12$W_n$) | Smaller (10$W_n$) |
| Worst-Case Delay | $2 t_{p,\text{inv}}$ (NMOS stack) | $\sim 2 t_{p,\text{inv}}$ (PMOS stack) |
| Capacitive Load | Moderate | Higher (large PMOS) |
| Preferred Use | Standard cell default | OR-plane of PLAs |

**Preferred Use:** NAND is preferred in CMOS design because series NMOS is faster than series PMOS (higher mobility).

**(b) Pass Transistor Logic Variants:**

**1. Standard PTL (NMOS only):**
- **Merits:** Lowest transistor count; small input capacitance.
- **Demerits:** Degraded output swing ($V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$); reduced noise margin.

**2. Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL):**
- **Merits:** Full swing using TGs; differential signaling; good for XOR/MUX.
- **Demerits:** Requires dual-rail inputs; more wiring complexity.

**3. Double Pass Transistor Logic (DPL):**
- **Merits:** Two parallel TGs for each path; eliminates static power; robust.
- **Demerits:** Doubles transistor count; higher area.

**4. Swing Restored PTL (SRPL):**
- **Merits:** Cross-coupled feedback inverter restores full swing.
- **Demerits:** Risk of static power dissipation; sizing trade-off.

**5. Level Restoring PTL (LEAP):**
- **Merits:** PMOS feedback restores level; minimal area overhead.
- **Demerits:** Conditional stability; bidirectional control issues.

**Conclusion:** Choice of PTL variant depends on the trade-off between **transistor count, speed, power, and noise margin** in the target application (adders, multiplexers, etc.).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes (KTU 2024 Board Exam):**
> 1. **Confusing PUN/PDN for NAND vs NOR:** Many students interchange series/parallel PMOS and NMOS. **Remember:** NAND = PMOS parallel + NMOS series; NOR = PMOS series + NMOS parallel.
> 2. **Ignoring body effect:** In NMOS PTL, source-body connection creates body effect, further raising $V_{Tn}$ and worsening degradation. Always mention body effect in PTL derivations.
> 3. **Sizing derivation skipping:** Examiners award marks for **showing R-equivalent** calculations. Simply stating "PMOS = 4$W_n$" without derivation loses 2-3 marks.
> 4. **Missing rise/fall time asymmetry:** In PTL, rise and fall times are different (asymmetric). Failing to note this loses marks.
> 5. **Forgetting the inverter in SRPL/LEAP:** The feedback inverter's sizing (weak vs strong) is critical. Always discuss the **W/L ratio of the feedback inverter** relative to the pass transistor (typically 1:3 or 1:4).
> 6. **Static CMOS vs PTL confusion:** PTL does NOT have a PUN/PDN structure. It directly passes data — mention this clearly in answers.
> 7. **Worst-case input vector:** For NAND delay, worst case is A=B=1→0. For NOR, worst case is A=1, B=1→0. State this explicitly.
