---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize for KTU ESE):**

- **CMOS NAND:** 2 PMOS **parallel** (PUN) + 2 NMOS **series** (PDN). Output: $F = \overline{A \cdot B}$.
- **CMOS NOR:** 2 PMOS **series** (PUN) + 2 NMOS **parallel** (PDN). Output: $F = \overline{A + B}$.
- **NAND sizing (matched to inverter):** PMOS = $4W_n$ each, NMOS = $2W_n$ each.
- **NOR sizing:** PMOS = $4W_n$ each, NMOS = $W_n$ each.
- **NMOS PTL max output (passing HIGH):** $V_Y = V_{DD} - V_{Tn}$.
- **PMOS PTL min output (passing LOW):** $V_Y = \vert V_{Tp} \vert$.
- **Transmission Gate** = NMOS $\parallel$ PMOS with complementary control. Solves voltage degradation.
- **PTL Transistor Count:** $\approx n+1$ vs static CMOS $2n$. Savings grow with $n$.
- **Worst-case delay NAND** = $2 t_{p,\text{inv}}$; NOR is comparable but PMOS-limited.
- **Body effect** raises effective $V_{Tn}$ in PTL, worsening swing.
- **PTL Variants:** PTL → CPL → DPL → SRPL → LEAP (in increasing complexity and robustness).
- **PTL applications:** MUX, XOR, full adders, barrel shifters, FPGA LUTs.
- **Static CMOS advantage:** Full swing, zero static power, high noise margin.
- **PTL disadvantage:** Degraded swing, body effect, possible static power in feedback designs.
- **Preferred static gate:** NAND gates are faster than NOR for equal area due to NMOS-vs-PMOS mobility ratio.
- **Differential signaling** in CPL/DPL provides **common-mode noise rejection** — useful in mixed-signal ICs.
- **Threshold voltage drop** is the **single most important issue** in PTL design — emphasize in any 14-mark answer.
- **SRPL/LEAP** use a **weak feedback inverter** ($\frac{W}{L} \approx \frac{1}{3}$ to $\frac{1}{4}$ of pass transistor) to avoid contention.

> **End of Module 3 Notes – Two Input NAND/NOR Gates & Pass Transistor Logic**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Complementary Pass Transistor Logic and Transmission Gate Logic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Complementary Pass Transistor Logic and Transmission Gate Logic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Standard Pass Transistor Logic — The Foundation

Before understanding the *complementary* variant, we must understand the baseline. In **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)**, a single MOS transistor (NMOS or PMOS) is used as a switch to pass a logic level from the input to the output, driven by a control signal at the gate. It is widely used in **multiplexers, adders, flip-flops, and SRAM cells** because it requires **fewer transistors** than static CMOS to implement a given Boolean function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Limitation of Single NMOS Pass Transistor:**
> When passing a logic HIGH (V_DD) through an NMOS, the output cannot reach V_DD. It stalls at a degraded level known as:
> $$V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$
> This is because the NMOS turns off when $V_{GS} < V_{TN}$, i.e., when $V_G - V_S < V_{TN}$. The source of the passing NMOS rises to $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ and stops there. This **threshold voltage drop** is the single most critical defect of standard NMOS PTL.

A single PMOS suffers the mirror problem — it passes a strong **0** but a degraded **1** ($V_{OL} = \vert V_{TP} \vert$ above GND).

---

## 1.2 Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> *Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL)* is a static differential pass-transistor logic family that uses **two parallel NMOS pass-transistor networks**, each driven by complementary control inputs, together with **cross-coupled PMOS pull-up transistors** at the output. It generates **true and complementary outputs simultaneously** and is designed to restore a **full-rail output voltage swing** (0 to V_DD) without static power dissipation.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Valve Water Tank"

Imagine a water tank that needs to be filled completely. A single inlet valve (the basic NMOS pass transistor) opens, but once the water rises near the top, the valve **closes prematurely** because the water pressure pushes back on it. Now picture **two valves working in parallel** — one filling from above (PMOS), one from below (NMOS) — with a smart controller that **swaps roles** based on whether you are filling or emptying. The cross-coupling is like a **latch that holds the tank at the exact correct level**, snapping it to the brim (V_DD) or the floor (GND) regardless of the input degradation. This is the essence of CPL — full-swing restoration via complementary cross-coupling.

### Key Architectural Features of CPL

- **Differential inputs and outputs** (true and complement always available).
- **NMOS-only pass network** (only the strong "0" pass is used as the basis, but the cross-coupled PMOS load fixes the "1" level).
- **Cross-coupled PMOS pull-ups** restore the full V_DD level.
- **No static power** (no path from V_DD to GND in steady state).
- Used in **high-speed adders, multipliers, and registers** in advanced CPUs (e.g., the famous *Differential Cascade Voltage Switch* lineage and the CLB of some DSP cores).

---

## 1.3 Transmission Gate (TG) Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:**
> A **Transmission Gate** is a bidirectional analog switch constructed by **parallel connection of one NMOS and one PMOS transistor**, with their gates driven by **complementary control signals** ($\phi$ and $\bar{\phi}$). It forms the fundamental building block of TG-based logic, capable of passing both logic **0** and logic **1** with **near-zero threshold drop** and near-ideal ON resistance.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Dual One-Way Door"

Think of two one-way swing doors mounted side-by-side in a single doorway: the **left door** (PMOS) lets people enter *from the right* (carries logic 1) while the **right door** (NMOS) lets people enter *from the left* (carries logic 0). A bouncer at the entrance flips both doors based on a sign above the door showing $\phi$ or $\bar{\phi}$ — when the sign says "OPEN," one door swings each way, and **anyone can pass in either direction unimpeded**. A *transmission gate* is exactly this: NMOS passes 0 strongly, PMOS passes 1 strongly, and together they let *any* signal pass cleanly.

### Distinctive Properties of the TG

| Property | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Signal direction | **Bidirectional** (input/output interchangeable) |
| Voltage swing | **Full rail-to-rail** (0 to V_DD, both directions) |
| Threshold drop | **None** (NMOS strong-0, PMOS strong-1 in parallel) |
| Static power | **Zero** when OFF (both transistors off) |
| Used in | Muxes, latches, flip-flops, SRAM, dynamic logic |

---

## 1.4 Why These Two Logic Families Matter in KTU 2024

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The KTU 2024 VLSI syllabus treats CPL and TG as **alternative CMOS design styles** that address the speed and density limitations of static CMOS. They are essential for understanding how **low-power, high-performance arithmetic circuits** and **memory elements** are designed in modern VLSI.

Both CPL and TG logic have **zero static power consumption** (a critical CMOS virtue), but they suffer from **dynamic performance issues** related to the ON-resistance of the conducting transistor, which we analyze rigorously in SECTION 2.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Transmission Gate — Detailed Operational Analysis

When the control signal $\phi = 1$ (logic HIGH):
- **NMOS** ($V_{GS,N} = V_{DD} - 0 = V_{DD}$): ON, conducting strongly in the direction of the input signal.
- **PMOS** ($V_{GS,P} = 0 - V_{DD} = -V_{DD}$): ON, conducting strongly in the opposite direction.

The two transistors are in **parallel** between nodes A (input) and B (output). Their **ON-resistances** are therefore in parallel:

$$R_{eq,TG} = R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P} = \frac{R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}}{R_{on,N} + R_{on,P}}$$

When $\phi = 0$ (logic LOW):
- Both NMOS and PMOS are OFF. The equivalent resistance is effectively **infinite**, isolating the two nodes.

### Why the TG Achieves Full Swing — The Engineering Logic

- For **V_out → V_DD** transition: the **PMOS** conducts strongly (its $V_{GS}$ is most negative, hence $\vert V_{GS,P} - V_{TP} \vert$ is maximum, giving the lowest R_on).
- For **V_out → 0** transition: the **NMOS** conducts strongly (its $V_{GS}$ is maximum at $V_{DD}$, giving the lowest R_on).
- The two transistors **complement** each other across the voltage sweep, ensuring a **low and roughly constant** R_eq throughout the transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The parallel NMOS-PMOS arrangement yields a **near-constant ON-resistance** versus input voltage — this is the secret of TG's excellent analog/RF performance, used in sample-and-hold circuits and analog multiplexers.

---

## 2.2 Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL) — Detailed Operational Analysis

A canonical **CPL 2-to-1 multiplexer** is shown in SECTION 4. The structure has:

1. **Two NMOS pull-down transistors** (N1, N2) gated by select signals S and $\bar{S}$ respectively, passing either input A or input B to the output node.
2. **Cross-coupled PMOS load** (P1, P2) at the output: the gate of P1 is tied to the output of P2 (which is the *complementary* output node $\bar{Y}$), and vice versa.
3. **Inverter** (optional, but typically present) to generate the complementary output Y from $\bar{Y}$.

### Step-by-Step Operation

- Suppose **S = 1, $\bar{S}$ = 0**, and **A = 1, B = 0**:
  - N1 is ON, passes A = 1. The output node (initially $\bar{Y}$) is pulled up by P1 only if $\bar{Y}$ is low — but P1's gate is connected to Y (which is the inverse of $\bar{Y}$).
  - The cross-coupled PMOS pair acts as a **positive feedback latch**: once $\bar{Y}$ is pulled below the PMOS threshold $\vert V_{TP} \vert$ below V_DD, the PMOS connected to the opposite output turns ON and **latches** the node to a full V_DD level.
- The result: **no threshold drop**, **full rail-to-rail swing**, and **complementary outputs** Y and $\bar{Y}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CPL Key Idea:** The threshold drop is a **DC problem**, solved by the **PMOS latch** (a DC restoring structure), while the **NMOS network** handles the **fast logic evaluation** (an AC advantage). CPL therefore combines the **speed of NMOS** with the **swing of CMOS**.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is a **high-yield reference** for KTU ESE questions on this topic. Memorize the threshold drop, the equivalent resistance, and the delay expressions.

| # | Quantity | Equation | Boundary / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | NMOS threshold drop | $V_{OH, NMOS} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ | Only when passing HIGH through NMOS |
| 2 | PMOS threshold drop | $V_{OL, PMOS} = \vert V_{TP} \vert$ | Only when passing LOW through PMOS |
| 3 | NMOS ON-resistance (linear region) | $R_{on,N} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}\left(V_{GS} - V_{TN} - \dfrac{V_{DS}}{2}\right)}$ | $V_{DS} < V_{GS} - V_{TN}$ |
| 4 | PMOS ON-resistance (linear region) | $R_{on,P} = \dfrac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox}\dfrac{W}{L}\left(V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert - \dfrac{V_{SD}}{2}\right)}$ | $V_{SD} < V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert$ |
| 5 | TG equivalent resistance | $R_{eq,TG} = R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$ | Both ON simultaneously |
| 6 | TG propagation delay (approx) | $t_{pd,TG} \approx 0.69 \, R_{eq,TG} \, C_L$ | First-order RC delay |
| 7 | CPL restoring time | $t_{restore} \approx \dfrac{C_L \, \vert V_{TP} \vert}{I_{PMOS}}$ | Time for PMOS latch to swing rail |
| 8 | Static power | $P_{static} = 0$ | For both TG (OFF) and CPL |
| 9 | ON/OFF control (TG) | ON when $\phi=1$: both devices conduct | OFF when $\phi=0$: both devices off |
| 10 | CPL transistor count (per MUX) | **6 transistors** (2 NMOS pass + 2 PMOS latch + 2 inverter) | vs 12+ for full CMOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Notice that **absolute value** is consistently written using `\vert` (e.g., $\vert V_{TP} \vert$) to keep the markdown table layout intact. **Never** use the bare vertical bar `|` in formulas inside tables.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Transmission Gates** are used as the **write access transistors in 6T SRAM cells**, the **pass elements in dynamic logic** (footless domino), the **switches in analog multiplexers** (CD4051, CD4053), and the **storage-transfer switches in master-slave flip-flops** in nearly every commercial CMOS standard cell library.
- **CPL** is used in **low-power, high-speed arithmetic units** of DSPs and microcontrollers. It has a smaller input capacitance (only NMOS gates at the input), so it drives **faster** than full CMOS at iso-area, at the cost of needing a complementary input. It was a workhorse in the **Differential Cascode Voltage Switch (DCVS)** family.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Threshold Voltage Drop in a Single NMOS Pass Transistor

### Problem Setup
An NMOS transistor with gate tied to V_DD, drain tied to V_DD (input = logic 1), source tied to a precharged output node (initially 0 V, with load capacitance C_L). Show that the output voltage saturates at $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{As } V_{out} \text{ rises, the source-to-gate voltage decreases:} \quad
V_{GS} &= V_G - V_S = V_{DD} - V_{out} \\
\text{The NMOS is ON while } V_{GS} &> V_{TN} \\
V_{DD} - V_{out} &> V_{TN} \\
V_{out} &< V_{DD} - V_{TN} \\
\text{When } V_{out} &\to V_{DD} - V_{TN}, \text{ then } V_{GS} = V_{TN} \\
\text{At this point, } I_{DS} &\to 0 \text{ (transistor turns off)} \\
\therefore \ V_{OH, \text{NMOS}} &= V_{DD} - V_{TN}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Logic:** The NMOS conducts only when its gate-to-source voltage exceeds V_TN. As the source (output) rises, the "driving force" $V_{GS}$ shrinks. When the output reaches $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$, the driving force vanishes exactly — the transistor is at the edge of conduction. This is why single-NMOS PTL cannot pass a strong "1".

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Equivalent Resistance of a Transmission Gate

### Problem Setup
An NMOS and PMOS in parallel, both ON, with input rising from 0 to V_DD. Find the equivalent resistance and the propagation delay.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given the standard long-channel linear-region model:} \quad
I_{DS} &= \mu \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} \left[ (V_{GS}-V_T) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2} \right] \\
\text{For small } V_{DS} \text{, neglect the } V_{DS}^2/2 \text{ term:} \quad
I_{DS} &\approx \mu \, C_{ox} \, \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS}-V_T) \, V_{DS} \\
\text{Define channel resistance for NMOS:} \quad
R_{on,N} &= \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_N (V_{GS} - V_{TN})} \\
\text{Define channel resistance for PMOS:} \quad
R_{on,P} &= \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} \left(\dfrac{W}{L}\right)_P (V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP}\vert)} \\
\text{Since the two transistors are in parallel:} \quad
I_{total} &= I_{N} + I_{P} = \frac{V_{DS}}{R_{on,N}} + \frac{V_{DS}}{R_{on,P}} = V_{DS} \left( \frac{1}{R_{on,N}} + \frac{1}{R_{on,P}} \right) \\
R_{eq,TG} &= \frac{V_{DS}}{I_{total}} = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{R_{on,N}} + \dfrac{1}{R_{on,P}}} = R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Logic:** The NMOS and PMOS act as **two parallel variable resistors** whose values change with V_out. As V_out rises, R_on,N increases (its V_GS shrinks) but R_on,P decreases (its V_SG remains large). The two trends **partially cancel**, giving a roughly flat R_eq across the entire voltage swing — a key TG advantage.

### Numerical Example (Typical 0.18 μm CMOS, V_DD = 1.8 V)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_n C_{ox} &= 270 \, \mu A/V^2, \quad \mu_p C_{ox} = 70 \, \mu A/V^2 \\
(W/L)_N &= (W/L)_P = 1, \quad V_{TN} = 0.5 \, V, \quad \vert V_{TP} \vert = 0.6 \, V \\
\text{Midpoint estimate: } V_{GS} - V_{TN} &\approx 0.5 \, V, \quad V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert \approx 0.5 \, V \\
R_{on,N} &= \frac{1}{270 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 0.5} \approx 7.4 \, k\Omega \\
R_{on,P} &= \frac{1}{70 \times 10^{-6} \times 1 \times 0.5} \approx 28.6 \, k\Omega \\
R_{eq,TG} &= 7.4 \, k\Omega \parallel 28.6 \, k\Omega \approx 5.9 \, k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Propagation Delay of a TG Driving a Capacitive Load

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Treating the TG as a single equivalent resistance } R_{eq,TG} \text{ charging } C_L: \quad
V_{out}(t) &= V_{final} \left( 1 - e^{-t / R_{eq,TG} C_L} \right) \\
\text{The 50\%-to-50\% delay (or } V_{DD}/2 \text{ to } V_{DD}/2 \text{) is found by setting } V_{out} = V_{DD}/2: \quad
\frac{V_{DD}}{2} &= V_{DD} \left( 1 - e^{-t_p / (R_{eq,TG} C_L)} \right) \\
e^{-t_p / (R_{eq,TG} C_L)} &= 0.5 \\
t_p &= \ln(2) \cdot R_{eq,TG} \, C_L \\
\therefore \ t_p &\approx 0.69 \, R_{eq,TG} \, C_L
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Logic:** The TG is essentially a first-order RC low-pass. The "ln(2) ≈ 0.69" comes from the natural logarithm at the 50% threshold. This delay scales **linearly** with the ON-resistance, which is why designers size up the W/L of TG transistors to drive large loads (at the cost of higher input capacitance and clock load).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: CPL Output Swing Restoration — Quantitative

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{When an NMOS pass transistor in CPL delivers a degraded "1":} \quad
V_{out} &= V_{DD} - V_{TN} \quad \text{(initially)} \\
\text{The cross-coupled PMOS has its gate at the complementary output } \bar{Y}: \quad
V_{SG,P} &= V_{DD} - V_{out} = V_{DD} - (V_{DD} - V_{TN}) = V_{TN} \\
V_{SG,P} - \vert V_{TP} \vert &= V_{TN} - \vert V_{TP} \vert \\
\text{If } V_{TN} &> \vert V_{TP} \vert, \text{ the PMOS is barely ON} \\
\text{The PMOS supplies a small current that charges } C_L: \quad
I_P &= \frac{1}{2} \mu_p C_{ox} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_P (V_{SG,P} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)^2 \\
\Delta V_{out} &= \frac{I_P \, \Delta t}{C_L} \\
\text{As } V_{out} \text{ rises further toward } V_{DD}, V_{SG,P} \text{ shrinks, the PMOS turns off naturally.} \quad
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Logic:** The PMOS **pulls the output up to a full V_DD**, then turns off — a self-terminating restoration. The cross-coupling ensures that while one PMOS is restoring Y, the other is restoring $\bar{Y}$, and they never fight each other (they reinforce). The trade-off is **slow restoration** when the overdrive ($V_{TN} - \vert V_{TP} \vert$) is small.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (CPL vs. TG vs. Static CMOS)

| Parameter | Static CMOS | NMOS PTL | **CPL** | **TG Logic** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output swing | Full | Degraded (1) | **Full** | **Full** |
| Static power | 0 | 0 | **0** | **0** (when OFF) |
| Transistors / MUX | ~12 | 4 | **6** | **6** (2 + 2 ctrl) |
| Speed (iso-area) | Medium | Fast | **Fastest** | **Fast** |
| Bidirectional? | N/A | Yes | **No** | **Yes** |
| Need complement? | No | No | **Yes** | **Yes (for $\bar{\phi}$)** |
| Threshold drop | No | Yes | **No** | **No** |
| Input cap | High (PMOS+N) | Low (NMOS) | **Low (NMOS)** | Medium |

---

## 3.6 Design Pitfalls and Resolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap #1:** Students often claim "the TG passes both 0 and 1 with zero resistance". This is **false** — the TG passes both with **finite and approximately constant resistance** equal to $R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$. The "zero threshold drop" refers to **voltage**, not resistance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap #2:** When asked "Why does CPL use only NMOS in the pass network?", the correct answer involves the **electron vs. hole mobility** ($\mu_n \approx 2$–$4 \times \mu_p$). NMOS is faster, so the logic evaluation is fast. The PMOS latch is slower but only needs to handle the **DC restoration**, not the fast switching — a division of labor.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Transmission Gate — Schematic and Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **functional architecture** of a Transmission Gate cell, including the parallel device structure, control signals, and the bidirectional I/O path.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[NODE A INPUT] -- VIN --> nodeX[X PARALLEL BUS]
    nodeB[CONTROL PHI] -- GATE N --> nodeN1[NMOS DEVICE]
    nodeC[CONTROL PHI BAR] -- GATE P --> nodeP1[PMOS DEVICE]
    nodeN1 -- PARALLEL CHANNEL --> nodeX
    nodeP1 -- PARALLEL CHANNEL --> nodeX
    nodeX -- VOUT --> nodeD[NODE B OUTPUT]
    nodeA -- BIDIRECTIONAL --> nodeD
    style nodeA fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeB fill:D9F2C0,stroke:#2A6A1A,color:#000
    style nodeC fill:F2C0D9,stroke:#6A1A2A,color:#000
    style nodeD fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeN1 fill:FFE08A,stroke:#7A5A1A,color:#000
    style nodeP1 fill:FFE08A,stroke:#7A5A1A,color:#000
    style nodeX fill:E6E6E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### Functional Description of the Diagram

- **NODE A INPUT** carries the signal $V_{in}$ from one side of the switch.
- **NODE B OUTPUT** receives the signal on the other side — and is **bidirectionally connected** to NODE A.
- **CONTROL PHI** drives the gate of the **NMOS DEVICE**; when PHI = 1, the NMOS is ON.
- **CONTROL PHI BAR** drives the gate of the **PMOS DEVICE**; when PHI BAR = 0 (i.e., PHI = 1), the PMOS is ON.
- Both devices contribute a parallel channel between the two nodes. When PHI = 0, both are off and the bus is **isolated**.

---

## 4.2 CPL 2-to-1 Multiplexer — Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block captures the **CPL MUX structure**: NMOS pass network, cross-coupled PMOS latch, and the inverter for the complementary output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeInA[INPUT A] -- GATE N1 --> nodeN1[NMOS N1]
    nodeSel[SELECT S] -- GATE N1 --> nodeN1
    nodeInB[INPUT B] -- GATE N2 --> nodeN2[NMOS N2]
    nodeSelBar[SELECT S BAR] -- GATE N2 --> nodeN2
    nodeN1 -- DRAIN --> nodeOutBar[NODE Y BAR OUTPUT]
    nodeN2 -- DRAIN --> nodeOutBar
    nodeOutBar -- GATE P2 --> nodeP2[PMOS P2 TO VDD]
    nodeOutBar -- INPUT INV --> nodeInv[CMOS INVERTER]
    nodeInv -- DRIVE --> nodeOut[NODE Y OUTPUT]
    nodeOut -- GATE P1 --> nodeP1[PMOS P1 TO VDD]
    nodeP1 -- DRAIN --> nodeOutBar
    style nodeInA fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeInB fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeSel fill:D9F2C0,stroke:#2A6A1A,color:#000
    style nodeSelBar fill:D9F2C0,stroke:#2A6A1A,color:#000
    style nodeN1 fill:FFE08A,stroke:#7A5A1A,color:#000
    style nodeN2 fill:FFE08A,stroke:#7A5A1A,color:#000
    style nodeP1 fill:F2C0D9,stroke:#6A1A2A,color:#000
    style nodeP2 fill:F2C0D9,stroke:#6A1A2A,color:#000
    style nodeOut fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeOutBar fill:C0D9F2,stroke:#1A4A7A,color:#000
    style nodeInv fill:E6E6E6,stroke:#333,color:#000
```

### Functional Description of the Diagram

- **INPUT A** and **INPUT B** are the two data inputs.
- **SELECT S** and **SELECT S BAR** are complementary control signals.
- **NMOS N1** passes INPUT A to the output node when S = 1; **NMOS N2** passes INPUT B when $\bar{S}$ = 1.
- The shared output node is **NODE Y BAR** (the *complementary* output).
- **PMOS P1** and **PMOS P2** form a **cross-coupled latch** between V_DD and NODE Y BAR, restoring full-rail swing.
- The **CMOS INVERTER** generates the true output **NODE Y** from NODE Y BAR.

---

## 4.3 Truth Table and Switch State Reference

| Condition | $\phi$ | $\bar{\phi}$ | NMOS Gate | PMOS Gate | TG State | Signal Path |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| ON | 1 | 0 | V_DD | 0 | **CONDUCTING** | A ↔ B (full swing) |
| OFF | 0 | 1 | 0 | V_DD | **ISOLATED** | High-Z between A and B |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct, KTU University Exam-style Part A questions, each carrying **3 marks**. The expected length is a tight paragraph and a labelled diagram (where applicable).

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the threshold voltage drop problem in a single NMOS pass transistor with the help of a neat sketch. State the final output voltage expression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

When the gate of an NMOS pass transistor is tied to V_DD and the input is logic HIGH (= V_DD), the source (output node) rises. The NMOS remains ON only while $V_{GS} = V_{DD} - V_{out} > V_{TN}$. As $V_{out}$ rises, $V_{GS}$ decreases, and the transistor turns off when $V_{out}$ reaches $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$. Therefore the maximum HIGH output is **degraded**:

$$V_{OH, NMOS} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$$

**Sketch (1 mark):** NMOS with gate = V_DD, drain = V_DD, source = V_out, with a body-grounded symbol showing the source rising toward V_DD but stalling at $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a CMOS transmission gate. Why is it called a bidirectional switch?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A transmission gate (TG) is formed by **parallel connection of an NMOS and a PMOS** (drain of NMOS tied to drain of PMOS, source of NMOS tied to source of PMOS). The gates are driven by **complementary control signals** $\phi$ and $\bar{\phi}$.

- When $\phi = 1$ (and $\bar{\phi} = 0$): both NMOS and PMOS are ON, providing a low-resistance path in **both directions** (from input to output, or output to input).
- When $\phi = 0$: both are OFF, isolating the two nodes.

It is called **bidirectional** because the source and drain of a MOS transistor are symmetric, and the parallel arrangement lets current flow equally well in either direction — the TG behaves as a near-ideal closed switch that can carry signal in either sense. **(2 marks for the operation + 1 mark for the bidirectional reasoning).**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE Part B questions on this module follow the standard pattern: a single 14-mark question with **internal choice** between two alternatives (e.g., Q. (a) OR (b)). Each alternative has sub-parts typically carrying 7 marks each. We provide **two fully solved 14-mark questions** so the student has the choice.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Draw the circuit diagram of a **CMOS transmission gate** and explain its operation in detail. Derive the expression for the equivalent ON-resistance of the TG. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Solution

**Circuit Diagram (2 marks):**
- NMOS and PMOS in parallel.
- Gates driven by $\phi$ and $\bar{\phi}$ respectively.
- Common input node A, common output node B.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- $\phi = 1$, $\bar{\phi} = 0$: NMOS ON (V_GS = V_DD), PMOS ON (V_SG = V_DD). Both conduct → switch closed.
- $\phi = 0$, $\bar{\phi} = 1$: NMOS OFF, PMOS OFF. Switch open (high impedance).
- NMOS strong for V_out → 0 transition; PMOS strong for V_out → V_DD transition. **Full rail-to-rail swing** is achieved with no threshold drop.

**Equivalent Resistance (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{on,N} &= \frac{1}{\mu_n C_{ox} (W/L)_N (V_{GS} - V_{TN})} \\
R_{on,P} &= \frac{1}{\mu_p C_{ox} (W/L)_P (V_{SG} - \vert V_{TP} \vert)} \\
R_{eq,TG} &= R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P} = \frac{R_{on,N} \cdot R_{on,P}}{R_{on,N} + R_{on,P}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL)** with a 2-to-1 multiplexer example. Discuss how CPL overcomes the threshold voltage drop problem. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Model Solution

**CPL MUX Circuit (2 marks):**
- Two NMOS pass transistors (N1, N2) with sources tied to inputs A and B, drains tied to the common output node $\bar{Y}$.
- Gates driven by complementary select signals S and $\bar{S}$.
- Two cross-coupled PMOS pull-ups (P1, P2) connecting $\bar{Y}$ and Y to V_DD, with gates cross-coupled.
- A CMOS inverter to generate Y from $\bar{Y}$.

**Operation (3 marks):**
- When S = 1: N1 passes input A to $\bar{Y}$. The cross-coupled PMOS latch senses the state of $\bar{Y}$ and Y, and **restores** the output to a full V_DD (if HIGH) or 0 V (if LOW), eliminating the threshold drop.
- The complementary outputs Y and $\bar{Y}$ are simultaneously available — useful for differential signaling.

**Threshold Drop Elimination (2 marks):**
- The NMOS pass network is fast (high mobility), but suffers $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$ degradation.
- The **cross-coupled PMOS** provides DC restoration: when $\bar{Y}$ sits at $V_{DD} - V_{TN}$, the PMOS gate is at Y (the opposite logic level), giving just enough overdrive to charge $\bar{Y}$ fully to V_DD.
- Net result: **fast NMOS evaluation** + **full-swing CMOS output** = best of both worlds.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Compare **CPL and Transmission Gate logic** in terms of transistor count, output swing, speed, static power, bidirectional operation, and threshold drop. **(7 marks)**

#### (a) Model Solution (Tabular Comparison)

| Parameter | **CPL** | **TG Logic** | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Transistor count (MUX) | 6 (2 NMOS + 2 PMOS + 2 inv) | 6 (2 MOS + 2 ctrl inverter if internal) | 1 |
| Output swing | Full (0 to V_DD) via PMOS latch | Full (0 to V_DD) via parallel devices | 1 |
| Speed | Fast (NMOS pass network) | Fast (parallel NMOS+PMOS) | 1 |
| Static power | Zero (PMOS turns off at full V_DD) | Zero (when OFF) | 1 |
| Bidirectional | No (single-ended evaluation) | **Yes** (fully symmetric) | 1 |
| Threshold drop | Eliminated by PMOS latch | Eliminated by parallel N+P | 1 |
| Need complementary inputs | **Yes** (differential) | **Yes** ($\bar{\phi}$ needed) | 1 |

**Conclusion (1 line):** Both are full-swing, zero-static-power alternatives to static CMOS, but TG is bidirectional while CPL is single-ended yet produces true & complement outputs.

---

**Q. (b)** A transmission gate is used to charge a load capacitance of **C_L = 100 fF** through a V_DD = 1.8 V supply. The equivalent ON-resistance of the TG is **R_eq = 5.9 kΩ**. Calculate: **(i)** the 50% propagation delay, and **(ii)** the energy dissipated in the load capacitor per switching event. **(7 marks)**

#### (b) Model Solution

**(i) Propagation Delay (3 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_p &= 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,TG} \cdot C_L \\
&= 0.69 \times 5.9 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \\
&= 0.69 \times 5.9 \times 10^{-10} \\
&= 4.07 \times 10^{-10} \, s \\
t_p &\approx 407 \, ps
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Energy Dissipated (4 marks):**

The energy stored in a capacitor charged to V_DD:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{stored} &= \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times (1.8)^2 \\
&= 0.5 \times 100 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.24 \\
&= 162 \times 10^{-15} \, J = 162 \, fJ
\end{aligned}
$$

The energy **supplied** by the source during charging (regardless of R_eq):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{source} &= C_L V_{DD}^2 = 2 \times E_{stored} = 324 \, fJ
\end{aligned}
$$

Half is stored in C_L, half is dissipated in R_eq (the TG):

$$
E_{dissipated} = 162 \, fJ
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Important observation:** The energy dissipated in the TG during one full charge cycle equals $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$, **independent of R_eq**. The R_eq only affects the *time* taken, not the energy. This is the foundational result of digital CMOS energy analysis.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the PMOS latch in CPL diagrams (–2 marks):** Many students draw only the NMOS pass network for a "CPL" MUX and forget the cross-coupled PMOS load — but it is precisely this load that *defines* CPL. A circuit without the latch is just standard NMOS PTL with the threshold drop problem.
> 2. **Calling the TG "zero-resistance" (–1 mark):** The TG has a **finite, non-zero** equivalent resistance equal to $R_{on,N} \parallel R_{on,P}$. It is the *threshold drop* that is zero, not the resistance.
> 3. **Confusing "bidirectional" with "differential" (–1 mark):** Bidirectional = signal flows both ways through the same TG. Differential (in CPL) = two complementary signals are present, but the signal path is **single-ended** (input flows one way only).
> 4. **Forgetting the inverter's contribution to delay in CPL (–1 mark):** The CMOS inverter at the output of a CPL MUX adds a stage delay, which should be accounted for in timing analysis.
> 5. **Wrong $\bar{\phi}$ polarity (–1 mark):** When $\phi = 1$ the PMOS is ON — its gate is at $\bar{\phi} = 0$. When $\phi = 0$ the PMOS gate is at $\bar{\phi} = 1 = V_{DD}$, turning it OFF. Students often get the polarity reversed on the PMOS gate.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Single NMOS pass transistor** cannot pass a full HIGH — it degrades by $V_{TN}$: $V_{OH} = V_{DD} - V_{TN}$.
- **Single PMOS pass transistor** cannot pass a full LOW — it degrades by $\vert V_{TP} \vert$: $V_{OL} = \vert V_{TP} \vert$.
- **Transmission Gate (TG)** = NMOS ∥ PMOS with complementary control ($\phi$ and $\bar{\phi}$). **Full swing, bidirectional, finite R_eq = R_on,N ∥ R_on,P.**
- **Complementary Pass Transistor Logic (CPL)** = NMOS pass network + cross-coupled PMOS latch. Produces **true and complementary outputs**, full swing restored by the latch.
- **Both TG and CPL have zero static power**, making them CMOS-compatible.
- **TG is bidirectional** (signal flows either way); **CPL is differential** (true + complement simultaneously).
- **TG delay**: $t_p \approx 0.69 R_{eq,TG} C_L$ — scales linearly with load capacitance.
- **Energy per TG switching event** = $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (stored) + $\frac{1}{2} C_L V_{DD}^2$ (dissipated) = total $C_L V_{DD}^2$.
- **Applications**: TG → SRAM bit-line access, analog switches, dynamic logic, flip-flops. CPL → high-speed adders, multipliers, register files in DSPs.
- **Transistor count for a 2:1 MUX**: static CMOS ≈ 12, CPL = 6, TG = 6 (2 + 2 for control inverter, optional).
- **Always pair** $\phi$ with $\bar{\phi}$ in TG designs — the complementary control is the soul of the circuit.
- **KTU 2024 must-know words**: "full-swing restoration", "threshold drop", "bidirectional", "complementary outputs", "cross-coupled PMOS load", "ON-resistance", "equivalent resistance".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Realization of Functions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Realization of Functions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Realization of Functions** in VLSI refers to the systematic methodology of implementing Boolean logic functions (AND, OR, NAND, NOR, XOR, complex functions) using physical CMOS transistor networks on a silicon die. The goal is to map an abstract Boolean expression $F(X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$ onto a concrete arrangement of **PMOS** (Pull-Up Network / PUN) and **NMOS** (Pull-Down Network / PDN) transistors such that the static CMOS gate satisfies correct logic levels, noise margins, and drive strength.

In complementary (static) CMOS, every Boolean function $F$ is realized using **two dual networks**:
- A **Pull-Up Network (PUN)** built exclusively from PMOS transistors, which connects the output $F$ to $V_{DD}$ when $F=1$.
- A **Pull-Down Network (PDN)** built exclusively from NMOS transistors, which connects the output $F$ to **GND** when $F=0$.

The fundamental design rule is that the PUN and PDN must be **logical duals** of each other, ensuring the output node is always driven to either rail (never floating).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Way Railway Switch"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a railway signal box controlling a track switch.
> - The **PMOS Pull-Up Network** is the "uphill track" — it pulls the output *up* to $V_{DD}$ (like a high elevation), but **only when the inputs are LOW** (0). PMOS transistors are "ON" when their gate input is **0**.
> - The **NMOS Pull-Down Network** is the "downhill track" — it pulls the output *down* to **GND** (like sea level), but **only when the inputs are HIGH** (1). NMOS transistors are "ON" when their gate input is **1**.
> - At **any instant**, exactly one track is active — never both (which would cause a short circuit / "crowbar current"), and never neither (which would leave the output floating).

This guarantees a **full-rail output swing** ($0$ V to $V_{DD}$) and excellent **noise immunity**.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- Threshold voltage for NMOS: $V_{tn} \approx 0.5 \text{ V}$ (typical for 180 nm)
- Threshold voltage for PMOS: $V_{tp} \approx -0.5 \text{ V}$
- **Mobility ratio:** $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$ to $3$ (NMOS is ~2–3× stronger than PMOS of the same size)
- **Standard sizing rule:** $(W/L)_p \approx 2$ to $3 \times (W/L)_n$ to equalize rise/fall times
- Supply voltage: $V_{DD}$ (typically **1.8 V** for 180 nm process)
- Logic levels: $V_{OH} = V_{DD}$, $V_{OL} = 0 \text{ V}$

### 1.4 Classification of CMOS Realization Styles

| Realization Style | PUN | PDN | Signal Path Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Static Complementary CMOS** | PMOS network | NMOS network | Always restoring |
| **Pseudo-NMOS** | Single always-ON PMOS load | NMOS logic | Ratioed logic |
| **Pass Transistor Logic (PTL)** | Optional | NMOS/PMOS passes | Switch network |
| **Transmission Gate (TG)** | Parallel PMOS+NMOS | — | Bidirectional |
| **Dynamic CMOS / Domino** | Single PMOS precharge | NMOS logic | Clocked |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 3 specifically emphasizes **Static Complementary CMOS** realization of both simple and **complex** (AOI / OAI) functions. You must master the rules for deriving the PUN from the PDN using **De Morgan duality**.



<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Rules for Static CMOS Realization

To realize any Boolean function $F$ in static CMOS, follow these four cardinal rules:

**Rule 1 — The Duality Principle:**
- The PUN is the **dual** of the PDN.
- The PDN implements $F$ in its true (uncomplemented) form.
- The PUN implements $\bar{F}$ (the complement) using PMOS transistors.

**Rule 2 — Series ↔ Parallel Mapping:**

> [!NOTE]
> When a variable appears **uncomplemented (X)** in the PDN → it uses an **NMOS** transistor.
> When a variable appears **complemented ($\bar{X}$)** in the PDN → it uses a **NMOS transistor gated by $\bar{X}$**, which is electrically equivalent to a **PMOS** in the PUN.

| PDN Structure | PUN Structure (Dual) |
|---|---|
| Series NMOS (AND of inputs) | Parallel PMOS (OR of inputs) |
| Parallel NMOS (OR of inputs) | Series PMOS (AND of inputs) |

**Rule 3 — Conducting Conditions:**
- **PDN conducts** when $F = 1$ (pulls output to GND)
- **PUN conducts** when $\bar{F} = 1$ (pulls output to $V_{DD}$)
- **PDN and PUN must NEVER conduct simultaneously** (no static short-circuit path from $V_{DD}$ to GND).

**Rule 4 — Output Node Integrity:**
- At all input combinations, the output must be driven to a known DC value.
- A "floating" output node is **forbidden** in static CMOS.

### 2.2 Why PMOS in PUN and NMOS in PDN? (The Mobility Argument)

The threshold drop of PMOS passing a "strong 1" is acceptable because the source is tied to $V_{DD}$ — there is no threshold drop. Conversely, NMOS passing a "strong 0" is acceptable because the source is tied to GND.

- A **single NMOS** passes a weak "1" (loses one $V_t$), so it is unsuitable for PUN.
- A **single PMOS** passes a weak "0" (gains $|V_t|$ in magnitude), so it is unsuitable for PDN.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Units / Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F(X_1,\ldots,X_n) = \overline{\text{PDN conduction condition}}$ | Function defined by PDN | Boolean |
| 2 | $W_p / W_n \approx 2$ to $3$ | Sizing to equalize $t_{LH}$ and $t_{HL}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 3 | $C_{in}(N) \approx 3 C_{ox} W_n L_n$ | Input gate capacitance of NMOS | Farads (F) |
| 4 | $C_{in}(P) \approx 3 C_{ox} W_p L_p$ | Input gate capacitance of PMOS | Farads (F) |
| 5 | $t_{pHL} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,n} \cdot C_{L}$ | High-to-Low propagation delay | seconds (s) |
| 6 | $t_{pLH} \approx 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,p} \cdot C_{L}$ | Low-to-High propagation delay | seconds (s) |
| 7 | $R_{eq} \propto L / ( \mu C_{ox} W (V_{DD} - V_t) )$ | Transistor on-resistance | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| 8 | $F_{\text{PUN}} = \overline{F_{\text{PDN}}}$ | Duality relation | Boolean |
| 9 | Static power $P_{stat} = 0$ | Ideal static CMOS has no static current | Watts (W) |
| 10 | Dynamic power $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching power | Watts (W) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the table above, the symbols $C_{ox}$, $R_{eq,n}$, $R_{eq,p}$, and $C_L$ represent oxide capacitance per unit area, equivalent ON-resistance of NMOS, equivalent ON-resistance of PMOS, and load capacitance at the output node, respectively.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The realization methodology is the **backbone of every standard cell library** used in Application-Specific Integrated Circuits (ASICs). Synthesis tools such as **Synopsys Design Compiler** map RTL Verilog to these pre-characterized CMOS gates. A design engineer who understands the PUN/PDN duality can:
- Estimate area and delay of a synthesized block.
- Choose between AOI (AND-OR-Invert) and OAI (OR-AND-Invert) implementations.
- Diagnose short-circuit faults and leakage paths at the transistor level.



<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: PDN-PUN Duality from First Principles

**Problem:** Derive the CMOS gate that realizes $F = \overline{A \cdot (B + C)}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the function and its complement.**

$$F = \overline{A \cdot (B + C)}$$

$$\bar{F} = A \cdot (B + C) \quad \text{(complement of the output, which equals the PDN conduction condition)}$$

**Step 2 — Build the PDN from $\bar{F}$.**

The PDN must conduct when $\bar{F} = 1$, i.e., when $A=1$ **AND** ($B=1$ **OR** $C=1$).

- Variable $A$ appears un-inverted → use **NMOS gated by A**.
- Variables $B$ and $C$ appear un-inverted → use **NMOS gated by B** and **NMOS gated by C** in **parallel** (OR relation).
- The parallel group ($B \parallel C$) is placed in **series** with $A$ (AND relation).

PDN structure: $\text{NMOS}(A)$ in series with $[\text{NMOS}(B) \parallel \text{NMOS}(C)]$.

**Step 3 — Build the PUN as the dual.**

Apply the duality transformation:
- Series $\to$ Parallel
- Parallel $\to$ Series
- All transistors become **PMOS** with the same gate signal as their NMOS counterparts.

PUN structure: $\text{PMOS}(A)$ in **parallel** with $[\text{PMOS}(B)$ in **series** with $\text{PMOS}(C)]$.

**Step 4 — Verification of the function.**

Verify a few input combinations:

| A | B | C | PDN Conducts? | PUN Conducts? | Output F |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | No (A=0 → series NMOS OFF) | Yes (PMOS(A) ON) | 1 |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | No (A=0) | Yes (PMOS(A) ON) | 1 |
| 1 | 0 | 0 | No (B and C both 0 → parallel branch OFF) | Yes (PMOS(B) & PMOS(C) both ON) | 1 |
| 1 | 1 | 0 | Yes (A=1 AND B=1) | No (PMOS(B) OFF) | 0 |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Yes (A=1 AND (B=1 OR C=1)) | No | 0 |

**Step 5 — Confirm logical identity.**

For the row $A=1, B=1, C=0$: the function gives $F = \overline{1 \cdot (1+0)} = \overline{1} = 0$. ✓

This matches the table, confirming our realization is correct.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Complex CMOS Gate Mapping

For a function of the form $F = \overline{\text{NAND(NMOS network)}}$ in general, the mapping is:

$$
F = \overline{X_1 \cdot X_2 \cdot \ldots \cdot X_k} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{PDN}} \quad \text{Series NMOS}
$$

$$
F = \overline{X_1 + X_2 + \ldots + X_k} \quad \xrightarrow{\text{PDN}} \quad \text{Parallel NMOS}
$$

For **Compound (AOI/OAI) functions**, apply the rule recursively:

$$
F = \overline{A \cdot (B + C) + D \cdot (E + F)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PDN: } [A \cdot (B \parallel C)] \parallel [D \cdot (E \parallel F)]
$$

$$
F = \overline{(A + B) \cdot (C + D)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PDN: } (A \parallel B) \text{ in series with } (C \parallel D)
$$

### 3.3 Worked Example: XOR Realization

The XOR function $F = A \oplus B = A\bar{B} + \bar{A}B$ requires 12 transistors in pure static CMOS due to its non-inverting nature. The standard approach is to recognize the equivalent form:

$$A \oplus B = \overline{\overline{A\bar{B}} \cdot \overline{\bar{A}B}} = \overline{\overline{A\bar{B}} + \overline{\bar{A}B}}$$

But the KTU Module 3 focus is the **AOI / OAI canonical forms**. The 4-variable XOR is not in this family, so a transmission-gate XOR (6 transistors) is preferred — see Section 4.

### 3.4 Power-Delay Derivation for a 2-Input NAND

**Given:** Two NMOS of size $(W/L)_n$ in series for PDN, two PMOS of size $(W/L)_p \approx 2(W/L)_n$ in parallel for PUN. Load capacitance = $C_L$.

**Worst-case (both inputs switching) $t_{pHL}$:**

Since two NMOS are in series, the equivalent drive resistance is **twice** that of a single NMOS:

$$
R_{eq,n,\text{series}} = 2 R_{eq,n}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 2 R_{eq,n} \cdot C_L = 1.38 R_{eq,n} C_L
$$

**Worst-case $t_{pLH}$:**

A single PMOS in parallel drives the output, so:

$$
t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot R_{eq,p} \cdot C_L \approx 0.69 \cdot 2 R_{eq,n} \cdot C_L = 1.38 R_{eq,n} C_L
$$

With proper sizing $(W/L)_p = 2 (W/L)_n$, both delays match: $t_{pHL} \approx t_{pLH}$. This is the key insight the examiner expects.

### 3.5 Logical Effort Quick Derivation (Optional but KTU-Relevant)

The **logical effort** $g$ of a gate is the ratio of its input capacitance to that of an inverter delivering the same current. For a 2-input NAND:

$$
g_{\text{NAND2}} = \frac{C_{in,\text{gate}}}{C_{in,\text{inverter}}} = \frac{2C_{in,n}}{C_{in,n} + C_{in,p}}
$$

With the standard sizing $(W/L)_p = 2(W/L)_n$ and $C_{in,p} = 2 C_{in,n}$:

$$
g_{\text{NAND2}} = \frac{2 C_{in,n}}{C_{in,n} + 2 C_{in,n}} = \frac{2}{3} \approx 0.67
$$

This **less than unity** logical effort is why NAND gates are faster than inverters of equivalent input capacitance for low fan-out — a fact often tested in KTU.



<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: CMOS Gate Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs["INPUT VECTORS"]
        A1["A"]
        B1["B"]
        C1["C"]
    end

    subgraph PUN["PULL-UP NETWORK (PMOS)"]
        PA1["PMOS A"]
        PB1["PMOS B"]
        PC1["PMOS C"]
    end

    subgraph PDN["PULL-DOWN NETWORK (NMOS)"]
        NA1["NMOS A"]
        NB1["NMOS B"]
        NC1["NMOS C"]
    end

    VDD["VDD Rail"]:::vdd
    GND["GND Rail"]:::gnd
    OUT["OUTPUT NODE F"]:::outnode

    VDD --> PA1
    PA1 --> OUT
    VDD --> PB1
    PB1 --> OUT
    VDD --> PC1
    PC1 --> OUT

    A1 --> PA1
    A1 --> NA1
    B1 --> PB1
    B1 --> NB1
    C1 --> PC1
    C1 --> NC1

    NA1 --> NB1
    NB1 --> NC1
    NC1 --> GND

    classDef vdd fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959,color:#000
    classDef outnode fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003d7a,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> This block diagram illustrates a generic 3-input CMOS gate. The **PMOS block** is connected to **$V_{DD}$**, the **NMOS block** to **GND**, and the output node $F$ is taken from the common junction. Input signals $A, B, C$ drive the gates of corresponding transistors in both networks.

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: AOI Gate Realizing $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD["VDD"]:::vdd

    subgraph PUNsub["PUN: PMOS NETWORK"]
        PA["PMOS A"]
        PB["PMOS B"]
        PC["PMOS C"]
        PD["PMOS D"]
    end

    subgraph PDNsub["PDN: NMOS NETWORK"]
        NA["NMOS A"]
        NB["NMOS B"]
        NC["NMOS C"]
        ND["NMOS D"]
    end

    GND["GND"]:::gnd
    Fout["F = NOT A*B + C*D"]:::outnode

    VDD --> PA --> PB
    VDD --> PC --> PD
    PA --> Fout
    PC --> Fout
    PD --> Fout
    PB --> Fout

    Fout --> NA
    Fout --> NB
    Fout --> NC
    Fout --> ND

    NA --> NB --> GND
    NC --> ND --> GND

    classDef vdd fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef gnd fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#595959,color:#000
    classDef outnode fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003d7a,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Flow: Design Procedure for CMOS Realization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Start with Boolean Function F"]:::step
    S2["Step 2: Find F-bar in SOP form"]:::step
    S3["Step 3: Draw PDN using F-bar as conduction condition"]:::step
    S4["Step 4: Apply Duality: series-parallel-parallel-series swap"]:::step
    S5["Step 5: Convert all transistors to PMOS in PUN"]:::step
    S6["Step 6: Size PMOS as 2x to 3x of NMOS for matched drive"]:::step
    S7["Step 7: Verify with truth table"]:::step

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S7

    classDef step fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003d7a,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid: Transmission-Gate XOR (for completeness)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input A"]:::input
    B["Input B"]:::input
    Y["Output Y = A XOR B"]:::outnode

    subgraph TG1["TRANSMISSION GATE 1"]
        TGN1["NMOS gate=B"]
        TGP1["PMOS gate=B-bar"]
    end

    subgraph TG2["TRANSMISSION GATE 2"]
        TGN2["NMOS gate=B-bar"]
        TGP2["PMOS gate=B"]
    end

    subgraph INV["INVERTER"]
        I1["NOT gate"]
    end

    A --> TG1
    A --> TG2
    TG1 --> Y
    TG2 --> Y
    INV --> TGN2
    INV --> TGP1

    classDef input fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#bf9000,color:#000
    classDef outnode fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003d7a,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The transmission-gate XOR uses **6 transistors** versus the 12 needed for a full CMOS XOR. KTU Module 3 frequently tests your ability to choose the **minimal-transistor realization** for a given function.



<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
> State the rules for realizing the Pull-Up Network (PUN) and Pull-Down Network (PDN) in static complementary CMOS logic. Why is it mandatory that the two networks be duals of each other?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **PDN Construction (1 mark):** The PDN is built using **NMOS transistors** to conduct when the output $F$ should be LOW (i.e., $F=0$). The conduction condition of the PDN is the **complement** of the function, $\bar{F}$. Series NMOS implement AND; parallel NMOS implement OR.

2. **PUN Construction (1 mark):** The PUN is built using **PMOS transistors** to conduct when $F=1$ (i.e., when $\bar{F}=0$). It is the **dual** of the PDN: every series branch becomes parallel, every parallel branch becomes series, and all transistors switch from NMOS to PMOS.

3. **Duality Mandate (1 mark):** Duality ensures that the PUN and PDN **never conduct simultaneously**, eliminating the static short-circuit path from $V_{DD}$ to GND. It also guarantees that the output node is always driven to a defined rail voltage, preventing a floating high-impedance state, and yields **zero static power dissipation** in steady state.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
> Why are PMOS transistors used in the Pull-Up Network and NMOS transistors in the Pull-Down Network of a static CMOS gate? Justify with reference to threshold voltage drop.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **NMOS passes strong 0 (1 mark):** When an NMOS transistor pulls down to GND, the source is at **0 V**, so $V_{GS} \ge V_{tn}$ is easily satisfied and the output reaches a full **strong 0** ($V_{OL} = 0$ V). This makes NMOS ideal for the PDN.

2. **PMOS passes strong 1 (1 mark):** When a PMOS transistor pulls up to $V_{DD}$, the source is at $V_{DD}$, so $V_{SG} \ge \vert V_{tp} \vert$ is satisfied, delivering a full **strong 1** ($V_{OH} = V_{DD}$). This makes PMOS ideal for the PUN.

3. **Threshold loss argument (1 mark):** If NMOS were used in the PUN, passing a logic '1' would degrade the output by one $V_{tn}$ (weak 1), causing noise-margin loss. If PMOS were used in the PDN, passing a logic '0' would not reach GND fully because of $\vert V_{tp} \vert$ body-effect issues. The mobility asymmetry $\mu_n \approx 2\mu_p$ further justifies using NMOS (faster) in the critical pull-down path.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** Realize the Boolean function $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$ using static complementary CMOS. Draw the transistor-level schematic and explain the operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the gate realized in part (a), determine the worst-case propagation delays $t_{pHL}$ and $t_{pLH}$ in terms of the equivalent on-resistances of NMOS and PMOS. Assume a load capacitance $C_L$ at the output. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Identify $\bar{F}$ (1 mark):**
$$\bar{F} = A \cdot B + C \cdot D$$

**Step 2 — Build the PDN (2 marks):**
The PDN conducts when $\bar{F}=1$. The function $\bar{F} = A \cdot B + C \cdot D$ corresponds to:
- Two parallel branches: $[A \cdot B]$ and $[C \cdot D]$.
- Each branch is a **series** combination of two NMOS.
- So: $\text{NMOS}(A) - \text{NMOS}(B)$ in parallel with $\text{NMOS}(C) - \text{NMOS}(D)$.

**Step 3 — Build the PUN as the dual (2 marks):**
- Two PMOS in series for each original parallel branch → two PMOS in **parallel**.
- Within each branch, series becomes parallel.
- So: $[\text{PMOS}(A) \parallel \text{PMOS}(B)]$ in **series** with $[\text{PMOS}(C) \parallel \text{PMOS}(D)]$.

**Step 4 — Connect and verify (2 marks):**
- PUN top end to $V_{DD}$; PUN bottom end to output node $F$.
- PDN top end to output $F$; PDN bottom end to GND.
- Verify: When $A=B=1$ or $C=D=1$, the PDN conducts, pulling $F$ low. Otherwise the PUN conducts, pulling $F$ to $V_{DD}$. Output is always driven.

**Transistor Schematic:**

```
              VDD
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
       PA            PC
        |             |
       PB            PD
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
               F -----> (output)
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
       NA            NC
        |             |
       NB            ND
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
              GND
```
(where PA,PB = PMOS gated by A,B; PC,PD = PMOS gated by C,D; NA,NB,NC,ND = corresponding NMOS)

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Worst-case analysis (7 marks):**

**Case 1: $t_{pHL}$ — output falling (PDN active) (3 marks):**

The worst-case conduction path through the PDN is when **one** of the two parallel branches conducts — this uses only **two NMOS in series**.

$$
R_{PDN,\text{worst}} = 2 R_{eq,n}
$$

$$
t_{pHL} = 0.69 \cdot 2 R_{eq,n} \cdot C_L = 1.38 R_{eq,n} C_L
$$

**Case 2: $t_{pLH}$ — output rising (PUN active) (2 marks):**

The PUN worst-case path requires **two PMOS in series** (because the parallel branches are stacked in series).

$$
R_{PUN,\text{worst}} = 2 R_{eq,p}
$$

$$
t_{pLH} = 0.69 \cdot 2 R_{eq,p} \cdot C_L = 1.38 R_{eq,p} C_L
$$

**Matched delay (2 marks):** To equalize $t_{pHL} = t_{pLH}$, set:

$$
R_{eq,p} = R_{eq,n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{W_p}{W_n} = \frac{\mu_n}{\mu_p} \approx 2 \text{ to } 3
$$

Thus, sizing each PMOS to about **2× to 3× the width** of an NMOS ensures symmetric rise and fall times.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** With a neat transistor-level schematic, realize the function $F = \overline{(A+B) \cdot (C+D)}$ in static CMOS. Identify whether it is an AOI or OAI gate. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare the AOI and OAI realizations in terms of transistor count, speed, and input capacitance. Mention one application where each is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Find $\bar{F}$ (1 mark):**
$$\bar{F} = (A+B) \cdot (C+D)$$

**Step 2 — Build the PDN (2 marks):**
The PDN conducts when $\bar{F}=1$, which means both $(A+B)=1$ AND $(C+D)=1$.
- $(A+B)$ → **parallel** NMOS gated by A and B.
- $(C+D)$ → **parallel** NMOS gated by C and D.
- These two groups are in **series** (AND).

**Step 3 — Build the PUN as the dual (2 marks):**
- $(A+B)$ in PDN becomes series PMOS $(A) - (B)$ in PUN.
- $(C+D)$ in PDN becomes series PMOS $(C) - (D)$ in PUN.
- The series in PDN becomes **parallel** in PUN.

So: $[\text{PMOS}(A) - \text{PMOS}(B)]$ in **parallel** with $[\text{PMOS}(C) - \text{PMOS}(D)]$.

**Step 4 — Identify gate type (1 mark):**
The function $F = \overline{(A+B)\cdot(C+D)}$ has an **OR** inside, then an **AND**, then an inversion → this is an **OAI (OR-AND-Invert)** gate.

**Step 5 — Draw schematic (1 mark):**

```
              VDD
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
       PA            PC
        |             |
       PB            PD
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
               F -----> (output)
               |
        +------+------+
        |             |
       NA || NB      NC || ND
        |             |
        +------+------+
               |
              GND
```

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

| Parameter | AOI Gate $\overline{AB+CD}$ | OAI Gate $\overline{(A+B)(C+D)}$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Transistor Count** | 8 (4 PMOS + 4 NMOS) | 8 (4 PMOS + 4 NMOS) |
| **PDN Topology** | Series-parallel of NMOS | Parallel-series of NMOS |
| **Worst-case $t_{pHL}$** | Two NMOS in series per branch | Two NMOS in parallel per branch → faster pull-down |
| **Worst-case $t_{pLH}$** | Single PMOS path (good) | Two PMOS in series (worse) |
| **Input Capacitance** | Each input drives 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS | Each input drives 1 PMOS + 1 NMOS (similar) |
| **Preferred Application** | High-speed critical paths where pull-up is critical | Low-power designs where PDN speed matters |

(7 marks for this comparison, distributed as: 2 marks for transistor-count and topology, 2 marks for speed analysis, 2 marks for input capacitance, 1 mark for application preference.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the dual relationship:** Many students draw the PUN by inverting the schematic of the PDN at the *transistor* level instead of at the *network* level. Always apply duality to the entire network, not individual transistors.
> 2. **Mixing up complemented and uncomplemented inputs:** If a variable appears as $A$ in $\bar{F}$, the NMOS is gated by $A$. If it appears as $\bar{A}$, the NMOS is gated by $\bar{A}$. Confusing this gives a wrong truth table.
> 3. **Static short-circuit paths:** If both PUN and PDN conduct simultaneously (e.g., a student accidentally puts PMOS in series and NMOS in parallel in the same branch), the examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for violating the cardinal CMOS rule.
> 4. **Sizing errors:** Mentioning the $\mu_n/\mu_p \approx 2$ ratio without showing the calculation $W_p \approx 2 W_n$ loses the "matched delay" mark.
> 5. **AOI vs OAI confusion:** Mis-identifying $F = \overline{(A+B)\cdot(C+D)}$ as AOI is a frequent error. Remember: AOI = AND-OR-Invert, OAI = OR-AND-Invert.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Cardinal rule:** PDN implements $\bar{F}$ in un-complemented form using NMOS; PUN is the **dual** of the PDN, using PMOS.
- **Series in PDN ↔ Parallel in PUN** (and vice versa).
- **Always-ON conditions:** PDN ON ↔ PUN OFF, and PUN ON ↔ PDN OFF — never both.
- **Transistor sizing rule:** $(W/L)_p \approx 2$ to $3 \times (W/L)_n$ to match rise and fall times, due to the mobility ratio $\mu_n / \mu_p \approx 2$–$3$.
- **AOI gate:** $F = \overline{A \cdot B + C \cdot D}$ — series NMOS groups in parallel.
- **OAI gate:** $F = \overline{(A + B) \cdot (C + D)}$ — parallel NMOS groups in series.
- **Static CMOS power:** $P_{static} = 0$ (no path from $V_{DD}$ to GND in steady state). Dynamic power is $\alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$.
- **Logical effort for 2-input NAND:** $g = 2/3$ (less than inverter — NAND is faster per unit input cap).
- **Worst-case delay for NAND2:** $t_{pHL} = 1.38 R_n C_L$, $t_{pLH} = 0.69 R_p C_L$ (with $R_p = R_n$).
- **Transmission-gate XOR uses 6 transistors** vs. 12 for full CMOS — choose the minimal implementation when asked.
- **Pseudo-NMOS is ratioed logic** — has static power; not preferred in low-power designs.
- **Dynamic/domino logic** is clocked and faster but requires careful timing — not in Module 3 scope but mentioned for completeness.
- **Examination mantra:** Always *draw the schematic*, *label all four rails* ($V_{DD}$, output, GND, inputs), and *verify with a truth table* of at least 4 rows to claim full marks.


<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Read Only Memory - 4x4 MOS ROM Cell Arrays (OR/NAND)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Read Only Memory - 4x4 MOS ROM Cell Arrays (OR/NAND)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology)

A **Read-Only Memory (ROM)** is a non-volatile semiconductor memory device in which the binary information is **permanently stored** during the fabrication process and can only be *read* repeatedly during normal operation. In the context of **MOS (Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) technology**, the ROM cell consists of a single MOS transistor (or its absence) at the intersection of a **Word Line (WL)** and a **Bit Line (BL)**.

A **4 × 4 MOS ROM Cell Array** is a memory organization containing exactly **four Word Lines** and **four Bit Lines**, providing a storage capacity of **16 bits (4 words × 4 bits/word)**. The contents are programmed by **mask-level customization** of the contact or via patterns on the chip during fabrication — hence the alternative name *Mask-Programmed ROM*.

Two principal architectural variants of the 4 × 4 MOS ROM are studied under the KTU syllabus:

1. **OR-Type (NOR) MOS ROM** — Bit lines are pulled HIGH through a load device; selected word-line transistors *discharge* the bit line.
2. **NAND-Type MOS ROM** — Bit lines are connected to a **series chain** of NMOS transistors; the bit line is pulled LOW only when *all* transistors in a chain conduct.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of *PCEVT503 – VLSI Technology* requires the explicit ability to *draw, label, and explain* the schematic of a 4 × 4 MOS ROM in **both** OR and NAND configurations, including the truth table, cell matrix representation, and the pull-up/pull-down network.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Printed Locker Bank" Intuition

Imagine a bank of **16 postal lockers** (the 4 × 4 cells) arranged in **4 rows and 4 columns**:
- Each **row** is opened by a unique **master key** (the **Word Line**). Selecting a row is like presenting one master key — only that row becomes accessible.
- Each **column** delivers a parcel (the **Bit Line**) — either an *empty box* (logic 0) or a *sealed parcel* (logic 1).
- The contents of every locker were **fixed at the time the bank was built** (mask programming). You may *open* the locker as many times as you like, but you can **never change** what is inside.

In an **OR-type ROM**, the parcel counter starts at "full" (bit line precharged HIGH). Opening a row unlocks all lockers in that row; any locker that has a transistor present (a 1) **drops** the parcel counter to "empty" (pulls bit line LOW). In a **NAND-type ROM**, the parcel is delivered only when **every** link in a chain is intact — even *one* missing link (a 0 cell) blocks the delivery.

### 1.3 Key Definitions & Standard Metrics

- **Word Line (WL):** Row-select line; one of *m* = log₂(N_rows) lines decoded by an *m*-to-*2ᵐ* row decoder.
- **Bit Line (BL):** Column data line; output of each column is buffered to produce the data word.
- **Cell:** A single NMOS transistor (or its deliberate absence) at the (i, j) intersection.
- **Mask Programmed:** Programming is achieved via the **diffusion or contact mask** — presence of a transistor = stored **1**, absence = stored **0** (or vice versa, depending on convention).
- **Storage Capacity:** Total bits = (Number of WLs) × (Number of BLs).
- **Access Time (t<sub>acc</sub>):** Time from address valid to data output valid.
- **Static Power:** Power dissipated in the OR-type ROM when cells are conducting (LOW state).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (Bold Constants):**
> - For a 4 × 4 ROM: Storage capacity = **16 bits**, address lines = **2**, data outputs = **4**.
> - Typical access time t<sub>acc</sub> ranges from **5 ns to 50 ns** depending on process node.
> - Static power of an OR-type ROM is dominated by the **P<sub>dc</sub> = V<sub>DD</sub>² / R<sub>pu</sub>** of the pull-up network.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Architecture of the 4 × 4 MOS ROM — Foundational Logic

The 4 × 4 MOS ROM is a two-dimensional array with the following logical structure:

- **2-bit address input (A₁, A₀)** → feeds a **2-to-4 row decoder** → activates exactly **one of 4 Word Lines** (WL₀, WL₁, WL₂, WL₃).
- **4 Bit Lines** (BL₀, BL₁, BL₂, BL₃) run vertically; each BL is terminated by a **sense/buffer amplifier** and a **load device** (pull-up resistor or PMOS transistor).
- The **cell at intersection (i, j)** contains an NMOS transistor if the corresponding bit of the programmed word is **1**; otherwise the cell is **empty** (no transistor).

The four programmed words (one per row) form a 4 × 4 binary matrix **M = [m<sub>ij</sub>]** where m<sub>ij</sub> ∈ {0, 1}.

### 2.2 Operation of the OR-Type (NOR Architecture) 4 × 4 MOS ROM

**Topology:** Each Bit Line is connected to **V<sub>DD</sub>** through a **pull-up device** (passive resistor R<sub>pu</sub> or enhancement PMOS). The sources of all NMOS cell transistors in a column are tied to **GND**. The drains are tied to the Bit Line; the gates are tied to their respective Word Lines.

**Operational steps (bullet-by-bullet logic):**

1. **Pre-charge / Steady State:** All Word Lines are held LOW (inactive). Every cell transistor is in **cut-off** (V<sub>GS</sub> < V<sub>Tn</sub>). The Bit Line remains pulled **HIGH** by the load device. Output reads logic **1** on every column when no row is selected.
2. **Address Decoding:** The 2-bit address (A₁, A₀) is decoded. Exactly **one** Word Line is driven HIGH to V<sub>DD</sub>.
3. **Conditional Discharge:** For each column *j*, the Bit Line BL<sub>j</sub> is examined. If the cell at the selected row *i* contains a transistor (m<sub>ij</sub> = 1), then V<sub>GS</sub> = V<sub>DD</sub> > V<sub>Tn</sub> and the transistor turns **ON**, creating a low-resistance path from BL<sub>j</sub> to GND. The bit line is pulled **LOW** (logic 0).
4. **Logic Function Realized:** The output on BL<sub>j</sub> is therefore the **NOR of all word-line activations combined with cell contents**, but in conventional KTU notation this is the **OR ROM**: the bit line stays HIGH *unless* a programmed "1" cell on the active row discharges it. The final buffered output is often inverted to yield the stored bit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OR ROM Logical Identity:** The output bit for column *j* when word *i* is selected is the stored bit **m<sub>ij</sub>** (or its complement, depending on the sense amplifier polarity). The "OR" designation arises from the **wired-OR** behavior of parallel pull-down NMOS transistors on the same bit line.

### 2.3 Operation of the NAND-Type 4 × 4 MOS ROM

**Topology:** Bit lines are *not* pulled up by a resistor. Instead, the **NMOS transistors of an entire column are connected in SERIES** between the Bit Line and GND (or between precharged nodes). All gates in column *j* are tied to their respective Word Lines WL₀ … WL₃.

**Operational steps:**

1. **Pre-charge:** Prior to read, each Bit Line is **precharged HIGH** (typically to V<sub>DD</sub> − V<sub>Tn</sub> via a clocked PMOS precharger).
2. **Word-Line Activation:** The selected Word Line is driven HIGH; all other Word Lines remain LOW.
3. **Series Path Evaluation:** The Bit Line BL<sub>j</sub> is discharged to GND **only if every NMOS transistor in the series chain is ON**. Because the cell transistor exists physically only at "1" locations, a stored "0" means the chain is *broken* (no conducting transistor at that position) — hence the bit line **stays HIGH**.
4. **Logic Function Realized:** The output equals the **NAND of cell contents along the column's active sub-chain**, which is why this structure is called the **NAND ROM**. The key advantage is the **absence of a static pull-up**, drastically reducing DC power consumption.

> [!NOTE]
> **NAND ROM Critical Property:** Because transistors are in series, the Bit Line does *not* discharge unless the **entire conducting path is intact**. A single missing transistor (a stored 0) blocks the discharge — yielding a logic 1 at the output. This is a **ratio-less** dynamic structure with near-zero static power.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Storage Capacity | $C \;=\; 2^{n} \times m$ | n = address bits, m = data bits | bits |
| Number of Cells | $N_{\text{cells}} \;=\; N_{\text{WL}} \times N_{\text{BL}}$ | N_WL = word lines, N_BL = bit lines | cells |
| Address Lines | $n \;=\; \log_{2} N_{\text{WL}}$ | N_WL = word lines | dimensionless |
| Static Power (OR ROM) | $P_{\text{dc}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{DD}^{2}}{R_{pu}}$ | V_DD, R_pu | Watts |
| Pull-up Ratio (OR ROM) | $Z_{pu} / Z_{pd}$ | Z_pu = (W/L) of pull-up, Z_pd = (W/L) of cell | dimensionless |
| Discharge Time (NAND) | $t_{d} \;\approx\; N \cdot C_{BL} \cdot R_{on}$ | N = series transistors, C_BL = bit-line cap, R_on = on-resistance | seconds |
| Precharge Time | $t_{\text{pre}} \;\approx\; R_{p} C_{BL} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{V_{DD}}{V_{DD}-V_{\text{pre}}}\right)$ | R_p, C_BL, V_pre | seconds |
| Power-Delay Product | $\text{PDP} \;=\; P_{\text{avg}} \cdot t_{\text{acc}}$ | P_avg, t_acc | Joules |
| Cell Area (approx.) | $A_{\text{cell}} \;\approx\; F^{2} \cdot k$ | F = minimum feature size, k = layout constant | m² |
| Output High (OR) | $V_{OH} \;\approx\; V_{DD}$ | — | Volts |
| Output Low (OR) | $V_{OL} \;\approx\; V_{DD} \cdot \dfrac{R_{on}}{R_{pu}+R_{on}}$ | R_on of cell | Volts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** OR-type ROMs dominate **high-speed look-up tables** (LUTs) in FPGAs and microcode ROMs in processors due to parallel-read capability. NAND-type ROMs are preferred in **high-density mask ROMs** (firmware storage, character generators, code-storage in embedded MCUs) due to ultra-low static power and smaller cell area.

### 2.5 Comparative Insight: OR vs NAND ROM

| Parameter | OR-Type (NOR Architecture) | NAND-Type |
|---|---|---|
| Cell Connection | Parallel pull-down to GND | Series chain along column |
| Static Power | High (DC path through pull-up) | Near-zero (no DC path) |
| Read Speed | Fast (parallel evaluation) | Slower (series chain delay) |
| Cell Area per Bit | Larger | Smaller |
| Sense Amplifier | Simple DC sense | Needs precharge + dynamic sense |
| Programming | Mask (contact/via) | Mask (diffusion) |
| Typical Application | BIOS, microcode LUTs, PLDs | Firmware storage, NAND flash |
| Bit-line Capacitance | Moderate | Larger (many series gates) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Sizing the 4 × 4 OR-Type ROM Cell Array

We derive the minimum **(W/L) ratio** of the cell transistor to guarantee a valid LOW output (V<sub>OL</sub> ≤ 0.2 V<sub>DD</sub>) when its word line is selected.

**Given:** V<sub>DD</sub> = 5 V, V<sub>Tn</sub> = 1 V, desired V<sub>OL</sub> ≤ 1 V (i.e., 0.2 V<sub>DD</sub>), pull-up implemented as an enhancement PMOS with (W/L)<sub>pu</sub> = 1.

**Step 1 — Identify the worst-case condition.** Worst case is when **only one cell transistor** in a column conducts (all other word lines LOW). The pull-up is in saturation, the cell is in the triode region.

**Step 2 — Write the KVL equation** at the bit-line node (steady state LOW):

$$ I_{D,\text{pu,sat}} \;=\; I_{D,\text{cell,triode}} $$

**Step 3 — Express the currents** using the standard MOS square-law model. For the PMOS pull-up in saturation (assuming V<sub>SG</sub> − |V<sub>Tp</sub>| is fixed):

$$ I_{D,\text{pu}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} k_{p}' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} \bigl(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert\bigr)^{2} $$

For the cell NMOS in triode (V<sub>DS</sub> = V<sub>OL</sub> < V<sub>GS</sub> − V<sub>Tn</sub>):

$$ I_{D,\text{cell}} \;=\; k_{n}' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{cell}} \!\left[\bigl(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}\bigr) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right] $$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for (W/L)<sub>cell</sub>**. Assume k'<sub>n</sub> = k'<sub>p</sub> = k' for simplicity:

$$ \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} \bigl(V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp} \vert\bigr)^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{cell}} \!\left[\bigl(V_{DD} - V_{Tn}\bigr) V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right] $$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values.** Let V<sub>DD</sub> = 5 V, V<sub>Tn</sub> = |V<sub>Tp</sub>| = 1 V, (W/L)<sub>pu</sub> = 1, V<sub>OL</sub> = 1 V.

**Step 5a — Compute the right-hand side bracket:**

$$ (5 - 1)(1) - \frac{1^{2}}{2} \;=\; 4 - 0.5 \;=\; 3.5 $$

**Step 5b — Compute the left-hand side:**

$$ \frac{1}{2} (1) (5 - 1)^{2} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \cdot 16 \;=\; 8 $$

**Step 5c — Solve for (W/L)<sub>cell</sub>:**

$$ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{cell}} \;=\; \frac{8}{3.5} \;\approx\; 2.286 $$

**Step 6 — Conclusion.** For a guaranteed V<sub>OL</sub> ≤ 1 V, the cell transistor must be sized at **approximately (W/L)<sub>cell</sub> ≈ 2.3** when the pull-up has (W/L)<sub>pu</sub> = 1. This sets the geometric **pull-up ratio Z<sub>pu</sub>/Z<sub>pd</sub> ≈ 1/2.3** in classical OR-ROM design.

### 3.2 Derivation: Static Power Dissipation of the 4 × 4 OR-Type ROM

**Step 1 — Identify current path.** When *any* word line is selected, every cell along that row whose stored bit is 1 creates a conducting path from V<sub>DD</sub> to GND through the pull-up and the cell transistor.

**Step 2 — Worst-case power.** Worst case occurs when **all four cells** in a row are programmed to 1, drawing current through all four bit lines simultaneously. Worst-case DC current per bit line:

$$ I_{DC} \;=\; \frac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{R_{pu} + R_{on,\text{cell}}} $$

For simplicity, taking V<sub>OL</sub> ≈ 0.2 V<sub>DD</sub> and R<sub>on,cell</sub> ≪ R<sub>pu</sub>:

$$ I_{DC} \;\approx\; \frac{0.8 \, V_{DD}}{R_{pu}} $$

**Step 3 — Total worst-case static current** (4 conducting bit lines):

$$ I_{\text{total}} \;=\; 4 \cdot \frac{0.8 \, V_{DD}}{R_{pu}} $$

**Step 4 — Worst-case static power:**

$$ P_{\text{DC,max}} \;=\; V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{total}} \;=\; \frac{3.2 \, V_{DD}^{2}}{R_{pu}} $$

**Step 5 — Numerical example.** If V<sub>DD</sub> = 5 V and R<sub>pu</sub> = 10 kΩ:

$$ P_{\text{DC,max}} \;=\; \frac{3.2 \cdot 25}{10{,}000} \;=\; \frac{80}{10{,}000} \;=\; 8 \; \text{mW} $$

This is the **dominant dissipation term** in the OR-type 4 × 4 ROM — the principal reason NAND-type ROMs are used in large arrays.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stored Word Representation

Consider a sample 4 × 4 ROM whose stored content is the following truth table (this is the *canonical KTU 4×4 example*):

| Word Line | A₁A₀ | Stored Word (BL₃ BL₂ BL₁ BL₀) |
|---|---|---|
| WL₀ | 0 0 | 1 0 1 0 |
| WL₁ | 0 1 | 0 1 1 1 |
| WL₂ | 1 0 | 1 1 0 0 |
| WL₃ | 1 1 | 0 0 1 1 |

**Derivation of BL output expressions** (OR-type, with cell-transistor present = stored 1 ⇒ BL pulled LOW ⇒ output 0 in raw form, output 1 after sense-inverter):

**Step 1 — Write raw bit-line voltage for each column** when WL<sub>i</sub> is active. Let D<sub>j</sub>(WL<sub>i</sub>) be the raw BL<sub>j</sub> value.

**Step 1a — BL₀ (column 0):**

$$ D_{0} \;=\; \overline{WL_{0} \cdot 1 \;+\; WL_{1} \cdot 1 \;+\; WL_{2} \cdot 0 \;+\; WL_{3} \cdot 1} $$

Simplifying (only ON cells pull low; column 0 has cells at rows 0, 1, 3):

$$ D_{0} \;=\; \overline{WL_{0} + WL_{1} + WL_{3}} $$

**Step 1b — BL₁ (column 1):** Cells at rows 0, 1, 2:

$$ D_{1} \;=\; \overline{WL_{0} + WL_{1} + WL_{2}} $$

**Step 1c — BL₂ (column 2):** Cells at rows 1, 2:

$$ D_{2} \;=\; \overline{WL_{1} + WL_{2}} $$

**Step 1d — BL₃ (column 3):** Cells at rows 0, 2:

$$ D_{3} \;=\; \overline{WL_{0} + WL_{2}} $$

**Step 2 — Generate the buffered (non-inverted) outputs** by passing through the sense inverter Y = D̄:

$$ Y_{0} \;=\; WL_{0} + WL_{1} + WL_{3} $$

$$ Y_{1} \;=\; WL_{0} + WL_{1} + WL_{2} $$

$$ Y_{2} \;=\; WL_{1} + WL_{2} $$

$$ Y_{3} \;=\; WL_{0} + WL_{2} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These four sum-of-products expressions constitute the OR-plane output logic** of the 4 × 4 OR-type ROM. The 2-to-4 row decoder activates exactly one WL<sub>i</sub> at a time, so the output reduces to the **stored bit** in that row — the OR topology naturally realizes the stored word.

### 3.4 Derivation: NAND-Type 4 × 4 ROM Output Expression

For the same truth table, in the NAND configuration the bit line is pulled LOW only when *all* transistors in the column series-chain conduct. The bit line stays HIGH if *any* cell in the chain is missing.

**Step 1 — Raw NAND-ROM bit-line expression** for column 0 (cells at rows 0, 1, 3; row 2 has no transistor):

$$ \text{BL}_{0,\text{raw}} \;=\; \overline{WL_{0} \cdot WL_{1} \cdot 1 \cdot WL_{3}} \;\equiv\; \text{(always 0 when row 2 is selected — chain broken)} $$

**Step 2 — Accounting for the broken chain at row 2:** the cell at (2, 0) is open-circuit, so the column-0 series path is **never** fully conducting. The bit line is *always* HIGH (logic 1) regardless of WL activation.

**Step 3 — Reading back the stored bits** for column 0:
- WL₀ active ⇒ stored 1 (chain WL₀–WL₁–WL₃ conducts after row-2 is bypassed) ⇒ BL stays HIGH ⇒ output 1
- WL₁ active ⇒ stored 1 ⇒ output 1
- WL₂ active ⇒ stored 0 (chain broken) ⇒ output 0
- WL₃ active ⇒ stored 1 ⇒ output 1

This matches the column-0 stored vector **(1, 1, 0, 1)** from the truth table.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 High-Level Block Diagram of the 4 × 4 MOS ROM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    addrInput[Address A1 A0] --> rowDecoder[2 to 4 Row Decoder]
    rowDecoder --> WL0[Word Line 0]
    rowDecoder --> WL1[Word Line 1]
    rowDecoder --> WL2[Word Line 2]
    rowDecoder --> WL3[Word Line 3]

    WL0 --> cellMatrix[4x4 Cell Matrix]
    WL1 --> cellMatrix
    WL2 --> cellMatrix
    WL3 --> cellMatrix

    cellMatrix --> BL0[Bit Line 0]
    cellMatrix --> BL1[Bit Line 1]
    cellMatrix --> BL2[Bit Line 2]
    cellMatrix --> BL3[Bit Line 3]

    BL0 --> senseAmp[Sense Amplifiers and Buffers]
    BL1 --> senseAmp
    BL2 --> senseAmp
    BL3 --> senseAmp

    senseAmp --> dataOut[Data Output D3 D2 D1 D0]
    ctrl[Read Enable] --> rowDecoder
    ctrl --> senseAmp
```

### 4.2 OR-Type (NOR) 4 × 4 MOS ROM Cell Array Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VDD[(VDD)] --> Rpu0[PULLUP0]
    VDD --> Rpu1[PULLUP1]
    VDD --> Rpu2[PULLUP2]
    VDD --> Rpu3[PULLUP3]

    Rpu0 --> BL0node((BL0))
    Rpu1 --> BL1node((BL1))
```
