```mermaid
    Rpu2 --> BL2node((BL2))
    Rpu3 --> BL3node((BL3))

    BL0node --> outD0[D0]
    BL1node --> outD1[D1]
    BL2node --> outD2[D2]
    BL3node --> outD3[D3]

    WL0[WL0] --> m00[M00]
    WL0 --> m02[M02]
    WL1[WL1] --> m10[M10]
    WL1 --> m11[M11]
    WL1 --> m13[M13]
    WL2[WL2] --> m21[M21]
    WL2 --> m22[M22]
    WL2 --> m23[M23]
    WL3[WL3] --> m30[M30]
    WL3 --> m32[M32]
    WL3 --> m33[M33]

    m00 --> BL0node
    m02 --> BL2node
    m10 --> BL0node
    m11 --> BL1node
    m13 --> BL3node
    m21 --> BL1node
    m22 --> BL2node
    m23 --> BL3node
    m30 --> BL0node
    m32 --> BL2node
    m33 --> BL3node

    m00 --> GND1[(GND)]
    m02 --> GND1
    m10 --> GND1
    m11 --> GND1
    m13 --> GND1
    m21 --> GND1
    m22 --> GND1
    m23 --> GND1
    m30 --> GND1
    m32 --> GND1
    m33 --> GND1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the schematic above:** Each cell labeled `Mij` represents an NMOS transistor whose gate is tied to `WLi`, drain to `BLj`, and source to GND. Cells absent from the array (e.g., positions M01, M03, M12, M20, M31) correspond to stored logic 0. The **four pull-ups** sustain bit-line HIGH potential when no cell conducts.

### 4.3 NAND-Type 4 × 4 MOS ROM Cell Array Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    precharge[Precharge Clock phi] --> prechTran[PMOS Precharger]

    BL0nodeN((BL0)) --> senseN[Sense and Invert]
    BL1nodeN((BL1)) --> senseN
    BL2nodeN((BL2)) --> senseN
    BL3nodeN((BL3)) --> senseN

    senseN --> outN[Data Output N3 N2 N1 N0]

    WL0N[WL0] --> N00[N00]
    WL0N --> N02[N02]
    WL1N[WL1] --> N10[N10]
    WL1N --> N11[N11]
    WL1N --> N13[N13]
    WL2N[WL2] --> N21[N21]
    WL2N --> N22[N22]
    WL2N --> N23[N23]
    WL3N[WL3] --> N30[N30]
    WL3N --> N32[N32]
    WL3N --> N33[N33]

    N00 --- N02
    N10 --- N11 --- N13
    N21 --- N22 --- N23
    N30 --- N32 --- N33

    N00 --> BL0nodeN
    N10 --> BL0nodeN
    N30 --> BL0nodeN
    N02 --> BL1nodeN
    N11 --> BL1nodeN
    N21 --> BL1nodeN
    N13 --> BL2nodeN
    N22 --> BL2nodeN
    N32 --> BL2nodeN
    N23 --> BL3nodeN
    N33 --> BL3nodeN

    N00 --> GNDN[(GND)]
    N02 --> GNDN
    N10 --> GNDN
    N11 --> GNDN
    N13 --> GNDN
    N21 --> GNDN
    N22 --> GNDN
    N23 --> GNDN
    N30 --> GNDN
    N32 --> GNDN
    N33 --> GNDN
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical reading of the NAND schematic:** Transistors in the same column are wired **in series** between the precharged bit line and ground. A stored "0" creates an **open circuit** in the chain, preventing the bit line from discharging. The sense amplifier detects whether the bit line dropped (stored 0) or remained HIGH (stored 1).

### 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow (Read Operation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Address Valid] --> B{Decoder Enable}
    B --> C[Row Decoder Activates 1 of 4 WLs]
    C --> D{Cell at Selected Row in Column J}
    D -->|Transistor Present 1| E[ORROM BL pulls LOW / NANDROM BL stays HIGH]
    D -->|No Transistor 0| F[ORROM BL stays HIGH / NANDROM BL discharges]

    E --> G[Sense Amplifier Latches Output]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Buffered Data Word on Output Bus]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define a **4 × 4 MOS ROM** cell array. With the help of a neat sketch, explain the structure of an **OR-type MOS ROM** and identify the role of the pull-up device.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Definition:** A 4 × 4 MOS ROM is a non-volatile memory array with 4 word lines and 4 bit lines, providing 16 cells (4 words of 4 bits each). Information is permanently programmed at the mask level.
- **(1 mark) OR-type structure:** Each bit line is tied to V<sub>DD</sub> through a pull-up load (resistor or PMOS). NMOS cell transistors are connected with drains to the bit line, sources to GND, and gates to the respective word line. Presence of a transistor represents stored 1; absence represents stored 0.
- **(1 mark) Role of pull-up:** Maintains the bit line at logic HIGH (V<sub>DD</sub>) when no cell is conducting; defines the output HIGH level and provides the wired-OR logic with parallel pull-down cells.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Differentiate between **OR-type** and **NAND-type** MOS ROM cell arrays based on (i) cell connection, (ii) static power dissipation, and (iii) read mechanism.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark) Cell connection:** OR-type uses *parallel* pull-down NMOS transistors per bit line; NAND-type uses *series* connection of NMOS transistors along the bit line.
- **(1 mark) Static power:** OR-type dissipates continuous DC power through the pull-up when cells conduct; NAND-type has near-zero static power since no DC path exists between V<sub>DD</sub> and GND.
- **(1 mark) Read mechanism:** OR-type is a *static read* — bit line is either precharged HIGH or pulled LOW by conducting cells. NAND-type is a *dynamic read* — bit line is precharged, then evaluated based on whether the series chain can discharge it.

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Paper Pattern]*

**(a)** Draw the schematic of a **4 × 4 OR-type MOS ROM** cell array and explain its read operation. Assume the following stored data: Word 0 = 1010, Word 1 = 0111, Word 2 = 1100, Word 3 = 0011. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the same array, derive the **pull-up ratio** required to obtain V<sub>OL</sub> ≤ 0.4 V when V<sub>DD</sub> = 5 V, V<sub>Tn</sub> = 1 V, V<sub>Tp</sub> = −1 V, and the cell (W/L) = 4. Use the standard square-law model. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **(2 marks) Schematic:** Draw 4 word lines (WL₀–WL₃) horizontally; 4 bit lines (BL₀–BL₃) vertically, each with a pull-up to V<sub>DD</sub>. Place NMOS transistors at intersections corresponding to stored 1s. Word 0 (1010) places transistors at BL₀, BL₂; Word 1 (0111) at BL₁, BL₂, BL₃; Word 2 (1100) at BL₀, BL₁; Word 3 (0011) at BL₂, BL₃.
- **(2 marks) Read operation:** When A₁A₀ selects WL<sub>i</sub>, that word line goes HIGH. For every column j where the cell exists, the corresponding NMOS conducts and pulls BL<sub>j</sub> LOW; where the cell is absent, BL<sub>j</sub> stays HIGH. The sense amplifier buffers and presents the stored word at the output.
- **(2 marks) Truth table verification:** Tabulate the 4 address combinations against the 4 stored words. Show that for each input (00, 01, 10, 11) the outputs (D₃D₂D₁D₀) are (1010, 0111, 1100, 0011) — matching the programming.
- **(1 mark) Logical insight:** The OR-type ROM is essentially a wired-OR of cell transistors, and the row decoder ensures only one row evaluates at a time, yielding a unique output word per address.

**Model Solution (b):**
- **(1 mark) Given data restated:** V<sub>DD</sub> = 5 V, V<sub>Tn</sub> = 1 V, |V<sub>Tp</sub>| = 1 V, (W/L)<sub>cell</sub> = 4, V<sub>OL</sub> = 0.4 V. Assume worst case: one cell conducting per bit line.
- **(1 mark) Current in PMOS pull-up (saturation):**

$$ I_{D,\text{pu}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} k_{p}' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{Tp}\vert)^{2} $$

- **(1 mark) Current in NMOS cell (triode):**

$$ I_{D,\text{cell}} \;=\; k_{n}' \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{cell}} \!\left[(V_{DD}-V_{Tn})V_{OL} - \frac{V_{OL}^{2}}{2}\right] $$

- **(1 mark) Numerical evaluation of triode bracket:**

$$ (5-1)(0.4) - \frac{(0.4)^{2}}{2} \;=\; 1.6 - 0.08 \;=\; 1.52 $$

- **(1 mark) Numerical evaluation of pull-up side** (assume k'<sub>p</sub> = k'<sub>n</sub> for the ratio derivation, so the k' terms cancel):

$$ I_{D,\text{pu}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} (4)^{2} \;=\; 8 \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} $$

- **(1 mark) Equate the currents:**

$$ 8 \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} \;=\; 4 \cdot 1.52 \;=\; 6.08 $$

- **(1 mark) Solve for (W/L)<sub>pu</sub>:**

$$ \left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{pu} \;=\; \frac{6.08}{8} \;=\; 0.76 $$

**Final Answer:** **(W/L)<sub>pu</sub> ≈ 0.76** for V<sub>OL</sub> ≤ 0.4 V. The required pull-up ratio is (W/L)<sub>pu</sub> / (W/L)<sub>cell</sub> = 0.76 / 4 ≈ **0.19**.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Paper Pattern — Internal Choice]*

**(a)** Explain the structure and operation of a **4 × 4 NAND-type MOS ROM** cell array. Compare its power dissipation with the OR-type ROM for the same stored data as in Question A. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **bit-line discharge time** of the NAND-type ROM column. Compute the discharge time for N = 4 series transistors, each with on-resistance R<sub>on</sub> = 2 kΩ, and bit-line capacitance C<sub>BL</sub> = 5 pF, assuming the bit line must discharge from 5 V to 0.5 V. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
- **(2 marks) Structure:** Draw 4 word lines running horizontally; 4 vertical bit lines with **no pull-up resistor**, only a clocked PMOS precharger. NMOS transistors in each column are connected in series between the bit line and ground. The presence of a transistor at intersection (i, j) means stored 1; absence means stored 0 (and *breaks* the series chain at that point).
- **(2 marks) Read operation:** Precharge all bit lines HIGH (φ<sub>pre</sub> LOW turns on the PMOS precharger). Apply the address to the decoder; the selected WL goes HIGH. Evaluate each column: if the chain is intact (all series transistors of active cells conduct), the bit line is pulled LOW; if any link is missing (a stored 0 in the active row), the chain is broken and the bit line remains HIGH. The sense amplifier latches the result.
- **(2 marks) Power comparison:** In the OR-type ROM, the worst-case static current flows whenever an entire row of all-1 cells is read. For the given pattern, Word 1 (0111) and Word 3 (0011) cause DC conduction through 3 and 2 bit lines respectively. In the NAND-type ROM, **no DC path exists** in steady state, so static power is theoretically zero — only dynamic switching power is consumed during precharge/evaluate cycles.
- **(1 mark) Trade-off note:** NAND-type achieves lower power and smaller cell area at the cost of longer read access time due to the series chain resistance and the need for precharge/evaluation sequencing.

**Model Solution (b):**
- **(1 mark) Governing equation:** The bit-line capacitance C<sub>BL</sub> discharges through *N* series NMOS transistors, each in deep triode, modeled collectively as a series resistance N·R<sub>on</sub>:

$$ V_{BL}(t) \;=\; V_{\text{pre}} \cdot e^{-t / (N R_{on} C_{BL})} $$

- **(1 mark) Identify the time constant:**

$$ \tau \;=\; N R_{on} C_{BL} $$

- **(1 mark) Substitute N = 4, R<sub>on</sub> = 2 kΩ, C<sub>BL</sub> = 5 pF:**

$$ \tau \;=\; 4 \cdot 2 \times 10^{3} \cdot 5 \times 10^{-12} \;=\; 40 \times 10^{-9} \;=\; 40 \; \text{ns} $$

- **(1 mark) Invert the discharge equation** to solve for the discharge time t<sub>d</sub>:

$$ t_{d} \;=\; \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{V_{\text{pre}}}{V_{\text{final}}}\right) $$

- **(1 mark) Substitute V<sub>pre</sub> = 5 V, V<sub>final</sub> = 0.5 V:**

$$ \ln\!\left(\frac{5}{0.5}\right) \;=\; \ln(10) \;\approx\; 2.3026 $$

- **(1 mark) Final numerical computation:**

$$ t_{d} \;=\; 40 \times 10^{-9} \cdot 2.3026 \;\approx\; 92.1 \; \text{ns} $$

- **(1 mark) Interpretation:** The bit line takes approximately **92 ns** to discharge from 5 V to 0.5 V through 4 series NMOS transistors — a slow but acceptable access time for non-volatile firmware storage. Designers can speed this up by increasing cell (W/L) (reducing R<sub>on</sub>) or by segmenting the column into smaller sub-chains.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks**
>
> 1. **Confusing "OR ROM" with "OR logic gate":** A common error is to state that the OR-type ROM "implements an OR gate." It does not — it implements a *wired-OR pull-down network with row-decoder exclusivity*. Marks are lost for failing to mention the **row decoder** that activates only one word line.
> 2. **Pull-up omission:** Many students draw the OR-type ROM without showing the **pull-up device** on each bit line. This is treated as a **schematic error** and costs 1–2 marks.
> 3. **NAND ROM drawn with pull-up:** Conversely, drawing a pull-up resistor on every bit line of a NAND-type ROM shows a fundamental misunderstanding of the architecture. Examiners deduct marks for this.
> 4. **Programming convention reversal:** Some texts use "transistor present = 0" while others use "transistor present = 1." Students must explicitly state their **convention at the start** of the answer to avoid ambiguity.
> 5. **Skipping the sense amplifier:** Marks are reserved for showing the **sense amplifier / output buffer** — omitting it loses at least 1 mark.
> 6. **Algebraic shortcut in derivation:** For Part B derivations, do not write the final formula and substitute — KTU examiners award **step marks** for clearly separating the symbolic expression from the numerical substitution.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **ROM definition:** Non-volatile, mask-programmed, read-only memory. Cell = (NMOS transistor presence) at (WL, BL) intersection.
> - **4 × 4 capacity:** 4 word lines × 4 bit lines = **16 bits**; 2 address bits; 4 data outputs.
> - **OR-type topology:** Bit line tied to V<sub>DD</sub> via pull-up; cell NMOS in **parallel** between BL and GND; **static read**.
> - **NAND-type topology:** Bit line precharged HIGH; cell NMOS in **series** chain between BL and GND; **dynamic read**.
> - **Programming convention:** Transistor present ⇒ stored 1 (OR-type pulls BL LOW; NAND-type keeps BL HIGH).
> - **OR-ROM power:** High static power P<sub>DC</sub> = V<sub>DD</sub>² / R<sub>pu</sub> per conducting bit line.
> - **NAND-ROM power:** Near-zero static power; only dynamic dissipation during precharge/evaluate.
> - **Cell sizing rule (OR-ROM):** (W/L)<sub>cell</sub> must be larger than (W/L)<sub>pu</sub> to ensure low V<sub>OL</sub>; typical pull-up ratio ≈ 1 : 4.
> - **Discharge time (NAND-ROM):** t<sub>d</sub> = N R<sub>on</sub> C<sub>BL</sub> ln(V<sub>pre</sub> / V<sub>final</sub>).
> - **Output expressions (OR-ROM, sample):** Y₀ = WL₀ + WL₁ + WL₃; Y₁ = WL₀ + WL₁ + WL₂; Y₂ = WL₁ + WL₂; Y₃ = WL₀ + WL₂.
> - **NAND ROM read sequence:** Precharge → Address decode → WL activation → Evaluate series chain → Sense → Latch.
> - **Applications:** OR-ROM → LUTs, BIOS, microcode (speed-critical); NAND-ROM → firmware, code storage (density + low power).
> - **Trade-off summary:** OR-type = *fast, power-hungry*; NAND-type = *slow, ultra-low power, dense*.
> - **Decoding rule:** A 2-to-4 row decoder is required for the 4 × 4 array; only one WL is HIGH at any time.
> - **Examiner pet topic:** The OR-vs-NAND comparison and the derivation of pull-up ratio (W/L) appear in nearly every KTU Module-4 question paper — practice them thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Random Access Memory - SRAM - Six Transistor CMOS SRAM Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Random Access Memory - SRAM - Six Transistor CMOS SRAM Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Six-Transistor (6T) CMOS SRAM Cell** is the fundamental, industry-standard Static Random-Access Memory (SRAM) bit-cell used in virtually all modern CMOS VLSI cache memories (L1/L2/L3 caches in microprocessors, register files, and look-up tables). It is composed of **six MOSFETs** arranged as two cross-coupled CMOS inverters forming a bistable storage latch, plus two NMOS access (pass) transistors that selectively connect the internal storage nodes to the complementary bit-lines during Read and Write operations.

- **Pull-up network:** 2 PMOS transistors (load devices, $M_{P1}, M_{P2}$)
- **Pull-down network:** 2 NMOS transistors (driver devices, $M_{N1}, M_{N2}$)
- **Access network:** 2 NMOS transistors (pass/transfer devices, $M_{N3}, M_{N4}$)
- **Storage nodes:** Internal nodes Q and $\overline{Q}$ (logic complements of each other)
- **External lines:** 1 Word Line (WL) and 2 complementary Bit Lines ($BL, \overline{BL}$)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A 6T SRAM cell is the *only* SRAM topology examiners ask about in Module 4. Master its structure, sizing ratios, Read/Write operation, and the Static Noise Margin (SNM) butterfly curve — these four items carry the maximum weightage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Static"?** Unlike DRAM, SRAM does **not** require periodic refreshing. As long as $V_{DD}$ is supplied, the cross-coupled latch regeneratively holds its state indefinitely. The term "static" refers to this *non-destructive, refresh-free* data retention.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **see-saw (teeter-totter)** with two strong people sitting on opposite ends, holding the plank in a perfectly balanced, frozen position. Whoever's heavier end is "down" represents the stored bit. Now, two more people (the *access* assistants) stand at the sides, only stepping in to push the see-saw one way or the other when the controller (the Word Line) gives the signal. When the assistants leave, the two original strong people keep the see-saw exactly where it is — indefinitely, even if no one is watching. The two original people are the **cross-coupled inverters** (regenerative feedback), and the assistants are the **access transistors** (controlled by WL).

A second analogy is a **locked coin-flip box**: once the coin is set to HEADS or TAILS by a controlled write, the box's internal latch mechanism (cross-coupled inverters) keeps the coin fixed forever, and the access transistors act as the "door" opened only by the Word Line key during Read/Write.

### 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Technology node:** typically **45 nm, 32 nm, 22 nm, 14 nm, 7 nm** planar/bulk or FinFET
- **Supply voltage ($V_{DD}$):** **1.0 V – 1.2 V** (modern); legacy **3.3 V / 5 V**
- **Cell area (6T):** approximately **100 – 150 $F^2$** where $F$ = minimum feature size
- **Access time:** **1 – 5 ns** (on-chip cache)
- **Static power:** leakage dominated — **nW per cell** range
- **Bistable states:** exactly **2** (Q = 1, $\overline{Q}$ = 0) OR (Q = 0, $\overline{Q}$ = 1)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Transistor-by-Transistor Functional Breakdown

The 6T cell is logically a **cross-coupled bistable latch** with gated access. Let node Q be the output of Inverter-1 (formed by $M_{P1}$–$M_{N1}$) and node $\overline{Q}$ the output of Inverter-2 (formed by $M_{P2}$–$M_{N2}$). The input of Inverter-1 is $\overline{Q}$, and the input of Inverter-2 is Q — this cross-connection is the source of **positive (regenerative) feedback** that creates bistability.

**Step 1 — Power-off / Idle (Hold state):**
- WL = 0 V → $M_{N3}$ and $M_{N4}$ are OFF.
- BL and $\overline{BL}$ are isolated from internal nodes.
- The cross-coupled inverters sustain the previously stored logic value.

**Step 2 — Read operation:**
- Precharge BL and $\overline{BL}$ to $V_{DD}$.
- Assert WL = $V_{DD}$ → $M_{N3}, M_{N4}$ turn ON.
- The internal node storing '0' pulls its bit-line down through the access + driver NMOS stack.
- A sense amplifier detects the small differential voltage ($\Delta V \approx 50$–$200$ mV) between BL and $\overline{BL}$.
- The cell must NOT flip during read (read-disturb-free).

**Step 3 — Write operation:**
- Drive BL to the *new* data and $\overline{BL}$ to its complement.
- Assert WL = $V_{DD}$.
- The strong driver+access path must overpower the opposite inverter's PMOS to flip the latch.
- The cell must successfully flip during write (write-ability satisfied).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Tension (KTU favourite):** A 6T cell must be simultaneously **read-stable** (won't flip during Read) AND **writable** (will flip during Write). These are opposing requirements, resolved by the **cell ratio (CR)** and **pull-up ratio (PR)** — the fundamental design knobs.

### 2.2 The Three Sizing Ratios (KTU Formula Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following sizing ratios are the single most-asked concept in KTU Module 4. Memorize the table.

$$
\text{Cell Ratio (CR)} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{driver NMOS}}}{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{access NMOS}}} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\beta_{N1}}{\beta_{N5}} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\beta_{N2}}{\beta_{N6}}
$$

$$
\text{Pull-up Ratio (PR)} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{load PMOS}}}{\left(\frac{W}{L}\right)_{\text{access NMOS}}} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\beta_{P1}}{\beta_{N5}} \;\triangleq\; \frac{\beta_{P2}}{\beta_{N6}}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Symbol | Quantity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{DD}$ | Supply voltage | $V_{tn}$ | NMOS threshold voltage |
| $V_{tp}$ | PMOS threshold voltage (magnitude) | $I_{leak}$ | Subthreshold leakage current |
| $C_{BL}$ | Bit-line capacitance | $C_{bit}$ | Storage node capacitance |
| $V_{trip,N}$ | Inverter switching threshold (NMOS) | $V_{trip,P}$ | Inverter switching threshold (PMOS) |
| $SNM$ | Static Noise Margin | $CR$ | Cell ratio |
| $PR$ | Pull-up ratio | $I_{read}$ | Read current |
| $t_{read}$ | Read access time | $t_{write}$ | Write time |
| $\Delta V_{BL}$ | Bit-line differential developed | $P_{static}$ | Static power (leakage) |
| $P_{dyn}$ | Dynamic switching power | $f_{clk}$ | Operating frequency |

**Read-stability constraint (KTU's most-tested inequality):**
$$
CR \;\geq\; \frac{2\,(V_{tn} - \Delta V_{Q,\text{max}})}{V_{DD} - 2\,V_{tn} + \Delta V_{Q,\text{max}}} \quad \text{(simplified form: } CR \geq 1.0 \text{ to } 2.0\text{)}
$$

**Write-ability constraint (PR-based):**
$$
PR \;<\; \frac{(V_{DD} - 2V_{tn})}{(V_{DD} - 2V_{tp})} \quad \text{(simplified: } PR \leq 1.0 \text{ to } 1.5 \text{ typically)}
$$

**Static Noise Margin (SNM) — Seevinck definition:**
$$
SNM \;=\; V_{IH} - V_{IL} \;\approx\; \text{side of largest inscribed square in butterfly curve}
$$

**Bit-line voltage developed during Read (approximate):**
$$
\Delta V_{BL} \;\approx\; \frac{I_{\text{read}} \cdot t_{\text{read}}}{C_{BL}} \;\approx\; \frac{\beta_{N,\text{eff}}\, (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^{\alpha}\, t_{\text{read}}}{C_{BL}}
$$

**Total power dissipation per cell:**
$$
P_{\text{total}} \;=\; \underbrace{V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}}_{\text{static}} \;+\; \underbrace{\alpha \, C_{\text{eff}} \, V_{DD}^{2} \, f_{\text{clk}}}_{\text{dynamic}}
$$

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **On-die CPU cache:** Intel/AMD/ARM cores embed **MBs of 6T SRAM** per chip.
- **Register files:** Even faster, denser 6T/8T variants inside execution units.
- **Network routers / TCAMs:** Use modified 6T/16T cells for content-addressable lookup.
- **IoT microcontrollers:** Low-leakage 6T arrays in sleep-mode retention.
- **FPGA configuration memory:** Some FPGAs (e.g., SRAM-based LUTs) use 6T-style latches.
- **Smartphone SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon):** Multi-MB L1/L2/L3 caches are 6T arrays.

The 6T cell is the workhorse of digital silicon — every microprocessor-class chip in production today contains billions of them.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Read-Stability Condition (Driver vs. Access Voltage Divider)

During a Read of a stored "1" at node Q (so $\overline{Q} = 0$, and $M_{N2}$ OFF, $M_{P2}$ ON pulling Q up):

- Pre-charged $BL = \overline{BL} = V_{DD}$.
- $M_{N3}$ is ON; $M_{N2}$ is OFF. The discharge path for $\overline{BL}$ is through $M_{N3}$ (access) in series with $M_{N2}$ (driver).
- Since $M_{N2}$ is OFF, $\overline{BL}$ does not discharge (Q-side is being read out through $M_{N4}$–$M_{N1}$).
- The critical node is Q (storing '1' = $V_{DD}$). The Read path is from Q → $M_{N4}$ (access) → BL. But BL is also precharged to $V_{DD}$, so current flows from BL into the cell if Q < $V_{DD}$ — but since Q = $V_{DD}$, the cell is stable in this case.
- The dangerous Read case is reading a stored '0' at Q (so $\overline{Q} = V_{DD}$, $M_{N1}$ is OFF, $M_{P1}$ is OFF, $M_{N2}$ is ON holding Q low).
- During this Read, $M_{N3}$ (access to $\overline{Q}$) turns ON. Now $\overline{Q}$ (at $V_{DD}$) is connected via $M_{N3}$ to the precharged $BL = V_{DD}$, so $\overline{Q}$ stays high — good.
- But for Q (the '0' side), $M_{N4}$ turns ON, and Q is being *pulled up* through $M_{N4}$ toward $V_{DD}$. This is the **read-disturb**: if $V_Q$ rises above the switching threshold of Inverter-2, the cell flips — destroying the stored bit.

**Step-by-step analytical derivation of the maximum $V_Q$ during Read:**

Model the pull-up via $M_{N4}$ (NMOS access with gate = WL = $V_{DD}$, source = $V_Q$, drain = $BL = V_{DD}$) and the pull-down via $M_{N2}$ (NMOS driver with gate = $\overline{Q} = V_{DD}$, source = GND, drain = $V_Q$).

The maximum $V_Q$ occurs when the two NMOS currents are equal (DC steady-state during read).

Using the long-channel saturation/linear current equation (assuming both in saturation for simplicity):

$$
I_{N4,\text{sat}} = \frac{\beta_{N4}}{2} (V_{GS,N4} - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{\beta_{N4}}{2} (V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{tn})^2
$$

$$
I_{N2,\text{sat}} = \frac{\beta_{N2}}{2} (V_{GS,N2} - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{\beta_{N2}}{2} (V_{DD} - 0 - V_{tn})^2 = \frac{\beta_{N2}}{2} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2
$$

Equating for the critical read point:

$$
\beta_{N4} (V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{tn})^2 \;=\; \beta_{N2} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2
$$

Let $r = \beta_{N2}/\beta_{N4} = CR$ (cell ratio). Then:

$$
(V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{tn})^2 \;=\; \frac{1}{CR} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2
$$

Take the positive root:

$$
V_{DD} - V_Q - V_{tn} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{CR}} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})
$$

Solve for the maximum tolerable $V_Q$:

$$
V_{Q,\max} \;=\; V_{DD} - V_{tn} - \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{\sqrt{CR}} \;=\; (V_{DD} - V_{tn}) \left( 1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{CR}} \right)
$$

**Read-stability criterion (no flip):** $V_{Q,\max}$ must remain below the switching threshold $V_{M}$ of the opposite inverter. In the conservative limit where $V_M \approx V_{tn}$ for a balanced inverter:

$$
\boxed{ \; V_{Q,\max} \;<\; V_{tn} \; }
$$

This gives the **KTU-grade design rule** (substitute and simplify):

$$
CR \;>\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - 2 V_{tn}} \right)^{2}
$$

**Example numerical check (typical 0.18 µm, $V_{DD}=1.8$ V, $V_{tn}=0.4$ V):**

$$
CR > \left( \frac{1.8 - 0.4}{1.8 - 0.8} \right)^{2} = \left( \frac{1.4}{1.0} \right)^{2} = 1.96
$$

So **CR ≥ 2** is required — meaning the driver NMOS must be **at least twice as wide** as the access NMOS. Designers typically choose CR = 2 to 3.

### 3.2 Derivation of Write-Trip Condition (PMOS vs. Access Strength)

Consider writing a '0' to a cell that currently stores '1' (so Q = $V_{DD}$, $\overline{Q}$ = 0, $M_{P1}$ OFF, $M_{N1}$ ON, $M_{P2}$ ON, $M_{N2}$ OFF).

- Drive BL = 0 V, $\overline{BL}$ = $V_{DD}$.
- Assert WL = $V_{DD}$.
- $M_{N3}$ turns ON, connecting $\overline{Q}$ to BL = 0 V. So $\overline{Q}$ is being *pulled down* through $M_{N3}$.
- The write succeeds if $\overline{Q}$ drops below the switching threshold of Inverter-1, causing Inverter-1 to drive Q low — completing the regenerative flip.

The write-critical transistor pair is $M_{N3}$ (access, pulling $\overline{Q}$ down) versus $M_{P2}$ (PMOS load, holding $\overline{Q}$ high).

Using the same long-channel model, write succeeds when the access NMOS is stronger than the PMOS load:

$$
\beta_{N3} (V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2 \;>\; \beta_{P2} (V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

Define the **pull-up ratio** $PR = \beta_{P2}/\beta_{N3}$:

$$
PR \;<\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert} \right)^{2}
$$

**Example (same process):** $V_{tp} = -0.5$ V, so:

$$
PR < \left( \frac{1.4}{1.3} \right)^{2} \approx 1.16
$$

So **PR ≤ 1** is the standard design rule → the PMOS load must be **equal or weaker** than the access NMOS. In practice, designers use **PR ≈ 1.0 to 1.5** depending on read/write margin trade-off.

### 3.3 The Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Read Stability | Write Ability | Trade-off |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Driver NMOS $\beta$ | ↑ Stronger is better (high CR) | ↓ Weaker is fine (helps write) | Conflict |
| Access NMOS $\beta$ | ↓ Weaker is better (high CR) | ↑ Stronger is better (low PR) | Conflict |
| PMOS Load $\beta$ | No direct effect | ↓ Weaker is better (low PR) | One-sided |
| $V_{DD}$ | ↑ higher = more stable | ↑ higher = easier write | Both improve |
| $V_{tn}$ (low-$V_t$ cells) | ↓ low-$V_t$ = less stable | ↓ low-$V_t$ = easier write | Both improve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "size" a 6T cell, state the design as a **two-step constraint satisfaction**: pick **CR ≥ 2** for read-stability, then verify **PR ≤ ~1.0** for write-ability. If both hold, the cell is functional.

### 4.2 Operational State Machine of the 6T SRAM Cell

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Hold
    Hold: Hold State WL=0 Nodes Isolated Latch Regenerates
    Hold --> Read: WL asserted to VDD
    Read: Read Precharge BL=BL_bar=VDD Sense Differential
    Read --> Hold: WL deasserted
    Hold --> Write: WL asserted to VDD
    Write: Write Drive BL new data BL_bar complement Force Flip
    Write --> Hold: WL deasserted
    Read --> Hold: Sense amp captures Data returned to Hold
    Write --> Hold: New value latched Data returned to Hold
```

```mermaid

    A[Bit Line BL] -->|M_N3 controlled by WL| N1[Node Q]
    B[Bit Line BL_bar] -->|M_N4 controlled by WL| N2[Node Q_bar]
    N1 -->|input| INV1[CMOS Inverter 1: M_P1 and M_N1]
    INV1 --> N2
    N2 -->|input| INV2[CMOS Inverter 2: M_P2 and M_N2]

```

### 4.4 Butterfly Curve for SNM Visualisation (Conceptual Sketch)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph CurvePlot["SNM Butterfly Curve Conceptual"]
        XAxis["X axis: V_in of Inverter 1"]
        YAxis["Y axis: V_out of Inverter 1 and V_in of Inverter 2"]
        VTC1["VTC1 curve rising"]
        VTC2["VTC2 mirrored falling"]
        Square1["Inset square: side = SNM"]
        Note1["SNM = side of largest square that fits inside the two-eye butterfly"]
    end
    CurvePlot --> XAxis
    CurvePlot --> YAxis
    CurvePlot --> VTC1
    CurvePlot --> VTC2
    CurvePlot --> Square1
    CurvePlot --> Note1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Butterfly Curve:** Plot the Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC) of Inverter-1 ($V_{out} = f(V_{in})$) and the *inverted* VTC of Inverter-2 ($V_{in} = f^{-1}(V_{out})$) on the same axes. They cross at the two stable points (Q=1 and Q=0) and one metastable point. The largest square that fits inside the resulting "butterfly" wings defines the **Static Noise Margin** — the DC noise tolerance of the cell.

### 4.5 Read vs Write Bias Condition Table

| Operation | WL | BL (pre) | $\overline{BL}$ (pre) | BL (active) | $\overline{BL}$ (active) | Sense |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Hold | 0 V | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ (idle) | $V_{DD}$ (idle) | — |
| Read '1' | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | slight $\downarrow$ | holds $V_{DD}$ | $\Delta V > 0$ |
| Read '0' | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | holds $V_{DD}$ | slight $\downarrow$ | $\Delta V < 0$ |
| Write '1' | $V_{DD}$ | $V_{DD}$ | 0 V | $V_{DD}$ (force) | 0 V (force) | — |
| Write '0' | $V_{DD}$ | 0 V | $V_{DD}$ | 0 V (force) | $V_{DD}$ (force) | — |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**With a neat circuit diagram, explain the structure of a 6T CMOS SRAM cell. List the functions of each transistor.**

**Model Answer:**

A 6T CMOS SRAM cell consists of **6 MOSFETs**: two PMOS ($M_{P1}, M_{P2}$) as pull-up loads, two NMOS ($M_{N1}, M_{N2}$) as pull-down drivers, and two NMOS access transistors ($M_{N3}, M_{N4}$). The drivers and loads form two cross-coupled CMOS inverters, creating a bistable latch. The access transistors are gated by the Word Line (WL) and connect the internal storage nodes Q and $\overline{Q}$ to the complementary bit-lines BL and $\overline{BL}$.

**Transistor functions:**
- $M_{P1}, M_{P2}$: pull-up loads (active-high load, source = $V_{DD}$).
- $M_{N1}, M_{N2}$: pull-down drivers (active-low load, source = GND).
- $M_{N3}, M_{N4}$: access (pass) transistors, controlled by WL — they connect internal nodes to bit-lines only when WL = $V_{DD}$.

#### **Question A2 (3 marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Cell Ratio (CR) and Pull-up Ratio (PR) of a 6T SRAM cell. State their typical design values.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Cell Ratio (CR)** = $\dfrac{(W/L)_{\text{driver NMOS}}}{(W/L)_{\text{access NMOS}}}$ = $\dfrac{\beta_{N1}}{\beta_{N5}}$
- **Pull-up Ratio (PR)** = $\dfrac{(W/L)_{\text{load PMOS}}}{(W/L)_{\text{access NMOS}}}$ = $\dfrac{\beta_{P1}}{\beta_{N5}}$

**Typical design values:** $CR \approx 2\text{ to }3$ (ensures read-stability) and $PR \approx 1.0\text{ to }1.5$ (ensures write-ability).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a circuit diagram, explain the Read operation of a 6T CMOS SRAM cell. Derive the read-stability condition in terms of the cell ratio (CR).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Write operation and derive the write-ability condition in terms of the pull-up ratio (PR). For a 0.18 µm CMOS process with $V_{DD}=1.8$ V, $V_{tn}=0.4$ V, and $\vert V_{tp}\vert=0.5$ V, calculate the maximum allowable PR for a successful write.

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Read Operation Steps:**
1. **Precharge phase:** BL and $\overline{BL}$ are precharged to $V_{DD}$ (typically via precharge PMOS transistors).
2. **Word-line assertion:** WL is raised to $V_{DD}$, turning ON access transistors $M_{N3}$ and $M_{N4}$.
3. **Cell evaluation:** Suppose Q stores '0' and $\overline{Q}$ stores '1'. The internal node $\overline{Q}$ ($V_{DD}$) drives the gate of $M_{N1}$ (ON), and Q (0 V) drives gate of $M_{N2}$ (OFF).
4. **Bit-line discharge:** The '0'-stored side (Q) cannot discharge $\overline{BL}$ since $M_{N2}$ is OFF. The '1'-stored side ($\overline{Q}$) tries to keep BL high; the small leakage from $M_{N3}$/precharge imbalance is negligible.
5. **Differential development:** A small voltage differential $\Delta V_{BL}$ develops between BL and $\overline{BL}$ ($\sim$50–200 mV).
6. **Sense amplification:** A sense amplifier detects and amplifies this differential to a full-rail logic output.

**Read-disturb concern:** The '0'-storing node Q is at 0 V but is connected through $M_{N4}$ to BL = $V_{DD}$. This creates a source-follower action that *pulls Q up*. If $V_Q$ rises above the switching threshold of the opposite inverter, the cell flips — destroying the stored bit. This is the **read-disturb failure mode**.

**Derivation of Read-Stability Condition:**

The critical pull-up path during a read of a stored '0' at Q is $M_{N4}$ (access) and the pull-down is $M_{N2}$ (driver). Setting their currents equal at the worst-case DC read point:

$$
\beta_{N4}\,(V_{DD} - V_{Q,\max} - V_{tn})^2 \;=\; \beta_{N2}\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2
$$

With cell ratio $CR = \beta_{N2}/\beta_{N4}$:

$$
V_{Q,\max} \;=\; (V_{DD} - V_{tn})\left(1 - \frac{1}{\sqrt{CR}}\right)
$$

For read-stability, $V_{Q,\max} < V_{tn}$:

$$
\boxed{ \; CR \;>\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - 2V_{tn}} \right)^{2} \;}
$$

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Write Operation Steps:**
1. **Bit-line driving:** The write driver forces BL = new data D and $\overline{BL}$ = $\overline{D}$.
2. **Word-line assertion:** WL = $V_{DD}$ turns ON access transistors.
3. **Internal node forcing:** The new data is forced onto the internal storage nodes through the access transistors.
4. **Regenerative flip:** Once the forced node crosses the switching threshold of the opposite inverter, positive feedback rapidly completes the flip.

**Write-ability Derivation:**

Consider writing '0' to a cell storing '1' (so $\overline{Q} = 0$, Q = $V_{DD}$, $M_{P2}$ is ON holding $\overline{Q}$ high). The write driver forces BL = 0 V. The access transistor $M_{N3}$ (NMOS) tries to pull $\overline{Q}$ down, while $M_{P2}$ (PMOS) tries to hold $\overline{Q}$ at $V_{DD}$. Write succeeds when access overpowers PMOS.

At the write-trip point (both transistors in saturation, currents equal):

$$
\beta_{N3}\,(V_{DD} - V_{tn})^2 \;>\; \beta_{P2}\,(V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert)^2
$$

Define pull-up ratio $PR = \beta_{P2}/\beta_{N3}$. Write succeeds when:

$$
\boxed{ \; PR \;<\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert} \right)^{2} \;}
$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $V_{DD}=1.8$ V, $V_{tn}=0.4$ V, $\vert V_{tp}\vert=0.5$ V.

$$
PR_{\max} \;=\; \left( \frac{1.8 - 0.4}{1.8 - 0.5} \right)^{2} \;=\; \left( \frac{1.4}{1.3} \right)^{2} \;=\; (1.0769)^{2} \;\approx\; 1.16
$$

$$
\boxed{ \; PR_{\max} \;\approx\; 1.16 \;}
$$

So the PMOS load must be sized **at most 1.16× the access NMOS** to guarantee writability. In practice, designers choose $PR \approx 1.0$.

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is Static Noise Margin (SNM)? With a neat sketch of the butterfly curve, explain how SNM is extracted. State the Seevinck definition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 6T SRAM cell is fabricated in a 90 nm CMOS process with $V_{DD}=1.0$ V, $V_{tn}=0.3$ V, $\vert V_{tp}\vert=0.35$ V, and driver/access NMOS width ratio of 2.5. (i) Verify whether the cell is read-stable. (ii) If the PMOS load width is chosen equal to the access NMOS width, verify whether the cell is writable.

##### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Definition of SNM:**

The **Static Noise Margin (SNM)** is the maximum amount of DC noise voltage that can be superimposed on the storage nodes of a bistable SRAM cell without causing a logical state flip. It is a fundamental measure of **read stability** and **noise immunity** of the cell.

**Seevinck Definition (1987):**

$$
SNM \;=\; V_{IH} - V_{IL}
$$

where $V_{IH}$ and $V_{IL}$ are the high- and low-noise margins of the cross-coupled inverters, determined from the **largest square that can be inscribed** within the two "eyes" of the butterfly curve.

**Butterfly Curve Extraction Steps:**

1. Obtain the **Voltage Transfer Characteristic (VTC)** of Inverter-1: $V_{out1} = f(V_{in1})$.
2. Mirror (invert) this VTC to obtain Inverter-2's characteristic: $V_{in1} = f^{-1}(V_{out1})$.
3. Plot both curves on the same $V_{in1}$–$V_{out1}$ axes.
4. The plot forms a **"butterfly"** with three intersection points: two **stable equilibria** (Q=0 and Q=1) and one **metastable point** (where the cell is most vulnerable).
5. Inscribe the largest possible square in the upper-left and lower-right "lobes."
6. The **side length of the largest square = SNM**.

A typical 6T cell in 90 nm has SNM ≈ 0.15–0.25 V under nominal $V_{DD}$.

##### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:** $V_{DD}=1.0$ V, $V_{tn}=0.3$ V, $\vert V_{tp}\vert=0.35$ V, $CR = 2.5$, $PR = 1.0$ (PMOS width = access NMOS width).

**(i) Read-stability check:**

Apply the read-stability criterion:

$$
CR_{\text{required}} \;>\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - 2V_{tn}} \right)^{2} \;=\; \left( \frac{1.0 - 0.3}{1.0 - 0.6} \right)^{2} \;=\; \left( \frac{0.7}{0.4} \right)^{2} \;=\; (1.75)^{2} \;=\; 3.0625
$$

Available $CR = 2.5 < 3.0625 = CR_{\text{required}}$.

**Result: Cell is NOT read-stable.** Read-disturb will cause a stored '0' to flip. **Recommendation:** Increase driver NMOS width to raise $CR$ to at least 3.1 (i.e., $\sim$24% wider driver).

**(ii) Write-ability check:**

Apply the write-ability criterion:

$$
PR_{\text{required}} \;<\; \left( \frac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert} \right)^{2} \;=\; \left( \frac{1.0 - 0.3}{1.0 - 0.35} \right)^{2} \;=\; \left( \frac{0.7}{0.65} \right)^{2} \;=\; (1.0769)^{2} \;\approx\; 1.16
$$

Available $PR = 1.0 < 1.16 = PR_{\text{required}}$.

**Result: Cell IS writable.** The access NMOS is strong enough to overcome the PMOS load and flip the cell.

**Summary for (b):** The cell as designed passes write but **fails read-stability** — a classic design trade-off failure. The designer must increase CR (e.g., to 3.2) while keeping PR ≤ 1.16 to satisfy both.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cause Mark Deductions in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CR and PR** — CR uses the *driver NMOS* in the numerator; PR uses the *PMOS load*. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for swapped definitions.
> 2. **Forgetting that CR controls READ and PR controls WRITE** — A very common sign of half-baked preparation.
> 3. **Using $\geq$ instead of $>$ for the read criterion** — The strict inequality is required; $CR = $ critical value puts the cell at the metastable point (a single noise glitch flips it).
> 4. **Ignoring the D.C. read-disturb mechanism** — When asked "why does the cell flip during read?", many students say "WL is ON" (too vague). The correct answer involves the *voltage divider* between access and driver, raising the '0'-stored node above the inverter threshold.
> 5. **Drawing the 6T cell with PMOS access transistors** — Access transistors are **always NMOS** in a standard 6T cell (for symmetric read and write drive strengths).
> 6. **Skipping the precharge step in the Read operation** — Always mention BL = $\overline{BL}$ = $V_{DD}$ precharge.
> 7. **Confusing SNM (DC) with dynamic noise margin** — KTU Module 4 expects the **Seevinck butterfly-curve** definition specifically.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] The **6T SRAM cell** uses 2 PMOS loads, 2 NMOS drivers, and 2 NMOS access transistors.
- [x] It is **bistable** — two stable states held by regenerative positive feedback of cross-coupled inverters.
- [x] **Hold** state: WL = 0; **Read/Write** state: WL = $V_{DD}$.
- [x] **Cell Ratio (CR)** = driver β / access β — controls **read stability**. Typical $CR \geq 2$.
- [x] **Pull-up Ratio (PR)** = PMOS load β / access β — controls **write ability**. Typical $PR \leq 1$.
- [x] **Read-stability criterion:** $CR > \left( \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - 2V_{tn}} \right)^{2}$
- [x] **Write-ability criterion:** $PR < \left( \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{tn}}{V_{DD} - \vert V_{tp}\vert} \right)^{2}$
- [x] **SNM (Seevinck)** = side of largest inscribed square in the butterfly curve = $V_{IH} - V_{IL}$.
- [x] **Precharge BL and $\overline{BL}$ to $V_{DD}$** before every Read.
- [x] **Access transistors are NMOS** (not PMOS) for symmetric drive.
- [x] **Static power** = leakage dominated; **Dynamic power** $\propto C V_{DD}^{2} f$.
- [x] **No refresh needed** in SRAM (unlike DRAM) — hence "static".
- [x] **Read is non-destructive** (when properly sized) and **sense-amplifier-based**.
- [x] **Write is destructive of old value** and is driven by external bit-line drivers.
- [x] **Cell area** $\approx$ 100–150 $F^{2}$, where $F$ is the minimum feature size.
- [x] **6T cells are used in caches, register files, and TCAMs** — the workhorse of digital VLSI.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DRAM - Three Transistor and One Transistor Dynamic Memory Cell
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCEVT503 - VLSI TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** DRAM - Three Transistor and One Transistor Dynamic Memory Cell

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM) — Formal Definition

**Dynamic Random Access Memory (DRAM)** is a type of semiconductor memory that stores each bit of data as a **charge (or absence of charge) on a tiny capacitor** within an integrated circuit. The term *dynamic* signifies that the stored charge **leaks away with time** and therefore must be **periodically refreshed** (rewritten) to maintain data integrity. DRAM achieves the **highest density per unit silicon area** of all semiconductor memory types, making it the universal choice for the main memory in computing systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The two canonical DRAM cell architectures studied are the **Three-Transistor (3T) DRAM cell** and the **One-Transistor (1T) DRAM cell**. The 1T cell is the industry-standard because it occupies the smallest silicon footprint, while the 3T cell is historically significant and pedagogically important.

## 1.2 The Three-Transistor (3T) DRAM Cell — Definition

The **3T DRAM cell** is a dynamic memory cell composed of **three nMOS transistors (T1, T2, T3)**. Data is stored as **charge on the parasitic gate capacitance of T3**. Transistor **T1** acts as the read-access device, **T2** acts as the write-access device, and **T3** is the **storage transistor** whose gate node holds the data as a stored voltage.

- **Word Line (WL / Read Line):** Controls the read transistor T1.
- **Write Word Line (WWL):** Controls the write transistor T2.
- **Read Bit Line (RBL):** Precharged node whose voltage swing is sensed during a read.
- **Write Bit Line (WBL):** Carries the data to be written into the cell.
- **Storage Node (S):** The gate of T3, which is a high-impedance floating node holding the data.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property:** The **read operation in a 3T cell is NON-DESTRUCTIVE** — the storage capacitor does NOT share its charge with the bit line, so a read does NOT consume the stored data. This is a major advantage over the 1T cell.

## 1.3 The One-Transistor (1T) DRAM Cell — Definition

The **1T DRAM cell** is the **smallest possible dynamic memory cell**, consisting of just **one nMOS access transistor (T)** and **one storage capacitor (Cs)**. Data is stored as a **packet of charge on Cs**. The access transistor connects the storage capacitor to the bit line under the control of the word line.

- **Word Line (WL):** Controls the gate of the access transistor.
- **Bit Line (BL):** Carries data to/from the cell.
- **Storage Capacitor (Cs):** Stores the bit as an electrical charge.
- **Bit Line Capacitance (CBL):** The parasitic capacitance of the bit line, which is typically **5 to 10 times larger than Cs**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** The **read operation in a 1T cell is DESTRUCTIVE** — the charge stored on Cs is **redistributed (shared)** with the bit line capacitance CBL. Therefore, every read must be **immediately followed by a rewrite (refresh) operation**, which is automatically handled by the sense amplifier.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogies

### 🪣 The "Water Bucket" Analogy for the Storage Capacitor
Imagine each memory cell as a **small leaky water bucket**:
- A **full bucket** = logic '1' (stored charge = high voltage).
- An **empty bucket** = logic '0' (no charge = 0 V).
- The bucket has **small holes** (leakage currents), so water slowly drains out — this is why **periodic refilling (refresh)** is required.

### 🔌 The 3T Cell — "Three Switches Controlling a Bucket"
Think of the 3T cell as a **water tank (T3's gate) with two guarded taps**:
- **T2** is the **inlet tap** (controlled by the write word line). When opened, you can fill or empty the tank from the write bit line.
- **T1** is the **outlet tap** (controlled by the read word line). When opened, the tank's level controls whether the read bit line is pulled down to ground (via T3) or stays at its precharged level.
- The tank itself (T3) is the **sensor** that responds to the water level.

### 🛢️ The 1T Cell — "One Tap on a Tank"
The 1T cell is like a **single tap connecting the tank to a large communal pipe (the bit line)**. When you open the tap:
- The water in the tank **mixes** with the water in the pipe (this is **charge sharing** — the destructive read).
- A **delicate measuring instrument** (the sense amplifier) must detect the tiny change in the pipe's level.
- After measuring, you must **immediately refill the tank** to its original level (the auto-rewrite).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 3T DRAM Cell — Operational Analysis

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology

The 3T cell has the following terminal assignments:

| Transistor | Function | Drain | Gate | Source |
|------------|----------|-------|------|--------|
| **T1** (Read access) | Connects RBL to T3 drain | RBL | WL (Read) | Drain of T3 |
| **T2** (Write access) | Connects WBL to T3 gate | WBL | WWL (Write) | Storage Node S (Gate of T3) |
| **T3** (Storage) | Acts as a switch controlled by S | Source of T1 | Storage Node S | Ground (VSS) |

The **storage node S** is a **floating node** (connected only via the source of T2). Its voltage is maintained by the **gate capacitance of T3 plus parasitic capacitances**, which we denote as **Cs**.

### 2.1.2 Write Operation (Logic '1' and Logic '0')

- **Step 1:** WBL is driven to the desired data voltage (VDD for '1', 0 V for '0').
- **Step 2:** WWL is pulsed HIGH, turning ON T2.
- **Step 3:** The voltage on WBL is **transferred through T2 (pass transistor)** to the storage node S. The voltage at S is approximately V(WBL) − Vtn (the **threshold drop** of T2) when writing a '1'.
- **Step 4:** WWL returns LOW, isolating S. The charge is now **trapped on Cs**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does charge stay on S?** Once WWL goes LOW, T2 is OFF, and the storage node S is connected to a **high-impedance network** (only the gate of T3, which is a MOS gate — essentially infinite DC resistance). The only discharge paths are **subthreshold leakage** and **junction leakage**, which is why **periodic refresh is required**.

### 2.1.3 Read Operation (Non-Destructive)

- **Step 1:** RBL is **precharged to a high voltage** (typically VDD or VDD − Vtn) using a precharge transistor.
- **Step 2:** WL (read line) is pulsed HIGH, turning ON T1.
- **Step 3:** Now T3 is connected between RBL and ground, controlled by its gate voltage Vs.

**Two cases arise:**

- **If stored data = '1' (Vs is HIGH):** T3 is ON, so RBL is **pulled LOW** through T1 and T3. The sense amplifier detects this pull-down.
- **If stored data = '0' (Vs is LOW):** T3 is OFF, so RBL **stays HIGH**. The sense amplifier detects no change.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The data sensed on RBL is the INVERSE of the stored data** (a '1' on S produces a '0' swing on RBL, and a '0' on S produces a '1' on RBL). This inversion is handled externally by the sense amplifier.

### 2.1.4 Why is the 3T Read Non-Destructive?

Because **T1 connects the drain of T3 to the bit line, not the gate of T3**. The **storage node S is NOT connected to the bit line** during a read. The bit line is only connected to the **drain** of T3, which simply acts as a switch. Therefore, no charge is removed from Cs during a read.

### 2.1.5 Refresh Operation in 3T DRAM

Since S leaks through subthreshold current of T2 (when OFF) and T3 (when ON), a refresh cycle (read + rewrite of the original data) is performed every **few milliseconds** (typically **1–4 ms**).

## 2.2 The 1T DRAM Cell — Operational Analysis

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology

- **Access transistor T:** Gate = WL, Drain = BL, Source = top plate of Cs.
- **Storage capacitor Cs:** One plate connected to T's source, the other plate connected to **VSS/2 (or a reference plate voltage)** in modern designs.
- The capacitor is typically implemented as a **trench capacitor** (deep etched into the silicon) or a **stacked capacitor** (built above the transistor) to maximize capacitance per unit area.

### 2.2.2 Write Operation

- **Step 1:** BL is driven to the desired voltage (VDD for '1', 0 V for '0').
- **Step 2:** WL is raised to VDD + Vtn (a **boosted word-line voltage**) to ensure the full VDD is written onto Cs without a threshold drop.
- **Step 3:** The storage capacitor Cs charges (or discharges) to the BL voltage through the ON transistor T.
- **Step 4:** WL is lowered, isolating Cs. Charge is trapped on Cs.

### 2.2.3 Read Operation (Destructive — Charge Sharing)

This is the most critical operation to understand for KTU exams.

- **Step 1:** BL is **precharged to a reference voltage Vpre = VDD/2**.
- **Step 2:** WL is raised, turning ON T.
- **Step 3:** The storage capacitor Cs (charged to either 0 V or VDD) is **connected to CBL** (the bit-line capacitance, precharged to VDD/2).
- **Step 4:** **Charge redistribution** occurs between Cs and CBL. The two capacitors are now in parallel, and the final equilibrium voltage is:

$$V_{BL}^{final} = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

- **Step 5:** A **sense amplifier** detects the small voltage difference (ΔV) between VBL and the reference VDD/2, and amplifies it to a full-swing logic level.
- **Step 6:** **Auto-rewrite:** The sense amplifier drives the bit line back to a full '0' or '1', and this signal is **rewritten into the cell** through the still-ON access transistor, restoring the charge on Cs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the **read in 1T DRAM is destructive** and that a **refresh is MANDATORY after every read**. This is a guaranteed 2-mark trap question in KTU exams.

### 2.2.4 Why is the 1T Read Destructive?

Because **Cs is directly connected to the bit line through the access transistor**. After charge sharing, **Cs is no longer at 0 V or VDD** — it has been pulled to VBL, which is close to VDD/2. Without a rewrite, the data is effectively destroyed.

## 2.3 Sense Amplifier — The Heart of DRAM

A **sense amplifier** is a bistable latch (typically a cross-coupled pair of inverters) that detects the **tiny voltage differential** (~150–250 mV) developed on the bit line and amplifies it to a **full-rail CMOS level** (0 V or VDD). It also performs the **automatic rewrite** in 1T DRAM.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the sense amplifier so important?** The voltage developed on the bit line during a 1T read is typically only **~250 mV** (if Cs = 30 fF and CBL = 300 fF, ΔV ≈ 250 mV). Without amplification, this small signal would be lost in noise. The sense amplifier is what makes high-density 1T DRAM practical.

## 2.4 Refresh — The "Dynamic" in DRAM

Both 3T and 1T DRAM cells suffer from charge leakage, but for **different physical reasons**:

| Cell Type | Leakage Path | Approximate Refresh Interval |
|-----------|--------------|------------------------------|
| **3T** | Subthreshold current of T2 (OFF) and T3 (ON); junction leakage at storage node | 1 – 4 ms |
| **1T** | Subthreshold current of access transistor T; junction leakage at storage node | 64 ms (standard) |

**Refresh operation** is performed by:
- Cycling through each row of the memory array
- Reading and rewriting each cell
- This consumes time and reduces effective memory bandwidth

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Bit-line voltage after charge sharing | $V_{BL} = \dfrac{C_s V_s + C_{BL} V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$ | Final BL voltage after 1T read |
| 2 | Voltage swing on bit line | $\Delta V = V_{BL} - V_{pre} = \dfrac{C_s (V_s - V_{pre})}{C_s + C_{BL}}$ | Differential read signal |
| 3 | Approx. ΔV (when Vs = VDD, Vpre = VDD/2) | $\Delta V \approx \dfrac{C_s \cdot V_{DD}}{2(C_s + C_{BL})}$ | Maximum signal for stored '1' |
| 4 | Typical cell-to-bitline capacitance ratio | $r = \dfrac{C_s}{C_{BL}} \approx \dfrac{1}{5}\ \text{to}\ \dfrac{1}{10}$ | Determines ΔV magnitude |
| 5 | Charge stored on capacitor | $Q = C_s \cdot V_s$ | Fundamental capacitor equation |
| 6 | Energy per write operation | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} C_s V_{DD}^2$ | Energy to charge Cs to VDD |
| 7 | Refresh time per row | $t_{ref} = N_{rows} \times t_{access}$ | Total time to refresh entire array |
| 8 | Effective refresh interval | $T_{ref} = 64\ \text{ms}$ (industry standard) | Max time between refreshes |
| 9 | Refresh overhead | $\% \text{overhead} = \dfrac{t_{ref}}{T_{refresh\_cycle}} \times 100$ | Performance loss due to refresh |
| 10 | Boosted word-line voltage | $V_{WL} = V_{DD} + V_{tn}$ | Ensures full VDD write (no Vt drop) |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **1T DRAM** is the backbone of **all main memory in computers, smartphones, and servers** (DDR4, DDR5, LPDDR).
- **3T DRAM** was used in early DRAM products (e.g., Intel 1103, 1970) but is now obsolete in commercial products. It is studied in VLSI courses because its **non-destructive read** makes it useful in **specialty analog memory** and **in-pixel memory in CMOS image sensors**.
- The **charge-sharing read** of 1T DRAM is the physical basis for **DRAM-based Physical Unclonable Functions (PUFs)**, used in hardware security.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Bit-Line Voltage After Charge Sharing in 1T DRAM Read

This is the most important derivation for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. We derive the expression for the final bit-line voltage after the storage capacitor Cs is connected to the bit-line capacitance CBL.

### 3.1.1 Initial Conditions (Before WL is Raised)

- The storage capacitor Cs is charged to a voltage **Vs**, where:
  - **Vs = VDD** for a stored logic '1'
  - **Vs = 0 V** for a stored logic '0'
- The bit line is precharged to **Vpre = VDD/2** using a precharge circuit.

Therefore:
- **Initial charge on Cs:** $Q_s^{initial} = C_s \cdot V_s$
- **Initial charge on CBL:** $Q_{BL}^{initial} = C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}$

### 3.1.2 Conservation of Charge

When the word line is raised and the access transistor T turns ON, the storage node S and the bit line BL are connected through a low-resistance channel. Because the channel is a **conducting wire** (in the ideal switch model), the **total charge is conserved** (the switch itself has no capacitance, ignoring channel charge for now).

The **total initial charge** on the floating system (Cs + CBL, isolated from other supplies) is:

$$Q_{total}^{initial} = Q_s^{initial} + Q_{BL}^{initial} = C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}$$

### 3.1.3 Final State (After Charge Redistribution)

After charge sharing, the two capacitors are effectively in **parallel** (because the access transistor shorts them), and they reach a common final voltage **VBL** (which is the new voltage on both the BL and the top plate of Cs).

The **total final charge** on the system is:

$$Q_{total}^{final} = (C_s + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL}$$

### 3.1.4 Equating Initial and Final Charge

By the **principle of conservation of charge** (no charge is added or removed during the switching transient):

$$Q_{total}^{initial} = Q_{total}^{final}$$

Substituting:

$$C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre} = (C_s + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL}$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for VBL

Rearranging the equation to isolate VBL:

$$(C_s + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL} = C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}$$

Dividing both sides by $(C_s + C_{BL})$:

$$V_{BL} = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

This is the **fundamental charge-sharing equation** for the 1T DRAM read.

### 3.1.6 Deriving the Voltage Swing (ΔV)

The **differential signal** that the sense amplifier must detect is:

$$\Delta V = V_{BL} - V_{pre}$$

Substituting the VBL expression:

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}} - V_{pre}$$

Multiplying Vpre by $\frac{C_s + C_{BL}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$ to get a common denominator:

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre} - V_{pre} \cdot (C_s + C_{BL})}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

The numerator simplifies (CBL·Vpre cancels with the CBL·Vpre term from the expansion):

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s - C_s \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}} = \frac{C_s \cdot (V_s - V_{pre})}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

Factoring Cs:

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot (V_s - V_{pre})$$

This is the **KTU 14-mark standard answer** for the voltage-swing derivation.

### 3.1.7 Special Case: Reading a Stored '1'

When reading a stored '1', Vs = VDD, and Vpre = VDD/2:

$$\Delta V_{1} = \frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot \left(V_{DD} - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) = \frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

For a typical case where CBL = 10·Cs:

$$\Delta V_{1} = \frac{C_s}{C_s + 10 C_s} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2} = \frac{1}{11} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

If VDD = 1.0 V (modern DRAM), then:

$$\Delta V_{1} = \frac{1}{11} \times 0.5\ \text{V} \approx 45.5\ \text{mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The sense amplifier must reliably detect voltage swings of **less than 100 mV** in modern 1T DRAM. This is why the sense amplifier is one of the most critical analog circuits in any DRAM chip.

### 3.1.8 Special Case: Reading a Stored '0'

When reading a stored '0', Vs = 0 V, and Vpre = VDD/2:

$$\Delta V_{0} = \frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot \left(0 - \frac{V_{DD}}{2}\right) = -\frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot \frac{V_{DD}}{2}$$

The negative sign indicates that the bit line voltage is **pulled below VDD/2** by approximately 45.5 mV (for the same CBL/Cs = 10 case).

### 3.1.9 Symmetry Check

The two cases (reading '1' and reading '0') produce **equal and opposite voltage swings** about Vpre = VDD/2. This is by design — it maximizes noise immunity and allows a differential sense amplifier to detect the polarity of the swing.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: 3T vs 1T DRAM Comparison

This comparison matrix is a high-yield KTU question (often asked as a 7-mark sub-question).

| Parameter | **3T DRAM Cell** | **1T DRAM Cell** |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Number of transistors** | 3 | 1 |
| **Storage element** | Gate capacitance of T3 | Dedicated capacitor Cs |
| **Cell area** | Large (~3T + cap) | Smallest (~1T + cap) |
| **Read mechanism** | Drain of T3 connected to RBL; T3 acts as switch | Charge sharing between Cs and CBL |
| **Read destructive?** | **No** (non-destructive) | **Yes** (destructive) |
| **Read output polarity** | Inverted (sense amp corrects) | Same as stored data |
| **Write mechanism** | Pass-transistor transfer to gate of T3 | Direct connection of BL to Cs |
| **Vt drop on write** | Yes (~Vt loss, but acceptable) | No (boosted WL used: VDD + Vt) |
| **Sense amplifier** | Simple (detects full swing) | Complex (detects small ΔV ~250 mV) |
| **Refresh needed?** | Yes (1–4 ms) | Yes (64 ms) |
| **Process complexity** | Standard CMOS | Needs special capacitor process |
| **Density** | Low | **Highest** (industry standard) |
| **Power per bit** | Higher | Lower |
| **Used in modern systems?** | No (obsolete) | **Yes (DDR, LPDDR, GDDR)** |

## 3.3 Derivation: Refresh Overhead

The total time required to refresh an entire DRAM array is:

$$t_{ref}^{total} = N_{rows} \times t_{access}^{row}$$

The **percentage of time** spent on refresh (refresh overhead) is:

$$\%\ \text{overhead} = \frac{t_{ref}^{total}}{T_{refresh\_cycle}} \times 100$$

For a standard DDR4 chip with N = 8192 rows, row access time t = 50 ns, and T = 64 ms:

$$t_{ref}^{total} = 8192 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 409.6\ \mu s$$

$$\%\ \text{overhead} = \frac{409.6 \times 10^{-6}}{64 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100 \approx 0.64\%$$

This is why refresh is acceptable — the overhead is small (typically < 1%).

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following diagrams use Mermaid's **Block-Level Functional Architecture** format to clearly represent the signal flow and operational steps of the 3T and 1T DRAM cells. Physical transistor-level schematics are described in textual form, while the mermaid diagrams convey the operational logic and signal flow.

## 4.1 3T DRAM Cell — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    WWL[Write Word Line WWL] --> T2[Transistor T2 - Write Access]
    WBL[Write Bit Line WBL] --> T2
    T2 --> SNode[Storage Node S - Gate of T3]
    SNode --> T3[Transistor T3 - Storage Switch]
    T3 --> GND[Ground VSS]
    WL[Read Word Line WL] --> T1[Transistor T1 - Read Access]
    RBL[Read Bit Line RBL] --> T1
    T1 --> T3
    SNode -.-> CsLeak[Parasitic Capacitance Cs]
    PreChg[Precharge Circuit VDD] --> RBL
    SenseAmp[Sense Amplifier] --> RBL
    SenseAmp --> DataOut[Data Out Inverted]
```

**Signal Flow Description:**
- **Write Path:** WBL → T2 (gated by WWL) → Storage Node S → charges Cs
- **Read Path:** RBL (precharged to VDD) → T1 (gated by WL) → T3 (gated by Vs at S) → GND
- **Storage Path:** Cs holds the gate voltage of T3

## 4.2 1T DRAM Cell — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    WL[Word Line WL] --> T1[Access Transistor T]
    BL[Bit Line BL] --> T1
    T1 --> Cs[Storage Capacitor Cs]
    Cs --> Plate[Reference Plate VSS or VSS/2]
    BLPre[Precharge to VDD/2] --> BL
    BL --> SenseAmp[Differential Sense Amplifier]
    SenseAmp --> SAOut[Amplified Data Out]
    SenseAmp -.Auto Rewrite.-> T1
    RefreshCtrl[Refresh Controller every 64 ms] --> SenseAmp
```

**Signal Flow Description:**
- **Write Path:** BL (driven to VDD or 0) → T (gated by boosted WL) → Cs
- **Read Path:** BL (precharged to VDD/2) ↔ Cs (charged to VDD or 0) — destructive charge sharing
- **Sense & Restore:** Sense amplifier detects ΔV and rewrites the cell

## 4.3 1T DRAM Read Operation — Detailed Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start([Read Cycle Initiated]) --> A1[Step 1 Precharge BL to VDD/2]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Isolate BL from Precharge]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Raise WL to VDD plus Vtn]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Access Transistor Turns ON]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Charge Sharing Cs with CBL]
    A5 --> A6{Step 6 Was Stored Data}
    A6 -->|Logic 1| B1[VBL rises to VDD/2 plus deltaV]
    A6 -->|Logic 0| B2[VBL falls to VDD/2 minus deltaV]
    B1 --> C1[Step 7 Sense Amplifier Latches]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Step 8 Auto Rewrite Cell to Full VDD or 0]
    C2 --> C3[Step 9 Lower WL to 0 V]
    C3 --> End([Read Complete Data Restored])
```

## 4.4 DRAM Refresh Operation — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    Timer[Refresh Timer e.g. 64 ms interval] --> FSM[Refresh State Machine]
    FSM --> RowSel[Row Address Counter 0 to N-1]
    RowSel --> Act[Activate Row Read All Cells in Row]
    Act --> Sense[Sense Amplifiers Detect and Amplify]
    Sense --> Write[Rewrite All Cells in Row - Full Voltage]
    Write --> Inc[Increment Row Counter]
    Inc --> Chk{All Rows Refreshed}
    Chk -->|No| Act
    Chk -->|Yes| Wait[Wait for Next Timer Tick]
    Wait --> Timer
```

**Key Insight:** Refresh is essentially a **read-rewrite cycle performed on every row**, with the only difference being that the sense amplifier output is **written back into the same cell** — there is no data transfer to the output buffers.

## 4.5 Sense Amplifier Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    BL[Bit Line BL with small deltaV signal] --> SAInput[Sense Amplifier Differential Inputs]
    BLBar[Bit Line Bar - Reference] --> SAInput
    SAInput --> Latch[Cross Coupled Inverter Latch]
    LatchCtrl[Sense Enable Signal SAE] --> Latch
    Latch --> FullSwing[Full Rail Output 0 V or VDD]
    FullSwing --> OutData[Output Data to Buffer]
    FullSwing --> Restore[Write Back to Cell for 1T DRAM]
    Restore --> Cell[Restore Charge on Cs]
```

**Functional Stages:**
1. **Equalization:** BL and BL_bar brought to the same voltage (VDD/2)
2. **Sensing:** SAE rises, latch regeneratively amplifies the small ΔV
3. **Restoration:** Amplified signal is driven back onto the bit line, rewriting the cell
