# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a 3T and a 1T DRAM cell. Which one is used in modern memory systems and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 3T DRAM | 1T DRAM |
|---------|---------|---------|
| Transistors per cell | 3 | 1 |
| Cell area | Larger | Smallest (highest density) |
| Read type | Non-destructive | Destructive (needs rewrite) |
| Storage element | Gate capacitance of T3 | Dedicated capacitor |
| Read signal | Full swing (simple sense amp) | Small ΔV from charge sharing |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **1T DRAM cell** is used in **all modern memory systems (DDR, LPDDR, GDDR, HBM)** because it offers the **highest density per unit area** (smallest cell), enabling multi-gigabit memory chips at low cost. Although it requires a destructive read and a sensitive sense amplifier, the area advantage overwhelmingly dominates.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**"Why does a 1T DRAM cell require periodic refresh? Explain briefly."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A 1T DRAM cell stores data as **charge on a tiny capacitor Cs**. Once the word line is lowered after a write, the storage node is **electrically floating** (connected only to the source of the access transistor, whose gate is off).

The charge leaks away through the following paths:
- **Subthreshold leakage current** of the access transistor (off-state).
- **Junction leakage** at the storage node's p-n junction.
- **Gate-oxide tunneling leakage** (in advanced nodes).

To prevent the stored data from being corrupted, the cell must be **read and rewritten** periodically. The **standard refresh interval is 64 ms**, during which every row of the array is read and rewritten. Without refresh, the stored data would be lost within a few hundred milliseconds.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. Students are given an **internal choice** between two question alternatives.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"With a neat schematic, explain the construction and operation of a 3-transistor (3T) dynamic RAM cell. Clearly describe the write, read, and refresh operations."**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
The 3T DRAM cell consists of three nMOS transistors T1, T2, and T3, plus a parasitic gate capacitance Cs on the storage node.
- **T1 (Read access):** Drain = Read Bit Line (RBL), Gate = Word Line (WL), Source = Drain of T3
- **T2 (Write access):** Drain = Write Bit Line (WBL), Gate = Write Word Line (WWL), Source = Storage Node S
- **T3 (Storage):** Gate = Storage Node S, Drain = Source of T1, Source = Ground (VSS)
- **Storage node S** is a high-impedance floating node holding the data as a voltage on Cs.

**Write Operation (2 marks):**
- WBL is driven to the data voltage (VDD for '1' or 0 V for '0').
- WWL is pulsed HIGH, turning ON T2, which connects WBL to S.
- The voltage on S becomes approximately V(WBL) − Vtn (for writing a '1').
- WWL returns LOW; S is now isolated and holds the charge on Cs.

**Read Operation (2 marks):**
- RBL is precharged HIGH (to VDD or VDD − Vtn).
- WL is pulsed HIGH, turning ON T1.
- **If Vs = '1':** T3 is ON, RBL is pulled LOW through T1 and T3.
- **If Vs = '0':** T3 is OFF, RBL remains HIGH.
- The sense amplifier detects the swing and inverts it to recover the original data.
- **The read is non-destructive** because S is not connected to the bit line.

**Refresh Operation (1 mark):**
- Because S leaks charge, the cell must be refreshed every 1–4 ms.
- Refresh is performed by reading the cell and immediately rewriting the same data back.

---

#### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]**
**"A 1T DRAM cell has a storage capacitance of 25 fF, and the bit line has a parasitic capacitance of 250 fF. The bit line is precharged to 0.5 V. If the cell stores a '1' (i.e., Cs is charged to 1.0 V), calculate the voltage developed on the bit line after the word line is raised. Assume VDD = 1.0 V. Comment on the magnitude of the signal."**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Cs = 25 fF
- CBL = 250 fF
- Vs (stored '1') = 1.0 V
- Vpre = 0.5 V (= VDD/2)

**Step 1: Apply the charge-sharing equation (2 marks)**

$$V_{BL} = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks)**

$$V_{BL} = \frac{(25 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (1.0) + (250 \times 10^{-15}) \cdot (0.5)}{(25 \times 10^{-15}) + (250 \times 10^{-15})}$$

$$V_{BL} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 1.0 + 125 \times 10^{-15}}{275 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$V_{BL} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-15} + 125 \times 10^{-15}}{275 \times 10^{-15}}$$

**Step 3: Simplify (1 mark)**

$$V_{BL} = \frac{150 \times 10^{-15}}{275 \times 10^{-15}} = \frac{150}{275} = \frac{6}{11} \approx 0.545\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4: Calculate ΔV (1 mark)**

$$\Delta V = V_{BL} - V_{pre} = 0.545 - 0.500 = 0.045\ \text{V} = 45\ \text{mV}$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The voltage developed on the bit line is only **45 mV**, which is a very small signal. It is highly susceptible to noise and process variations, which is why a **high-gain, low-offset differential sense amplifier** is essential for reliable operation of 1T DRAM.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
**"Explain in detail the operation of a 1-transistor (1T) DRAM cell during a read cycle. Derive the expression for the bit-line voltage after charge sharing. Why is the read destructive?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Schematic Description (1 mark):**
The 1T DRAM cell has a single nMOS access transistor T and a storage capacitor Cs. The gate of T is connected to the word line (WL), its drain to the bit line (BL), and its source to the top plate of Cs. The bottom plate of Cs is connected to a reference (VSS/2).

**Read Operation Sequence (3 marks):**
1. **Precharge:** The bit line is precharged to a reference voltage Vpre = VDD/2 through a precharge transistor.
2. **Word-line activation:** WL is raised to VDD + Vtn, turning ON T.
3. **Charge sharing:** Cs (charged to 0 V or VDD) is connected to CBL (precharged to VDD/2). Charge redistributes between them.
4. **Sensing:** A differential sense amplifier detects the small ΔV developed on the bit line.
5. **Auto-rewrite:** The sense amplifier drives the bit line to a full rail, and the cell is automatically rewritten.
6. **Word-line deactivation:** WL is lowered, isolating the cell.

**Derivation of Bit-Line Voltage (2 marks):**
By conservation of charge:

$$C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre} = (C_s + C_{BL}) \cdot V_{BL}$$

$$V_{BL} = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

**Why the read is destructive (1 mark):**
After charge sharing, the storage capacitor Cs is no longer at 0 V or VDD — it has been pulled close to VDD/2, destroying the original data. The sense amplifier's auto-rewrite is therefore mandatory; without it, the data would be permanently lost.

---

#### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]**
**"Compare the 3T and 1T DRAM cells in terms of: (i) cell area, (ii) read mechanism, (iii) destructive vs non-destructive read, (iv) sense amplifier complexity, (v) refresh requirement, and (vi) industrial relevance. Justify why the 1T cell became the industry standard despite its destructive read."**

**Model Answer (organized as a comparative table + justification):**

| Parameter | 3T DRAM | 1T DRAM |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| (i) Cell area | Large (3T + parasitic cap) | Smallest (1T + cap) |
| (ii) Read mechanism | T3 acts as switch, RBL pulled to GND or stays HIGH | Charge sharing between Cs and CBL |
| (iii) Read nature | **Non-destructive** | **Destructive** (requires rewrite) |
| (iv) Sense amp complexity | Simple (full-swing detection) | Complex (small ΔV detection) |
| (v) Refresh | Required (1–4 ms) | Required (64 ms) |
| (vi) Industrial relevance | Obsolete | Industry standard (DDR, LPDDR) |

**Justification for 1T dominance (2 marks):**
The 1T DRAM cell became the industry standard for the following reasons:
1. **Highest density:** 1T + 1 capacitor occupies the smallest silicon area (~6F² per cell using modern layout techniques), enabling multi-gigabit chips.
2. **Lower cost per bit:** Smaller area → more chips per wafer → lower cost.
3. **Lower power:** Less capacitance to switch per cell.
4. **The destructive-read penalty is acceptable** because the sense amplifier can rewrite the cell in a single cycle, and the refresh overhead is < 1% of total bandwidth.
5. The **complex sense amplifier** is amortized over millions of cells, so its silicon cost is negligible.

Hence, the 1T cell's area advantage outweighs the cost of the sense amplifier, making it the universal choice for modern main memory.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in DRAM questions (KTU 2024 Scheme):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the threshold drop:** When writing to a 3T cell, students often write "S is charged to VDD" — but the correct answer is **VDD − Vtn** (for an nMOS pass transistor). **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Inverting/non-inverting read:** In a 3T cell, the read output on RBL is the **INVERSE** of the stored data. Students who state "RBL = '1' when Vs = '1'" lose a mark.
> 3. **Destructive read in 1T:** Forgetting to mention that the 1T read is destructive and that a refresh is mandatory is a **very common 2-mark loss**.
> 4. **Charge-sharing equation setup:** Students often write $V_{BL} = \dfrac{C_s}{C_{BL}} \cdot V_s$ (dimensionally and physically wrong). The correct setup is the **weighted average with CBL and Cs in the denominator**.
> 5. **No mention of sense amplifier:** In 1T DRAM, the sense amplifier is **not optional** — it is essential to detect the tiny ΔV (~250 mV) and to perform the auto-rewrite. **(−1 mark if omitted)**
> 6. **Boosted word line:** When writing to a 1T cell, the WL must be raised to **VDD + Vtn** to overcome the threshold drop of the access transistor. Forgetting this loses a mark.
> 7. **Refresh interval:** For 1T DRAM, the standard is **64 ms**, not "a few milliseconds" (that is the 3T value).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **DRAM:** Memory that stores data as **charge on a capacitor**; requires **periodic refresh** because charge leaks.
- **3T DRAM:** 3-transistor cell; **non-destructive read**; obsolete in industry.
- **1T DRAM:** 1-transistor + 1-capacitor cell; **destructive read**; industry standard.

### 🔑 Key Transistor Roles in 3T Cell
- **T1:** Read access (gate = WL, drain = RBL, source = drain of T3).
- **T2:** Write access (gate = WWL, drain = WBL, source = storage node S).
- **T3:** Storage switch (gate = S, source = GND, drain = source of T1).

### 🔑 Key Equations to Memorize
$$V_{BL} = \frac{C_s \cdot V_s + C_{BL} \cdot V_{pre}}{C_s + C_{BL}}$$

$$\Delta V = \frac{C_s}{C_s + C_{BL}} \cdot (V_s - V_{pre})$$

$$Q = C_s \cdot V_s \quad ; \quad E = \frac{1}{2} C_s V_{DD}^2$$

### 🔑 Critical Numbers to Know
- **CBL / Cs ratio:** typically 5:1 to 10:1.
- **ΔV on bit line:** typically 100 – 250 mV (very small signal).
- **3T refresh interval:** 1 – 4 ms.
- **1T refresh interval:** 64 ms (industry standard).
- **Boosted WL voltage:** VDD + Vtn.
- **Vpre in 1T read:** VDD/2.

### 🔑 Operational Mnemonics
- **3T Read:** "RBL goes LOW when stored '1' — **inverted output**."
- **1T Read:** "Tiny ΔV, then **auto-rewrite by sense amp**."
- **Both cells need refresh:** "Stored charge is **fugitive** — it must be **renewed** periodically."

### 🔑 Comparison Verdict
- **Density winner:** 1T (smallest cell).
- **Read simplicity winner:** 3T (no sense amp needed).
- **Industrial winner:** 1T (used in **every computer and smartphone** in the world).
- **Modern innovation:** 1T cell with **3D stacked capacitors** (used in DDR5 and HBM) — but the fundamental cell topology is unchanged.

### 🔑 One-Sentence Summary for Last-Minute Revision
> *"A 1T DRAM cell is the smallest memory cell — one transistor gates a tiny storage capacitor to the bit line; reading destroys the charge, so a sense amplifier must amplify a ~200 mV differential and immediately rewrite the cell, with the entire array refreshed every 64 ms; a 3T DRAM cell uses three transistors for non-destructive read but is too large for modern high-density memory."*
